Paper Title: The environmental factors affecting solar photovoltaic output
Outcome(s) of Interest: Reduced solar PV electricity output and efficiency.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: raising module temperature reduces efficiency by 0.4–0.5 % per degree Celsius, limiting productivity in hotter climates...atmospheric conditions (clouds, aerosols, pollutants, and dust) can reduce electricity output by up to 60 %, especially in desert regions...extreme weather like wildfires and hailstorms cause substantial damage...long-term changes in solar irradiance, driven by climate change and air pollutants, present future challenges for maintaining PV efficiency.
Drivers of Risk: Solar irradiance, ambient temperature, atmospheric conditions, terrain effects, extreme weather, climate change.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Page 2: The drivers of risk include solar irradiance, ambient temperature, atmospheric conditions (clouds, aerosols, pollutants, and dust), terrain effects (albedo, snow), extreme weather events (wildfires, hailstorms, solar eclipses), and long-term irradiance changes driven by climate change and air pollutants. Page 13: The drivers of risk identified are snow accumulation on modules, temperature, humidity, and climate change impacts like increased wildfires and hailstorms. Page 15: Climate change will affect many of the factors that reduce solar PV performance, including increasing humidity and frequency of wildfires and hailstorms as discussed in the previous sections. Page 16: Solar irradiance, temperature, cloud cover, dust and pollutants, snow cover, albedo, and extreme weather events
Category-sourcematerial: Page 2: This paper provides an overview of factors affecting solar photovoltaic (PV) output.; Page 7: Evaluates the impact of temperature on solar PV module power output and efficiency.; Page 11: The page discusses the impact of dust and humidity on solar PV power output and efficiency.; Page 13: The paper provides an overview of the impact of snow cover on solar PV modules, highlighting energy loss due to snow accumulation and the influence of snow on solar irradiance and energy generation.; Page 15: The page evaluates the impact of solar eclipses on solar PV systems, particularly focusing on capacity factors as a measure of productivity.; Page 16: Solar PV performance
Approach-sourcematerial: The paper is a comprehensive review of the effects of environmental factors on solar PV output. It considers geographic and temporal variability. Parameters are measured using the STC and PTC standards. Analysis of I-V and P-V curves at different temperatures at a constant irradiance intensity, and statistical analysis of temperature coefficients from a large database of commercially available solar PV modules. Results are applicable to commercially available solar PV modules. Global maps of average albedo in summer and winter months; Measurements averaged over June, July, August 2014–2024 for summer, December, January, February 2014–2024 for winter.
Methodology: Methodology includes: STC and PTC standards for parameter measurement; daily average capacity factor summarized over 30 years; Average Photon Energy (APE) calculation (ratio of integrated irradiance to photon flux density); module operating temperature determination via heat balance; analysis of I-V and P-V curves at varying temperatures; statistical analysis of temperature coefficients; Nominal Operating Cell Temperature (NOCT) testing; categorization of cleaning methods (mechanical, self-cleaning, electrostatic, natural) based on dust particle size; and albedo measurements averaged over June-August and December-February 2014-2024.
Methodology-sourcematerial: The paper references key methodologies, including: "Parameters are measured using the STC and PTC standards..."; "The daily average capacity factor is summarized over 30 years"; "APE is the ratio of integrated irradiance to photon flux density"; "Module operating temperature is determined by the balance of heat generation and heat loss to the environment"; "Analysis of I-V and P-V curves at different temperatures"; "Statistical analysis of temperature coefficients from a large database of commercially available solar PV modules"; "Nominal Operating Cell Temperature (NOCT) provides a standardized test to assess PV module temperature"; "Cleaning methods can be classified under four broad categories...The choice...depends on dust particle size"; "Maps are based on measurements averaged over the months of June, July, and August 2014–2024 for summer, and December, January, and February 2014–2024 for winter."
Highlights: Hurricanes can disrupt power generation, but resilient systems like Babcock Ranch maintained power during Hurricane Ian; Fresh snow has a high albedo; Technologies that monitor and remove impurities using wave motion can be effective; Electrostatic cleaning is suitable in regions facing water scarcity; Wind can increase efficiency by cooling modules.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Page 14: Hurricanes can disrupt power generation, but resilient systems like Babcock Ranch maintained power during Hurricane Ian.; Page 13: Fresh snow has a high albedo, reflecting 96–98% of solar irradiance in the visible and near-UV range and ~80% across the full spectrum; Page 11: Technologies that monitor and remove impurities using wave motion can be effective.; Page 11: Electrostatic cleaning is suitable in regions facing water scarcity, offering swift removal of dust with less energy and no need for water and human intervention.; Page 12: Wind can increase efficiency by cooling modules during peak sunlight hours; dynamics of wind flow create lift forces.
More Notes: Solar PV performance is affected by geographical variations in irradiance (GHI), as well as aerosol optical thickness. Dust can reduce reflections and humidity can improve module efficiency by lowering its temperature, while wind impacts depend on module placement and array spacing, and fences can provide a sheltering effect. Albedo can increase energy generation, but wildfires reduce performance due to aerosol blockage.
More Notes-sourcematerial: "Fig. 4. The geographical variation in global horizontal irradiance (GHI) from 1994/1999/2007 (depending on the location) to 2018 [31]." | "Fig. 13. Global map of aerosol optical thickness averaged over January 2019 to December 2023." | "...a tenuous positive impact is that dust reduces large-angle reflections from the cover glass, increasing captured radiation." | "Increased humidity improves a module’s efficiency by lowering its temperature…" | "Modules at edges shelter those at the interior, reducing wind stress, with the effect dependent on array spacing; fences provide similar sheltering effect." | "The increased reflection from higher albedo surfaces increases the insolation incident on solar modules, leading to increased energy generation [186]. Wildfires produce large quantities of aerosols that reduce solar PV performance by blocking sunlight. In California, where solar power provides nearly 20% of electricity, the extreme wildfires in September 2020 reduced solar energy production by 30% [212]."
Hazard definition-sourcematerial: First, solar irradiance has strong geographic and temporal variability, making it the most significant factor. Second, raising module temperature reduces efficiency by 0.4–0.5 % per degree Celsius, limiting productivity in hotter climates. Third, atmospheric conditions (clouds, aerosols, pollutants, and dust) can reduce electricity output by up to 60 %, especially in desert regions. Fourth, terrain factors like albedo and snow present mixed effects, with increased reflection boosting output but snow obstructing panels. Fifth, extreme weather like wildfires and hailstorms cause substantial damage, while solar eclipses lead to large but short-lived output losses. Finally, long-term changes in solar irradiance, driven by climate change and air pollutants, present future challenges for maintaining PV efficiency.

Adaptation considered: Cleaning methods (mechanical, self-cleaning, electrostatic, natural) address dust accumulation, increasing capacity factors by 50-100% even with quarterly cleaning. Self-cleaning coatings and technologies using wave motion offer efficient dust removal. Electrostatic cleaning suits water-scarce regions. Wind interaction, module temperature and humidity influence efficiency.

"Self-cleaning coatings, along with chemical cleaning, can achieve a cleaning efficiency of 72 % without extra components or water." Paper Title: Climate Shocks in the Anthropocene Era: Should Net Domestic Product Reflect Climate Disasters?
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased asset depreciation, lower NDP and net domestic investment.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "...implies a faster depreciation of real assets...the expected depreciation from these events should be included in consumption of fixed capital (CFC), leading to lower levels and slightly lower growth rates for net domestic product (NDP) and net domestic investment."
Drivers of Risk: Extreme weather; floods; storms; fires; droughts; heat waves; hurricanes; earthquakes; volcanic eruptions; tidal waves; political events; technological accidents; asset damage.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "Floods, storms, fires, droughts, heat waves (US weather and climate disasters)": "Disasters such as floods, storms, fires, droughts, heat waves."; "Large scale, discrete and recognizable events that may destroy a significantly large number of assets within any of the asset categories. Such events will generally be easy to identify. They include major earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, tidal waves, exceptionally severe hurricanes, drought and other natural disasters; acts of war, riots and other political events; and technological accidents such as major toxic spills or release of radioactive particles into the air."
Category-sourcematerial: Page 6: Asset damage from weather and climate disasters; Page 8: Costs of weather and climate disasters (floods, storms, fires, droughts, heat waves); Page 9: The figure describes 'BEA and NOAA Costs,' suggesting an evaluation of economic costs related to climate or weather events tracked by these agencies.; Page 10: The paper provides an overview of economic impacts of billion-dollar weather and climate disasters on consumption of fixed capital and net domestic product (NDP).
Approach-sourcematerial: Uses NOAA data on weather and climate disasters since 1980 (costs > $1 billion, CPI deflated); Uses BEA data on disasters bigger than 0.1% of GDP; Extrapolates government and private insurance data to cover uninsured claims for NOAA data.
Methodology: The methodology employs Poisson pseudo-maximum-likelihood regressions to estimate expected asset depreciation from climate disasters and uses NOAA data on weather and climate disasters since 1980 (costs > $1 billion, CPI deflated) and BEA data on disasters bigger than 0.1% of GDP, extrapolating government and private insurance data to cover uninsured claims for NOAA data.

Paper Title: Projected response of algal blooms in global lakes to future climatic and land use changes: Machine learning approaches
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased algal blooms in lakes, worsening intensity under high forcing scenarios (SSP370 & SSP585), primarily in Africa, Asia, and North America.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: The predicted results showed that, by the end of this century, the number of lakes experiencing algal blooms and the intensity of these blooms will worsen under higher forcing scenarios (SSP370 and SSP585) (p < 0.05). In different regions, lakes with increasing algal blooms are mainly distributed in Africa, Asia, and North America...This study revealed the spatiotemporal distribution of algal blooms in global lakes from 2020 to 2100 and suggested that the intensifying algal blooms due to global warming and human activities may offset the effort of controlling the water quality.
Drivers of Risk: Global warming, human activities, socio-economic development scenarios (SSP370, SSP585), population growth, economic growth.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: With ongoing global warming and intensifying human activities, water quality trends in lakes worldwide varied significantly... the number of lakes experiencing algal blooms and the intensity of these blooms will worsen under higher forcing scenarios (SSP370 and SSP585)... underdeveloped regions, such as Africa, exhibit greater sensitivity to different SSP scenarios due to high variability in population and economic growth... intensifying algal blooms due to global warming and human activities may offset the effort of controlling the water quality.
Category-sourcematerial: "The eutrophication of lakes and the subsequent algal blooms have become significant environmental issues of global concern in recent years." "We aimed to develop a global projection model to evaluate the future trend in algal bloom occurrences in large lakes under various socio-economic development scenarios. We focused our research on 161 natural lakes worldwide, each exceeding 500 km2." "This study revealed the spatiotemporal distribution of algal blooms in global lakes from 2020 to 2100 and suggested that the intensifying algal blooms due to global warming and human activities may offset the effort of controlling the water quality."
Approach-sourcematerial: The results indicated that the Random Forest model performed best (Overall Accuracy: 0.9697, Kappa: 0.8721) among various machine learning models which were applied in this study.
Methodology: A global projection model was developed to evaluate future trends in algal bloom occurrences in 161 large natural lakes (>500 km2) worldwide, utilizing various machine learning models, with the Random Forest model performing best (Overall Accuracy: 0.9697, Kappa: 0.8721) to predict algal bloom occurrences from 2020-2100 under different socio-economic development scenarios (SSP370 and SSP585).
Methodology-sourcematerial: The study aimed to develop a global projection model to evaluate future trends in algal bloom occurrences in large lakes under various socio-economic development scenarios, focusing on 161 natural lakes exceeding 500 km2 worldwide. The Random Forest model outperformed other machine learning models (Overall Accuracy: 0.9697, Kappa: 0.8721), predicting that algal blooms in lakes will worsen by the end of this century under higher forcing scenarios (SSP370 and SSP585).
Highlights: Algal blooms in large lakes globally are projected to worsen by the end of the century, particularly in Africa, Asia, and North America, under high forcing scenarios (SSP370 and SSP585); underdeveloped regions are more sensitive to socio-economic changes, potentially offsetting water quality control efforts.
Highlights-sourcematerial: The predicted results showed that, by the end of this century, the number of lakes experiencing algal blooms and the intensity of these blooms will worsen under higher forcing scenarios (SSP370 and SSP585) (p < 0.05). In different regions, lakes with increasing algal blooms are mainly distributed in Africa, Asia, and North America, while lakes with decreasing occurrence are primarily found in Europe. Additionally, underdeveloped regions, such as Africa, exhibit greater sensitivity to different SSP scenarios due to high variability in population and economic growth. This study revealed the spatiotemporal distribution of algal blooms in global lakes from 2020 to 2100 and suggested that the intensifying algal blooms due to global warming and human activities may offset the effort of controlling the water quality.
More Notes: Algal blooms in large lakes globally are projected to worsen under higher warming scenarios (SSP370 & SSP585) by the end of the century, particularly in Africa, Asia, and North America, potentially offsetting water quality control efforts.

In different regions, lakes with increasing algal blooms are mainly distributed in Africa, Asia, and North America, while lakes with decreasing occurrence are primarily found in Europe. Additionally, underdeveloped regions, such as Africa, exhibit greater sensitivity to different SSP scenarios due to high variability in population and economic growth.

Paper Title: Daytime radiative cooling coating for cooling energy efficiency of conventional air conditioners
Outcome(s) of Interest: Reduced AC electricity consumption; improved cooling energy efficiency (12.4-13.4%); enhanced heat dissipation and condensation; decreased interior air temperature of AC external units; sub-ambient cooling effects; good long-term duration; payback time ≤ 2 years.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "the interior air temperature of the semi-open cabinet by 11.4 °C", "the cooling energy efficiencies of the wall-hanging and central ACs with PSDRC-coated external units were 12.4 % and 13.4 %, respectively.", "the condenser in the PSDRC-coated external units dissipated heat and condensed more effectively", "ACs with PSDRC-coated external units have excellent long-term duration and good energy and economic performance, with a payback time of at most two years"
Drivers of Risk: Increased energy demand for cooling due to rising global temperatures.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Amidst the escalating energy and global warming challenges, the reduction in electricity consumption by air conditioners for cooling has become a pressing issue.
Category-sourcematerial: Amidst the escalating energy and global warming challenges, the reduction in electricity consumption by air conditioners for cooling has become a pressing issue. [...] Accordingly, the cooling energy efficiencies of the wall-hanging and central ACs with PSDRC-coated external units were 12.4 % and 13.4 %, respectively. [...] ACs with PSDRC-coated external units have excellent long-term duration and good energy and economic performance, with a payback time of at most two years, showing broad application prospects for ACs, telecommunication base stations, power distribution cabinets, and energy-storage cabinets.
Approach-sourcematerial: "In this study, a self-manufactured PSDRC coating was successfully applied to metal-based cabinets and the external units of various types of conventional ACs... the PSDRC-coated top surfaces exhibited remarkable PSDRC effects... the cooling energy efficiencies of the wall-hanging and central ACs with PSDRC-coated external units were 12.4 % and 13.4 %, respectively... ACs with PSDRC-coated external units have excellent long-term duration and good energy and economic performance, with a payback time of at most two years..."
Methodology: A self-manufactured passive sub-ambient daytime radiative cooling (PSDRC) coating was applied to metal-based cabinets and external units of various conventional ACs, and the temperature changes and cooling energy efficiencies were measured to assess the coating's effectiveness.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "In this study, a self-manufactured PSDRC coating was successfully applied to metal-based cabinets and the external units of various types of conventional ACs...the PSDRC coating exhibited pronounced sub-ambient cooling effects...the cooling energy efficiencies of the wall-hanging and central ACs with PSDRC-coated external units were 12.4 % and 13.4 %, respectively."
Highlights: Passive sub-ambient daytime radiative cooling (PSDRC) coatings applied to air conditioner (AC) exteriors significantly reduce internal unit temperatures, improving cooling energy efficiency by 12.4% to 13.4% with a payback time of up to two years.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "the PSDRC coating exhibited pronounced sub-ambient cooling effects during both day and night...significantly reducing the interior air temperature...by 11.4 °C...the interior air temperatures of the external units were significantly lower...the cooling energy efficiencies of the wall-hanging and central ACs with PSDRC-coated external units were 12.4 % and 13.4 %, respectively...payback time of at most two years"
More Notes: Passive daytime radiative cooling (PSDRC) coatings applied to air conditioner (AC) exteriors significantly improve cooling energy efficiency (12.4-13.4%) and outperform misting for heat dissipation, offering a payback within two years.
More Notes-sourcematerial: "When applied to the external units of wall-hanging and central ACs, the PSDRC-coated top surfaces exhibited remarkable PSDRC effects...Consequently, the condenser in the PSDRC-coated external units dissipated heat and condensed more effectively than its counterparts. Accordingly, the cooling energy efficiencies of the wall-hanging and central ACs with PSDRC-coated external units were 12.4 % and 13.4 %, respectively. In terms of the heat dissipation and condensation effects, the PSDRC coating performed better than spraying a mist toward the AC's external unit during hot summers. ACs with PSDRC-coated external units have excellent long-term duration and good energy and economic performance, with a payback time of at most two years..."
Hazard definition-sourcematerial: The reduction in electricity consumption by air conditioners for cooling has become a pressing issue. ... In this study, a self-manufactured PSDRC coating was successfully applied to metal-based cabinets and the external units of various types of conventional ACs. ... the PSDRC coating exhibited pronounced sub-ambient cooling effects during both day and night, significantly reducing the interior air temperature...
Impact persistence-sourcematerial: Irrespective of whether the painted cabinet was an enclosed, heat-generating or semi-open structure, the PSDRC coating exhibited pronounced sub-ambient cooling effects during both day and night, significantly reducing the interior air temperature of the semi-open cabinet by 11.4 °C. [...] Accordingly, the cooling energy efficiencies of the wall-hanging and central ACs with PSDRC-coated external units were 12.4 % and 13.4 %, respectively. ACs with PSDRC-coated external units have excellent long-term duration and good energy and economic performance, with a payback time of at most two years, showing broad application prospects for ACs, telecommunication base stations, power distribution cabinets, and energy-storage cabinets.
Impact interactions-sourcematerial: The abstract focuses on the direct impact of a passive sub-ambient daytime radiative cooling (PSDRC) coating on the energy efficiency of air conditioners. It does not mention or imply any interactions between different climate impacts, spatial regions, or economic sectors.
Economic dynamics-sourcematerial: N/A. The abstract focuses on the technical performance and economic payback period of a specific cooling technology (PSDRC coating). It does not discuss broader economic systems, how they might change over time, or how economic actors might adapt to climate impacts. The payback time calculation provides some economic context, but it's a static assessment of cost-effectiveness rather than a dynamic economic analysis.
Climate features-sourcematerial: No mention of tipping points or other large-scale climate complexities. The focus is on a technological application and its specific performance metrics.
Adaptation considered: The paper considers adaptation through the application of a passive sub-ambient daytime radiative cooling (PSDRC) coating to air conditioning units. This adaptation aims to reduce electricity consumption by improving cooling efficiency, thus mitigating the impact of increased cooling demand due to global warming. The extent of adaptation is quantified by the 12.4% and 13.4% improvement in cooling energy efficiency for wall-hanging and central ACs, respectively.

Paper Title: Harnessing drought-induced immune priming for intergenerational resistance to spot blotch in wheat
Outcome(s) of Interest: Improved disease resistance; enhanced photosynthetic pigments; increased stress markers; reduced oxidative damage; upregulated antioxidant and defense enzymes; improved yield.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "progeny of drought-primed plants showed enhanced defense responses under pathogen stress...Significant increases in photosynthetic pigments-chlorophyll a (3.80-fold), chlorophyll b (3.27-fold), and carotenoids (2.69-fold) were observed, along with key stress markers like proline (1.78-fold), total phenolics (1.30-fold), and ascorbic acid (2.30-fold)...exhibited an 8.37-fold reduction in malondialdehyde (MDA) levels, indicating reduced oxidative damage...displayed increased activities of antioxidant enzymes, including superoxide dismutase (2.35-fold), catalase (2.48-fold), and ascorbate peroxidase (3.37-fold), along with upregulated defense enzymes like peroxidase (1.44-fold), phenylalanine ammonia-lyase (1.97-fold) and chitinase (1.88-fold)...drought-primed progeny showed improved yield performance compared to non-primed progeny under stress."
Drivers of Risk: Drought, pathogen stress (Bipolaris sorokiniana), oxidative damage (MDA), climate challenges.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "This study demonstrates that drought-induced priming in wheat (Triticum aestivum) not only improves resistance to Bipolaris sorokiniana (spot blotch)..."; "...drought-primed progeny displayed increased activities of antioxidant enzymes...along with upregulated defense enzymes..."; "...an 8.37-fold reduction in malondialdehyde (MDA) levels, indicating reduced oxidative damage."; "...offering a path toward resilient crop varieties in the face of global climate challenges."
Category-sourcematerial: "Drought priming has proven to be an effective strategy in modern agriculture for enhancing crop resistance to pathogens...This study demonstrates that drought-induced priming in wheat (Triticum aestivum)...improves resistance to Bipolaris sorokiniana (spot blotch)...drought-induced intergenerational priming as a sustainable, chemical-free strategy for long-term crop protection, offering a path toward resilient crop varieties in the face of global climate challenges."
Approach-sourcematerial: This study demonstrates that drought-induced priming in wheat (Triticum aestivum) not only improves resistance to Bipolaris sorokiniana (spot blotch) but also transfers this protection to the subsequent generation (G1). Using growth metrics, disease assessments, biochemical analysis, and yield evaluations, we found that progeny of drought-primed plants showed enhanced defense responses under pathogen stress, despite no morphological differences in stress-free conditions.
Methodology: Growth metrics, disease assessments, biochemical analyses, and yield evaluations were used to study drought-induced priming in wheat (Triticum aestivum) and its impact on resistance to Bipolaris sorokiniana across generations.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Using growth metrics, disease assessments, biochemical analysis, and yield evaluations, we found that progeny of drought-primed plants showed enhanced defense responses under pathogen stress, despite no morphological differences in stress-free conditions.
Highlights: Drought priming in wheat enhances resistance to spot blotch across generations, improving photosynthetic pigments, stress markers, antioxidant and defense enzyme activity, reducing oxidative damage, and ultimately boosting yield under pathogen stress, suggesting a sustainable crop protection strategy.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Drought-induced priming in wheat improves resistance to Bipolaris sorokiniana, transfers protection to the subsequent generation, increases chlorophyll a (3.80-fold), chlorophyll b (3.27-fold), and carotenoids (2.69-fold), enhances stress markers proline (1.78-fold), total phenolics (1.30-fold), ascorbic acid (2.30-fold), and reduces malondialdehyde by 8.37-fold. It increases activities of antioxidant enzymes including superoxide dismutase (2.35-fold), catalase (2.48-fold), and ascorbate peroxidase (3.37-fold), and upregulates defense enzymes peroxidase (1.44-fold), phenylalanine ammonia-lyase (1.97-fold), and chitinase (1.88-fold), optimizing growth and defense resource allocation and improving yield under stress.
More Notes: Drought priming in wheat enhances resistance to spot blotch in the current and subsequent generations, leading to improved photosynthetic pigments, stress markers, antioxidant and defense enzyme activity, reduced oxidative damage, and improved yield under stress, suggesting a sustainable crop protection strategy.

Methodology: The study employed downscaled wind projections from the PRECIS regional climate model to simulate and assess changes in wind-generated waves in the Gulf of Mexico under a warmer climate using a high-resolution, validated third-generation wave model, starting with historical wind data from the CFSR for model evaluation, and then using HadGEM winds from PRECIS to project future wave climate changes from 1980–2005 to 2030–2054 under a high emission scenario, analyzed using the COWCLIP framework.
Methodology-sourcematerial: The present study utilized downscaled wind projections from the PRECIS regional climate model to project and assess changes in wind-generated waves in the Gulf of Mexico under a warmer climate. The methodology entailed simulating waves using a high-resolution and validated third-generation wave model. The wave model was first forced with historical winds from the Climate Forecast Systems Reanalysis (CFSR) to evaluate the accuracy of the model for studying wave climate. The wave model was then forced by downscaled HadGEM winds from PRECIS (HadRM3P) to quantify wave climate change from the historical period (1980–2005) to a future period (2030–2054) under a high emission scenario. Wave climate patterns were analyzed using the framework developed by the Coordinated Ocean Wave Climate Project (COWCLIP), which ensures consistency across different studies, allowing researchers to compare results from various regions and models more effectively.
Highlights: Projects more intense wave conditions in the Gulf of Mexico under a high-emission warming scenario using downscaled wind projections and a validated wave model, analyzed using the COWCLIP framework, with implications for key economic sectors.

Paper Title: Heatwaves and occupational injuries and illnesses risk varied at localised spatial scale: A national study in Australia
Outcome(s) of Interest: Occupational injuries and illnesses (OI); Cumulative OI risk during heatwaves (HWs); Heatwave-related attributable fractions (AF) of OI.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "examining the impact of HWs on occupational injuries and illnesses (OI)...the association between HWs...and OI...reveals a substantial cumulative OI risk...Projected HW impacts indicate a potential increase in HW-related attributable fractions (AF) of OI..."
Drivers of Risk: Younger workers; males; injury-related claims; higher temperatures; less vegetation; outer peripheries; rapidly urbanizing peri-urban areas.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Younger workers, males, injury-related claims, and workers in areas with higher temperatures and less vegetation were identified as particularly susceptible to HWs...outer peripheries and rapidly urbanizing peri-urban areas showing heightened vulnerability.
Category-sourcematerial: The study reveals a substantial cumulative OI risk ranging between 5.1 and 29.0 % during heatwaves...These findings emphasise the importance of developing localized, climate-related adaptation and intervention strategies to safeguard workers and reduce both the disease burden and associated economic costs and productivity loss.
Approach-sourcematerial: Using time series study design and distributed lag nonlinear models we establish the association between HWs, assessed via the Excess Heat Factor (EHF), and OI in Adelaide, Brisbane, Melbourne, and Sydney at the Statistical Area Level 3 (SA3). By utilising high-resolution geospatial data and future projections, our study provides new insights into the spatial heterogeneity of HW impacts on OI within cities, a previously underexplored area of research. Projected HW impacts indicate a potential increase in HW-related attributable fractions (AF) of OI, for example, in Greater Sydney, where the overall OI AF is projected to rise from 0.89 % to 1.64 % under high-emission climate scenarios.
Methodology: Employs a time series study design and distributed lag nonlinear models to assess the association between heatwaves (using the Excess Heat Factor) and occupational injuries/illnesses at the Statistical Area Level 3 in four Australian cities; utilizes high-resolution geospatial data and future climate projections to project future impacts.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Using time series study design and distributed lag nonlinear models we establish the association between HWs, assessed via the Excess Heat Factor (EHF), and OI in Adelaide, Brisbane, Melbourne, and Sydney at the Statistical Area Level 3 (SA3). By utilising high-resolution geospatial data and future projections, our study provides new insights into the spatial heterogeneity of HW impacts on OI within cities, a previously underexplored area of research. Projected HW impacts indicate a potential increase in HW-related attributable fractions (AF) of OI, for example, in Greater Sydney, where the overall OI AF is projected to rise from 0.89 % to 1.64 % under high-emission climate scenarios.
Highlights: Heatwaves significantly increase occupational injuries and illnesses (OI) in Australian cities, with risks higher in outer and peri-urban areas, especially for younger, male workers in hotter, less vegetated areas; climate change projections indicate a substantial increase in heatwave-related OI attributable fractions, highlighting the need for localized adaptation strategies.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "study reveals a substantial cumulative OI risk ranging between 5.1 and 29.0 % during heatwaves, with outer peripheries and rapidly urbanizing peri-urban areas showing heightened vulnerability. Younger workers, males, injury-related claims, and workers in areas with higher temperatures and less vegetation were identified as particularly susceptible to HWs. Projected HW impacts indicate a potential increase in HW-related attributable fractions (AF) of OI, for example, in Greater Sydney, where the overall OI AF is projected to rise from 0.89 % to 1.64 % under high-emission climate scenarios. These findings emphasise the importance of developing localized, climate-related adaptation and intervention strategies to safeguard workers"
More Notes: Heatwaves in Australian cities increase occupational injuries and illnesses, with projections showing a potential doubling of heatwave-related injury fractions in high-emission scenarios, highlighting the need for localized adaptation strategies to protect workers.

Projected HW impacts indicate a potential increase in HW-related attributable fractions (AF) of OI...These findings emphasise the importance of developing localized, climate-related adaptation and intervention strategies..."

Climate features-sourcematerial: Heatwaves (HWs) pose significant risks to occupational safety in Australia, particularly for workers in roles requiring prolonged heat exposure. This study, for the first time, addresses critical gaps in the existing literature by examining the impact of HWs on occupational injuries and illnesses (OI) at a fine spatial scale and projecting future OI burdens under climate change scenarios. Using time series study design and distributed lag nonlinear models we establish the association between HWs, assessed via the Excess Heat Factor (EHF), and OI in Adelaide, Brisbane, Melbourne, and Sydney at the Statistical Area Level 3 (SA3). The study reveals a substantial cumulative OI risk ranging between 5.1 and 29.0 % during heatwaves, with outer peripheries and rapidly urbanizing peri-urban areas showing heightened vulnerability. Younger workers, males, injury-related claims, and workers in areas with higher temperatures and less vegetation were identified as particularly susceptible to HWs. By utilising high-resolution geospatial data and future projections, our study provides new insights into the spatial heterogeneity of HW impacts on OI within cities, a previously underexplored area of research. Projected HW impacts indicate a potential increase in HW-related attributable fractions (AF) of OI, for example, in Greater Sydney, where the overall OI AF is projected to rise from 0.89 % to 1.64 % under high-emission climate scenarios. These findings emphasise the importance of developing localized, climate-related adaptation and intervention strategies to safeguard workers and reduce both the disease burden and associated economic costs and productivity loss. © 2024 The Authors

Future projections of increased heatwave-related occupational injuries highlight the need for such adaptation measures.
Adaptation considered-sourcematerial: These findings emphasise the importance of developing localized, climate-related adaptation and intervention strategies to safeguard workers and reduce both the disease burden and associated economic costs and productivity loss. Projected HW impacts indicate a potential increase in HW-related attributable fractions (AF) of OI, for example, in Greater Sydney, where the overall OI AF is projected to rise from 0.89 % to 1.64 % under high-emission climate scenarios.

Paper Title: Severe Convective Weather in the Central and Eastern United States: Present and Future
Outcome(s) of Interest: Intensified SCW activity (esp. early-mid spring, foothills/higher latitudes) and potential for increased SCW severity/damage in a warmer climate.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Results indicate that the retrospective simulation captures reasonably well the spatial distributions and seasonal variations of the observed SCW events, with an exception of an overestimate along the Atlantic and Gulf coast. In a warmer-moister future, most regions experience intensified SCW activity, most notably in the early-middle spring, with the largest percentage increase in the foothills and higher latitudes. In addition, a shift of simulated radar reflectivity toward higher values, in association with the significant thermodynamic environmental response to climatic warming, potentially increases the SCW severity and resultant damage.
Drivers of Risk: Anthropogenic climate warming; increased moisture; thermodynamic environmental response; shift in radar reflectivity; intensified SCW activity; earlier spring peak.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: the potential change in SCW frequency and intensity in response to anthropogenic climate warming is highly uncertain due to deficient and sparse historical records and the global and regional climate model’s inability to resolve thunderstorms...In a warmer-moister future, most regions experience intensified SCW activity, most notably in the early-middle spring, with the largest percentage increase in the foothills and higher latitudes. In addition, a shift of simulated radar reflectivity toward higher values, in association with the significant thermodynamic environmental response to climatic warming, potentially increases the SCW severity and resultant damage.
Category-sourcematerial: The continental United States is a global hotspot of severe thunderstorms and therefore is particularly vulnerable to social and economic damages from high-impact severe convective weather (SCW), such as tornadoes, thunderstorm winds, and large hail.
Approach-sourcematerial: This study investigates SCW in the Central and Eastern United States in spring and early summer for the current and future warmed climate using two multi-year continental-scale convection-permitting Weather Research and Forecasting (WRF) model simulations.
Methodology: Convection-permitting Weather Research and Forecasting (WRF) model simulations downscaled ERA-Interim reanalysis (Oct 2000-Sept 2013) and a future climate sensitivity simulation (CMIP5 high-end emission scenario) to investigate SCW occurrence using a composite reflectivity and updraft helicity threshold proxy in the Central and Eastern U.S. in spring and early summer.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "This study investigates SCW in the Central and Eastern United States in spring and early summer for the current and future warmed climate using two multi-year continental-scale convection-permitting Weather Research and Forecasting (WRF) model simulations. The pair of simulations consist of a retrospective simulation, which downscales the ERA-Interim reanalysis during October 2000–September 2013, and a future climate sensitivity simulation based on the perturbed reanalysis-derived boundary conditions with the CMIP5 ensemble-mean high-end emission scenario climate change. A proxy based on composite reflectivity and updraft helicity threshold is applied to infer the simulated SCW occurrence."
Highlights: The U.S. is a hotspot for severe thunderstorms, facing challenges in predicting changes in storm frequency and intensity due to climate warming; new simulations suggest a future increase in severe convective weather, particularly in early spring and at higher latitudes, with potential for greater storm severity and damage.
Highlights-sourcematerial: The continental United States is a global hotspot of severe thunderstorms...potential change in SCW frequency and intensity in response to anthropogenic climate warming is highly uncertain...Results indicate that...most regions experience intensified SCW activity, most notably in the early-middle spring, with the largest percentage increase in the foothills and higher latitudes...a shift of simulated radar reflectivity toward higher values...potentially increases the SCW severity and resultant damage.
More Notes: Convection-permitting model simulations suggest intensified severe convective weather (SCW) activity in the Central and Eastern U.S. during a warmer-moister future, particularly in early-middle spring, with a shift toward higher radar reflectivity potentially increasing SCW severity.

Paper Title: Long-term effects of agronomic practices on winter wheat yield and NUE in dryland regions of USA and China: a long-term meta-analysis
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased wheat yield and nitrogen use efficiency (NUE) with conservation tillage at high nitrogen fertilizer (NF) levels, and with leguminous cover crops (LCC) and manure application at low NF levels, compared to conventional tillage (CT).
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: The meta-analysis presented that conservation tillage at high-NF enhanced the yield and NUE, and reduced the yield and NUE at low-NF, compared to CT. The interaction of conservation tillage with leguminous cover crops (LCC) and manure application increased the yield and NUE at low-NF, compared to CT...the no-tillage (NT) with leguminous cover crops (LCC) significantly increased wheat yield by 58% and NUE by 47% under low-NF compared to CT.
Drivers of Risk: Conservation tillage (at low-NF), conventional tillage (CT), low nitrogen fertilizer (NF), lack of leguminous cover crops (LCC), lack of manure application.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "The meta-analysis presented that conservation tillage at high-NF enhanced the yield and NUE, and reduced the yield and NUE at low-NF, compared to CT...The interaction of conservation tillage with leguminous cover crops (LCC) and manure application increased the yield and NUE at low-NF, compared to CT...The adverse impact of CT on yield and NUE could be alleviated with the application of LCC and manure under moderate NF."
Category-sourcematerial: Dryland agriculture is fundamental to global crop production and vital to food security. ... This meta-analysis compared the yield and nitrogen use efficiency (NUE) between conservation tillage and conventional tillage (CT) with different types of cropping systems, mulching methods, levels of nitrogen fertilizer (NF), and addition of manure. ...moderate NF with LCC and manure application is recommended for growing winter wheat in dryland regions of the USA and China.
Approach-sourcematerial: This meta-analysis compared the yield and nitrogen use efficiency (NUE) between conservation tillage and conventional tillage...
Methodology: A meta-analysis was conducted comparing yield and nitrogen use efficiency (NUE) between conservation tillage and conventional tillage (CT), considering cropping systems, mulching methods, nitrogen fertilizer (NF) levels, and manure addition.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "This meta-analysis compared the yield and nitrogen use efficiency (NUE) between conservation tillage and conventional tillage (CT) with different types of cropping systems, mulching methods, levels of nitrogen fertilizer (NF), and addition of manure."
Highlights: Conservation tillage at high nitrogen fertilizer (NF) enhances yield and nitrogen use efficiency (NUE) compared to conventional tillage (CT), while the opposite occurs at low-NF; leguminous cover crops (LCC) and manure increase yield and NUE at low-NF with conservation tillage; non-leguminous cover crops (NLCC) increase yield and NUE under high-NF; no-tillage (NT) with LCC significantly increases wheat yield and NUE under low-NF; cropping systems, mulching, and manure affect conservation tillage's impact on yield and NUE; LCC and manure can alleviate CT's adverse effects under moderate NF; moderate NF with LCC and manure is recommended for winter wheat in dryland regions.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "conservation tillage at high-NF enhanced the yield and NUE, and reduced the yield and NUE at low-NF, compared to CT"; "the interaction of conservation tillage with leguminous cover crops (LCC) and manure application increased the yield and NUE at low-NF, compared to CT"; "Non-leguminous cover crops (NLCC) increased the yield and NUE under high-NF than low-NF"; "the no-tillage (NT) with leguminous cover crops (LCC) significantly increased wheat yield by 58% and NUE by 47% under low-NF compared to CT"; "Cropping systems, mulching types, and manure application mainly determined the effects of conservation tillage on wheat yield and NUE"; "The adverse impact of CT on yield and NUE could be alleviated with the application of LCC and manure under moderate NF"; "moderate NF with LCC and manure application is recommended for growing winter wheat in dryland regions of the USA and China."
More Notes: Conservation tillage with leguminous cover crops and manure can improve wheat yield and nitrogen use efficiency in dryland regions, potentially reducing the need for high nitrogen fertilizer applications.
More Notes-sourcematerial: The interaction of conservation tillage with leguminous cover crops (LCC) and manure application increased the yield and NUE at low-NF, compared to CT...the no-tillage (NT) with leguminous cover crops (LCC) significantly increased wheat yield by 58% and NUE by 47% under low-NF compared to CT...adding LCC and manure have a generally substitutive relationship with N fertilizer, resulting in a significant increase in wheat yield and NUE at low-NF doses as at high N fertilizer dosages. Therefore, based on the obtained results, moderate NF with LCC and manure application is recommended for growing winter wheat in dryland regions of the USA and China.

Paper Title: Changing climate risks for high-value tree fruit production across the United States
Outcome(s) of Interest: Reduced dormancy, altered bloom timing, increased sunburn risk, diminished apple appearance/quality.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: These trends could negatively affect apple production by reducing the dormancy period, altering bloom timing, increasing sunburn risk, and diminishing apple appearance and quality.
Drivers of Risk: Decreasing cold degree days; increasing growing degree days (GDD); increasing extreme heat days; increasing warm nights; earlier last day of spring frost.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "We found significant trends across large parts of the U.S. in all metrics… Yakima County, WA, Kent County, MI, Wayne County, NY—leading apple-producers—showed significant decreasing trends in cold degree days and increasing trends in GDD and warm fall nights… Yakima county…showed significant changes in five of the six metrics—earlier last day of spring frost, fewer cold degree days, increasing GDD over the overall growth period, and more extreme heat days and warm nights."
Category-sourcematerial: Climate change poses growing risks to global agriculture including perennial tree fruit such as apples that hold important nutritional, cultural, and economic value. This study quantifies historical trends in climate metrics affecting apple growth, production, and quality...These trends could negatively affect apple production by reducing the dormancy period, altering bloom timing, increasing sunburn risk, and diminishing apple appearance and quality.
Approach-sourcematerial: Utilizing the high-resolution gridMET dataset, we analyzed trends (1979-2022) in several key metrics across the U.S.—cold degree days, chill portions, last day of spring frost, growing degree days (GDD), extreme heat days (daily maximum temperature >34 °C), and warm nights (daily minimum temperatures >15 °C). We found significant trends across large parts of the U.S. in all metrics...
Methodology: Trends in climate metrics affecting apple growth (cold degree days, chill portions, last day of spring frost, growing degree days, extreme heat days, warm nights) were analyzed across the U.S. from 1979-2022 using the high-resolution gridMET dataset.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "Utilizing the high-resolution gridMET dataset, we analyzed trends (1979-2022) in several key metrics across the U.S.—cold degree days, chill portions, last day of spring frost, growing degree days (GDD), extreme heat days (daily maximum temperature >34 °C), and warm nights (daily minimum temperatures >15 °C)."
Highlights: Significant climate trends (1979-2022) across major U.S. apple-producing regions (WA, MI, NY) include decreased cold degree days, increased growing degree days, warmer nights, and earlier last spring frost, potentially harming apple production through reduced dormancy, altered bloom timing, increased sunburn, and diminished fruit quality, highlighting the need for adaptation strategies due to compounding negative impacts.

Paper Title: Agrivoltaics for small ruminants: A review
Outcome(s) of Interest: Mutual benefits; vegetation management, improved animal welfare (heat stress reduction, weather protection), positive soil impacts (moisture retention, vegetation growth), reduced leachate, positive environmental performance, low material/carbon footprint, short payback periods.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: The sheep aid solar farm operators with maintenance aspects, specifically vegetation management and associated costs. The PV arrays influence the microclimate conditions on the farm in terms of wind speed (and direction), temperature and humidity, including rain distribution, providing for better animal welfare in terms of heat stress and protection against harsh weather. Once operational, the soil is positively impacted with better moisture retention and vegetation growth improves in hotter months. The widespread shading also means (potentially) a better distribution of urination events with less overloading on the land and lower leachate. Overall, the available evidence suggests that the environmental performance of agrivoltaics is positive. Apart from the impact on the land, the life cycle of the systems have low material and carbon footprints, with short carbon and energy payback periods.
Drivers of Risk: Utility-scale solar, transformation of agricultural land, uncertainties, construction impact on soil, need for field trials, climate context variability.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: The mitigation of climate change will require the large-scale uptake of renewable energy generation, in particular wind and solar. Utility-scale solar photovoltaics (PVs), or solar farms, will necessitate the transformation of agricultural land [...] However, many uncertainties remain and this review paper provides more insights into the technology [...] The construction process does have a negative impact on the soil [...] although more field trials are required. [...] Further quantifications of many aspects are needed with longitudinal data from different climate contexts around the world.
Category-sourcematerial: Agrivoltaics is the dual land-usage solution with the integration of solar PV arrays with ongoing agricultural production. The farming of ruminants, especially sheep, is particularly suited for agrivoltaics in that little modification of the infrastructure is required to accommodate sheep grazing beneath and between the PV arrays.
Approach-sourcematerial: The farming of ruminants, especially sheep, is particularly suited for agrivoltaics in that little modification of the infrastructure is required to accommodate sheep grazing beneath and between the PV arrays. However, many uncertainties remain and this review paper provides more insights into the technology, the role of small ruminants, the potential impacts on agriculture productivity and the land, as well as other sustainability aspects of agrivoltaics.
Methodology: This review paper synthesizes existing research on agrivoltaics, focusing on the integration of solar PV arrays with sheep farming, to assess its potential impacts on agricultural productivity, land sustainability, and overall environmental performance.
Methodology-sourcematerial: The review provides insights into the technology, the role of small ruminants, potential impacts on agriculture productivity and land, and sustainability aspects of agrivoltaics. It also explores mutual benefits from simultaneous operations on the same land, such as maintenance aspects, vegetation management, and improved microclimate conditions.
Highlights: Agrivoltaics, integrating solar PV arrays with agriculture (especially sheep grazing), offers potential mutual benefits: sheep provide vegetation management for solar farms, while PV arrays improve animal welfare via microclimate modification (wind, temperature, humidity). While construction impacts soil, operational agrivoltaics improve soil moisture and vegetation growth, with positive environmental performance, low material/carbon footprints, and short payback periods.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "Agrivoltaics is the dual land-usage solution with the integration of solar PV arrays with ongoing agricultural production... The farming of ruminants, especially sheep, is particularly suited for agrivoltaics... Previous and ongoing research suggests there are (potentially) mutual benefits for both operations to occur on the same land – if the integration is designed well... The sheep aid solar farm operators with maintenance aspects, specifically vegetation management and associated costs. The PV arrays influence the microclimate conditions on the farm in terms of wind speed (and direction), temperature and humidity... The construction process does have a negative impact on the soil, but the land can be restored in a short period of time. Once operational, the soil is positively impacted with better moisture retention and vegetation growth improves in hotter months... Overall, the available evidence suggests that the environmental performance of agrivoltaics is positive... the life cycle of the systems have low material and carbon footprints, with short carbon and energy payback periods..."
More Notes: Agrivoltaics, integrating solar PV with sheep farming, offers potential mutual benefits: sheep manage vegetation, while PV arrays improve microclimate for animal welfare and soil health; further research is needed to quantify impacts across different climates.

Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased crop yields, soil carbon storage & stability, greenhouse gas emissions (mitigated by optimization); Increased agroecosystem multiservices; Climate-related yield loss mitigation; Climate neutrality; Soil stabilization.

Approach-sourcematerial: Using meta-analytic and data-driven quantifications of 2302 observations, we optimized cover crop practices and evaluated their benefits for global agroecosystems.
Methodology: The research employed meta-analytic and data-driven quantifications of 2302 observations to optimize cover crop practices globally, assessing their effects on yield, soil carbon, stability, and greenhouse gas emissions.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Using meta-analytic and data-driven quantifications of 2302 observations, we optimized cover crop practices and evaluated their benefits for global agroecosystems.
Highlights: Optimized cover crop practices (long-term implementation, climate-smart practices like no-tillage, legume/non-legume biculture terminated 25 days pre-planting, residue mulching) enhance crop yields, soil carbon storage, and soil stability while mitigating climate-related yield losses, contributing to climate neutrality and soil stabilization, particularly in vulnerable areas.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Cover crops have historically boosted crop yields, soil carbon storage, and stability, but also stimulated greenhouse gas emissions. However, combining them with long-term implementation (five years or more) and climate-smart practices (such as no-tillage) can enhance these services synergistically. A biculture of legume and non-legume cover crops, terminated 25 days before planting the next crop and followed by residue mulching, is the optimal portfolio. By 2100, the continued implementation of optimized practices could mitigate climate-related yield losses and contribute to climate neutrality and soil stabilization, especially in harsh and underdeveloped areas.

Paper Title: Global Land Use Change and Its Impact on Greenhouse Gas Emissions
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased global GHG emissions from land use changes; projected future emissions based on land use scenarios.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "Land use was significantly associated with GHG emissions (p < 0.05). Artificial surface dominates global GHG emissions, followed by cropland, pasture, and barren land...In total, global GHG emissions increased from 31 to 46 GtCO2eq from 1992 to 2020...if current trends in global land use continue at the same rates, our model projects that global GHG emissions will reach 76 ± 8 GtCO2eq in 2050. In contrast, reducing the rates of land use change by half could limit global GHG emissions to 60 ± 3 GtCO2eq in 2050."
Drivers of Risk: Land use change (artificial surface & cropland expansion), population growth, socioeconomic development, energy consumption, deforestation.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Anthropogenic activities have altered approximately two-thirds of the Earth's land surface. Urbanization, industrialization, agricultural expansion, and deforestation are increasingly impacting the terrestrial landscapes, leading to shifts of areas in artificial surface (i.e., humanmade), cropland, pasture, forest, and barren land. ... Our results show that, from 1992 to 2020, the global terrestrial areas covered by artificial surface and cropland have expanded by 133% and 6% because of population growth and socioeconomic development, resulting in 4.0% and 3.8% of declines in pasture and forest areas, respectively. ... The increase in artificial surfaces has driven up global GHG emissions through the increase in energy consumption.
Category-sourcematerial: Land use patterns and associated greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions play a critical role in global climate change... from 1992 to 2020, the global terrestrial areas covered by artificial surface and cropland have expanded by 133% and 6% because of population growth and socioeconomic development, resulting in 4.0% and 3.8% of declines in pasture and forest areas, respectively... Forest, on the other hand, serves as a sink of GHG.
Approach-sourcematerial: Here we synthesized 29 years of global historical data and demonstrated how land use impacts global GHG emissions using structural equation modeling. We then obtained predictive estimates of future global GHG emissions using a deep learning model.
Methodology: Land use impacts on global GHG emissions were analyzed using structural equation modeling with 29 years of global historical data (1992-2020), and future GHG emissions were predicted using a deep learning model based on land use trends.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "Here we synthesized 29 years of global historical data and demonstrated how land use impacts global GHG emissions using structural equation modeling. We then obtained predictive estimates of future global GHG emissions using a deep learning model."
Highlights: Global land use changes from 1992-2020, specifically expansion of artificial surfaces and cropland, significantly increased global GHG emissions, with projections indicating a further rise to 76 ± 8 GtCO2eq by 2050 if current trends continue, but mitigation is possible by reducing land use change rates.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "Land use patterns and associated greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions play a critical role in global climate change...from 1992 to 2020, the global terrestrial areas covered by artificial surface and cropland have expanded...resulting in 4.0% and 3.8% of declines in pasture and forest areas, respectively. Land use was significantly associated with GHG emissions...Artificial surface dominates global GHG emissions...In total, global GHG emissions increased from 31 to 46 GtCO2eq from 1992 to 2020. Looking ahead, if current trends in global land use continue at the same rates, our model projects that global GHG emissions will reach 76 ± 8 GtCO2eq in 2050. In contrast, reducing the rates of land use change by half could limit global GHG emissions to 60 ± 3 GtCO2eq in 2050."

Paper Title: Evaluating the impact of climate change on yield and water use efficiency of different dry-season rice varieties cultivated under conventional and alternate wetting and drying conditions
Outcome(s) of Interest: Rice yield reduction, AWD vs CF, heat stress impact, drought adaptation, different rice varieties, SSP3-7.0, SSP5-8.5, AquaCrop model accuracy.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "In addition, the study found that varieties with growing periods longer than 93 days after transplanting (DAT), such as CAR15 and Sen Kra Ob, were most impacted by heat stress conditions, leading to reduced yield, harvest index (HI), and water use efficiency (WUE). In our case, CAR15 and Sen Kra Ob grain yields were reduced by 53% and 8%, respectively. AWD maintains superior WUE compared with CF regardless of the type of varieties, suggesting this technique is a drought-adaptive strategy. The simulation results demonstrated the effectiveness of AquaCrop in capturing crop development across treatments and varieties. ... We used two shared socioeconomic pathways scenarios (SSP3-7.0 and SSP5-8.5) developed within Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 6 (CMIP6)..."
Drivers of Risk: Increased temperature (Tmin, Tmax), potentially increased rainfall, heat stress impacting longer-duration rice varieties.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: The analysis of future climate shows an increasing trend in the monthly average temperature by 0.8 °C (Tmin) and 1.3 °C (Tmax) in both scenarios. While ETo values were not anticipated, rainfall was expected to increase with average values of 5.62 mm to 11.25 mm. In addition, the study found that varieties with growing periods longer than 93 days after transplanting (DAT), such as CAR15 and Sen Kra Ob, were most impacted by heat stress conditions, leading to reduced yield, harvest index (HI), and water use efficiency (WUE).
Category-sourcematerial: This study is the first attempt to assess rice cultivation under alternate wetting and drying (AWD) and continuous flooding (CF) using the latest scenarios from the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC), utilizing AquaCrop Model. ... projected the rice growth during 2040–2070. ... varieties with growing periods longer than 93 days after transplanting (DAT) ... were most impacted by heat stress conditions, leading to reduced yield. ... CAR15 and Sen Kra Ob grain yields were reduced by 53% and 8%, respectively.
Approach-sourcematerial: This study is the first attempt to assess rice cultivation under alternate wetting and drying (AWD) and continuous flooding (CF) using the latest scenarios from the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC), utilizing AquaCrop Model.
Methodology: Rice cultivation under alternate wetting and drying (AWD) and continuous flooding (CF) was assessed using the AquaCrop model, calibrated and validated with field experiments from the dry season 2023, and driven by IPCC's CMIP6 scenarios (SSP3-7.0 and SSP5-8.5) to project rice growth during 2040–2070.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "This study is the first attempt to assess rice cultivation under alternate wetting and drying (AWD) and continuous flooding (CF) using the latest scenarios from the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC), utilizing AquaCrop Model. Field experiments were conducted during the dry season 2023 to get the model calibration and validation input. We used two shared socioeconomic pathways scenarios (SSP3-7.0 and SSP5-8.5) developed within Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 6 (CMIP6) and projected the rice growth during 2040–2070."
Highlights: AquaCrop model accurately simulates rice growth under different irrigation and climate scenarios; future climate projections (SSP3-7.0 and SSP5-8.5) indicate increased temperatures and rainfall; longer-duration rice varieties are more susceptible to heat stress, leading to yield reductions; alternate wetting and drying (AWD) enhances water use efficiency compared to continuous flooding (CF).
Highlights-sourcematerial: Field experiments were conducted during the dry season 2023 to get the model calibration and validation input. We used two shared socioeconomic pathways scenarios (SSP3-7.0 and SSP5-8.5) developed within Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 6 (CMIP6) and projected the rice growth during 2040–2070. The simulation results demonstrated the effectiveness of AquaCrop in capturing crop development across treatments and varieties. The analysis of future climate shows an increasing trend in the monthly average temperature by 0.8 °C (Tmin) and 1.3 °C (Tmax) in both scenarios. Rainfall was expected to increase with average values of 5.62 mm to 11.25 mm. The study found that varieties with growing periods longer than 93 days after transplanting (DAT), such as CAR15 and Sen Kra Ob, were most impacted by heat stress conditions, leading to reduced yield, harvest index (HI), and water use efficiency (WUE). CAR15 and Sen Kra Ob grain yields were reduced by 53% and 8%, respectively. AWD maintains superior WUE compared with CF regardless of the type of varieties, suggesting this technique is a drought-adaptive strategy.
More Notes: Rice cultivation under future climate scenarios (2040-2070) using SSP3-7.0 and SSP5-8.5 shows increased temperatures and rainfall; longer-duration rice varieties are most susceptible to heat stress, reducing yield; alternate wetting and drying (AWD) improves water use efficiency compared to continuous flooding.

Paper Title: Synecoculture experiments: Human augmentation of ecosystems and Planetary Hhealth
Outcome(s) of Interest: Promotes diversity/productivity, reduces inputs/environmental load, enhances human wellbeing (nutrition, health), economic benefits, land restoration.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: The overall results showed that the self-organized primary production of ecosystems performs better compared with conventional monoculture methods in 1) promoting diversity and total quantity of products along with a rapid increase of in-field biodiversity that autonomously adapt to environmental variability; 2) a fundamental reduction of inputs and environmental load; and 3) multiple benefits to human wellbeing through enhanced nutrition and environmental quality...Measured economic impacts in sub-Saharan Africa and established collaborative efforts towards scaled-up implementation in the Global South with local governments and NGOs, highlighting Synecoculture's contribution to sustainable food systems and land restoration.
Drivers of Risk: Climate change, biodiversity loss, agriculture, monoculture limitations, environmental variability, land degradation
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "Transformative change in primary food production is urgently needed in the face of climate change and biodiversity loss...conventional inputs (e.g., tillage, fertilizers, and agrochemicals), assessing biodiversity, overall productivity, and soil health...self-organized primary production of ecosystems performs better compared with conventional monoculture methods in 1) promoting diversity and total quantity of products along with a rapid increase of in-field biodiversity that autonomously adapt to environmental variability; 2) a fundamental reduction of inputs and environmental load...contribution to sustainable food systems and land restoration...overcome the historical trade-off between productivity and biodiversity"
Category-sourcematerial: Transformative change in primary food production is urgently needed in the face of climate change and biodiversity loss. Synecoculture (synecological farming) is designed on a variety of environmental responses within ecological optimum in high-density mixed polyculture, where various edible species were intentionally introduced...Field Experiments: Conducted in Japan and Sub-Saharan Africa, these experiments tested Synecoculture's diverse, mixed-species agroecosystems without conventional inputs (e.g., tillage, fertilizers, and agrochemicals), assessing biodiversity, overall productivity, and soil health...Socio-Economic Assessment: Measured economic impacts in sub-Saharan Africa and established collaborative efforts towards scaled-up implementation in the Global South with local governments and NGOs, highlighting Synecoculture's contribution to sustainable food systems and land restoration...The overall results showed that the self-organized primary production of ecosystems performs better compared with conventional monoculture methods in 1) promoting diversity and total quantity of products along with a rapid increase of in-field biodiversity that autonomously adapt to environmental variability; 2) a fundamental reduction of inputs and environmental load; and 3) multiple benefits to human wellbeing through enhanced nutrition and environmental quality.
Approach-sourcematerial: Field Experiments: Conducted in Japan and Sub-Saharan Africa, these experiments tested Synecoculture's diverse, mixed-species agroecosystems without conventional inputs (e.g., tillage, fertilizers, and agrochemicals), assessing biodiversity, overall productivity, and soil health. Big Data and AI: Developed an open-source biological interaction database, used AI and drone image analysis for automated field assessment, and 3D ecosystem modeling to manage and understand ecological complexity. Ecological Data Analysis: Monitored seasonal community dynamics of Synecoculture plots and used CSR triangle theory to analyze plant adaptive strategies. These analyses demonstrated adaptive diversification and its link to ecosystem productivity. Theoretical Modeling: Established the Integrated Model of Physiological and Ecological Optima (IMPEO) to analyze the comprehensive productivity of densely mixed polyculture in marginal environments. Health Impact Studies: Evaluated Synecoculture's health benefits through metabolome analysis of tea products, as well as biomarker analysis and clinical assessments for elderly patients, showing anti-inflammatory effects and improved physical and cognitive health outcomes through rehabilitation activities in biodiverse environments. Socio-Economic Assessment: Measured economic impacts in sub-Saharan Africa and established collaborative efforts towards scaled-up implementation in the Global South with local governments and NGOs, highlighting Synecoculture's contribution to sustainable food systems and land restoration.
Methodology: The methodology for Synecoculture research includes field experiments in Japan and Sub-Saharan Africa assessing mixed-species agroecosystems without conventional inputs to evaluate biodiversity, productivity, and soil health; use of big data and AI for biological interaction databases, automated assessments, and ecosystems modeling; ecological data analysis to understand plant adaptive strategies and community dynamics; theoretical modeling with the Integrated Model of Physiological and Ecological Optima (IMPEO) to study productivity in polycultures; health impact studies via metabolome and biomarker analysis in human participants; socio-economic assessments in sub-Saharan Africa and Japan focusing on economic impacts, participatory studies, and the educational Syneco Portal program.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Field Experiments: Conducted in Japan and Sub-Saharan Africa, these experiments tested Synecoculture's diverse, mixed-species agroecosystems without conventional inputs (e.g., tillage, fertilizers, and agrochemicals), assessing biodiversity, overall productivity, and soil health. Big Data and AI: Developed an open-source biological interaction database, used AI and drone image analysis for automated field assessment, and 3D ecosystem modeling to manage and understand ecological complexity. Ecological Data Analysis: Monitored seasonal community dynamics of Synecoculture plots and used CSR triangle theory to analyze plant adaptive strategies. Theoretical Modeling: Established the Integrated Model of Physiological and Ecological Optima (IMPEO) to analyze the comprehensive productivity of densely mixed polyculture in marginal environments. Health Impact Studies: Evaluated Synecoculture's health benefits through metabolome analysis of tea products, as well as biomarker analysis and clinical assessments for elderly patients, showing anti-inflammatory effects and improved physical and cognitive health outcomes through rehabilitation activities in biodiverse environments. Socio-Economic Assessment: Measured economic impacts in sub-Saharan Africa and established collaborative efforts towards scaled-up implementation in the Global South with local governments and NGOs, highlighting Synecoculture's contribution to sustainable food systems and land restoration. In the urban area of Japan, participatory studies were performed on green infrastructure to enhance biodiversity and mitigate environmental risks. Educational programs based on the “Syneco Portal” engaged students in hands-on learning about the self-organization of ecosystems.
Highlights: Synecoculture, a mixed polyculture farming method, enhances biodiversity, increases total product quantity, reduces environmental inputs/load, promotes autonomous adaptation to environmental variability, and improves human wellbeing through enhanced nutrition and environmental quality in both rural and urban settings, suggesting a new sustainable farming approach.
Highlights-sourcematerial: overall results showed that the self-organized primary production of ecosystems performs better compared with conventional monoculture methods in 1) promoting diversity and total quantity of products along with a rapid increase of in-field biodiversity that autonomously adapt to environmental variability; 2) a fundamental reduction of inputs and environmental load; and 3) multiple benefits to human wellbeing through enhanced nutrition and environmental quality. These benefits imply substantial possibilities for a new typology of sustainable farming based on human-guided augmentation of ecosystem that could overcome the historical trade-off between productivity and biodiversity, which provide a fundamental platform for human and ecosystem health in both rural and urban environments.
More Notes: Synecoculture, demonstrated in Japan and Sub-Saharan Africa, enhances biodiversity and ecosystem productivity in agroecosystems by integrating diverse species without conventional inputs, utilizes AI and big data for assessment, and shows benefits for human health and socio-economic improvement, providing a sustainable agriculture alternative overcoming the productivity-biodiversity trade-off.

Methodology: Conventional narrative literature review assesses Climate Resilient Development Pathways (CRDP) across highlands, drylands, and islands to understand climate impacts and adaptation/mitigation strategies.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Using conventional narrative literature review, this paper assesses CRDP across some of the world’s most vulnerable geographies: highlands (mountains), drylands and islands. It asks: (1) how are climate impacts experienced across highlands, drylands and islands and (2) what types of adaptation and mitigation are being employed across these vulnerable geographies?
Highlights: Climate impacts commonly affect ecosystems, fisheries, agriculture, water systems, livelihoods, food security, human health, equality, and economic sectors across highlands, drylands, and islands, leading to migration, conflict, and transboundary impacts; adaptation and mitigation often involve nature-based solutions, livelihood diversification, economic development, mixed knowledge integration, and policy/institutional measures; accelerating CRDP requires addressing the socio-cultural context, politics, governance, economic/financial systems, knowledge, and technology specific to each region.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Findings show shared impacts across geographies include impacts on ecosystems, fisheries, agriculture and water systems, livelihood failure, food insecurity, and degradation of human health, inequality, losses to economic sectors, human migration and conflict, and cascading transboundary impacts. Adaptation and mitigation actions tend to focus on promoting nature-based approaches, livelihood diversification and economic development, harnessing mixed knowledges and policy and institutional measures. Actions with potential to accelerate transitions towards CRDP should focus on the specific arenas of engagement shaping the underlying vulnerability context of each geography, including the socio-cultural context, politics, governance and institutions, the economic and financial systems, knowledge availability, and technological capabilities.
