Paper Title: Projected impacts of climate change on global irrigation water withdrawals
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased AIWW leading to greater water resource depletion risks, exacerbated by population growth and food demand.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Results show that the temporal AIWW average variations have shown a statistically significant increase during 1981–2014 (p<0.05 and R2 = 0.98) ranging from 101.82 to 136.24 mm yr−1 (mean = 120.5 mm yr−1)... Substantiated with population increase, and higher food demand, an increased AIWW will further aggravate globally. Thus, exploring future AIWW changes is a key step forward that requires greater attention in irrigation research interventions helpful to inform societies to reduce future risks of water resource depletion.
Drivers of Risk: Excessive irrigation water withdrawal; Population increase; Higher food demand; Climate models (CMIP6); Shared Socioeconomic Scenarios (SSP370 & 585); Global hydrological model (H08)
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "Investigating processes causing water resource depletion risks is key to understanding past, present, and future excessive irrigation water withdrawal...Although multiple drivers typically contribute to this risk, we studied, for the first time, whether and how Actual Irrigation Water Withdrawals (AIWW) could affect global future water resources projection...Substantiated with population increase, and higher food demand, an increased AIWW will further aggravate globally...Here, we used AIWW datasets from five CMIP6 climate models based on two Shared Socioeconomic Scenarios (SSP370 and 585) and a global hydrological model (H08) to compare the historical (1981–2014) with the future periods: 2041–2070 and 2071–2100."

Paper Title: Climate change and heat stress resilient outdoor workers: findings from systematic literature review
Outcome(s) of Interest: Decreased resilience to thermal stress; lack of awareness regarding adaptation strategies; heat stress prevalence.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Failure to implement effective control strategies for outdoor workers will result in decreased resilience to thermal stress. The findings underscore a lack of awareness regarding certain adaptation strategies and interventions aimed at preventing and enhancing resilience to the impact of climate change on heat stress prevalence among workers in outdoor tropical and subtropical environments.
Drivers of Risk: Extreme heat events, outdoor workers, limited cooling access, thermal stress resilience, awareness deficit, tropical and subtropical climates
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "Global warming has led to an increase in the number and intensity of extreme heat events...workers, especially those working outdoors, as they often have limited access to cooling strategies...decreased resilience to thermal stress...lack of awareness regarding certain adaptation strategies and interventions...impact of climate change on heat stress prevalence among workers in outdoor tropical and subtropical environments."
Category: Health > Heat and Cold (Cardiovascular, Respiratory, etc.)
Category-sourcematerial: Purpose: Global warming has led to an increase in the number and intensity of extreme heat events, posing a significant threat to the health and safety of workers, especially those working outdoors, as they often have limited access to cooling strategies. The present systematic literature review (a) summarizes the current knowledge on the impacts of climate change on outdoor workers... Results: Failure to implement effective control strategies for outdoor workers will result in decreased resilience to thermal stress... Conclusion: The integration of climate change adaptation strategies into occupational health programs can enhance occupational heat resilience among outdoor workers.
Approach-sourcematerial: In this systematic review, we searched scientific databases including Scopus (N = 855), Web of Science (N = 828), and PubMed (N = 202).
Methodology: A systematic literature review was conducted using Scopus, Web of Science, PubMed, and Google Scholar, initially monitoring 2001 articles relating to worker populations across 14 countries, narrowing down to 29 eligible papers for data extraction after full-text assessment.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Materials and methods: In this systematic review, we searched scientific databases including Scopus (N = 855), Web of Science (N = 828), and PubMed (N = 202). Additionally, we identified relevant studies on climate change and heat-stress control measures through Google Scholar (N = 116) using specific search terms. In total, we monitored 2001 articles pertaining to worker populations (men = 2921; women = 627) in various outdoor climate conditions across 14 countries. After full-text assessment, 55 studies were selected for inclusion, and finally, 29 eligible papers were included for data extraction.
Highlights: Climate change and extreme heat threaten outdoor worker health and safety due to limited access to cooling; effective adaptation strategies and awareness are lacking, particularly in tropical and subtropical regions, highlighting the need for integrated occupational health programs, cost-benefit evaluations of heat stress measures, and implementation of risk management, adaptive measures, training, and protective policies.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Purpose: Global warming has led to an increase in the number and intensity of extreme heat events, posing a significant threat to the health and safety of workers, especially those working outdoors, as they often have limited access to cooling strategies...The findings underscore a lack of awareness regarding certain adaptation strategies and interventions aimed at preventing and enhancing resilience to the impact of climate change on heat stress prevalence among workers in outdoor tropical and subtropical environments...The integration of climate change adaptation strategies into occupational health programs can enhance occupational heat resilience among outdoor workers...Conducting cost-benefit evaluations of health and safety measures for thermal stress adaptation strategies among outdoor workers is crucial for professionals and policymakers in low- and middle-income tropical and subtropical countries...Risk management strategies, adaptive measures, heat risk awareness, practical interventions, training programs, and protective policies should be implemented in hot-dry and hot-humid climates to boost the tolerance and resilience of outdoor workers.
More Notes: Outdoor workers in tropical and subtropical regions face increased heat stress due to climate change, requiring integrated adaptation strategies, cost-benefit evaluations of interventions, and implementation of risk management, training, and protective policies to enhance resilience.

The findings underscore a lack of awareness regarding certain adaptation strategies and interventions... Conducting cost-benefit evaluations of health and safety measures for thermal stress adaptation strategies among outdoor workers is crucial for professionals and policymakers in low- and middle-income tropical and subtropical countries... Risk management strategies, adaptive measures, heat risk awareness, practical interventions, training programs, and protective policies should be implemented in hot-dry and hot-humid climates to boost the tolerance and resilience of outdoor workers.

Paper Title: LSTM networks provide efficient cyanobacterial blooms forecasting even with incomplete spatio-temporal data
Outcome(s) of Interest: Cyanobacterial blooms, Early Warning Systems, predictive models, LSTM neural networks, forecasting time horizons, prediction accuracy, skill metric, PC alarm level.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "cyanobacterial blooms are expected to increase in frequency and global distribution...Early Warning Systems (EWS) for cyanobacterial blooms development allow timely implementation of management measures...we propose an effective EWS for cyanobacterial bloom forecasting...six different non-site/species-specific predictive models were compared...Results were analyzed for seven forecasting time horizons...classification metrics (Accuracy, F1, Kappa) for a proposed alarm level of 10 µg PC/L, and a forecasting-specific metric to measure prediction improvement over the displaced signal (skill)...The multivariate version of LSTM showed the best and most consistent results across all forecasting horizons and metrics, achieving accuracies of up to 90 % in predicting the proposed PC alarm level...positive skill values indicated its outstanding effectiveness to forecast cyanobacterial blooms"
Drivers of Risk: Anthropogenic activities, global warming.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Enhanced by anthropogenic activities and global warming, cyanobacterial blooms are expected to increase in frequency and global distribution.
Category-sourcematerial: Cyanobacteria are the most frequent dominant species of algal blooms in inland waters, threatening ecosystem function and water quality, especially when toxin-producing strains predominate. Enhanced by anthropogenic activities and global warming, cyanobacterial blooms are expected to increase in frequency and global distribution.
Approach-sourcematerial: "Using these pre-processed data, six different non-site/species-specific predictive models were compared including the autoregressive and multivariate versions of Linear Regression, Random Forest, and Long-Term Short-Term (LSTM) neural networks."
Methodology: An Early Warning System (EWS) for cyanobacterial bloom forecasting is proposed, using 6 years of incomplete spatio-temporal data from multiparametric probes (including phycocyanin fluorescence). The methodology involves a probe-agnostic data pre-processing method to generate time series specific for cyanobacterial bloom forecasting, followed by a comparison of six non-site/species-specific predictive models (autoregressive and multivariate Linear Regression, Random Forest, and LSTM) across seven forecasting time horizons (4-28 days). Model performance is evaluated using regression, classification, and forecasting-specific metrics.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Cyanobacterial blooms are expected to increase in frequency and global distribution... Early Warning Systems (EWS) for cyanobacterial blooms development allow timely implementation of management measures... In this paper, we propose an effective EWS for cyanobacterial bloom forecasting, which uses 6 years of incomplete high-frequency spatio-temporal data from multiparametric probes, including phycocyanin (PC) fluorescence as a proxy for cyanobacteria. A probe agnostic and replicable method is proposed to pre-process the data and to generate time series specific for cyanobacterial bloom forecasting. Using these pre-processed data, six different non-site/species-specific predictive models were compared including the autoregressive and multivariate versions of Linear Regression, Random Forest, and Long-Term Short-Term (LSTM) neural networks... Results... analyzed for seven forecasting time horizons ranging from 4 to 28 days... evaluated with a hybrid system that combined regression metrics (MSE, R2, MAPE)... classification metrics (Accuracy, F1, Kappa)... and a forecasting-specific metric to measure prediction improvement over the displaced signal (skill).
Highlights: Early warning system (EWS) using multivariate LSTM neural networks effectively forecasts cyanobacterial blooms in inland waters 16-28 days in advance with up to 90% accuracy in predicting a phycocyanin (PC) alarm level of 10 µg PC/L.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "we propose an effective EWS for cyanobacterial bloom forecasting...The multivariate version of LSTM showed the best and most consistent results across all forecasting horizons and metrics, achieving accuracies of up to 90 % in predicting the proposed PC alarm level...positive skill values indicated its outstanding effectiveness to forecast cyanobacterial blooms from 16 to 28 days in advance."
More Notes: Cyanobacterial blooms, exacerbated by human activities and global warming, are increasingly frequent and widespread; an early warning system using LSTM neural networks and phycocyanin fluorescence data can effectively forecast these blooms up to 28 days in advance with high accuracy.
More Notes-sourcematerial: Cyanobacteria are the most frequent dominant species of algal blooms in inland waters...Enhanced by anthropogenic activities and global warming, cyanobacterial blooms are expected to increase in frequency and global distribution...In this paper, we propose an effective EWS for cyanobacterial bloom forecasting...The multivariate version of LSTM showed the best and most consistent results across all forecasting horizons and metrics, achieving accuracies of up to 90 % in predicting the proposed PC alarm level...positive skill values indicated its outstanding effectiveness to forecast cyanobacterial blooms from 16 to 28 days in advance.

Paper Title: Population at risk of dengue virus transmission has increased due to coupled climate factors and population growth
Outcome(s) of Interest: Dengue transmission, geographical distribution, population growth, climate suitability expansion, North America, East Asia, Mediterranean basin, Global North, Global South, increased population in favorable areas.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Dengue virus transmission has increased...examined how shifts in these climatic variables and human population growth have contributed to the change in the geographical distribution and size of the global population...found an expansion in climate suitability in North America, East Asia and the Mediterranean basin...Globally, we estimated that the population in areas with high climate suitability has grown by approximately 2.5 billion...in the Global South...in the Global North...
Drivers of Risk: Climate change (temperature, humidity), population growth, urbanization.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "The increased risk of dengue transmission is driven by changes in climate, particularly temperature and humidity, along with urbanization and population growth." "Climate-dependent variables, specifically temperature and humidity, are drivers of dengue transmission risk." "The increase in the global population living in areas with high climate suitability for DENV transmission emphasizes the impact of population growth and urbanization in high-risk regions, particularly the Global South."
Category-sourcematerial: The paper evaluates the increased population at risk of dengue virus transmission due to climate factors and population growth. Dengue virus transmission potential is evaluated. The study evaluates climate suitability changes for dengue virus transmission using Index P, a mechanistic measure based on temperature and relative humidity. This page evaluates changes in land area and population with high climate suitability for dengue virus transmission between 1979–1983 and 2018–2022. Dengue incidence and the proportion of the global population living in areas with high climate suitability for dengue transmission.
Approach-sourcematerial: Page 2: Mechanistic and statistical (species distribution modeling) approaches are used to estimate dengue transmission potential based on climate variables. Page 3: Analysis uses Index P based on temperature and humidity data, evaluates trends from 1979 to 2022, and displays results using spatiotemporal maps. Page 5: Long-term trends in climate suitability are quantified using a seasonal Mann–Kendall test. Page 9: The study uses Tobit models to estimate relationships between climate suitability (Index P) and DENV transmission, with a meta-analysis performed for country-level estimates. Long-term trends are assessed using a seasonal Mann-Kendall test, and autocorrelation is handled using a pre-whitening algorithm.
Methodology: Dengue transmission potential is estimated using mechanistic models to quantify potential and statistical models for climate-dengue associations, based on temperature and humidity. The Index P evaluates trends from 1979-2022 with spatiotemporal maps, and past/present climate suitability is defined using averages from 1979-1983 and 2018-2022, respectively. Mann-Kendall test quantifies long-term trends, accounting for temperature and humidity effects on mosquito-viral traits across varying demographics and climates. Data contextualized by economic and climate zones using Köppen–Geiger, with Tobit models and meta-analysis used for country-level estimates, and autocorrelation addressed through pre-whitening.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Page 2: "Mechanistic and statistical (species distribution modeling) approaches are used to estimate dengue transmission potential based on climate variables. Mechanistic models quantify transmission potential, while statistical models estimate associations between climate variables and dengue/vector observations. "; Page 3: "Analysis uses Index P based on temperature and humidity data, evaluates trends from 1979 to 2022, and displays results using spatiotemporal maps."; Page 5: "The study examines changes in climate suitability for DENV transmission from 1979 to 2022. It defines past climate suitability as the average from 1979 to 1983 and present climate suitability as the average from 2018 to 2022. Long-term trends in climate suitability are quantified using a seasonal Mann–Kendall test."; Page 6: "Climate suitability measure incorporates effects of temperature and relative humidity on mosquito-viral traits, focusing on shifts from 1979-1983 to 2017-2022 across various population densities, income groups, and climate zones."; Page 8: "The analysis uses demographic, economic, and climate zone categorizations to contextualize data, with specific datasets obtained for population counts and density, income groups categorized by World Bank criteria, and climate zones by the Köppen–Geiger system."; Page 9: "The study uses Tobit models to estimate relationships between climate suitability (Index P) and DENV transmission, with a meta-analysis performed for country-level estimates. Long-term trends are assessed using a seasonal Mann-Kendall test, and autocorrelation is handled using a pre-whitening algorithm."
Highlights: Expansion of climate suitability for dengue is occurring in North America, East Asia, and the Mediterranean, leading to a global increase in both land area and population at high risk, particularly in tropical and subtropical regions; population growth drives this increase more than climate change in the Global South, while climate change is more influential in the Global North, with temperate zones and high-density areas experiencing the greatest changes in suitability.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Page 1: "The study finds an expansion of climate suitability for dengue in North America, East Asia, and the Mediterranean basin."; Page 4: "There is an increase in both land area and population with high climate suitability for dengue virus transmission, with notable expansion in tropical and subtropical regions."; Page 5: "A significant increase in the proportion of the global population living in areas with high climate suitability for dengue transmission, primarily driven by changes in Africa and Asia. Population growth is responsible for a larger increase in the population living in high-risk areas than climate change alone, except in the Global North."; Page 6: "Significant increase in climate suitability occurred globally for DENV transmission, with highest changes seen in high and very high-density areas and temperate climate zones experiencing the largest net change."; Page 7: "There is a substantial increase in the global population living in areas with high climate suitability for DENV transmission, with significant differences in the driving processes between the Global South and Global North."
More Notes: The study compares climate suitability and dengue incidence in several countries and finds population growth disproportionately occurred in areas with historically high suitability, with increases in suitability in the southern US and other regions, and decreases in other areas.
More Notes-sourcematerial: The paper compares climate suitability and dengue incidence across municipalities/provinces/cantons in Brazil, Colombia, Costa Rica, Taiwan, Thailand, and Vietnam. The study also considers the independent contributions of climate change and population growth to the observed increase, finding that population growth has disproportionately occurred in areas with historically high climate suitability. They observed increases in climate suitability in the southern United States, northeastern Brazil, western and southern Africa, the Indian subcontinent, Southeast Asia, and coastal areas of continental Europe. Decreases in climate suitability were observed in northern Australia, parts of South America, and the northeast of Africa.
Hazard definition-sourcematerial: The study examines changes in climate suitability for DENV transmission from 1979 to 2022.
Impact persistence-sourcematerial: The study examines changes in climate suitability for DENV transmission from 1979 to 2022. It defines past climate suitability as the average from 1979 to 1983 and present climate suitability as the average from 2018 to 2022. Long-term trends in climate suitability are quantified using a seasonal Mann–Kendall test. Climate suitability measure incorporates effects of temperature and relative humidity on mosquito-viral traits, focusing on shifts from 1979-1983 to 2017-2022 across various population densities, income groups, and climate zones. The study uses Tobit models to estimate relationships between climate suitability (Index P) and DENV transmission, with a meta-analysis performed for country-level estimates. Long-term trends are assessed using a seasonal Mann-Kendall test, and autocorrelation is handled using a pre-whitening algorithm.

Paper Title: Fall armyworm management in a changing climate: an overview of climate-responsive integrated pest management (IPM) strategies for long-term control
Outcome(s) of Interest: IPM strategies (agro-ecological, biological approaches); yield loss mitigation; sustainable FAW control.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Significant progress has been made in implementing integrated pest management (IPM) strategies that integrate agro-ecological and biological approaches...This review article focuses on the compilation of IPM methods...Approaches such as intercropping, agronomic practices, and the use of parasitoids and viruses have shown promising results in controlling FAW...insights into successful management methods, recommendations and suggestions for the sustainable control of FAW using agro-ecological practices, biological control agents or their combination.
Drivers of Risk: Invasive alien insects; climate change; rapid reproduction; adaptation; high distribution capability; ecological damage; economic losses; fall armyworm.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Invasive alien insects have the potential to pose a significant threat to global agriculture, with their distinctive traits enabling rapid reproduction, successful adaptation to new environments and high distribution capability. These pests can devastate crops, livestock, biodiversity and ecosystem functioning, resulting in ecological damage and substantial economic losses. Climate change plays a crucial role in driving the invasion of these pests, creating favorable conditions for their development, and negatively impacting global biodiversity. Among invasive alien insects, fall armyworm (FAW) (Spodoptera frugiperda) has emerged as a major pest species, causing significant yield losses in maize cropping outside his native range.
Category-sourcematerial: "Invasive alien insects have the potential to pose a significant threat to global agriculture, with their distinctive traits enabling rapid reproduction, successful adaptation to new environments and high distribution capability. These pests can devastate crops, livestock, biodiversity and ecosystem functioning, resulting in ecological damage and substantial economic losses."
Approach-sourcematerial: This review article focuses on the compilation of IPM methods, combining agro-ecological practices and biological control agents such as parasitoids and viruses, for the effective management of FAW. Approaches such as intercropping, agronomic practices, and the use of parasitoids and viruses have shown promising results in controlling FAW. This review article provides insights into successful management methods, recommendations and suggestions for the sustainable control of FAW using agro-ecological practices, biological control agents or their combination.
Methodology: The methodology involves compiling integrated pest management (IPM) strategies that combine agro-ecological practices, such as intercropping and agronomic techniques, with biological control agents like parasitoids and viruses to manage fall armyworm (FAW) effectively.

Approaches such as intercropping, agronomic practices, and the use of parasitoids and viruses have shown promising results...
Highlights: Fall armyworm (FAW), an invasive insect, threatens global agriculture due to climate change-driven favorable conditions; integrated pest management (IPM) strategies utilizing agro-ecological practices and biological control agents (parasitoids, viruses) offer promising sustainable control alternatives to ineffective pesticides.

The fall armyworm has caused significant yield losses, but integrated pest management strategies, combining agro-ecological practices and biological controls like parasitoids and viruses, show promise for sustainable control.

Paper Title: Conservation agriculture improves soil health and sustains crop yields after long-term warming
Outcome(s) of Interest: Conservation agriculture, soil health, microbial diversity, crop yields, soil organic carbon, microbial biomass carbon, wheat yield increase, resilience, climate warming.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Climate warming threatens global food security by exacerbating pressures on degraded soils under intensive crop production. Conservation agriculture is promoted as a sustainable solution that improves soil health and sustains crop yields in a changing climate, but these benefits may be affected by long-term warming. Here, we investigate the effects of conservation agriculture compared to conventional agriculture on 17 soil properties, microbial diversity and crop yields, during eight-years’ experimental warming. An overall positive effect of warming on soil health over time under conservation agriculture is characterized by linear increases in soil organic carbon and microbial biomass carbon. Warming-triggered shifts in microbial biomass carbon and fungal diversity (saprogen richness) are directly linked to a 9.3% increase in wheat yields over eight years, but only under conservation agriculture. Overall, conservation agriculture results in an average 21% increase in soil health and supports similar levels of crop production after long-term warming compared to conventional agriculture. Our work provides insights into the potential benefits of conservation agriculture for long-term sustainable food production because improved soil health improves resilience to the effects of climate warming.

Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "plants emitted more CO2 in countries where more assets would be devalued under a 1.5 °C scenario...Although the extra amount of carbon emitted each year trigged by imperiled assets is relatively small, it would exhaust a sizable portion of the electricity sector’s remaining carbon budget when added up over time. This is especially true in the U.S. and Russia where up to 16% and 12% of their budgets, respectively, could be spent within ten years due solely to the stranded asset effect."
Drivers of Risk: Regulatory leniency; vested interest in long-term fossil fuel contracts; imperiled fossil fuel assets; anticipation of stronger climate policies.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "plants emitted more CO2 in countries where more assets would be devalued under a 1.5 °C scenario, which we theorize is due to these countries’ regulatory leniency and plants’ vested interest in long-term fossil fuel contracts"
Category-sourcematerial: Here, using a worldwide data source on individual power plants’ CO2 emissions ... plants emitted more CO2 in countries where more assets would be devalued under a 1.5 °C scenario ... This is especially true in the U.S. and Russia where up to 16% and 12% of their budgets, respectively, could be spent within ten years due solely to the stranded asset effect.
Approach-sourcematerial: Here, using a worldwide data source on individual power plants’ CO2 emissions and the value of countries’ at-risk fossil fuel assets, we show that between 2009 and 2018, plants emitted more CO2 in countries where more assets would be devalued under a 1.5 °C scenario, which we theorize is due to these countries’ regulatory leniency and plants’ vested interest in long-term fossil fuel contracts.
Methodology: Analysis of a worldwide dataset of individual power plant CO2 emissions and national at-risk fossil fuel asset values to demonstrate a correlation between higher emissions (2009-2018) and greater asset devaluation risk under a 1.5°C warming scenario.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "using a worldwide data source on individual power plants’ CO2 emissions and the value of countries’ at-risk fossil fuel assets, we show that between 2009 and 2018, plants emitted more CO2 in countries where more assets would be devalued under a 1.5 °C scenario"
Highlights: Between 2009 and 2018, power plants in countries with more at-risk fossil fuel assets emitted more CO2, particularly in the U.S. and Russia, due to regulatory leniency and long-term fossil fuel contracts, potentially consuming a significant portion of the electricity sector's carbon budget over time.
Highlights-sourcematerial: using a worldwide data source on individual power plants’ CO2 emissions and the value of countries’ at-risk fossil fuel assets, we show that between 2009 and 2018, plants emitted more CO2 in countries where more assets would be devalued under a 1.5 °C scenario, which we theorize is due to these countries’ regulatory leniency and plants’ vested interest in long-term fossil fuel contracts... This is especially true in the U.S. and Russia where up to 16% and 12% of their budgets, respectively, could be spent within ten years due solely to the stranded asset effect.

Paper Title: Forest landowner values and perspectives of prescribed fire in the Northeast/Mid-Atlantic region of the United States
Outcome(s) of Interest: Protecting forest health, controlling invasive species, and wildlife habitat.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Preferred outcomes include protecting forest health, controlling invasive species, and wildlife habitat.
Drivers of Risk: Limited knowledge/experience with burning; perceived low risk; cost of burning; governance (state coordination, cost-share, access to consultants); demographic factors.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Respondents had limited knowledge and experience with burning overall, but many also perceived fire as a low-risk tool and were trusting of burning professionals. Also significant in explaining landowner choices were economic (e.g., cost of burning), governance (e.g., state coordination, cost-share assistance, and access to consultants), and demographic factors.
Category-sourcematerial: Background: Fire is an important ecological process that shapes structures and compositions in many ecosystems worldwide...To help inform policies that promote prescribed burning on private lands, we conducted a regional survey of forest landowners regarding their perspectives and willingness to pay (WTP) for prescribed fire as a management tool.
Approach-sourcematerial: Results: According to two models, the mean WTP for the prescribed fire was $10 ha−1 and $40 ha−1 ($4 ac−1 and $16 ac−1) but could be as high as $220 ha−1 ($89 ac−1) for specific outcomes and programs. Spatial analysis revealed a north–south gradient in landowner opinions across the region, with opinions about burning more positive in the south. Also significant in explaining landowner choices were economic (e.g., cost of burning), governance (e.g., state coordination, cost-share assistance, and access to consultants), and demographic factors.
Methodology: A regional survey of forest landowners in the Northeast/Mid-Atlantic US was conducted to assess perspectives and willingness to pay (WTP) for prescribed fire as a management tool, coupled with spatial hotspot analysis to detect regional variations in landowner opinions.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "To help inform policies that promote prescribed burning on private lands, we conducted a regional survey of forest landowners regarding their perspectives and willingness to pay (WTP) for prescribed fire as a management tool. We also used spatial hotspot analysis to detect regional variations in landowner opinions."
Highlights: Landowners in the Northeast/Mid-Atlantic US show significant support for prescribed fire on private lands, particularly in Pennsylvania, despite limited knowledge; education, technical and financial support are crucial for effective implementation.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "There is significant support by landowners to use prescribed fire to achieve management objectives on private lands in the Northeast/Mid-Atlantic region. Pennsylvania landowners, in particular, were strongly motivated to use prescribed fire; however, knowledge and experience are severely limited. Education, technical support, financial assistance, and access to professionals will be important for helping landowners use prescribed fire to achieve management objectives."
More Notes: Landowners in the Northeast/Mid-Atlantic US, particularly in Pennsylvania, show support for prescribed fire on private lands to improve forest health, control invasives, and enhance wildlife habitat, but require education, technical and financial assistance, and professional support to implement it effectively.
More Notes-sourcematerial: Respondents had limited knowledge and experience with burning overall, but many also perceived fire as a low-risk tool and were trusting of burning professionals. Most landowners (64%) expressed interest in a variety of prescribed fire programs to help achieve management outcomes. Preferred outcomes include protecting forest health, controlling invasive species, and wildlife habitat. Pennsylvania landowners were unique within our study in that they placed the highest economic value on prescribed fire, despite having limited knowledge and experience. There is significant support by landowners to use prescribed fire to achieve management objectives on private lands in the Northeast/Mid-Atlantic region... Education, technical support, financial assistance, and access to professionals will be important for helping landowners use prescribed fire to achieve management objectives.
Hazard definition-sourcematerial: The abstract focuses on prescribed fire as a management tool and landowner perspectives, not direct climate hazards.
Space disaggregation-sourcematerial: Background: ...we conducted a regional survey of forest landowners regarding their perspectives and willingness to pay (WTP) for prescribed fire as a management tool. We also used spatial hotspot analysis to detect regional variations in landowner opinions. Results: ...Spatial analysis revealed a north–south gradient in landowner opinions across the region, with opinions about burning more positive in the south... Conclusions: There is significant support by landowners to use prescribed fire to achieve management objectives on private lands in the Northeast/Mid-Atlantic region...
Impact interactions-sourcematerial: Background: Fire is an important ecological process that shapes structures and compositions in many ecosystems worldwide. Changes in climate, land use, and long-term fire exclusion have altered historic fire regimes often leading to more intense and severe wildfires and loss of biodiversity.
Economic dynamics-sourcematerial: Respondents had limited knowledge and experience with burning overall, but many also perceived fire as a low-risk tool and were trusting of burning professionals. Most landowners (64%) expressed interest in a variety of prescribed fire programs to help achieve management outcomes. Preferred outcomes include protecting forest health, controlling invasive species, and wildlife habitat. Also significant in explaining landowner choices were economic (e.g., cost of burning), governance (e.g., state coordination, cost-share assistance, and access to consultants), and demographic factors. According to two models, the mean WTP for the prescribed fire was $10 ha−1 and $40 ha−1 ($4 ac−1 and $16 ac−1) but could be as high as $220 ha−1 ($89 ac−1) for specific outcomes and programs.
Climate features-sourcematerial: There is no mention of tipping points or other complexities of the climate system in the provided abstract. The paper focuses on landowner perspectives on prescribed burning.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is considered through resource managers' interest in reintroducing fire in historically fire-dependent ecosystems, focusing on enhancing ecosystem services and achieving management outcomes such as protecting forest health, controlling invasive species, and supporting wildlife habitat. Landowners' willingness to pay for prescribed fire suggests economic adaptation considerations, while governance factors (state coordination, cost-share assistance, access to consultants) also play a significant role. The survey highlights regional variations in landowner opinions, with a need for education, technical support, and professional access to facilitate adaptation through prescribed burning.
Adaptation considered-sourcematerial: "There is an increasing interest by resource managers to reintroduce fire in historically fire-dependent ecosystems while enhancing the provision of ecosystem services.", "Preferred outcomes include protecting forest health, controlling invasive species, and wildlife habitat.", "Also significant in explaining landowner choices were economic (e.g., cost of burning), governance (e.g., state coordination, cost-share assistance, and access to consultants), and demographic factors.", "Education, technical support, financial assistance, and access to professionals will be important for helping landowners use prescribed fire to achieve management objectives."

Paper Title: Projecting the Potential Global Distribution of Sweetgum Inscriber, Acanthotomicus suncei (Coleoptera: Curculionidae: Scolytinae) Concerning the Host Liquidambar styraciflua Under Climate Change Scenarios
Outcome(s) of Interest: Acanthotomicus suncei spread, Liquidambar styraciflua threat, economic and ecological losses, suitable distribution prediction, seasonal rainfall, future climate impact, habitat changes, quarantine focus, sentinel surveillance, control measures.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Acanthotomicus suncei is a newly discovered bark beetle in China that significantly threatens the American sweetgum Liquidambar styraciflua. In recent years, this pest has spread from its original habitat to many surrounding cities, causing substantial economic and ecological losses. Once the pest colonizes a new climatically suitable area, the consequences could be severe. The results showed that A. suncei was mainly distributed in Southern China, in South Hokkaido in Japan, Southern USA, the La Plata Plain in South America, southeastern Australia, and the northern Mediterranean; these areas are located in subtropical monsoon, monsoonal humid climates, or Mediterranean climate zones. Seasonal rainfall, especially in winter, is a key environmental factor that affects the suitable distribution of A. suncei. Under future climates, the total suitable area of A. suncei is projected to decrease to a certain extent. However, changes in its original habitat require serious attention. Suitable areas in some countries in Southeast and South Asia bordering China are also expected to show an increased distribution. Quarantine efforts should be concentrated in high-suitability regions determined in this study to protect against the occurrence of hosts that may contain A. suncei, thereby avoiding its long-distance spread. Long-term sentinel surveillance and control measures should be carried out as soon as A. suncei is detected, especially in regions with high suitability.
Drivers of Risk: Climate change impacts, seasonal rainfall, host distribution, sea transportation.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "Under future climates, the total suitable area of A. suncei is projected to decrease to a certain extent...Seasonal rainfall, especially in winter, is a key environmental factor that affects the suitable distribution of A. suncei...Considering the wide global distribution of its host, Liquidambar styraciflua, this pest is likely to continue to spread and expand...The outward spread of this pest via sea transportation cannot be ignored."

Methodology: The study employed the CLIMEX and Random Forests models to predict the global potential suitable distribution of Acanthotomicus suncei, focusing on climatic suitability affected by variables such as seasonal rainfall, and recommended focusing quarantine efforts on high-risk areas identified by these methods.

Paper Title: Losing ground: projections of climate-driven bloom shifts and their implications for the future of California’s almond orchards
Outcome(s) of Interest: Reduced almond farming land suitability; earlier bloom timing.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "...the available land to support almond orchard farming could decline between 48 to 73% by the end of the century... a projected earlier bloom between DOY 28–33 by 2100."
Drivers of Risk: Climate change; altered bloom timing (earlier); unchanged emission scenarios; land suitability decline.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "Climate change is expected to impact the spring phenology of perennial trees...under unchanged emission scenarios, the available land to support almond orchard farming could decline between 48 to 73% by the end of the century...This reduction corresponds with an early shift in bloom time...understanding and addressing these factors is essential for the sustainable management and preservation of agricultural land..."
Category-sourcematerial: "In this study, we investigate the effects of climate change on the bloom timing of almond orchards, focusing on California, the world's leading region for almond production... the available land to support almond orchard farming could decline between 48 to 73% by the end of the century... These results emphasize the critical role climate shifts have in shaping future land use strategies for almond production in Central Valley, California... ensuring long-term food security and economic stability in the face of a rapidly changing climate."
Approach-sourcematerial: By analyzing historical climatic data, employing a model that considers hourly temperatures and fall non-structural carbohydrates to predict bloom dates, and examining various Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 6 (CMIP6) scenarios, we assess the potential impacts of climate shifts on plant phenology...
Methodology: The study examines the impact of climate change on almond bloom timing in California by analyzing historical climate data, using a model incorporating hourly temperatures and fall non-structural carbohydrates to predict bloom dates, and assessing scenarios from the CMIP6 to evaluate potential shifts in plant phenology and land suitability for almond farming.
Methodology-sourcematerial: By analyzing historical climatic data, employing a model that considers hourly temperatures and fall non-structural carbohydrates to predict bloom dates, and examining various Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 6 (CMIP6) scenarios, we assess the potential impacts of climate shifts on plant phenology and, consequently, on land suitability for almond farming.
Highlights: Climate change is predicted to significantly reduce land suitability for almond farming in California by the end of the century (48-73% reduction under unchanged emission scenarios) due to earlier bloom times (shifting from Day of Year 64 to 28-33), although little change is expected in the next 30 years.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "Our findings reveal that, within the next 30 years, the land suitable for almond production will not undergo significant changes. However, under unchanged emission scenarios, the available land to support almond orchard farming could decline between 48 to 73% by the end of the century. This reduction corresponds with an early shift in bloom time from the average Day of Year (DOY) 64 observed over the past 40 years to a projected earlier bloom between DOY 28–33 by 2100."
More Notes: Climate change is projected to significantly reduce land suitability for almond production in California's Central Valley by the end of the century (48-73% reduction) due to earlier bloom times (shifting from DOY 64 to DOY 28-33), highlighting the need for sustainable management strategies to ensure long-term food security and economic stability.
More Notes-sourcematerial: "However, under unchanged emission scenarios, the available land to support almond orchard farming could decline between 48 to 73% by the end of the century. This reduction corresponds with an early shift in bloom time from the average Day of Year (DOY) 64 observed over the past 40 years to a projected earlier bloom between DOY 28–33 by 2100. These results emphasize the critical role climate shifts have in shaping future land use strategies for almond production in Central Valley, California. Consequently, understanding and addressing these factors is essential for the sustainable management and preservation of agricultural land, ensuring long-term food security and economic stability in the face of a rapidly changing climate."
Hazard definition-sourcematerial: "Climate change is expected to impact the spring phenology of perennial trees...Our findings reveal that, within the next 30 years, the land suitable for almond production will not undergo significant changes. However, under unchanged emission scenarios, the available land to support almond orchard farming could decline between 48 to 73% by the end of the century."
Impact persistence-sourcematerial: The abstract focuses on changes in land suitability and bloom timing, but it does not mention the persistence of impacts. It looks at shifts over time (30 years, end of century), but doesn't address whether impacts experienced in one year are likely to persist into subsequent years. Therefore, persistence is not considered in the abstract.

Consequently, understanding and addressing these factors is essential for the sustainable management and preservation of agricultural land, ensuring long-term food security and economic stability in the face of a rapidly changing climate.

Paper Title: Global projections of heat exposure of older adults
Outcome(s) of Interest: Doubled chronic heat exposure; 23%+ of 69+ population exposed to >37.5°C heat (vs. 14% today); 177-246 million more older adults exposed; severe effects in Asia/Africa.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: chronic exposure to heat doubles across all warming scenarios. Moreover, >23% of the global population aged 69+ will inhabit climates whose 95th percentile of daily maximum temperature exceeds the critical threshold of 37.5 °C, compared with 14% today, exposing an additional 177–246 million older adults to dangerous acute heat. Effects are most severe in Asia and Africa, which also have the lowest adaptive capacity.
Drivers of Risk: Climate change, population aging, rising temperatures, demographic changes, heat exposure.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Climate change and population aging are identified as the main drivers of increased heat exposure risks, particularly affecting older adults. (Page 5) Future climate-related risks are influenced by total population growth, demographic changes in the age structure, and heat exposure. (Page 4) The primary driver identified is climate change, particularly rising temperatures, which increases heat exposure and related mortality risks among the elderly. (Page 12) Value changes in population age structure and climate scenarios SSP2(45) and 5(85) affecting heat exposure determinants. (Page 6)
Category: Health > Heat and Cold (Cardiovascular, Respiratory, etc.)
Category-sourcematerial: Page 1: Heat exposure of older adults, Page 6: The paper evaluates the impacts of climate change on chronic and acute heat exposures among older adults., Page 8: The paper evaluates the effectiveness of adaptation measures in moderating morbidity and mortality risk due to climate impacts, particularly for older adults., Page 12: The paper assesses heat-related mortality risk, particularly in relation to climate change predictions and population aging.
Approach-sourcematerial: Combines age-stratified demographic projections with downscaled temperature projections to mid-century; constructs global gridded age-stratified demographic projections for different population scenarios and combines them with temperature projections from CMIP6 models; The analysis uses socio-demographic and radiative forcing scenarios, specifically SSP-RCP combinations, to forecast changes by around the year 2050; Analysis is based on CMIP6 global climate models with exclusions of hot models; The analysis is performed using gridded age-stratified population data with population projections under five different SSP scenarios, relying on WorldPop data and open-source R software for projections.
Methodology: The study combines age-stratified demographic projections with downscaled temperature projections to quantify heat exposure.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Building on our approach developed for the United States, we construct global gridded age-stratified demographic projections for different population scenarios and combine them with temperature projections from the Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 6 (CMIP6) downscaled, bias-corrected model output to quantify chronic exposure to high average temperatures and the frequency and intensity of acute exposure to extreme high temperatures for different age groups across the world.
Highlights: Projected acceleration of both chronic and acute heat exposures raises health concerns for older adults due to their reduced capacity to thermoregulate.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Our projected acceleration of both chronic and acute heat exposures of older adults raises a serious concern for global public health... Many 69+ individuals are disproportionately vulnerable to the effects of heat extremes due to chronic health conditions and illnesses, physical, sensory, or cognitive disabilities, and social isolation, marginalization, and lacking access to healthcare or resources for private adaptation.

Impact persistence-sourcematerial: Page 1: Theglobalpopulationisagingatthesametimeasheatexposuresareincreasingduetoclimatechange.Agestructure,anditsbiologicalandsocio-economicdrivers,determinepopulations’vulnerabilitytohightemperatures.Herewecombineage-stratiﬁeddemographicprojectionswithdownscaledtemperatureprojectionstomid-centuryandﬁndthatchronicexposuretoheatdoublesacrossallwarmingscenarios.

Impact interactions-sourcematerial: Building on our approach developed for the United States49,we construct global gridded age-stratiﬁed demographic projections for different population scenarios (see Methods) and combine them with temperature projections from the Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 6 (CMIP6) downscaled, bias-corrected model output50 to quantify chronic exposure to high average temperatures and the fre- quency and intensity of acute exposure to extreme high temperatures for different age groups across the world. Our gridded population projections are benchmarked against previous region-speciﬁcmodel- structureling work to assess their consistency (see Methods). We develop tem- perature exposure metrics at the same spatial resolution. For cumulativeexposures we use annual cooling degree days (CDDs) above the 24° C threshold. For acute exposures metrics we use the annual count of hot days (#HDs: days with maximum temperature exceeding 37.5° C) and the 95th percentile of the 20 y daily maximum temperature distribution(TMAX95). From the resulting dataset we compute the geographic intersection of current (2020) and projected mid-century (2050) changes in global and regional populations of adults age 69+, and chronic and acute heat exposures.
Economic dynamics-sourcematerial: The analysis uses socio-demographic and radiative forcing scenarios, specifically SSP-RCP combinations, to forecast changes by around the year 2050.

Adaptation considered: Adaptation is considered indirectly through the analysis of socio-demographic scenarios (SSP-RCP combinations) and the exclusion of "hot" climate models. Lowest adaptive capacity in Asia and Africa is noted. The paper suggests that increased heat exposure will likely drive up the demand for indoor thermal regulation appliances. The study mentions the potential for adaptation measures like increasing building albedo and enlarging green spaces.

Paper Title: Prescribed burn related increases of population exposure to PM2.5 and O3 pollution in the southeastern US over 2013–2020

Approach-sourcematerial: "We employed a multistage modeling framework to estimate year-round, long-term effects of prescribed burns on air quality and premature deaths. The framework integrates a chemical transport model with a data-fusion approach to estimate 24-h average PM2.5 and maximum daily 8-h averaged O3 (MDA8-O3) concentrations attributable to prescribed burns for the period 2013–2020. The Global Exposure Mortality Model and a log-linear exposure–response function were used to estimate the premature deaths ascribed to long-term prescribed burn PM2.5 and MDA8-O3 exposure in ten southeastern states."
Methodology: A multistage modeling framework integrated a chemical transport model with data fusion to estimate 24-h average PM2.5 and maximum daily 8-h averaged O3 (MDA8-O3) concentrations attributable to prescribed burns (2013-2020). The Global Exposure Mortality Model and a log-linear exposure–response function were used to estimate premature deaths from long-term PM2.5 and MDA8-O3 exposure in ten southeastern states.
Methodology-sourcematerial: We employed a multistage modeling framework to estimate year-round, long-term effects of prescribed burns on air quality and premature deaths. The framework integrates a chemical transport model with a data-fusion approach to estimate 24-h average PM2.5 and maximum daily 8-h averaged O3 (MDA8-O3) concentrations attributable to prescribed burns for the period 2013–2020. The Global Exposure Mortality Model and a log-linear exposure–response function were used to estimate the premature deaths ascribed to long-term prescribed burn PM2.5 and MDA8-O3 exposure in ten southeastern states.
Highlights: Prescribed burns in the southeastern US (2013-2020) contributed significantly to PM2.5 (10% of ambient) and MDA8-O3 (0.8% of ambient), leading to an estimated 20,416 and 1,332 premature deaths, respectively, highlighting the need for enhanced forest management.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "Our results indicate that prescribed burns contributed on annual average 0.59 ± 0.20 µg/m3 of PM2.5 (∼10 % of ambient PM2.5)... Additionally, prescribed burns are responsible for an average increase of 0.32 ± 0.12 ppb of MDA8-O3 (0.8 % of ambient MDA8-O3)... The combined effect of prescribed burn PM2.5 exposure...resulted in a total of 20,416 (95 % confidence interval (CI): 16,562–24,174) excess non-accidental premature deaths... Prescribed burn MDA8-O3 was responsible for an additional 1,332 (95 % CI: 858–1,803) premature deaths... These findings indicate significant impacts from prescribed burns, suggesting potential benefits of enhanced forest management strategies."

Paper Title: Applications of CRISPR Technologies in Forestry and Molecular Wood Biotechnology
Outcome(s) of Interest: Climate-resilient forests; disease resistance; abiotic stress tolerance; modified wood properties; accelerated growth.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "CRISPR technologies offer a transformative solution, enabling precise and efficient genome editing to accelerate the development of climate-resilient and productive forests...exploring its potential for enhancing disease resistance, improving abiotic stress tolerance, modifying wood properties, and accelerating growth."
Drivers of Risk: Climate change; emerging diseases; long generation times; limited genetic variation.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Forests worldwide are under increasing pressure from climate change and emerging diseases, threatening their vital ecological and economic roles. Traditional breeding approaches, while valuable, are inherently slow and limited by the long generation times and existing genetic variation of trees.
Category-sourcematerial: Forests worldwide are under increasing pressure from climate change and emerging diseases, threatening their vital ecological and economic roles. [...] CRISPR technologies offer a transformative solution, enabling precise and efficient genome editing to accelerate the development of climate-resilient and productive forests. [...] This review provides a comprehensive overview of CRISPR applications in forestry, exploring its potential for enhancing disease resistance, improving abiotic stress tolerance, modifying wood properties, and accelerating growth. [...] This review emphasizes the need for continued research, technological advancements, extensive long-term field trials, public engagement, and responsible innovation to fully harness the power of CRISPR for shaping a sustainable future for forests.
Approach-sourcematerial: The abstract is a review of CRISPR technology applications in forestry and does not present any original modeling or analysis of climate impacts.
Methodology: CRISPR technologies, including base editing, prime editing, and multiplexing strategies, are explored for enhancing disease resistance, abiotic stress tolerance, wood properties, and growth in forests, with a focus on overcoming reagent delivery and plant regeneration challenges; CRISPR gene drive and genetic containment are also discussed.
Methodology-sourcematerial: CRISPR technologies offer a transformative solution, enabling precise and efficient genome editing... We discuss the mechanisms and applications of various CRISPR systems, including base editing, prime editing, and multiplexing strategies... Additionally, we highlight recent advances in overcoming key challenges such as reagent delivery and plant regeneration... We also delve into the potential and ethical considerations of using CRISPR gene drive for population-level genetic alterations, as well as the importance of genetic containment strategies...
Highlights: CRISPR technology offers a transformative solution for developing climate-resilient and productive forests by enabling precise and efficient genome editing to enhance disease resistance, improve stress tolerance, modify wood properties, and accelerate growth; the review covers CRISPR systems, delivery and regeneration challenges, gene drive potential and ethical concerns, and the need for further research and responsible innovation.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Forests worldwide are under increasing pressure from climate change...CRISPR technologies offer a transformative solution...enabling precise and efficient genome editing to accelerate the development of climate-resilient and productive forests...enhancing disease resistance, improving abiotic stress tolerance, modifying wood properties, and accelerating growth...We discuss the mechanisms and applications of various CRISPR systems...highlight recent advances in overcoming key challenges such as reagent delivery and plant regeneration...We also delve into the potential and ethical considerations of using CRISPR gene drive...This review emphasizes the need for continued research...and responsible innovation.
More Notes: CRISPR technology offers a potential solution for developing climate-resilient and productive forests by enabling precise and efficient genome editing to enhance disease resistance, improve abiotic stress tolerance, modify wood properties, and accelerate growth, but requires continued research, technological advancements, extensive field trials, public engagement, and responsible innovation.
More Notes-sourcematerial: Forests worldwide are under increasing pressure from climate change...CRISPR technologies offer a transformative solution...enhancing disease resistance, improving abiotic stress tolerance, modifying wood properties, and accelerating growth...This review emphasizes the need for continued research, technological advancements, extensive long-term field trials, public engagement, and responsible innovation to fully harness the power of CRISPR for shaping a sustainable future for forests.
Hazard definition-sourcematerial: Forests worldwide are under increasing pressure from climate change and emerging diseases, threatening their vital ecological and economic roles. Traditional breeding approaches, while valuable, are inherently slow and limited by the long generation times and existing genetic variation of trees. CRISPR technologies offer a transformative solution, enabling precise and efficient genome editing to accelerate the development of climate-resilient and productive forests. This review provides a comprehensive overview of CRISPR applications in forestry, exploring its potential for enhancing disease resistance, improving abiotic stress tolerance, modifying wood properties, and accelerating growth. We discuss the mechanisms and applications of various CRISPR systems, including base editing, prime editing, and multiplexing strategies. Additionally, we highlight recent advances in overcoming key challenges such as reagent delivery and plant regeneration, which are crucial for successful implementation of CRISPR in trees. We also delve into the potential and ethical considerations of using CRISPR gene drive for population-level genetic alterations, as well as the importance of genetic containment strategies for mitigating risks. This review emphasizes the need for continued research, technological advancements, extensive long-term field trials, public engagement, and responsible innovation to fully harness the power of CRISPR for shaping a sustainable future for forests. © 2024 by the authors.
Impact persistence-sourcematerial: The abstract focuses on using CRISPR technology to enhance forest resilience to climate change and disease, but it does not explicitly address the persistence of impacts, either in terms of the climate impacts themselves or the long-term effectiveness of the CRISPR interventions.
Space disaggregation-sourcematerial: Forests worldwide are under increasing pressure from climate change and emerging diseases...
Impact interactions-sourcematerial: This review provides a comprehensive overview of CRISPR applications in forestry, exploring its potential for enhancing disease resistance, improving abiotic stress tolerance, modifying wood properties, and accelerating growth.
Economic dynamics-sourcematerial: No direct mention of economic analysis or modeling. The abstract focuses on CRISPR technology and its application to forestry.
Climate features-sourcematerial: The abstract focuses on CRISPR technology and its applications for improving forest resilience and productivity in the face of climate change and disease, without mentioning tipping points or other notable climate complexities.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is considered in terms of using CRISPR technologies to develop climate-resilient forests. The focus is on enhancing disease resistance, improving abiotic stress tolerance, modifying wood properties, and accelerating growth to cope with climate impacts. The review stresses the importance of extensive long-term field trials, public engagement, and responsible innovation to effectively utilize these genetic modifications for sustainable forest management.
Adaptation considered-sourcematerial: "CRISPR technologies offer a transformative solution, enabling precise and efficient genome editing to accelerate the development of climate-resilient and productive forests...exploring its potential for enhancing disease resistance, improving abiotic stress tolerance, modifying wood properties, and accelerating growth...This review emphasizes the need for continued research, technological advancements, extensive long-term field trials, public engagement, and responsible innovation to fully harness the power of CRISPR for shaping a sustainable future for forests."

Paper Title: Changing windstorm characteristics over the US Northeast in a single model large ensemble
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased windstorm intensity and scale (before LULC correction); decline in regionally averaged U99 after LULC correction; decline in weak cyclones; no decline in intense cyclones; positive trends in socioeconomic loss indices; potential for unprecedented future windstorms.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Projected declines in forest cover in the NE and the resulting reductions in surface roughness length under SSP3-7.0 lead to projections of large increases in U99 and derived windstorm intensity and scale. [...] After corrections are applied to remove the influence of LULC on wind speeds, regionally averaged U99 exhibit declines for most of the single model initial-condition large ensemble (SMILE) members which are broadly proportional to the radiative forcing and global air temperature increase in the SSPs, with a median value of −0.15 ms−1 °C−1. While weak cyclones are projected to decline in frequency in the NE, intense cyclones and the resulting windstorms and indices of socioeconomic loss do not. Where present, significant trends in these loss indices are positive, and some MPI SMILE members generate future windstorms that are unprecedented in the historical period.
Drivers of Risk: Climate change, land use, windstorm intensity, surface roughness, forest cover decline, socioeconomic pathways, radiative forcing, global temperature, cyclone frequency.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "...the influence climate change and changing land use will have on these events remains unclear...changes to projected land use land cover (LULC) change, externally forced changes and internal climate variability...Projected declines in forest cover in the NE and the resulting reductions in surface roughness length under SSP3-7.0 lead to projections of large increases in U99 and derived windstorm intensity and scale...regionally averaged U99 exhibit declines for most of the single model initial-condition large ensemble (SMILE) members which are broadly proportional to the radiative forcing and global air temperature increase in the SSPs...While weak cyclones are projected to decline in frequency in the NE, intense cyclones and the resulting windstorms and indices of socioeconomic loss do not."
Category-sourcematerial: Extreme windstorms pose a significant hazard to infrastructure and public safety... projections of NE windstorms... intense cyclones and the resulting windstorms and indices of socioeconomic loss... future windstorms that are unprecedented in the historical period.
Approach-sourcematerial: Extreme windstorms pose a significant hazard to infrastructure and public safety, particularly in the highly populated US Northeast (NE)...A large ensemble generated using the Max-Planck Institute (MPI) Earth system model is used to generate projections of NE windstorms under different shared socioeconomic pathways (SSPs) and to attribute changes to projected land use land cover (LULC) change, externally forced changes and internal climate variability...To reduce the influence of coarse grid cell resolution and uncertainties in surface roughness lengths, windstorms are identified using simultaneous widespread exceedance of local 99th percentile 10 m wind speeds (U99)...After corrections are applied to remove the influence of LULC on wind speeds, regionally averaged U99 exhibit declines for most of the single model initial-condition large ensemble (SMILE) members which are broadly proportional to the radiative forcing and global air temperature increase in the SSPs, with a median value of −0.15 ms−1 °C−1...While weak cyclones are projected to decline in frequency in the NE, intense cyclones and the resulting windstorms and indices of socioeconomic loss do not. Where present, significant trends in these loss indices are positive, and some MPI SMILE members generate future windstorms that are unprecedented in the historical period.
Methodology: Windstorms in the U.S. Northeast are identified using exceedance of local 99th percentile 10 m wind speeds in a large ensemble generated by the Max-Planck Institute Earth system model under different SSPs, with changes attributed to LULC change, external forcing, and internal climate variability; LULC influence on wind speeds is corrected to isolate climate change impacts.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "A large ensemble generated using the Max-Planck Institute (MPI) Earth system model is used to generate projections of NE windstorms under different shared socioeconomic pathways (SSPs) and to attribute changes to projected land use land cover (LULC) change, externally forced changes and internal climate variability...windstorms are identified using simultaneous widespread exceedance of local 99th percentile 10 m wind speeds (U99)...After corrections are applied to remove the influence of LULC on wind speeds..."
Highlights: Projected declines in Northeast forest cover under SSP3-7.0 could lead to increased windstorm intensity and scale, but these land-use changes may be unrealistic; after correcting for land-use impacts, regionally averaged wind speeds decline with warming, but intense cyclones and associated socioeconomic losses do not, with some simulations showing unprecedented future windstorms.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Projected declines in forest cover in the NE and the resulting reductions in surface roughness length under SSP3-7.0 lead to projections of large increases in U99 and derived windstorm intensity and scale. However, these projected changes in regional LULC under SSP3-7.0 are unprecedented in a historical context and may not be realistic. After corrections are applied to remove the influence of LULC on wind speeds, regionally averaged U99 exhibit declines for most of the single model initial-condition large ensemble (SMILE) members which are broadly proportional to the radiative forcing and global air temperature increase in the SSPs, with a median value of −0.15 ms−1 °C−1. While weak cyclones are projected to decline in frequency in the NE, intense cyclones and the resulting windstorms and indices of socioeconomic loss do not. Where present, significant trends in these loss indices are positive, and some MPI SMILE members generate future windstorms that are unprecedented in the historical period.
More Notes: Projected declines in Northeast forest cover under SSP3-7.0 could lead to increased windstorm intensity; however, after accounting for land use changes, wind speeds are projected to decline proportionally to radiative forcing, while intense cyclones and socioeconomic loss indices may increase, potentially exceeding historical levels.
More Notes-sourcematerial: Projected declines in forest cover in the NE and the resulting reductions in surface roughness length under SSP3-7.0 lead to projections of large increases in U99 and derived windstorm intensity and scale. ... After corrections are applied to remove the influence of LULC on wind speeds, regionally averaged U99 exhibit declines for most of the single model initial-condition large ensemble (SMILE) members which are broadly proportional to the radiative forcing and global air temperature increase in the SSPs... intense cyclones and the resulting windstorms and indices of socioeconomic loss do not. Where present, significant trends in these loss indices are positive, and some MPI SMILE members generate future windstorms that are unprecedented in the historical period.

It discusses the influence of land use changes, radiative forcing, and internal climate variability on these events. The analysis focuses on the relationship between radiative forcing, temperature, land use, and windstorm characteristics.

Paper Title: Probabilistic assessment of climate-related impacts and risks in ports
Outcome(s) of Interest: Economic losses; diminished operational capacity; degradation of reliability and functionality in port infrastructures.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: (5) the estimation of economic losses resulting from diminished operational capacity, in addition to the degradation of reliability and functionality in port infrastructures.
Drivers of Risk: Climate hazards (waves, extreme sea levels), extreme events, daily conditions, degradation of reliability and functionality in port infrastructures, diminished operational capacity.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Given their coastal locations, ports are inherently exposed to climate hazards, such as waves and extreme sea levels...the development of a port-specific risk framework capable of estimating impacts from both extreme events and daily conditions...the estimation of economic losses resulting from diminished operational capacity, in addition to the degradation of reliability and functionality in port infrastructures...in scenarios marked by extreme events, potential losses could escalate...
Category-sourcematerial: Given their coastal locations, ports are inherently exposed to climate hazards, such as waves and extreme sea levels, requiring large investments in resilient infrastructure. This study introduces an innovative methodology for assessing climate-related impacts and risks in ports.
Approach-sourcematerial: This study introduces an innovative methodology for assessing climate-related impacts and risks in ports...the development of a port-specific risk framework capable of estimating impacts from both extreme events and daily conditions; (2) the integration of the latest advancements in nearshore climate hazard modeling; (3) the application of high-resolution tools for accurately simulating wave propagation towards harbor basins and the interaction between waves and structures; (4) the probabilistic determination of failure modes and operational shutdowns susceptible to climate conditions; and (5) the estimation of economic losses resulting from diminished operational capacity...
Methodology: Introduces a port-specific risk framework for estimating impacts from extreme events and daily conditions, integrates advanced nearshore climate hazard modeling with high-resolution wave propagation simulation, applies probabilistic assessment for failure modes, operational shutdowns, and economic losses estimation using the IPCC risk framework and Spanish ROM Program guidelines.
Methodology-sourcematerial: (1) the development of a port-specific risk framework capable of estimating impacts from both extreme events and daily conditions; (2) the integration of the latest advancements in nearshore climate hazard modeling; (3) the application of high-resolution tools for accurately simulating wave propagation towards harbor basins and the interaction between waves and structures; (4) the probabilistic determination of failure modes and operational shutdowns susceptible to climate conditions; and (5) the estimation of economic losses resulting from diminished operational capacity, in addition to the degradation of reliability and functionality in port infrastructures. Formulated within the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) risk framework and anchored in established Spanish Recommendations for Maritime Works (ROM Program)
Highlights: Innovative methodology assesses climate risks for ports, incorporating a port-specific risk framework, advanced hazard modeling, high-resolution wave simulation, probabilistic failure analysis, and economic impact estimation, highlighting significant uncertainties and potential financial losses.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "This study introduces an innovative methodology for assessing climate-related impacts and risks in ports...The methodology's novelty resides in: (1) the development of a port-specific risk framework... (2) the integration of the latest advancements in nearshore climate hazard modeling; (3) the application of high-resolution tools for accurately simulating wave propagation... (4) the probabilistic determination of failure modes... (5) the estimation of economic losses... It stresses the significant uncertainties encountered when evaluating climate-related risks...potential losses could escalate to as much as 100 million euros..."
More Notes: Ports are vulnerable to climate hazards due to their coastal locations, and climate-related risks could lead to significant cumulative economic losses over a 50-year lifespan, emphasizing the need for advanced methodologies to accurately understand these risks.
More Notes-sourcematerial: "Given their coastal locations, ports are inherently exposed to climate hazards...climate-related risks could lead to cumulative losses nearing 10 million euros for such infrastructure...It stresses the significant uncertainties encountered when evaluating climate-related risks for critical infrastructure, including ports, and highlights the critical need for advanced methodologies to accurately understand these risks."
Hazard definition-sourcematerial: "ports are inherently exposed to climate hazards, such as waves and extreme sea levels" and "impacts from both extreme events and daily conditions" and "scenarios marked by extreme events, potential losses could escalate to as much as 100 million euros, despite their occurrence being relatively rare (with a probability of only 0.1%)."

This approach aims to facilitate climate-informed decision-making and enhance the management of coastal structures and ports under high uncertainty."
