Paper Title: Comparison of the risks and drivers of compound hot-dry and hot-wet extremes in a warming world
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased occurrences of compound hot-dry (CHD) and compound hot-wet (CHW) extremes, with CHW increasing at a higher rate.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Findings indicate a notable surge in CHD and CHW occurrences, with CHW experiencing a higher increasing rate.
Drivers of Risk: Anthropogenic climate change (dominant), land-atmosphere feedbacks (CHD, regional), negative precipitation-temperature coupling (CHD).
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Compound hot-dry (CHD) and compound hot-wet (CHW) extremes have both intensified under global warming... anthropogenic climate change predominantly drives the increase in both types of compound extremes, especially for CHW... land-atmosphere feedbacks... substantially contributes to the rise in CHD by reinforcing the negative precipitation-temperature coupling.
Category-sourcematerial: Compound hot-dry (CHD) and compound hot-wet (CHW) extremes have both intensified under global warming, posing exacerbated socio-economic threats compared to univariate extremes.
Approach-sourcematerial: Our investigation further reveals that anthropogenic climate change predominantly drives the increase in both types of compound extremes, especially for CHW. Paper Title: The LTAR Cropland Common Experiment at the Kellogg Biological Station
Outcome(s) of Interest: Economic returns, water quality, greenhouse gas mitigation, soil health, biodiversity, social well-being.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: integrated pest management to provide benefits related to economic returns, water quality, greenhouse gas mitigation, soil health, biodiversity, and social well-being.
Drivers of Risk: Conventional tillage; uniform fertilizer and pesticide application; lack of cover crops and animal integration; corn-soybean rotation; prevailing regional practices.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Regional cropping systems are dominated by corn (Zea mays)–soybean (Glycine max) rotations managed with conventional tillage, industry-average rates of fertilizer and pesticide inputs uniformly applied, few cover crops, and little animal integration. The prevailing practice (or Business as usual [BAU]) treatment mirrors regional prevailing practices as revealed by farmer surveys.
Category-sourcematerial: The Kellogg Biological Station Long-term Agroecosystem Research site (KBS LTAR) joined the national LTAR network in 2015 to represent a northeast portion of the North Central Region...Regional cropping systems are dominated by corn (Zea mays)–soybean (Glycine max) rotations...KBS LTAR initiated the Aspirational Cropping System Experiment...
Approach-sourcematerial: The Kellogg Biological Station Long-term Agroecosystem Research site (KBS LTAR) joined the national LTAR network in 2015 to represent a northeast portion of the North Central Region, extending across 76,000 km2 of southern Michigan and northern Indiana. Regional cropping systems are dominated by corn (Zea mays)–soybean (Glycine max) rotations managed with conventional tillage, industry-average rates of fertilizer and pesticide inputs uniformly applied, few cover crops, and little animal integration. In 2020, KBS LTAR initiated the Aspirational Cropping System Experiment as part of the LTAR Common Experiment, a co-production model wherein stakeholders and researchers collaborate to advance transformative change in agriculture. The Aspirational (ASP) cropping system treatment, designed by a team of agronomists, farmers, scientists, and other stakeholders, is a five-crop rotation of corn, soybean, winter wheat (Triticum aestivum), winter canola (Brassicus napus), and a diverse forage mix. All phases are managed with continuous no-till, variable rate fertilizer inputs, and integrated pest management to provide benefits related to economic returns, water quality, greenhouse gas mitigation, soil health, biodiversity, and social well-being. Cover crops follow corn and winter wheat, with fall-planted crops in the rotation providing winter cover in other years. The experiment is replicated with all rotation phases at both the plot and field scales and with perennial prairie strips in consistently low-producing areas of ASP fields. The prevailing practice (or Business as usual [BAU]) treatment mirrors regional prevailing practices as revealed by farmer surveys. Stakeholders and researchers evaluate the success of the ASP and BAU systems annually and implement management changes on a 5-year cycle.
Methodology: The KBS LTAR site compares an "Aspirational" (ASP) cropping system – a five-crop rotation with no-till, variable inputs, integrated pest management, cover crops, and prairie strips – against a "Business as Usual" (BAU) system mirroring regional conventional practices, with stakeholder evaluation and management changes every 5 years, replicated at plot and field scales.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "KBS LTAR initiated the Aspirational Cropping System Experiment...The Aspirational (ASP) cropping system treatment...is a five-crop rotation...managed with continuous no-till, variable rate fertilizer inputs, and integrated pest management...Cover crops follow corn and winter wheat...The experiment is replicated with all rotation phases at both the plot and field scales and with perennial prairie strips...The prevailing practice (or Business as usual [BAU]) treatment mirrors regional prevailing practices...Stakeholders and researchers evaluate the success of the ASP and BAU systems annually and implement management changes on a 5-year cycle."
Highlights: The Kellogg Biological Station Long-term Agroecosystem Research site (KBS LTAR) is conducting an experiment comparing an "Aspirational" (ASP) cropping system, designed with stakeholder input for improved sustainability outcomes, to a "Business as Usual" (BAU) system representing regional practices. The ASP system features a diverse five-crop rotation, no-till management, variable-rate fertilization, integrated pest management, cover crops, and prairie strips. The experiment's success is evaluated and adapted every five years.

Paper Title: Increasing irrigation-triggered landslide activity caused by intensive farming in deserts on three continents
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased ITL frequency and disaster potential, surface deformation, continuous movement, headscarp expansion, landslide cluster formation.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "The most severe surface deformation of ITL occurs on the landslide body...ITL will maintain continuous movement for at least 7 years...headscarp of ITL shows lateral expansion and longitudinal retrogression...transforms landslides into landslide clusters...frequency and disaster-causing ability of ITL will increase greatly with further population growth and related intensification in the agricultural sector."
Drivers of Risk: Population growth, agricultural intensification, land-use changes, irrigation-triggered landslides, dry climates
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Population growth and agricultural intensification lead to stress on landscapes that are highly sensitive to land-use changes. An increase in irrigation-triggered landslides (ITL) in dry climates has negative impacts on local communities.
Category-sourcematerial: Increase in irrigation-triggered landslides (ITL) in dry climates has negative impacts on local communities... the most severe surface deformation of ITL occurs on the landslide body... the frequency and disaster-causing ability of ITL will increase greatly with further population growth and related intensification in the agricultural sector.
Approach-sourcematerial: Here, we use Multi-Temporal Interferometric Synthetic Aperture Radar (MT-InSAR), vectorization, and differential method to study surface deformation, ground displacement, and changes in headscarp morphology and topography in regions prone to ITL, aiming to uncover the evolution and spatiotemporal distribution of ITL.
Methodology: Multi-Temporal Interferometric Synthetic Aperture Radar (MT-InSAR), vectorization, and a differential method are used to study surface deformation, ground displacement, and changes in headscarp morphology and topography in regions prone to irrigation-triggered landslides.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Here, we use Multi-Temporal Interferometric Synthetic Aperture Radar (MT-InSAR), vectorization, and differential method to study surface deformation, ground displacement, and changes in headscarp morphology and topography in regions prone to ITL, aiming to uncover the evolution and spatiotemporal distribution of ITL.
Highlights: Irrigation-triggered landslides (ITL) cause significant surface deformation, particularly on the landslide body, and continuous movement for at least 7 years. ITL headscarps expand laterally more than they retrogression longitudinally, leading to landslide cluster formation. Topographic changes indicate lateral expansion as the main development pattern, suggesting increased frequency and impact of ITL with further population growth and agricultural intensification.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "Findings show that the most severe surface deformation of ITL occurs on the landslide body. Meanwhile, the ITL displacement curve indicates the ITL will maintain continuous movement for at least 7 years... the headscarp of ITL shows lateral expansion and longitudinal retrogression on the horizontal ground, whereby the scale of expansion is greater than that of retrogression, which transforms landslides into landslide clusters. Finally, the topographic changes further reveal that the main development pattern of ITL is lateral expansion. We suggest that the frequency and disaster-causing ability of ITL will increase greatly with further population growth and related intensification in the agricultural sector."
More Notes: Irrigation-triggered landslides in dry climates, exacerbated by population growth and agricultural intensification, show continuous movement for at least 7 years, lateral expansion, and transformation into landslide clusters, suggesting increasing frequency and disaster potential.

Paper Title: Future Global River Ice in CMIP6 Models under Climate Change
Outcome(s) of Interest: Decreased river ice extent and duration, increased ice-free rivers, regional and seasonal variations.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Results indicate significant declines in global river ice due to global warming. With each 18C increase in surface air temperature (SAT) in the future, river ice extent of ice-affected rivers decrease by 2.11 percentage points, and ice duration shorten by 8.4 days. [...] Under the shared socioeconomic pathways 2-4.5 (SSP2-4.5) scenario, the long-term mean SAT is 1.28–2.58C higher than in the near term. This leads to a 1.9–4.4-percentage-point decrease in global river ice extent, a 6.8–15.1-day decrease in river ice duration, and ice-free rivers increasing by up to 4.02%. [...] Geographically, river ice loss is most notable in North America, Europe, Siberia, and the Tibetan Plateau (TIB), particularly in peninsular, coastal, and mountainous regions due to the combined effects of initial temperatures and temperature increases. [...] Monthly analyses show the most substantial decreases from October to June, indicating pronounced seasonal variability.
Drivers of Risk: Climate change, global warming, surface air temperature (SAT) increases, SSP2-4.5, SSP5-8.5.

Paper Title: Tracing Urban Drinking Water Sources: Global State of the Art and Insights From an IAEA-Coordinated Research Project
Outcome(s) of Interest: Better assessment, mapping and management of urban water resources, enhanced drinking water treatment, and improved urban water management in light of global warming.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: (a) current research trends for studying urban drinking water systems during the last two decades and (b) the development, testing and integration of new methodologies, aiming for a better assessment, mapping and management of water resources used for drinking water supply in urban settings... governmental, regional or community-based water operators and practitioners could enhance drinking water treatment strategies (including more accurate surface water blending estimations) and improve urban water management and conservation plans in the light of global warming.
Drivers of Risk: Climate change, precipitation variability, recurrent droughts, flash flooding, increasing water needs.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Climate change, inter-annual precipitation variability, recurrent droughts and flash flooding, coupled with increasing water needs, are shaping the co-evolution of socioeconomic and cultural assemblages, water laws and regulations, and equitable drinking water access and allocation worldwide.
Category-sourcematerial: Climate change, inter-annual precipitation variability, recurrent droughts and flash flooding, coupled with increasing water needs, are shaping the co-evolution of socioeconomic and cultural assemblages, water laws and regulations, and equitable drinking water access and allocation worldwide. ...Here, we report on (a) current research trends for studying urban drinking water systems during the last two decades and (b) the development, testing and integration of new methodologies, aiming for a better assessment, mapping and management of water resources used for drinking water supply in urban settings. ...The study revealed that urban areas depend on a large spectrum of water recharge across mountain ranges, extensive local groundwater extraction and water transfer from nearby or distant river basins.
Approach-sourcematerial: Here, we report on (a) current research trends for studying urban drinking water systems during the last two decades and (b) the development, testing and integration of new methodologies, aiming for a better assessment, mapping and management of water resources used for drinking water supply in urban settings. Selected examples of water isotope applications (Canada, USA, Costa Rica, Ecuador, Morocco, Botswana, Romania, Slovenia, India and Nepal) provide context to the insights and recommendations reported and highlight the versatility of water isotopes to underpin seasonal and temporal variations across various environmental and climate scenarios. The study revealed that urban areas depend on a large spectrum of water recharge across mountain ranges, extensive local groundwater extraction and water transfer from nearby or distant river basins. The latter is reflected in the spatial isotope snapshot variability. High-resolution monitoring (hourly and sub-hourly) isotope sampling revealed large diurnal variations in the wet tropics (Costa Rica) (up to 1.5‰ in δ18O) and more uniform diurnal variations in urban centres fed by groundwater sources (0.08‰ in δ18O) (Ljubljana, Slovenia). Similarly, while d-excess was fairly close to the global mean value (+10‰) across all urban centres (10‰–15‰), reservoir-based drinking water systems show lower values (up to ~ −20‰) (Arlington, TX, USA and Gaborone, Botswana), as a result of strong evapoconcentration processes. δ18O time series and depth-integrated sampling highlighted the influence of the catchment damping ratio in the ultimate intake water composition. By introducing new, traceable spatial and temporal tools that span from the water source to the end-user and are linked to the engineered and socio-economic structure of the water distribution system, governmental, regional or community-based water operators and practitioners could enhance drinking water treatment strategies (including more accurate surface water blending estimations) and improve urban water management and conservation plans in the light of global warming.
Methodology: The study developed, tested, and integrated new methodologies for improved assessment, mapping, and management of water resources used for urban drinking water supply, utilizing water isotope applications to understand seasonal and temporal variations across diverse environmental and climate scenarios and introducing traceable spatial and temporal tools spanning from water source to end-user.
Methodology-sourcematerial: (a) current research trends for studying urban drinking water systems during the last two decades and (b) the development, testing and integration of new methodologies, aiming for a better assessment, mapping and management of water resources used for drinking water supply in urban settings. Selected examples of water isotope applications (Canada, USA, Costa Rica, Ecuador, Morocco, Botswana, Romania, Slovenia, India and Nepal) provide context to the insights and recommendations reported and highlight the versatility of water isotopes to underpin seasonal and temporal variations across various environmental and climate scenarios... By introducing new, traceable spatial and temporal tools that span from the water source to the end-user and are linked to the engineered and socio-economic structure of the water distribution system...
Highlights: Isotope hydrology techniques reveal the diverse water sources for urban centers, including mountain recharge, groundwater extraction, and river basin transfers; highlight diurnal isotope variations tied to water source type; demonstrate the impact of evapoconcentration on reservoir-based systems; and offer spatial and temporal tools to enhance drinking water treatment and urban water management in a warming climate.
Highlights-sourcematerial: The study revealed that urban areas depend on a large spectrum of water recharge across mountain ranges, extensive local groundwater extraction and water transfer from nearby or distant river basins. High-resolution monitoring (hourly and sub-hourly) isotope sampling revealed large diurnal variations in the wet tropics (Costa Rica) (up to 1.5‰ in δ18O) and more uniform diurnal variations in urban centres fed by groundwater sources (0.08‰ in δ18O) (Ljubljana, Slovenia). Similarly, while d-excess was fairly close to the global mean value (+10‰) across all urban centres (10‰–15‰), reservoir-based drinking water systems show lower values (up to ~ −20‰) (Arlington, TX, USA and Gaborone, Botswana), as a result of strong evapoconcentration processes. By introducing new, traceable spatial and temporal tools that span from the water source to the end-user and are linked to the engineered and socio-economic structure of the water distribution system, governmental, regional or community-based water operators and practitioners could enhance drinking water treatment strategies (including more accurate surface water blending estimations) and improve urban water management and conservation plans in the light of global warming.
More Notes: Isotope techniques are used to assess urban drinking water sources and distribution, revealing dependencies on diverse water recharge sources, spatial isotope variability, diurnal variations influenced by source type, and evapoconcentration effects in reservoir systems; these findings can inform improved water management and treatment strategies.

Paper Title: Rice yield and electricity production in agro-photovoltaic systems
Outcome(s) of Interest: APV systems impact; rice growth: minimal impact on height, tillers; delayed leaf aging; yield drop: 20%; changes in head rice, broken rice; financial returns from electricity outweigh crop loss.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Growth analysis of rice under APV systems showed minimal impact on plant height and tiller numbers, though chlorophyll content analysis indicated delayed leaf aging and extended maturation time. Rice yield decreased due to altered panicle and spikelet numbers. Quality analysis revealed changes in head rice rate and broken rice, stressing optimal harvest timing in APV systems. Yields dropped by about 20% under APV systems, but the financial returns from electricity generation significantly outweighed the crop revenue loss.
Drivers of Risk: Fossil fuel depletion, CO2 emissions, climate change, impact on agriculture.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Fossil fuels, particularly oil, face sustainability challenges due to depletion and their role in increasing atmospheric CO2 levels, contributing to climate change and impacting global agriculture.
Category-sourcematerial: Fossil fuels, particularly oil, face sustainability challenges due to depletion and their role in increasing atmospheric CO2 levels, contributing to climate change and impacting global agriculture. Renewable energy sources like solar power offer a viable alternative. This study explores the feasibility of agro-photovoltaic (APV) systems, which integrate solar panels with agricultural land to generate electricity while cultivating crops. Specifically, the impact of APV systems on rice production and quality was investigated. [...] Growth analysis of rice under APV systems showed minimal impact on plant height and tiller numbers, though chlorophyll content analysis indicated delayed leaf aging and extended maturation time. Rice yield decreased due to altered panicle and spikelet numbers. Quality analysis revealed changes in head rice rate and broken rice, stressing optimal harvest timing in APV systems. [...] Yields dropped by about 20% under APV systems, but the financial returns from electricity generation significantly outweighed the crop revenue loss. Despite the high initial installation costs and regulatory challenges, APV systems present a promising dual-use approach for enhancing farm income and promoting renewable energy. Continued research and investment are essential for optimizing APV systems and expanding their adoption.
Approach-sourcematerial: Solar modules with a total capacity of 99.84 kW were installed on a 2580 m2 site, with two module configurations tested. Two rice (Oryza sativa L.) cultivars, Woonkwang and Saenuri, were transplanted and monitored for growth characteristics, chlorophyll content, and fluorescence, as well as yield and quality.
Methodology: Solar panels (99.84 kW total capacity) were installed on a 2580 m2 site with two module configurations to test agro-photovoltaic (APV) systems' impact on two rice cultivars (Woonkwang and Saenuri); growth characteristics, chlorophyll content, fluorescence, yield, and quality were monitored.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Solar modules with a total capacity of 99.84 kW were installed on a 2580 m2 site, with two module configurations tested. Two rice (Oryza sativa L.) cultivars, Woonkwang and Saenuri, were transplanted and monitored for growth characteristics, chlorophyll content, and fluorescence, as well as yield and quality.
Highlights: Agro-photovoltaic (APV) systems, integrating solar panels with rice cultivation, showed minimal impact on plant height and tiller numbers but altered chlorophyll content, delaying leaf aging. Rice yield decreased by approximately 20% due to changes in panicle and spikelet numbers, affecting rice quality and physicochemical properties. Financial gains from electricity generation outweighed crop revenue loss, suggesting APV's potential despite high initial costs and regulatory hurdles.

Despite the high initial installation costs and regulatory challenges, APV systems present a promising dual-use approach for enhancing farm income and promoting renewable energy.

Paper Title: Nitrogen fertilizer use and climate interactions: Implications for maize yields in Kansas
Outcome(s) of Interest: Maize yield response to N fertilizer under varying temperature and precipitation.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "clarify how maize yields respond to N fertilizer use under changes in temperature and precipitation"
Drivers of Risk: Extreme-degree-days (EDD), temperature, precipitation, N fertilizer use interaction.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "...an increase in N fertilizer use magnified the negative effects of higher EDD on maize yield but enhanced the positive effects of higher GDD and precipitation on maize yield...hotter environments reduced maize yield by 7% in the west; conversely, warmer and wetter environmental conditions contributed to 2.4% yield gains in the southeast; changes in N fertilizer use impacted maize yield to a lesser extent than climate changes..."
Category-sourcematerial: CONTEXT: While climate change threatens maize growth and production, appropriate N fertilizer use (N) can help mitigate this threat and stabilize or improve maize yields...OBJECTIVE: The objectives of this study are to: 1) clarify how maize yields respond to N fertilizer use under changes in temperature and precipitation...METHODS: We used a long-term and county-level maize N fertilizer use and climate dataset from 1981 to 2019 in Kansas to determine the impact of N fertilizer use on maize yield. RESULTS AND CONCLUSIONS: Our results show that: 1) an increase in N fertilizer use magnified the negative effects of higher EDD on maize yield but enhanced the positive effects of higher GDD and precipitation on maize yield...
Approach-sourcematerial: We developed a panel data model with fixed effects, incorporating explanatory variables, including the interaction of growing-degree-days (GDD) with N fertilizer use (GDDit*Nit), extreme-degree-days (EDD) with N fertilizer use (EDDit*Nit), and precipitation (Precip) with N fertilizer use (Precipit*Nit), along with a non-linear N-fertilizer use term.
Methodology: A long-term, county-level dataset on maize N fertilizer use and climate from 1981-2019 in Kansas was analyzed using a panel data model with fixed effects, incorporating interactions of growing-degree-days, extreme-degree-days, and precipitation with N fertilizer use, alongside a non-linear N-fertilizer use term, to examine the adaptive effects of N fertilizer on climatic risks, with results aggregated by crop reporting districts.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "We used a long-term and county-level maize N fertilizer use and climate dataset from 1981 to 2019 in Kansas to determine the impact of N fertilizer use on maize yield. We developed a panel data model with fixed effects, incorporating explanatory variables, including the interaction of growing-degree-days (GDD) with N fertilizer use (GDDit*Nit), extreme-degree-days (EDD) with N fertilizer use (EDDit*Nit), and precipitation (Precip) with N fertilizer use (Precipit*Nit), along with a non-linear N-fertilizer use term. Then, the adaptive effects of N fertilizer use on climatic risks for maize were examined and the county-level results were aggregated into the nine crop reporting districts, as defined by the U.S. Department of Agriculture's National Agricultural Statistics Service."
Highlights: An increase in N fertilizer use can magnify maize yield losses from high extreme-degree-days but boost yields with more growing-degree-days and precipitation, with effects varying across Kansas; optimal N rates differ by region, influenced by climate change.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Our results show that: 1) an increase in N fertilizer use magnified the negative effects of higher EDD on maize yield but enhanced the positive effects of higher GDD and precipitation on maize yield, impacts which were increasingly evident moving from western to eastern regions across Kansas; 3) under the averaged climate conditions, the optimal N fertilizer rate increased from northwestern (average 50 kg N ha−1) to eastern (average 158 kg N ha−1) regions in Kansas.
More Notes: Nitrogen fertilizer use can mitigate some climate change threats to maize yields, but its effectiveness is influenced by regional climate conditions, with hotter environments in western Kansas reducing yield despite N application, while warmer, wetter conditions in the southeast lead to gains with N application.
More Notes-sourcematerial: "While climate change threatens maize growth and production, appropriate N fertilizer use (N) can help mitigate this threat and stabilize or improve maize yields...an increase in N fertilizer use magnified the negative effects of higher EDD on maize yield but enhanced the positive effects of higher GDD and precipitation on maize yield...hotter environments reduced maize yield by 7% in the west; conversely, warmer and wetter environmental conditions contributed to 2.4% yield gains in the southeast."

Paper Title: Forecasting habitat suitability and niche shifts of two global maize pests: Ostrinia furnacalis and Ostrinia nubilalis (Lepidoptera: Crambidae)
Outcome(s) of Interest: Suitable habitats, niche shift, highly and extremely suitable regions, frequent occurrence, pest distribution, climate change response, prevention and control strategies.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "the overall suitable habitats of ACB and ECB in the future decrease but highly and extremely suitable habitat will become more widespread, suggesting these two insects may occur more frequently in specific regions...niche shifts were common for both ACB and ECB in different survival regions during their colonization of new habitat and their suitable habitat changes during the paleoclimate were associated with climatic changes...improve our understanding of the ecological characteristics of ACB and ECB worldwide, and will be useful in the development of prevention and control strategies for two insect pests worldwide."

Category-sourcematerial: BACKGROUND: Ostrinia furnacalis (ACB) and Ostrinia nubilalis (ECB) are devastating pests of the agricultural crop maize worldwide.
Approach-sourcematerial: By integrating the niche unfilling, stability, and expansion situations into a single framework, our study quantifies the patterns of niche shift in the spatial distribution of these two pests during the different periods.
Methodology: Utilized a framework integrating niche unfilling, stability, and expansion to quantify niche shifts in the spatial distribution of ACB and ECB pests across different periods.
Methodology-sourcematerial: By integrating the niche unfilling, stability, and expansion situations into a single framework, our study quantifies the patterns of niche shift in the spatial distribution of these two pests during the different periods.
Highlights: Future suitable habitats for ACB and ECB may decrease overall, but highly suitable habitats will expand, potentially increasing pest frequency in specific regions; niche shifts are common during colonization, with ACB showing expansion in China and ECB retaining niches in Eurasia but with expansion potential in North America; paleoclimate changes influenced suitable habitat changes.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "Our results show that the overall suitable habitats of ACB and ECB in the future decrease but highly and extremely suitable habitat will become more widespread, suggesting these two insects may occur more frequently in specific regions. Compared with Southeast Asia and Australia, the ACB niche in China exhibited expansion rather than unfilling. For ECB, initial niches have a tendency to be retained in Eurasia despite there also being potential for expansion in North America. The niche equivalency and similarity test results further indicate that niche shifts were common for both ACB and ECB in different survival regions during their colonization of new habitat and their suitable habitat changes during the paleoclimate were associated with climatic changes."

Paper Title: Understanding the benefits and challenges of harvested wood products in response to climate change
Outcome(s) of Interest: Climate mitigation potential of long-lived HWPs (construction, furniture); Uncertainties/net emissions from short-lived HWPs (bioenergy, paper); Overlooked factors (indirect land use change, substitution credits, end-of-life emissions/storage); Challenges (biomass shortages, substitution leakage, trade-offs: forest protection vs. utilization, short- vs. long-term, regional vs. global benefits, climate vs. other environmental/economic/social factors).
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Our review of existing studies highlights the significant climate mitigation potential of long-lived HWPs used in construction and furniture, while short-lived products such as bioenergy and paper exhibit uncertainties and can contribute to net emissions. Existing analyses often overlook factors such as indirect land use change emissions, substitution credits, as well as emissions and storage in the end-of-life stage. Moreover, there are misconceptions regarding the selection of time horizons and characterization of climate impacts, as well as emission leakage caused by international trade. Utilizing HWPs for climate mitigation faces challenges such as forest biomass shortages, substitution benefits leakage, and trade-offs between forest protection and utilization, long-term versus short-term goals, and regional versus global benefits. Additionally, extrinsic trade-offs exist between climate impacts and other environmental, economic, and social elements.
Drivers of Risk: Inconsistencies in LCA, overlooked factors (indirect land use change, substitution credits, end-of-life emissions), misconceptions (time horizons, climate impact characterization, emission leakage), forest biomass shortages, substitution benefits leakage, trade-offs (forest protection vs. utilization, long-term vs. short-term, regional vs. global), extrinsic trade-offs (climate impacts vs. other environmental/economic/social elements).
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: However, inconsistencies in system boundaries, time horizons, and scenario assumptions in life cycle assessment (LCA) have led to distorted climate impact assessments and misguided policy formulation... Paper Title: Robust and smart: Inference on phenotypic plasticity of Coffea canephora reveals adaptation to alternative environments
Outcome(s) of Interest: Climate-resilient coffee cultivars; Coffea canephora plasticity and cupping quality in high-altitude/cold weather; adaptation potential; molecular breeding.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "Developing climate-resilience cultivars is necessary...Coffea canephora emerges as a potential candidate if the crop combines plasticity and cupping quality...How the climate effects may affect the phenotypic plasticity in C. canephora?...cupping quality and yield performance of C. canephora cultivars when evaluated in high-altitude and cold weather...we emphasize the potential of molecular breeding in the long term in coffee...C. canephora could be a valid alternative for climate-smart cultivars."
Drivers of Risk: Climate change, susceptibility of Coffea arabica, biotic stresses, abiotic stresses, need for climate-resilient cultivars.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Coffee is an important crop...its sustainability is facing critical challenges given the projected climate changes. Coffea arabica...is a delicate crop, quite susceptible to diseases and biotic stresses. Developing climate-resilience cultivars is necessary, and it includes coffee plants adapted to new farming conditions that can meet the demand for biotic and abiotic tolerance and quality.
Category-sourcematerial: Coffee is an important crop with economic and social importance in several countries. ... Developing climate-resilience cultivars is necessary, and it includes coffee plants adapted to new farming conditions that can meet the demand for biotic and abiotic tolerance and quality.
Approach-sourcematerial: Using a multiple environment trial, we combined genomic and genotype-by-environment analyses... (iii) using stochastic simulation, we emphasize the potential of molecular breeding in the long term in coffee.
Methodology: A multiple environment trial combined with genomic and genotype-by-environment analyses was used to assess the phenotypic plasticity of C. canephora in response to climate effects, comparing it to C. arabica and utilizing stochastic simulation to evaluate the potential of molecular breeding.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "In this study, using a multiple environment trial, we combined genomic and genotype-by-environment analyses to answer the following main question: How the climate effects may affect the phenotypic plasticity in C. canephora?" and "we compared C. arabica and C. canephora phenotypic plasticity" and "using stochastic simulation, we emphasize the potential of molecular breeding in the long term in coffee."
Highlights: C. canephora cultivars exhibit good cupping quality and yield performance in high-altitude and cold weather, display comparable or superior phenotypic plasticity to C. arabica, and benefit significantly from molecular breeding for long-term climate resilience, suggesting its potential as a climate-smart alternative.
Highlights-sourcematerial: (i) we draw attention to the cupping quality and yield performance of C. canephora cultivars when evaluated in high-altitude and cold weather, (ii) we compared C. arabica and C. canephora phenotypic plasticity and highlight genotypes with broad and specific adaptation to certain environmental conditions, and finally, (iii) using stochastic simulation, we emphasize the potential of molecular breeding in the long term in coffee. Altogether, we present an emerging view on how C. canephora could be a valid alternative for climate-smart cultivars in a projected scenario of altered climatic conditions.
More Notes: Coffea canephora shows promise as a climate-resilient coffee cultivar due to its phenotypic plasticity and potential for improved cupping quality and yield performance in diverse environments, offering a possible alternative to vulnerable C. arabica in future climate scenarios.
More Notes-sourcematerial: "Coffea canephora emerges as a potential candidate if the crop combines plasticity and cupping quality... Plant plasticity refers to adjusted phenotypic performance when grown in different environments, a fact that may help mitigate the detrimental effect of climate changes... we draw attention to the cupping quality and yield performance of C. canephora cultivars when evaluated in high-altitude and cold weather, (ii) we compared C. arabica and C. canephora phenotypic plasticity and highlight genotypes with broad and specific adaptation to certain environmental conditions... Altogether, we present an emerging view on how C. canephora could be a valid alternative for climate-smart cultivars in a projected scenario of altered climatic conditions."

Paper Title: Climate Change and Nitrogen Dynamics: Challenges and Strategies for a Sustainable Future
Outcome(s) of Interest: Eutrophication; Harmful algal blooms; Hypoxic conditions; Disrupted plant growth and soil fertility; Imbalance in nitrogen availability; Adverse effects on ecosystem health and productivity.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: The proliferation of nitrogen pollution due to agricultural runoff, industrial effluents, and urban wastewater aggravates eutrophication, leading to significant environmental and economic consequences. The imbalance in nitrogen availability not only affects plant growth and soil fertility but also disrupts aquatic ecosystems, resulting in harmful algal blooms and hypoxic conditions. The review examines the connections between climate change and nitrogen cycling, highlighting the adverse effects on ecosystem health and productivity.
Drivers of Risk: Climate change; increased greenhouse gas emissions; nitrogen pollution; agricultural runoff; industrial effluents; urban wastewater.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Global warming driven by climate change; The increased emissions of greenhouse gases; The proliferation of nitrogen pollution due to agricultural runoff, industrial effluents, and urban wastewater.
Category-sourcematerial: The increased emissions of greenhouse gases alter the distribution and availability of nitrogen, which is a critical nutrient for all living organisms. This review examines the connections between climate change and nitrogen cycling, highlighting the adverse effects on ecosystem health and productivity.
Approach-sourcematerial: The abstract is a review, not an original research article presenting modeling or analysis. It summarizes existing knowledge and suggests future research directions. Therefore, no specific modeling or analysis approach is described.
Methodology: The review synthesizes existing knowledge about climate change impacts on nitrogen cycling in terrestrial and aquatic ecosystems to highlight adverse effects and suggest mitigation and adaptation strategies.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Global warming driven by climate change has profound impacts on nitrogen dynamics in terrestrial and aquatic ecosystems. This review examines the connections between climate change and nitrogen cycling, highlighting the adverse effects on ecosystem health and productivity. Effective mitigation and adaptation strategies are essential to addressing these challenges.
Highlights: Global warming impacts nitrogen dynamics, affecting ecosystem health through altered nitrogen cycling and pollution; sustainable practices and interdisciplinary research are vital for developing effective solutions.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Global warming driven by climate change has profound impacts on nitrogen dynamics in terrestrial and aquatic ecosystems... This review examines the connections between climate change and nitrogen cycling, highlighting the adverse effects on ecosystem health and productivity... Sustainable agricultural practices... along with robust policies and innovative technologies... are essential in managing nitrogen levels... This review underscores the importance of interdisciplinary approaches...
More Notes: Climate change impacts nitrogen cycling in terrestrial and aquatic ecosystems, exacerbating nitrogen pollution, eutrophication, and imbalances in plant growth, soil fertility, and aquatic ecosystems, necessitating sustainable agricultural practices, robust policies, innovative technologies, and interdisciplinary research.
More Notes-sourcematerial: Global warming driven by climate change has profound impacts on nitrogen dynamics in terrestrial and aquatic ecosystems...The proliferation of nitrogen pollution due to agricultural runoff, industrial effluents, and urban wastewater aggravates eutrophication, leading to significant environmental and economic consequences. The imbalance in nitrogen availability not only affects plant growth and soil fertility but also disrupts aquatic ecosystems, resulting in harmful algal blooms and hypoxic conditions...Sustainable agricultural practices, such as precision farming and the use of slow-release fertilizers, along with robust policies and innovative technologies, like biochar application and nitrification inhibitors, are essential in managing nitrogen levels...This review underscores the importance of interdisciplinary approaches...

The imbalance in nitrogen availability not only affects plant growth and soil fertility but also disrupts aquatic ecosystems, resulting in harmful algal blooms and hypoxic conditions. Effective mitigation and adaptation strategies are essential to addressing these challenges.

The imbalance in nitrogen availability not only affects plant growth and soil fertility but also disrupts aquatic ecosystems, resulting in harmful algal blooms and hypoxic conditions. This review underscores the importance of interdisciplinary approaches that involve integrating insights from ecology, agronomy, and the social sciences to develop comprehensive solutions.

Paper Title: Can today's and tomorrow's world uniformly gain from carbon taxation?
Outcome(s) of Interest: GDP reduction (high damage scenarios), regional welfare gains (carbon tax + redistribution), emissions reduction (carbon tax), limited climate change mitigation (carbon tax alone).
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Absent policy, climate change may, under high-damage scenarios, dramatically reduce GDP in most regions, with India, Brazil, and the South Asian Pacific suffering long-term catastrophic damages. Carbon taxation, coupled with region- and generation-specific transfers, can both correct the carbon externality and raise the welfare of all current and future agents across all regions by 4.3 percent. The impact on the use and duration of fossil fuels is dramatic as is the reduction in the path of global emissions. However, carbon taxes set through time, at carbon's marginal social cost, do far too little to mitigate climate change unless all major emitters, particularly China, adopt them and do so immediately.
Drivers of Risk: Anthropogenic climate change, temperature increases, fossil fuel utilization, regional economic policies, carbon policy participation (China), clean/dirty energy substitutability, temperature deviations from optimal, productivity rate differentials, population dynamics, shape of damage function upper tail, lack of carbon taxation/transfers.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: The primary driver of risk discussed is anthropogenic climate change and its associated economic damages. Climate change impacts are driven by temperature increases due to prolonged use of dirty energy. Utilization of fossil fuels, regional economic policies, and participation in carbon policy (specifically China's participation) are key factors impacting the outcomes. The transition speed to full substitutability between clean and dirty energy impacts the short- and medium-term use of dirty energy. Temperature deviations from the optimal temperature (T*) are a driver of regional climate damages, with cold regions potentially benefiting and hot regions being harmed. The risk drivers mentioned include productivity rate differentials, population dynamics, and regional temperature increases. The shape of the upper tail of the damage function significantly affects global damages. Lack of carbon taxation and transfers from future generations can exacerbate regional damages. Absent transfers, they range from $22.8 per ton CO2 with the 1x damage specification up to $111.7 per ton with 6x calibration. Regional emissions from fossil fuels, substitution to clean energy, and the impact of different carbon tax policies.
Category-sourcematerial: Page 10: The paper evaluates the impacts of temperature on regional total factor productivity (TFP), considering productivity differences due to temperature changes, with regional climate damages calculated as deviations from optimal temperature.
Approach-sourcematerial: Page 1: The analysis is based on a large-scale, annually calibrated, multi-region, overlapping generations model of climate change and carbon policy.; Page 2: The analysis is performed using a large-scale, multi-region, overlapping generations (OLG) model calibrated to UN demographic data, and considers carbon taxation and regional climate impacts.; Page 3: The study uses a large-scale OLG IAM, with region-specific emissions and damages determined using pattern scaling.
Methodology: The analysis uses a large-scale, annually calibrated, multi-region, overlapping generations (OLG) integrated assessment model (IAM) with region-specific emissions and damages determined via pattern scaling from global temperatures, modeling total factor productivity (TFP) with a climate emulator calibrated to CMIP5 simulations and integrated with the economic model; it employs a recursive solution algorithm to solve one-period problems and uses an iterative procedure involving guessing welfare changes, updating transfers via an Auerbach and Kotlikoff Lump-Sum Redistribution Authority (LSRA), and calculating the present value of transfers until it approaches zero to find optimal carbon tax paths based on the social cost of carbon (SCC), considering varying assumptions about coal supply, energy substitutability (using CES production functions calibrated with US Energy Information Administration data), and regional coalition impacts on emissions.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Page 1: "This paper develops the first large-scale, annually calibrated, multi-region, overlapping generations model of climate change and carbon policy."; Page 3: "The study uses a large-scale OLG IAM, with region-specific emissions and damages determined using pattern scaling."; If it is positive, decrease  𝜆. If it is negative, increase  𝜆...Iterate on steps 2.2–2.3 until the present value of transfers approaches zero."; Page 34: "Step 3.1: Guess the time path of the optimal carbon tax based on the time path of the SCC (social costs of carbon) in the BAU scenario. Step 3.2: For a given tax path, find the LSRA solution (Steps 2.1–2.4)."; Page 6: "L. Kotlikoff et al.: Our model includes finite-lived selfish agents, dirty energy and capital mobility, and region-specific technologies for producing output. In addition, we focus on Pareto efficiency rather than social welfare."
Highlights: Climate change is projected to significantly impact GDP differently across regions; global carbon taxation can reduce peak global damages (e.g., by 44.3 percent with UWI policy) and emissions but requires global participation, especially from China, to be truly effective.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Page 2: "Important outcomes include the projection that climate change will significantly impact GDP differently across regions, but carbon taxation coupled with redistribution can mitigate these damages considerably."; Page 3: "The striking messages here are both encouraging – global carbon taxation can significantly mitigate losses from climate change – and discouraging – atmospheric carbon concentration is already so high that the optimal UWI carbon tax policy can only limit peak global damages by 44.3 percent."; Page 4: "...no region can materially improve climate change if it operates solely on its own...China’s participation in carbon policy is a sine qua non for real progress against climate change."
More Notes: The paper includes data on GDP, energy consumption, and CO2 emissions by region relative to the US (Table 8), UN-projected demographics in the 18 regions (Fig. 12), region-specific social cost of carbon (SCC) for the 6x damage function specification (Tables 9 and 10), and projected oil, gas, and coal use under both business-as-usual (BAU) and optimal policy scenarios (Figs. 14, 15, and 16).

Hazard definition-sourcematerial: Global and regional temperature impacts and economic damages are discussed in terms of long-term effects, indicating a focus on chronic trends. For example, the abstract notes the "phased impact of emissions on global and regional temperature" and considers "long-term catastrophic damages," while the notes on page 1 mention that "absent policy, climate change may, under high-damage scenarios, dramatically reduce GDP in most regions." The mention of region-specific temperature and damage functions in a model calibrated to "the latest scientific evidence" further supports this chronic trend orientation. Additionally, page 28 states, "Climate change produces peak GDP regional losses under our ‘high damage scenario’," indicating the evaluation of sustained economic impacts. Gold mentions of specific future years (e.g., "in 2100," "by the century’s end") also emphasize the chronic nature of the analysis. The use of integrated assessment models (IAMs) and long-term projections in the discussion further highlight that the study focuses on projections over extensive time periods, consistent with chronic trends.

Paper Title: Addition of Biochar to Green Roof Substrate to Enhance Plant Performance: A Long-Term Field Study
Outcome(s) of Interest: Enhanced plant growth, reduced building energy costs, shorter payback, reduced structural requirements, increased GR feasibility, reduced GR maintenance costs, reduced substrate temperature.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Results demonstrate the positive impacts of biochar on plant growth in different biochar-GR setups and types of plant species...The GR with medium biochar particles at the amendment rate of 15% v/v had the best plant performance...observed data indicating reduced substrate temperature, which in turn reduces building energy consumption...enhanced plant growth, reduced building energy costs, a shorter payback period, and reduced structural requirements.
Drivers of Risk: Extreme weather, rooftop temperatures, structural safety, limited nutrients, plant-substrate incompatibility, biochar parameters (rate, method, size).
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "Structural safety concerns impact stakeholders' willingness to implement GRs, especially on old buildings with weak structures." "Extreme weather like intense solar radiation and strong winds, as well as low moisture and nutrient availability typical of extensive GR systems, impact plant health on green roofs." "The drivers of risk in this study are rooftop ambient temperatures (both hot and cold periods)." "The plants on GRs faced exposure to extreme solar heat, strong winds, limited nutrient availability, and possible plant-substrate incompatibility, impacting their growth." "Biochar amendment rate, application method (mixing, bottom-applied, top-dressed), and particle size."
Category-sourcematerial: The conclusions were further supported by observed data indicating reduced substrate temperature, which in turn reduces building energy consumption...including aspects like building energy saving and structural loading...Reduction in building temperature and energy consumption is one of the most notable benefits of GRs, which has attracted significant attention from researchers.
Approach-sourcematerial: A one-year long observational data of plant height, coverage area, and dry weight was collected and analyzed from six green roof test beds.
Methodology: Six 1 m² green roof test beds were constructed to investigate the impact of biochar on plant performance over a year, with observations of plant height (fortnightly), coverage area (at the end), and dry weight (at the end); biochar parameters included amendment rates, particle sizes, and application methods (mixing, top-dressing, bottom-application); substrate temperature was measured at 10 cm depth.
Methodology-sourcematerial: A one-year-long observational data of plant height, coverage area, and dry weight was collected and analyzed from six green roof test beds... research investigates the performance of plants in six different 1 m2 biochar-amended GR test beds with varying biochar-related parameters (amendment rates, particle sizes, and application methods)... The experimental setup includes different application methods and particle sizes of biochar, specifically mixing, top-dressing, and bottom-application... Plant heights were recorded fortnightly over four observation periods... Substrate temperature was measured using thermal sensors at 10 cm depth... Plant coverage area was reported at the end of the monitoring period... Six 1 m2 GR test beds were constructed on the building rooftop to investigate the impacts of biochar on plant performance.
Highlights: Biochar-amended green roofs demonstrate enhanced plant growth, improved substrate water retention, and reduced substrate temperatures, contributing to increased building energy savings. A 15% v/v amendment rate with medium-sized biochar particles is optimal, offering the best plant performance and reduced irrigation and maintenance costs. By mitigating harsh rooftop conditions, biochar integration encourages broader adoption of green roofs by maximizing benefits and minimizing investment costs and structural requirements, ultimately reducing payback periods.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Abstract: "Results demonstrate the positive impacts of biochar on plant growth...The GR with medium biochar particles at the amendment rate of 15% v/v had the best plant performance...observed data indicating reduced substrate temperature, which in turn reduces building energy consumption...enhanced plant growth, reduced building energy costs, a shorter payback period, and reduced structural requirements." Page 2: "Biochar improves plant growth in green roofs, and medium biochar particles at a 15% amendment rate resulted in the best plant performance. (b) The test bed modified by 15% v/v medium biochar particles had the best plant performance in terms of plant height, plant dry weight, and plant coverage area. (c) A 15% v/v amendment is recommended as the optimal biochar amendment rate for plant growth." Page 20: "In addition to improving plant performance, biochar amendment also offers solutions to improve building energy savings, reduce structural loading, and shorten the pay-back period."

Given that biochar offers multiple benefits to increase the feasibility of widespread application of GRs on rooftops of either existing or new buildings, this paper focuses on evaluating the effectiveness of biochar in improving plant performance. The aim is to maintain GR serviceability, maximize the benefits to the buildings where they are installed, and reduce operational and maintenance costs, ultimately encouraging the broader adoption of GRs...

Paper Title: Developing an annual global Sub-National scale economic data from 1992 to 2021 using nighttime lights and deep learning
Outcome(s) of Interest: Quantified global economic losses attributed to extreme heat.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: By linking to historical global climate change data, we quantify global economic losses attributed to extreme heat to demonstrate how the estimated GDP data can be useful in the climate change impact analysis.
Drivers of Risk: GDP per capita; sub-national scale variations; extreme heat; economic losses; climate change impact analysis; regional heterogeneity.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: The abstract mentions "a national scale assessment may induce considerable bias because it conceals any internal variations within a country" and highlights the importance of "a novel modeling strategy... to alleviate scale effects at this granularity, while explicitly capturing regional heterogeneity effect." It also notes that the estimated GDP data is linked to "historical global climate change data" to "quantify global economic losses attributed to extreme heat," demonstrating the utility for "climate change impact analysis."
Category-sourcematerial: By linking to historical global climate change data, we quantify global economic losses attributed to extreme heat to demonstrate how the estimated GDP data can be useful in the climate change impact analysis.
Approach-sourcematerial: Leveraging the close relationship between nighttime lights and GDP, we address this gap by developing a novel methodological framework in two steps. First, under the modeling philosophy of spatial statistics, we developed a novel approach based on deep and machine learning techniques to establish a complex mapping between two inconsistent nighttime lights (NTL) datasets...Next, a novel modeling strategy based on multi-layer perceptron was developed to derive the non-linear relationship between NTL and GDP per capita at sub-national scale...By linking to historical global climate change data, we quantify global economic losses attributed to extreme heat to demonstrate how the estimated GDP data can be useful in the climate change impact analysis.
Methodology: A novel two-step methodology was developed: first, deep and machine learning techniques were used to create a complex mapping between inconsistent nighttime lights datasets (DMSP and VIIRS) to extend the DMSP data to 2021; second, a multi-layer perceptron model was developed to derive the non-linear relationship between nighttime lights and GDP per capita at the sub-national scale.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "Leveraging the close relationship between nighttime lights and GDP, we address this gap by developing a novel methodological framework in two steps. First, under the modeling philosophy of spatial statistics, we developed a novel approach based on deep and machine learning techniques to establish a complex mapping between two inconsistent nighttime lights (NTL) datasets: the Defense Meteorological Satellite Program's Operational Linescan System (DMSP) and the National Polar-Orbiting Partnership's Visible Infrared Imaging Radiometer Suite (VIIRS). ... By taking the estimations ensemble of the two techniques, the time series of DMSP data was extended to 2021. Next, a novel modeling strategy based on multi-layer perceptron was developed to derive the non-linear relationship between NTL and GDP per capita at sub-national scale to alleviate scale effects at this granularity, while explicitly capturing regional heterogeneity effect."
Highlights: A novel framework leverages the correlation between nighttime lights and GDP to overcome national-scale bias in climate impact assessments, using deep learning to map inconsistent data sets and derive sub-national GDP estimates, achieving high accuracy and providing a valuable tool for quantifying economic losses due to climate change.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "However, a national scale assessment may induce considerable bias because it conceals any internal variations within a country... Leveraging the close relationship between nighttime lights and GDP, we address this gap by developing a novel methodological framework... based on deep and machine learning techniques... derive the non-linear relationship between NTL and GDP per capita at sub-national scale... The trained models achieve average accuracies of 0.967, 0.959, and 0.959 on the training, validation, and test sets, respectively... By linking to historical global climate change data, we quantify global economic losses attributed to extreme heat to demonstrate how the estimated GDP data can be useful in the climate change impact analysis."
More Notes: The study addresses the challenge of national GDP assessments concealing internal variations by leveraging nighttime lights (NTL) to estimate sub-national GDP per capita data, achieving high accuracy through deep learning techniques and providing valuable insights into economic impacts of climate change attributed to extreme heat.
More Notes-sourcematerial: "The Gross Domestic Product (GDP) per capita is one of the most widely used socioeconomic indicators, serving as an integral component for climate change impact analysis. However, a national scale assessment may induce considerable bias because it conceals any internal variations within a country...Leveraging the close relationship between nighttime lights and GDP, we address this gap by developing a novel methodological framework...The models achieve accuracies ranging from 0.945 to 0.980 (correlation coefficients)...The trained models achieve average accuracies of 0.967, 0.959, and 0.959 on the training, validation, and test sets, respectively...By linking to historical global climate change data, we quantify global economic losses attributed to extreme heat to demonstrate how the estimated GDP data can be useful in the climate change impact analysis."

Approach-sourcematerial: Here we develop a method for quantifying ecological risks from fire to sequestration investments and show how these risks can be factored into an analysis of long-term financial returns relative to opportunity costs. Methodology: A method is developed to quantify ecological risks from fire to carbon sequestration investments and factor these risks into an analysis of long-term financial returns relative to opportunity costs, providing a framework to compare planting options considering key uncertainties.

Hazard definition-sourcematerial: Energy audits directly provided the industrial sector with reduced energy costs and avoided emissions. Still, they also lead to far-reaching indirect and induced local, regional, and national benefits. This paper aims to present the techno-economic-environmental analysis to achieve decarbonization through implementing industrial energy efficiency at micro and macro levels. An integrated techno-economic-environmental methodology is developed. Case studies of micro-level carbon reduction efforts through industrial energy efficiency technologies are presented. The broader macroeconomic and environmental effects of technology on society are analyzed using data from 206 energy audits of industrial compressed air systems conducted over 13 years. The impacts show that energy-efficient improvements lead to direct cost savings for manufacturers, boost economic activity across sectors, and affect carbon dioxide emissions both short-term and long-term in the region. Given their extensive benefits, energy audits significantly influence policymaking. We devised a methodology to link micro-level energy audit data with macroeconomic and environmental analyses to quantify these cascading benefits. The economic scenario analysis shows that $228 M has been saved from direct industrial energy savings from implementing all compressed air recommendations in the studied periods and the region. In addition, the investment made through manufacturers would create 2,025 jobs and $383 M annually, cascading regional economic impacts. The environmental analysis shows that the regional manufacturers have directly avoided about 2.8 M metric tons of carbon dioxide emissions. © The Author(s) 2024.

Paper Title: Electric Vehicle Adoption: A Comprehensive Systematic Review of Technological, Environmental, Organizational and Policy Impacts
Outcome(s) of Interest: Substantial GHG emission reductions, lifecycle emissions lower (EVs vs. ICEVs), operational efficiency/sustainability (fleet management).
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "The environmental analysis reveals substantial reductions in greenhouse gas emissions, with lifecycle assessments showing significant reductions for EVs compared to internal combustion engine vehicles, particularly when charged with renewable energy sources...The organizational implications are evident, as businesses adopt new models for fleet management and logistics, leveraging EVs for operational efficiency and sustainability."
Drivers of Risk: EV adoption, GHG emissions, carbon-intensive manufacturing, battery production, grid dependency, petroleum demand reduction, supply chain emissions, recycling challenges, energy consumption variance, seasonal and geographic factors.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Page 3: EVs are more carbon-intensive to manufacture due to large lithium-ion batteries but compensate by operating more cleanly, emitting less carbon than gasoline cars over their lifetime.; Page 13: The analysis considers the impact of greenhouse gas emissions, carbon emissions, and air pollution, which are drivers of climate-related risks.; Page 16: Contextual factors like route diversity and seasonal variations impact energy consumption and efficiency of EVs.; Page 21: Increasing adoption of electric vehicles (EVs) reduces the demand for petroleum products.; Page 25: Increased emissions due to production processes, particularly from battery materials in BEVs.; Page 27: The need to consider local energy grid composition and infrastructure sustainability when deploying EVs to optimize emissions reductions.; Page 29: Energy-intensive processes and use of carbon-rich materials in tire production contribute to the carbon footprint of EVs.
Category-sourcematerial: The analysis evaluates the impacts of EV adoption on energy efficiency and consumption, focusing on the transportation sector...The findings underscore the profound impact that targeted innovations in EV technology and management can have on energy efficiency...Significant energy savings with EV adoption can be strategically advantageous for fleet electrification, reducing costs and environmental impact.
Approach-sourcematerial: Page 1: Comprehensive systematic review drawing from 88 peer-reviewed articles.; Page 6: The analysis involves manual screening of titles and abstracts by two independent reviewers, data extraction using structured Excel spreadsheets, and bibliometric analysis using VOSviewer software to create co-occurrence maps.; Page 7: The analysis uses bibliometric and content analysis, including a bibliometric analysis using VOSviewer to map EV adoption research and a co-occurrence map based on text data to identify core themes.; Page 24: Lifecycle assessment (LCA) is used to evaluate the environmental performance of vehicles through different phases, including raw material extraction, manufacturing, use phase, and end-of-life.; Page 16: The analysis uses modeling tools, such as AIMSUN software, operational data, and adaptive strategies to assess energy efficiency.
Methodology: The study employed a systematic review of 88 peer-reviewed articles (2019-2024) following PRISMA 2020 guidelines, focusing on the environmental, organizational, technological, and policy dimensions of EV adoption. The selection process involved rigorous screening and exclusion criteria, including manual title and abstract screening by two independent reviewers using Excel spreadsheets. Data extraction targeted GHG emissions, air quality, economic viability, energy efficiency, battery technology advancements, and policy frameworks. Bibliometric analysis using VOSviewer software created co-occurrence maps of keywords and countries of co-authorship to identify thematic relationships and global collaborations. Both bibliometric and content analysis were performed. The analysis also used lifecycle assessment (LCA) to evaluate environmental performance from raw material extraction to end-of-life, including battery recycling methods (pyrometallurgical, hydrometallurgical, and direct physical). Modeling tools like AIMSUN were used, as were operational data and adaptive strategies, to assess energy efficiency. Keyword analysis mapped themes and trends in EV adoption research. While a formal risk of bias assessment wasn't conducted, studies with robust methodologies were prioritized.
Methodology-sourcematerial:
Page 1: Comprehensive systematic review drawing from 88 peer-reviewed articles.
Page 5: The analysis was performed by reviewing literature following PRISMA 2020 guidelines with a focus on EV adoption from 2019 to 2024. Initial filtering based on publication year (2019–2024) reduced the pool to 498 papers, reflecting a surge in EV adoption research. Further refinement to include only English-language journal articles narrowed it down to 312 papers. Next, we reviewed the titles and abstracts of the remaining papers, narrowing our selection to 130 studies closely aligned with our focus on the integration of EVs with sustainability and organizational strategy. We applied strict exclusion criteria to ensure the included studies comprehensively addressed the impact of EV adoption on organizational performance, technological innovation, and sustainable infrastructure, excluding those that solely focused on the technical aspects of EVs.
Page 6: Two independent reviewers screened the titles and abstracts of the retrieved articles using an Excel spreadsheet, marking each study as 'relevant' or 'not relevant.' This manual approach, without the use of automated tools, enhanced the rigor and reproducibility of our selection process. We employed VOSviewer software for bibliometric analysis to create co-occurrence maps of key terms from the titles and abstracts of the selected articles. Data collection targeted four primary outcome dimensions to assess the comprehensive impacts of EV adoption: environment, organizations, technology, and policy. • Environment: Focus on GHG emissions, air quality improvements. • Organizations: Examination of economic viability, energy efficiency. • Technology: Analysis of advancements in battery technologies. • Policy: Assessment of policy frameworks, regulatory impacts.
Page 7: BibliometricAnalysis: We conducted a bibliometric analysis using VOSviewer to map the field of EV adoption research. Each map uses variably sized circles to depict entities such as concepts, authors, or studies, with the size and proximity of circles indicating the level of research activity and thematic relationships, respectively.

Climate features-sourcematerial:
Page 1: This comprehensive systematic review explores the multifaceted impacts of electric vehicle (EV) adoption across technological, environmental, organizational, and policy dimensions. Adopting EVs refers to the increased choice of electric vehicles over traditional fossil fuel-powered vehicles by individuals and companies. With the International Energy Agency (IEA) projecting a 35% global market share for electric cars by 2030, with China, the United States, and Europe leading the charge, these policies not only lower financial barriers but also strengthen the commitment to sustainable mobility. Drawing from 88 peer-reviewed articles, the study addresses a critical gap in the existing literature, which often isolates the impact of EV adoption without considering holistic effects. The environmental analysis reveals substantial reductions in greenhouse gas emissions, with lifecycle assessments showing significant reductions for EVs compared to internal combustion engine vehicles, particularly when charged with renewable energy sources. The review identifies future research areas such as efficient battery recycling methods, the potential impact of EVs on grid stability, and long-term economic implications.

Page 6: Two independent reviewers screened the titles and abstracts of the retrieved articles using an Excel spreadsheet, marking each study as 'relevant' or 'not relevant.' This manual approach, without the use of automated tools, enhanced the rigor and reproducibility of our selection process. We employed VOSviewer software for bibliometric analysis to create co-occurrence maps of key terms from the titles and abstracts of the selected articles. Data collection targeted four primary outcome dimensions to assess the comprehensive impacts of EV adoption: environment, organizations, technology, and policy. 
• Environment: Focus on GHG emissions, air quality improvements. 
• Organizations: Examination of economic viability, energy efficiency. 
• Technology: Analysis of advancements in battery technologies. 
• Policy: Assessment of policy frameworks, regulatory impacts.

This subsection explores the multifaceted environmental impacts of EV adoption, examining how these vehicles influence air quality, carbon footprints, and overall ecological sustainability across various sectors and regions.

Battery swap technology addresses range anxiety (p. 31). Region-specific solutions are also explored (p. 31). A routing algorithm integrates battery health (p. 31). A model predicts energy needs of battery electric buses using environmental and operational variables (p. 31).
