Paper Title: The Impact of Climate Change on Human Fungal Pathogen Distribution and Disease Incidence
Outcome(s) of Interest: Emergence, expansion, virulence of human pathogenic fungi; increased disease burden; antifungal resistance; opportunistic fungal infections.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Long-term shifts in temperatures and weather patterns are anticipated to influence emergence, expansion, and virulence of human pathogenic fungi. ...warming temperatures and stochastic precipitation that may aid in expanding the distribution of several endemic fungal pathogenic lineages and increase disease burden. ...Diverse evolutionary pressures, including higher average temperatures caused by climate change and conditioning of pathogens to endothermy, has contributed to antifungal resistance in several fungal lineages. The increasing frequency of extreme weather events presents favorable circumstances for opportunistic fungal infections.
Drivers of Risk: Climate change (warming temperatures, stochastic precipitation), evolutionary pressures (conditioning to endothermy), extreme weather events, population growth, urbanization, intercontinental travel, increased human longevity.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "Emerging infectious diseases are increasing in frequency and impact in response to anthropogenic expedited climate change... Diverse evolutionary pressures, including higher average temperatures caused by climate change and conditioning of pathogens to endothermy, has contributed to antifungal resistance... The increasing frequency of extreme weather events presents favorable circumstances for opportunistic fungal infections... Population growth, urbanization, intercontinental travel, and increased human longevity coupled with progressively damaging climate change effects generate conditions that make communities increasingly vulnerable to fungal pathogens...warming temperatures and stochastic precipitation that may aid in expanding the distribution of several endemic fungal pathogenic lineages and increase disease burden."
Category-sourcematerial: Emerging infectious diseases are increasing in frequency and impact in response to anthropogenic expedited climate change. The simultaneous global emergence of the multidrug-resistant fungus Candida auris has been hypothesized to be intimately related to warming temperatures. Endemic fungi (e.g., species of Coccidioides, Blastomyces, and Histoplasma) known to be largely constrained by environment and host range are expanding in distribution and increasing disease burden. The COVID-19 pandemic illuminated the vulnerability of human populations to aspergillosis and mucormycosis co-infections. The increasing frequency of extreme weather events presents favorable circumstances for opportunistic fungal infections. Summary: Long-term shifts in temperatures and weather patterns are anticipated to influence emergence, expansion, and virulence of human pathogenic fungi.
Approach-sourcematerial: There is no mention of any specific modeling or analysis approach (Econometric, Macro-model, or Stylised) in the abstract. The abstract describes a review of existing literature and knowledge on the impact of climate change on human pathogenic fungi.
Methodology: The methodology involves compiling current knowledge on the impact of climate change on human pathogenic fungi, focusing on surveillance to predict disease burden and geographic expansion, and analyzing long-term climate patterns and short-term environmental events affecting fungal dispersal and incidence.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Ongoing surveillance of the fungal lineages described here will be imperative to predict disease burden and geographic expansion. [...] Here, we review both long-term climate patterns and short-term environmental disruption events that affect dispersal and incidence of human pathogenic fungi.
Highlights: Anthropogenic climate change drives increased emergence and expansion of human pathogenic fungi, with warming temperatures and extreme weather aiding their distribution and disease burden, necessitating enhanced fungal surveillance.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Emerging infectious diseases are increasing in frequency and impact in response to anthropogenic expedited climate change...warming temperatures and stochastic precipitation that may aid in expanding the distribution of several endemic fungal pathogenic lineages and increase disease burden...increasing frequency of extreme weather events presents favorable circumstances for opportunistic fungal infections...Ongoing surveillance of the fungal lineages described here will be imperative to predict disease burden and geographic expansion.
More Notes: Climate change, through warming temperatures, altered precipitation patterns, and extreme weather events, is contributing to the emergence, expansion, virulence, and geographic distribution of human pathogenic fungi, increasing disease burden and vulnerability, especially in conjunction with factors like population growth and travel.
More Notes-sourcematerial: "Long-term shifts in temperatures and weather patterns are anticipated to influence emergence, expansion, and virulence of human pathogenic fungi...warming temperatures and stochastic precipitation that may aid in expanding the distribution of several endemic fungal pathogenic lineages and increase disease burden...increasing frequency of extreme weather events presents favorable circumstances for opportunistic fungal infections...Population growth, urbanization, intercontinental travel, and increased human longevity coupled with progressively damaging climate change effects generate conditions that make communities increasingly vulnerable to fungal pathogens."

Paper Title: Global differences in urbanization dynamics from 1985 to 2015 and outlook considering IPCC climate scenarios
Outcome(s) of Interest: Areas with high urban growth face combined climate impacts; South Asia/West Africa: high urban growth & rainfall increase; Europe/North America: extreme temperatures & adaptation focus.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: The key findings are: ... In South Asia and West Africa, for example, very high urban growth rates and a very high increase in predicted maximum rainfall are forecast. The greatest increase in extreme temperatures is predicted for Europe and North America. Settlement growth has already slowed there, so adaptation measures are taking center stage.
Drivers of Risk: Urban growth (pace and scale), climate change (rainfall, temperature), geographic location (South Asia, West Africa, Europe, North America).
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "…to estimate where spatial focal areas of urbanization and climate-change meet… In South Asia and West Africa, for example, very high urban growth rates and a very high increase in predicted maximum rainfall are forecast. The greatest increase in extreme temperatures is predicted for Europe and North America. Settlement growth has already slowed there, so adaptation measures are taking center stage."
Category-sourcematerial: The key findings are: ... In South Asia and West Africa, for example, very high urban growth rates and a very high increase in predicted maximum rainfall are forecast. The greatest increase in extreme temperatures is predicted for Europe and North America.
Approach-sourcematerial: The study is based on remote sensing-derived geodata such as the World Settlement Footprint Evolution dataset for the period 1985 to 2015 and morphological urban areas. The measured past urban trends are projected into the future and linked to climate scenarios used by the Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) to estimate where spatial focal areas of urbanization and climate-change meet.
Methodology: Uses remote sensing-derived geodata (World Settlement Footprint Evolution dataset, 1985-2015, and morphological urban areas) to delineate city boundaries, enabling consistent comparisons of urban growth dynamics across 1567 cities worldwide, related to continents, geographic regions, national levels, and city sizes; past trends are then projected into the future and linked to IPCC climate scenarios.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "The study is based on remote sensing-derived geodata such as the World Settlement Footprint Evolution dataset for the period 1985 to 2015 and morphological urban areas. Third, a data-driven approach to delineate city boundaries allows for consistent comparisons across geographies. Fourth, the measured past urban trends are projected into the future and linked to climate scenarios used by the Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) to estimate where spatial focal areas of urbanization and climate-change meet. First, the study is comprehensive including all 1567 cities worldwide that had >300,000 inhabitants in 2015. Second, comparison of settlement dynamics of cities is performed in relation to continents, to geographic regions, to national levels and to city sizes."
Highlights: Highest relative urban growth is in Asia, particularly East Asia, while Oceania and Europe have the lowest; absolute settlement expansion rates remain constant globally; urban growth varies widely within Asia but less so in Europe; South Asia and West Africa face high urban growth and increased rainfall, while Europe and North America, despite slower growth, will experience the greatest increase in extreme temperatures, necessitating adaptation measures.
Highlights-sourcematerial: At continental scale, highest relative urban growth rates are measured in Asia. Among the IPCC regions, highest relative urban growth rates are in East Asia. In contrast, lowest growth rates are in Oceania and Europe. As expected, across most regions, there is a general decline in relative growth rates over the study period. One surprising finding is that absolute settlement expansion rates are not declining over time, but are rather relatively constant. It is also interesting to see that across and within countries growth rates of urban settlements vary widely in Asia, whereas in Europe there are only marginal differences. And finally, a look into the future reveals that certain regions can expect particularly high exposures. In South Asia and West Africa, for example, very high urban growth rates and a very high increase in predicted maximum rainfall are forecast. The greatest increase in extreme temperatures is predicted for Europe and North America. Settlement growth has already slowed there, so adaptation measures are taking center stage.
More Notes: Urban growth varies significantly across continents and regions, with Asia experiencing the highest relative growth rates, while future projections indicate high exposure to climate impacts in South Asia and West Africa (increased rainfall) and Europe/North America (extreme temperatures), where adaptation is crucial due to slower growth.

Paper Title: Global socioeconomic risk assessment of rainstorms based on different CMIP6 scenarios in the future
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased socioeconomic risk from rainstorms globally, especially in Asia, with variations across scenarios and continents influenced by socioeconomic factors and rainstorm intensity.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: (1) The socioeconomic risk from rainstorms in most regions of the world will increase in the future... (2) ...the largest socioeconomic risk from rainstorms is in Asia, especially the southern and southeastern parts of Asia... (3) The socioeconomic risk changes of rainstorms in North and South America, Africa, Asia, and Oceania are more closely related to socioeconomic exposure changes, while the socioeconomic risk changes of rainstorms in Europe are more closely related to the three rainstorm indicators.
Drivers of Risk: Socioeconomic exposure, rainstorm indicators, SSP scenarios, uncertainty, regional differences
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: The socioeconomic risks from rainstorms in different periods (1995–2014, 2041–2060, and 2081–2100) were compared at global and continental scales. The main factors that cause the differences in the socioeconomic risks from rainstorms on different continents were explored. The uncertainties of the three rainstorm indicators calculated from the 22 GCMs were analyzed. ... (3) The socioeconomic risk changes of rainstorms in North and South America, Africa, Asia, and Oceania are more closely related to socioeconomic exposure changes, while the socioeconomic risk changes of rainstorms in Europe are more closely related to the three rainstorm indicators. (4) Among the three scenarios, the SSP5-8.5 scenario has the largest uncertainty, and among the three rainstorm indicators, ARDs have the largest uncertainty. The regions with the greatest uncertainty are concentrated in central North America, Central America, central and southern Africa, central South America, eastern Asia, and southern Oceania.
Category-sourcematerial: Based on the daily precipitation simulation data of 22 global climate models (GCMs) under three scenarios, shared socioeconomic pathways (SSPs) 1–2.6, 2–4.5, and 5–8.5 in the Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 6 (CMIP6), this study calculated three rainstorm indicators (annual rainstorm days, ARDs; annual rainstorm intensity, ARI; and annual rainstorm maximum, ARM), combined them into an index to indicate rainstorm hazard, calculated the socioeconomic exposure index using the projected future population and GDP data of SSP1, SSP2, and SSP3, and finally combined the rainstorm hazard index and socioeconomic exposure index to calculate the socioeconomic risk index of rainstorms.
Approach-sourcematerial: Based on the daily precipitation simulation data of 22 global climate models (GCMs) under three scenarios, shared socioeconomic pathways (SSPs) 1–2.6, 2–4.5, and 5–8.5 in the Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 6 (CMIP6), this study calculated three rainstorm indicators (annual rainstorm days, ARDs; annual rainstorm intensity, ARI; and annual rainstorm maximum, ARM), combined them into an index to indicate rainstorm hazard, calculated the socioeconomic exposure index using the projected future population and GDP data of SSP1, SSP2, and SSP3, and finally combined the rainstorm hazard index and socioeconomic exposure index to calculate the socioeconomic risk index of rainstorms.
Methodology: Rainstorm hazard was assessed using daily precipitation data from 22 GCMs under SSP1-2.6, SSP2-4.5, and SSP5-8.5 from CMIP6, measuring annual rainstorm days, intensity, and maximum, forming a hazard index; socioeconomic exposure was gauged by projected population and GDP under SSP1, SSP2, and SSP3, leading to a combined socioeconomic risk index, with evaluations for 1995-2014, 2041-2060, and 2081-2100 globally and continentally, while also analyzing uncertainty across regions.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Based on the daily precipitation simulation data of 22 global climate models (GCMs) under three scenarios, shared socioeconomic pathways (SSPs) 1–2.6, 2–4.5, and 5–8.5 in the Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 6 (CMIP6), this study calculated three rainstorm indicators (annual rainstorm days, ARDs; annual rainstorm intensity, ARI; and annual rainstorm maximum, ARM), combined them into an index to indicate rainstorm hazard, calculated the socioeconomic exposure index using the projected future population and GDP data of SSP1, SSP2, and SSP3, and finally combined the rainstorm hazard index and socioeconomic exposure index to calculate the socioeconomic risk index of rainstorms. The socioeconomic risks from rainstorms in different periods (1995–2014, 2041–2060, and 2081–2100) were compared at global and continental scales. The uncertainties of the three rainstorm indicators calculated from the 22 GCMs were analyzed.
Highlights: Future socioeconomic risk from rainstorms will increase globally, particularly in Asia; risk changes are influenced by socioeconomic exposure and rainstorm indicators, with the highest uncertainty under the SSP5-8.5 scenario and for annual rainstorm days (ARDs).
Highlights-sourcematerial: (1) The socioeconomic risk from rainstorms in most regions of the world will increase in the future, with the largest increase in the SSP5-8.5 scenario and the smallest increase in the SSP1-2.6 scenario. (2) Overall, in the two future periods, the least socioeconomic risk from rainstorms is in Europe, and the largest socioeconomic risk from rainstorms is in Asia, especially the southern and southeastern parts of Asia, which have both the highest mean socioeconomic risk and the largest growth of socioeconomic risk. (3) The socioeconomic risk changes of rainstorms in North and South America, Africa, Asia, and Oceania are more closely related to socioeconomic exposure changes, while the socioeconomic risk changes of rainstorms in Europe are more closely related to the three rainstorm indicators. (4) Among the three scenarios, the SSP5-8.5 scenario has the largest uncertainty, and among the three rainstorm indicators, ARDs have the largest uncertainty.
More Notes: Socioeconomic risk from rainstorms is projected to increase globally, with the highest risk and growth in Asia (especially southern and southeastern parts) and the lowest in Europe; socioeconomic exposure is a stronger driver of risk changes in North and South America, Africa, Asia, and Oceania, while rainstorm indicators are more influential in Europe; SSP5-8.5 has the greatest uncertainty, with annual rainstorm days (ARDs) being the most uncertain indicator, particularly in central North America, Central America, central and southern Africa, central South America, eastern Asia, and southern Oceania.

Paper Title: Organic blue carbon sequestration in vegetated coastal wetlands: Processes and influencing factors
Outcome(s) of Interest: Short-term C storage increases with mild warming, but decreases long-term as warming intensifies; sea level rise harms plant growth and inhibits C decomposition; human activities decrease C storage.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "As a result, C storage could increase under mild warming conditions in the short-term, but decrease in the long-term as the severity of warming intensifies. Elevated salinity, caused by sea level rise, can be harmful to plant growth and inhibit organic C decomposition because of the reduced biomass and the weakened metabolic capacity of microorganisms. Most of human activities, such as reclamation, can lead to less C input and more C output, resulting in decreased C storage in coastal wetlands."
Drivers of Risk: Elevated CO2, climate warming, sea level rise (salinity), anthropogenic activities (reclamation).
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Elevated atmospheric CO2, global climate warming, sea level rise and anthropogenic activities. Increased atmospheric CO2 concentration and associated climate warming often enhanced vegetation growth, while climate warming also promoted soil C decomposition. Elevated salinity, caused by sea level rise, can be harmful to plant growth... Most of human activities, such as reclamation, can lead to less C input and more C output...
Category-sourcematerial: Coastal wetlands play a vital role in carbon (C) sequestration...The review aims to disentangle the processes and influencing factors, including elevated atmospheric CO2, global climate warming, sea level rise and anthropogenic activities...This review offers valuable theoretical support and scientific references for the sustainable development and management of coastal wetlands in a changing climate.
Approach-sourcematerial: This review aims to disentangle the processes and influencing factors... Firstly, we provided an overview of C processes... We then summarized the impacts of different factors on C processes... Additionally, we also illustrate various coastal wetland restoration methods... This review offers valuable theoretical support and scientific references...
Methodology: The review synthesizes existing literature to analyze the impacts of elevated CO2, climate warming, sea level rise, and anthropogenic activities on carbon input, output, and deposition processes in coastal wetlands by examining their effects on soil properties, plant growth, vegetation type, and microbial communities.
Methodology-sourcematerial: The review aims to disentangle the processes and influencing factors, including elevated atmospheric CO2, global climate warming, sea level rise and anthropogenic activities. ... we summarized the impacts of different factors on C processes by modifying soil physicochemical properties, plant growth, vegetation type, and microbial community composition. ... we provided an overview of C processes, including input, output, and deposition, in coastal wetlands.
Highlights: Coastal wetlands' carbon sequestration ("blue carbon") is affected by increased CO2, warming, sea level rise, and human activities; short-term C storage may increase under mild warming but decrease long-term, while sea level rise inhibits both plant growth and decomposition. Human activities reduce C storage, but restoration methods like vegetation management and hydrological restoration can enhance C sequestration.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Increased atmospheric CO2 concentration and associated climate warming often enhanced vegetation growth, while climate warming also promoted soil C decomposition. As a result, C storage could increase under mild warming conditions in the short-term, but decrease in the long-term as the severity of warming intensifies. Elevated salinity, caused by sea level rise, can be harmful to plant growth and inhibit organic C decomposition because of the reduced biomass and the weakened metabolic capacity of microorganisms. Most of human activities, such as reclamation, can lead to less C input and more C output, resulting in decreased C storage in coastal wetlands. Vegetation management could benefit plant growth and enhance C input effectively, and hydrological restoration can maintain the harmonious development of coastal wetland ecosystems.
More Notes: Coastal wetlands' carbon storage is affected by climate change; increased CO2 and mild warming initially enhance storage, but long-term warming and sea level rise (elevated salinity) reduce it by impacting vegetation and microbial activity. Human activities also decrease carbon storage. Restoration methods, including vegetation management and hydrological restoration, can help enhance carbon sequestration.
More Notes-sourcematerial: Coastal wetlands play a vital role in carbon (C) sequestration, named ‘blue carbon’... Elevated atmospheric CO2, global climate warming, sea level rise, and anthropogenic activities... Increased atmospheric CO2 concentration and associated climate warming often enhanced vegetation growth, while climate warming also promoted soil C decomposition... As a result, C storage could increase under mild warming conditions in the short-term, but decrease in the long-term as the severity of warming intensifies... Elevated salinity, caused by sea level rise, can be harmful to plant growth and inhibit organic C decomposition... Most of human activities, such as reclamation, can lead to less C input and more C output, resulting in decreased C storage in coastal wetlands... Various coastal wetland restoration methods aimed at enhancing C sequestration, including... vegetation management, hydrological restoration... could protect coastal wetlands, especially facing sea level rise.
Hazard definition-sourcematerial: Firstly, we provided an overview of C processes, including input, output, and deposition, in coastal wetlands. We then summarized the impacts of different factors on C processes by modifying soil physicochemical properties, plant growth, vegetation type, and microbial community composition. Increased atmospheric CO2 concentration and associated climate warming often enhanced vegetation growth, while climate warming also promoted soil C decomposition. Elevated salinity, caused by sea level rise, can be harmful to plant growth and inhibit organic C decomposition because of the reduced biomass and the weakened metabolic capacity of microorganisms. Most of human activities, such as reclamation, can lead to less C input and more C output, resulting in decreased C storage in coastal wetlands. This review offers valuable theoretical support and scientific references for the sustainable development and management of coastal wetlands in a changing climate.

Space disaggregation-sourcematerial: The abstract does not specify a spatial scale for the analysis. It discusses general processes and factors affecting coastal wetlands globally.

Elevated salinity, caused by sea level rise, can be harmful to plant growth and inhibit organic C decomposition because of the reduced biomass and the weakened metabolic capacity of microorganisms.

Elevated salinity, caused by sea level rise, can be harmful to plant growth and inhibit organic C decomposition because of the reduced biomass and the weakened metabolic capacity of microorganisms.
Adaptation considered: Various adaptation strategies are considered, including coastal wetland restoration methods aimed at enhancing carbon sequestration. These strategies cover legal frameworks, scientific theories, vegetation management, and hydrological restoration, which are intended to improve plant growth, carbon input, and overall ecosystem stability. Additionally, structural interventions like breakwaters, spillways, and dredged materials are mentioned as protective measures against sea level rise. The review offers scientific insights to support sustainable development and management of coastal wetlands in response to climate change impacts.
Adaptation considered-sourcematerial: "Additionally, we also illustrate various coastal wetland restoration methods aimed at enhancing C sequestration, including legal frameworks, scientific theories, vegetation management, hydrological restoration, and other relevant constructions. Vegetation management could benefit plant growth and enhance C input effectively, and hydrological restoration can maintain the harmonious development of coastal wetland ecosystems. Other constructions, including breakwater, spillway, and dredged material, could protect coastal wetlands, especially facing sea level rise. This review offers valuable theoretical support and scientific references for the sustainable development and management of coastal wetlands in a changing climate."

Paper Title: Integrating deep learning, satellite image processing, and spatial-temporal analysis for urban flood prediction
Outcome(s) of Interest: Rapid and accurate (97.05% accuracy, 92.49% F-1 Score, 93% AUC) near real-time flood extent predictions at 10m resolution in urban areas, validated with historical floodplain data (84.05% Ground Truth Index), applicable for continuous flood monitoring, infrastructure impact assessment, early warning systems, and insurance risk assessment.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "The results demonstrated the capability of the model to provide rapid and accurate flood extent predictions at a spatial resolution of 10 m, with an overall accuracy of 97.05 %, F-1 Score of 92.49 %, and AUC of 93 % in the study area...resulting in a Ground Truth Index of 84.05 % that shows the accuracy of the model in identifying flooded areas...The presented NRT flood extent mapping model has a broad range of applicability, including, but not limited to, the continuous monitoring of flood events and their potential impacts on civil infrastructure assets (e.g., construction, operation, and maintenance of roads and bridges), early warning systems for timely evacuation and preparedness measures, and insurance risk assessment."
Drivers of Risk: Urban flooding; rainfall; dynamic urban environments; climate change; insufficient flood event data.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "Drivers of Risk: Urban flooding is not easily trackable because of: Dynamic urban environments, Impacts of climate change, Insufficient flood event data."
Category-sourcematerial: Objectives: Developing a deep learning model for predicting near-real time urban flood extents resulting from rainfall (pluvial flood) using a CNN network (U-Net). Urban flooding is escalating worldwide due to the increasing impervious surfaces from urban developments and frequency of extreme rainfall events by climate change. The paper evaluates the capability of a deep learning model for near real-time urban flood mapping in Miami-Dade County, FL.
Approach-sourcematerial: Objectives: Developing a deep learning model for predicting near-real time urban flood extents resulting from rainfall (pluvial flood) using a CNN network (U-Net). Data: Utilizing daily rainfall and Sentinel-1 imagery.
Methodology: The study develops a deep learning U-Net model for near real-time urban flood extent prediction using Sentinel-1 imagery, daily rainfall data, and spatial datasets such as slope, hydrologic soil group (HSG), and imperviousness, focusing on urban areas in Miami-Dade County, Florida.

Paper Title: FeliX 2.0: An integrated model of climate, economy, environment, and society interactions
Outcome(s) of Interest: Future projections of multiple variables up to 2100, insights into interactions within the human-Earth system, analysis of complex economic-environmental-social challenges in short- and long-term future.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Future projections of multiple variables up to 2100 demonstrate coherences between FeliX 2.0 and the IAMs used in IPCC assessments...FeliX 2.0 stands as an informative tool and offers insights into interactions within the human-Earth system and the analysis of complex economic-environmental-social challenges in short- and long-term future.
Drivers of Risk: Population dynamics, food and land use systems, socioeconomic settings, human behaviors, interactions among global systems.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: FeliX explicitly incorporating human behaviors and their dynamic interactions among global systems. FeliX 2.0 refined its original version in population dynamics, food and land use systems, and socioeconomic settings for poverty analysis.
Category-sourcematerial: The Full of Economic-Environment Linkages and Integration dX/dt (FeliX) model is a System Dynamics-based Integrated Assessment Model (IAM), explicitly incorporating human behaviors and their dynamic interactions among global systems.
Approach-sourcematerial: The Full of Economic-Environment Linkages and Integration dX/dt (FeliX) model is a System Dynamics-based Integrated Assessment Model (IAM), explicitly incorporating human behaviors and their dynamic interactions among global systems.
Methodology: FeliX 2.0 is a System Dynamics-based Integrated Assessment Model (IAM) incorporating human behaviors and their interactions among global systems, featuring nine integrated modules with refinements in population dynamics, food and land use, and socioeconomic settings for poverty analysis; key variables are calibrated against historical data since 1950, projecting multiple variables to 2100.
Methodology-sourcematerial: The Full of Economic-Environment Linkages and Integration dX/dt (FeliX) model is a System Dynamics-based Integrated Assessment Model (IAM), explicitly incorporating human behaviors and their dynamic interactions among global systems. This paper presents FeliX 2.0, describing the detailed framework and key interactions among nine integrated modules. FeliX 2.0 refined its original version in population dynamics, food and land use systems, and socioeconomic settings for poverty analysis. Robust calibration is applied to key variables against their historical data since 1950. Future projections of multiple variables up to 2100 demonstrate coherences between FeliX 2.0 and the IAMs used in IPCC assessments.
Highlights: FeliX 2.0, a System Dynamics-based IAM, features refined modules for population, food, land use, and socioeconomic poverty analysis, robustly calibrated against historical data since 1950, and demonstrates coherence with IPCC IAMs in future projections up to 2100, highlighting its efficacy in capturing global system interdependencies for analyzing complex challenges.
Highlights-sourcematerial: The Full of Economic-Environment Linkages and Integration dX/dt (FeliX) model is a System Dynamics-based Integrated Assessment Model (IAM), explicitly incorporating human behaviors and their dynamic interactions among global systems. This paper presents FeliX 2.0, describing the detailed framework and key interactions among nine integrated modules. FeliX 2.0 refined its original version in population dynamics, food and land use systems, and socioeconomic settings for poverty analysis. Robust calibration is applied to key variables against their historical data since 1950. Future projections of multiple variables up to 2100 demonstrate coherences between FeliX 2.0 and the IAMs used in IPCC assessments. Both outputs (the robust calibration results and future projections) underscore the efficacy of FeliX 2.0 in capturing complex interdependencies within global systems. FeliX 2.0 stands as an informative tool and offers insights into interactions within the human-Earth system and the analysis of complex economic-environmental-social challenges in short- and long-term future.
More Notes: FeliX 2.0 is a System Dynamics-based Integrated Assessment Model (IAM) that simulates global systems, incorporating human behaviors and their dynamic interactions, calibrated against historical data and projecting future trends to 2100, making it useful for analyzing complex economic-environmental-social challenges.

Paper Title: Heat Stress and Plant–Biotic Interactions: Advances and Perspectives
Outcome(s) of Interest: Lower crop yields; reduced disease resistance; vulnerability to multiple stressors; increased frequency/severity of concurrent heat stress and biotic stresses; impact on plant physiology/growth.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: HS threatens global food security and biodiversity...HS events have negatively impacted plant physiology, reducing plant’s ability to maintain disease resistance and resulting in lower crop yields...selective breeding and long-term domestication for higher yields have made crop varieties vulnerable to multiple stressors, making them more susceptible to frequent HS events...concurrent HS and biotic stresses will become more frequent and severe in the future...the simultaneous occurrence of HS and biotic stresses pose a growing threat to agricultural productivity...combined impact on crop growth and yields.
Drivers of Risk: Climate change; elevated temperatures; heat stress (HS); reduced disease resistance; lower crop yields; selective breeding; vulnerability to stressors; concurrent HS and biotic stresses.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Climate change presents numerous challenges for agriculture, including frequent events of plant abiotic stresses such as elevated temperatures that lead to heat stress (HS). As the primary driving factor of climate change, HS threatens global food security and biodiversity...Studies on climate change predict that concurrent HS and biotic stresses will become more frequent and severe in the future...selective breeding and long-term domestication for higher yields have made crop varieties vulnerable to multiple stressors, making them more susceptible to frequent HS events.
Category-sourcematerial: Climate change presents numerous challenges for agriculture...HS threatens global food security...HS events have negatively impacted plant physiology, reducing plant’s ability to maintain disease resistance and resulting in lower crop yields...the simultaneous occurrence of HS and biotic stresses pose a growing threat to agricultural productivity.
Approach-sourcematerial: Here, we aim to shed light on the physiological and molecular effects of HS and biotic factor interactions (bacteria, fungi, oomycetes, nematodes, insect pests, pollinators, weedy species, and parasitic plants), as well as their combined impact on crop growth and yields. We also examine recent advances in designing and developing various strategies to address multi-stress scenarios related to HS and biotic factors.
Methodology: The review synthesizes existing research on the physiological and molecular effects of interactions between heat stress and biotic factors (bacteria, fungi, oomycetes, nematodes, insect pests, pollinators, weedy species, and parasitic plants) on crop growth and yields, and examines recent advances in strategies to address these combined stressors.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Here, we aim to shed light on the physiological and molecular effects of HS and biotic factor interactions (bacteria, fungi, oomycetes, nematodes, insect pests, pollinators, weedy species, and parasitic plants), as well as their combined impact on crop growth and yields. We also examine recent advances in designing and developing various strategies to address multi-stress scenarios related to HS and biotic factors.
Highlights: Heat stress (HS) exacerbated by climate change threatens food security and biodiversity by negatively impacting plant physiology, disease resistance, and crop yields; concurrent HS and biotic stresses are predicted to increase, yet the combined effects on plant-biotic interactions are understudied, hindering the development of effective mitigation strategies.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Climate change presents numerous challenges for agriculture, including frequent events of plant abiotic stresses such as elevated temperatures that lead to heat stress (HS)....HS threatens global food security and biodiversity...HS events have negatively impacted plant physiology, reducing plant’s ability to maintain disease resistance and resulting in lower crop yields...Studies on climate change predict that concurrent HS and biotic stresses will become more frequent and severe in the future...Few studies have explored the interactions between HS and plant–biotic interactions.
More Notes: Heat stress, exacerbated by climate change, compromises plant physiology, reduces disease resistance, and lowers crop yields, particularly in domesticated varieties. Concurrent heat stress and biotic stresses (bacteria, fungi, oomycetes, nematodes, insect pests, pollinators, weedy species, and parasitic plants) are becoming more frequent and severely impacting crop growth and yields.
More Notes-sourcematerial: Climate change presents numerous challenges for agriculture, including frequent events of plant abiotic stresses such as elevated temperatures that lead to heat stress (HS). As the primary driving factor of climate change, HS threatens global food security and biodiversity. In recent years, HS events have negatively impacted plant physiology, reducing plant’s ability to maintain disease resistance and resulting in lower crop yields. Furthermore, selective breeding and long-term domestication for higher yields have made crop varieties vulnerable to multiple stressors, making them more susceptible to frequent HS events. Studies on climate change predict that concurrent HS and biotic stresses will become more frequent and severe in the future, potentially occurring simultaneously or sequentially. Few studies have explored the interactions between HS and plant–biotic interactions.

Paper Title: Mechanism of crop-endophytic microbes interactions in response to elevated CO2 concentrations and drought stress: a review; [作物-内生微生物响应 CO2 浓度升高与干旱胁迫的作用机制研究进展]
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased crop yield, enhanced stress resistance (CO2 and drought), improved stomatal regulation, increased root water and nutrient absorption, activated host defense system, enhanced pathogen resistance.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: ...endophytic microbes can help crops adapt to increased CO2 concentrations and drought stress by enhancing the stomatal regulation ability of the host and increasing its root water and nutrient absorption capacity, thereby reducing the negative impact of environmental changes on crop growth and increasing crop yield. Additionally, endophytic microbes can activate the host defense system, enhance resistance to pathogens, and reduce the impact of diseases on crops...
Drivers of Risk: Increasing CO2 concentrations; worsening local droughts; environmental changes; pathogens.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Global warming impacts such as increasing carbon dioxide (CO2) concentrations and effect of worsening local droughts on crop growth and yield are becoming progressively evident...Additionally, endophytic microbes can activate the host defense system, enhance resistance to pathogens, and reduce the impact of diseases on crops.
Category-sourcematerial: Global warming impacts such as increasing carbon dioxide (CO2) concentrations and effect of worsening local droughts on crop growth and yield are becoming progressively evident. ... This article reviews the crop-endophyte symbiotic system and its regulation of crop physiological processes under CO2 and drought stress. ... Specifically, endophytic microbes can help crops adapt to increased CO2 concentrations and drought stress by enhancing the stomatal regulation ability of the host and increasing its root water and nutrient absorption capacity, thereby reducing the negative impact of environmental changes on crop growth and increasing crop yield.
Approach-sourcematerial: This article reviews the crop-endophyte symbiotic system and its regulation of crop physiological processes under CO2 and drought stress.
Methodology: The paper reviews the crop-endophyte symbiotic system by discussing diversity in microbial communities, activation of host resistance, enhancement of environmental tolerance, and microbial antagonism against pathogens, emphasizing the system's roles in enhanced stomatal regulation, increased root absorption, and activated defense responses under elevated CO2 and drought conditions.

Paper Title: Global health costs of ambient PM2·5 from combustion sources: a modelling study supporting air pollution control strategies
Outcome(s) of Interest: Health costs, PM2.5, combustion sources, coal, liquid fuel, natural gas, solid biofuel, fossil fuels, air quality improvements
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Global long-term exposure to ambient PM2·5 from combustion sources imposed US$1·1 (95% uncertainty interval 0·8–1·5) trillion in health costs in 2019, accounting for 56% of the total health costs from all PM2·5 sources. ... the share of health costs for total coal was 36% higher than the source's contributions to corresponding PM2·5 concentrations and the share of health costs for liquid fuel and natural gas was 12% higher. ... South Asia was expected to show 16% greater relative health benefits than the percentage reduction in PM2·5 from the abatement of solid biofuel emissions.
Drivers of Risk: Combustion sources (coal, liquid fuel, natural gas, solid biofuel, agricultural waste burning, other fires); PM2.5 exposure; Non-linear relationship between PM2.5 concentration and health costs; Fossil fuel usage; Source-specific health costs.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "Climate actions targeting combustion sources can generate large ancillary health benefits via associated air-quality improvements...Global long-term exposure to ambient PM2·5 from combustion sources imposed US$1·1 (95% uncertainty interval 0·8–1·5) trillion in health costs in 2019, accounting for 56% of the total health costs from all PM2·5 sources...we observed a higher share of health costs from combustion sources compared to their contribution to population-weighted PM2·5 concentration...This disparity was primarily attributed to the non-linear relationship between PM2·5 concentration and its associated health costs...Specifically, the share of health costs for total coal was 36% higher than the source's contributions to corresponding PM2·5 concentrations and the share of health costs for liquid fuel and natural gas was 12% higher...Mitigation efforts designed according to source-specific health costs can more effectively avoid health costs than strategies that depend solely on the source contributions to overall PM2·5 concentration."
Category-sourcematerial: Background: Climate actions targeting combustion sources can generate large ancillary health benefits via associated air-quality improvements. Therefore, understanding the health costs associated with ambient fine particulate matter (PM2·5) from combustion sources can guide policy design for both air pollution and climate mitigation efforts.
Approach-sourcematerial: Methods: In this modelling study, we estimated the health costs attributable to ambient PM2·5 from six major combustion sources across 204 countries using updated concentration–response models and an age-adjusted valuation method.
Methodology: Health costs attributable to ambient PM2.5 from six major combustion sources across 204 countries in 2019 were estimated using updated concentration–response models and an age-adjusted valuation method; major combustion sources were defined as total coal, liquid fuel and natural gas, solid biofuel, agricultural waste burning, other fires, and 50% of the anthropogenic fugitive, combustion, and industrial dust source.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Methods: In this modelling study, we estimated the health costs attributable to ambient PM2·5 from six major combustion sources across 204 countries using updated concentration–response models and an age-adjusted valuation method. We defined major combustion sources as the sum of total coal, liquid fuel and natural gas, solid biofuel, agricultural waste burning, other fires, and 50% of the anthropogenic fugitive, combustion, and industrial dust source.
Highlights: Understanding health costs linked to ambient PM2·5 from combustion sources, this study reveals that phasing out fossil fuels offers 23% higher health benefits relative to their share of PM2·5 reductions; targeting these sources in climate actions, especially fossil fuels and solid biofuels, may yield significant health advantages, emphasizing tailored mitigation strategies over general concentration reduction.

Paper Title: Characterizing the multisectoral impacts of future global hydrologic variability
Outcome(s) of Interest: Uncertainty, water supply, multisector impacts, hydrologic variability, water and agricultural sectors, regional impacts, unsustainable water use, irrigation variability, Indus River basin.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "It is critical to characterize the potential impacts of uncertainty in future water supply given its importance for food and energy production... Impacts are felt most strongly in the water and agricultural sectors for basins that are expected to have unsustainable water use in the future, such as the Indus River basin. For this basin, we find that the variability in future irrigation water withdrawals and irrigated cropland increase over time due to uncertainty in renewable water supply."
Drivers of Risk: Uncertain climate, socioeconomic change, land use change, variability of hydrologic processes.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: There is significant uncertainty in how global water supply will evolve in the future, due to uncertain climate, socioeconomic, and land use change drivers and variability of hydrologic processes.
Category-sourcematerial: There is significant uncertainty in how global water supply will evolve in the future...It is critical to characterize the potential impacts of uncertainty in future water supply...we introduce a framework that integrates stochastic hydrology and human-environmental systems to characterize uncertainty in future water supply and its multisector impacts...We develop a global stochastic watershed model...to explore the impacts of runoff variability on the water and agricultural sectors across spatial scales...Impacts are felt most strongly in the water and agricultural sectors for basins that are expected to have unsustainable water use in the future, such as the Indus River basin...we find that the variability in future irrigation water withdrawals and irrigated cropland increase over time due to uncertainty in renewable water supply.
Approach-sourcematerial: We develop a global stochastic watershed model and demonstrate that this model can generate a large ensemble of realizations of basin-scale runoff with global coverage that preserves the mean, variance, and spatial correlation of a historical benchmark. We couple this model with a well-known human-environmental systems model to explore the impacts of runoff variability on the water and agricultural sectors across spatial scales.
Methodology: A framework integrating stochastic hydrology and human-environmental systems is used to characterize uncertainty in future water supply and its multisector impacts; a global stochastic watershed model generates an ensemble of basin-scale runoff realizations, preserving historical characteristics, and is coupled with a human-environmental systems model to explore runoff variability impacts on water and agricultural sectors across spatial scales.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "In this work, we introduce a framework that integrates stochastic hydrology and human-environmental systems to characterize uncertainty in future water supply and its multisector impacts. We develop a global stochastic watershed model and demonstrate that this model can generate a large ensemble of realizations of basin-scale runoff with global coverage that preserves the mean, variance, and spatial correlation of a historical benchmark. We couple this model with a well-known human-environmental systems model to explore the impacts of runoff variability on the water and agricultural sectors across spatial scales."
Highlights: A framework integrating stochastic hydrology and human-environmental systems characterizes uncertainty in future water supply and its multisector impacts, revealing that impacts of hydrologic variability vary across sectors and regions, particularly affecting unsustainable water use basins like the Indus, where irrigation water withdrawals and irrigated cropland variability increase due to uncertain renewable water supply; the stochastic ensemble can also explore global multisector consequences of local extreme runoff conditions.
Highlights-sourcematerial: There is significant uncertainty in how global water supply will evolve in the future, due to uncertain climate, socioeconomic, and land use change drivers and variability of hydrologic processes. It is critical to characterize the potential impacts of uncertainty in future water supply given its importance for food and energy production... We couple this model with a well-known human-environmental systems model to explore the impacts of runoff variability on the water and agricultural sectors across spatial scales. Paper Title: Statistical Methods for Analyzing Economic Impacts of Climate Change
Outcome(s) of Interest: Annual GDP decrease, agricultural productivity decline, increased healthcare costs, increased disaster-related costs.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: The analysis shows that by 2050, climate change might cause a yearly decrease of around 2% in global GDP, amounting to a possible loss of $2.5 trillion. It is anticipated that agricultural productivity will decrease by 8%, leading to an annual loss of $150 billion. Healthcare expenses linked to illnesses caused by climate change could rise by 12%, leading to an extra $200 billion in spending. Projected to increase by 20%, disaster-related costs are expected to grow by an additional $250 billion, increasing the annual economic burden.
Drivers of Risk: Agricultural output changes, health outcomes, infrastructure vulnerability, disaster-related costs.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "...affecting worldwide economies by changing agricultural output, health results, and infrastructure strength...agricultural productivity will decrease by 8%...Healthcare expenses linked to illnesses caused by climate change could rise by 12%...Projected to increase by 20%, disaster-related costs are expected to grow..."

Approach-sourcematerial: We used a wide-ranging data set from 1990 to 2020 to evaluate the economic effects of climate change through the use of multiple regression analysis, time-series forecasting, and econometric modeling. Important factors consist of GDP, agricultural production, healthcare expenditures, and costs associated with disasters.
Methodology: Utilizes multiple regression analysis, time-series forecasting, econometric modeling, and Monte Carlo simulations on data from 1990 to 2020 to assess climate change's economic impacts on sectors like GDP, agriculture, healthcare, and disaster-related costs.

Paper Title: Climate change can impair bacterial pathogen defences in sablefish via hypoxia-mediated effects on adaptive immunity
Outcome(s) of Interest: Hypoxia impairs adaptive immunity (IgM response) in sablefish following bacterial antigen exposure, despite sablefish's natural hypoxia tolerance.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: We show that innate immune parameters were typically elevated in response to the bacterial antigens, but were not substantially affected by hypoxia. In contrast, hypoxia completely prevented the ∼1.5-fold increase in blood IgM level that was observed under normoxic conditions following bacterin exposure, implying a serious impairment of adaptive immunity. Since the sablefish is naturally hypoxia tolerant, our results demonstrate that climate change-related deoxygenation may be a serious threat to the immune competency of fishes.
Drivers of Risk: Global warming; eutrophication; deoxygenation.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Low-oxygen levels (hypoxia) in aquatic habitats are becoming more common because of global warming and eutrophication... climate change-related deoxygenation may be a serious threat to the immune competency of fishes.
Category-sourcematerial: Low-oxygen levels (hypoxia) in aquatic habitats are becoming more common because of global warming and eutrophication... the effects on the health/disease status of fishes, the world's largest group of vertebrates, are unclear... we assessed how long-term hypoxia affected the immune function of sablefish, an ecologically and economically important North Pacific species... Since the sablefish is naturally hypoxia tolerant, our results demonstrate that climate change-related deoxygenation may be a serious threat to the immune competency of fishes. © 2024 Elsevier Ltd
Approach-sourcematerial: Sablefish were held at normoxia or hypoxia (100% or 40% air saturated seawater, respectively) for 6–16 weeks, while we measured a diverse array of immunological traits.
Methodology: Species-specific methods were developed, including qPCR primers for 16 immune genes, assays for blood antibacterial defenses, ELISA for blood IgM levels, and flow cytometry/confocal microscopy.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Given that the sablefish is a non-model organism, this involved the development of a species-specific methodological toolbox comprised of qPCR primers for 16 key immune genes, assays for blood antibacterial defences, the assessment of blood immunoglobulin (IgM) levels with ELISA, and flow cytometry and confocal microscopy techniques.
Highlights: Long-term hypoxia impairs adaptive immunity in sablefish, specifically preventing the normal increase in IgM levels after vaccination, despite the species' natural hypoxia tolerance, suggesting climate change-related deoxygenation poses a threat to fish immune competency.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "hypoxia completely prevented the ∼1.5-fold increase in blood IgM level that was observed under normoxic conditions following bacterin exposure, implying a serious impairment of adaptive immunity" and "Since the sablefish is naturally hypoxia tolerant, our results demonstrate that climate change-related deoxygenation may be a serious threat to the immune competency of fishes."
More Notes: The study highlights that long-term hypoxia, exacerbated by climate change, disrupts the adaptive immune response in sablefish, evidenced by the inhibited increase in blood IgM levels after bacterial antigen exposure, indicating a potential threat to fish immune health due to deoxygenation.
More Notes-sourcematerial: "hypoxia completely prevented the ∼1.5-fold increase in blood IgM level that was observed under normoxic conditions following bacterin exposure, implying a serious impairment of adaptive immunity...climate change-related deoxygenation may be a serious threat to the immune competency of fishes."
Hazard definition-sourcematerial: Low-oxygen levels (hypoxia) in aquatic habitats are becoming more common because of global warming and eutrophication... sablefish were held at normoxia or hypoxia (100% or 40% air saturated seawater, respectively) for 6–16 weeks... our results demonstrate that climate change-related deoxygenation may be a serious threat to the immune competency of fishes.
Impact persistence-sourcematerial: Since the sablefish is naturally hypoxia tolerant, our results demonstrate that climate change-related deoxygenation may be a serious threat to the immune competency of fishes.
Space disaggregation-sourcematerial: Sablefish, an ecologically and economically important North Pacific species...Sablefish were held at normoxia or hypoxia (100% or 40% air saturated seawater, respectively) for 6–16 weeks...
Impact interactions-sourcematerial: There is no evidence of interactions between impacts being considered in the abstract.
Economic dynamics-sourcematerial: Low-oxygen levels (hypoxia) in aquatic habitats are becoming more common because of global warming and eutrophication. However, the effects on the health/disease status of fishes, the world's largest group of vertebrates, are unclear. Therefore, we assessed how long-term hypoxia affected the immune function of sablefish, an ecologically and economically important North Pacific species, including the response to a formalin-killed Aeromonas salmonicida bacterin. Sablefish were held at normoxia or hypoxia (100% or 40% air saturated seawater, respectively) for 6–16 weeks, while we measured a diverse array of immunological traits. Given that the sablefish is a non-model organism, this involved the development of a species-specific methodological toolbox comprised of qPCR primers for 16 key immune genes, assays for blood antibacterial defences, the assessment of blood immunoglobulin (IgM) levels with ELISA, and flow cytometry and confocal microscopy techniques. We show that innate immune parameters were typically elevated in response to the bacterial antigens, but were not substantially affected by hypoxia. In contrast, hypoxia completely prevented the ∼1.5-fold increase in blood IgM level that was observed under normoxic conditions following bacterin exposure, implying a serious impairment of adaptive immunity. Since the sablefish is naturally hypoxia tolerant, our results demonstrate that climate change-related deoxygenation may be a serious threat to the immune competency of fishes. © 2024 Elsevier Ltd
Climate features-sourcematerial: The abstract focuses on the effects of hypoxia (low oxygen levels) on the immune function of sablefish. It does not mention or allude to climate tipping points or other complex climate phenomena beyond the direct impact of deoxygenation.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is not explicitly considered in this study. The paper focuses on the negative impacts of climate change-related deoxygenation (hypoxia) on the immune competency of sablefish, specifically the impairment of adaptive immunity. It does not investigate or discuss any adaptive mechanisms or potential adaptation strategies.
Adaptation considered-sourcematerial: "Since the sablefish is naturally hypoxia tolerant, our results demonstrate that climate change-related deoxygenation may be a serious threat to the immune competency of fishes." and the absence of any text mentioning adaptive strategies.

Paper Title: Deterioration of steel structures due to corrosion considering the global effects of climate change
Outcome(s) of Interest: Region-dependent corrosion rate changes; Increased corrosion in Australia, North America, Europe, and all oceans (especially Antarctic and Arctic); Decreased corrosion in Asia and Africa.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "The results indicate that the effect of climate change on the corrosion rate depends on the considered region. While regions such as Australia, North America and Europe see an increase, others such as Asia and Africa observe a decline. Notably, all oceans, especially the Antarctic and Arctic, experience a significant increase in corrosion by the end of the century."
Drivers of Risk: Changes in temperature, relative humidity, and wind speed due to climate change. Stochastic nature of atmospheric variables.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: The impact of climate variability on the stochastic nature of atmospheric variables, which greatly influence corrosion conditions... It considers the changes in environmental conditions, specifically temperature, relative humidity and wind speed and their effects on atmospheric corrosion.
Category-sourcematerial: "Climate change has the potential to alter the long-term characteristics of these factors over the lifespan of steel structures, both those already existing and those newly built." and "This paper introduces an integrated framework to quantify the impact of climate change on corrosion rates of steel structures worldwide."
Approach-sourcematerial: Global Climate Models are employed to evaluate the long-term impacts of climate change on these environmental conditions. An analytical model for predicting corrosion rate is integrated with climate change models to predict alterations in the corrosion rates of steel components relative to historical conditions.
Methodology: An integrated framework is used to quantify the impact of climate change on steel corrosion rates by combining Global Climate Models (to project changes in temperature, relative humidity, and wind speed) with an analytical corrosion rate prediction model, allowing comparison between historical data and projected conditions.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "This paper introduces an integrated framework to quantify the impact of climate change on corrosion rates of steel structures worldwide. It considers the changes in environmental conditions, specifically temperature, relative humidity and wind speed and their effects on atmospheric corrosion. Global Climate Models are employed to evaluate the long-term impacts of climate change on these environmental conditions. An analytical model for predicting corrosion rate is integrated with climate change models to predict alterations in the corrosion rates of steel components relative to historical conditions. This paper also discusses the impact of climate change on the variations of these climatic parameters and offers a comparison between historical data and projected conditions across the globe."
Highlights: Climate change impacts the corrosion rates of steel structures differently across the globe; increases are projected for Australia, North America, Europe, and all oceans (especially Antarctic and Arctic), while decreases are projected for Asia and Africa.
Highlights-sourcematerial: The results indicate that the effect of climate change on the corrosion rate depends on the considered region. Paper Title: INTERNATIONAL CONVERGENCE TOWARDS A CLIMATE-NEUTRAL ECONOMY: MODELING THE AGRICULTURAL SECTOR
Outcome(s) of Interest: Inertial and divergent trend towards decarbonization in agriculture, slowing down "net zero" progress; EU shows accelerated climate convergence, but weakening recently; decarbonization capacity is key for global convergence; proactive compliance with agreements, technology sharing, digitalisation, and green finance are important factors.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: The results of the study reveal an inertial and divergent trend of the agricultural economy towards decarbonisation, which slows down the overall movement towards “net zero” due to the presence of clubs of lagging agricultural countries that increase emissions in violation of international agreements. The EU is the most prominent example of accelerated climate convergence, but markers of its weakening in recent years are identified due to the inability of economies to maintain the pace of decarbonisation caused by economic constraints, technological barriers, policy and regulatory issues, and misunderstandings of climate neutrality goals. It is shown that the long-term decarbonisation capacity of the agricultural sector is the key trigger for a country to make a positive contribution to the global convergence towards climate neutrality. At the current stage, the pace of decarbonisation plays a much greater role for consolidating efforts in the agricultural economy and achieving climate neutrality than the initial level of emissions in the sector. Factors in this process include proactive compliance with global climate agreements, technology sharing and cooperation, digitalisation and smart agriculture, and green financing and investment.
Drivers of Risk: Inertial/divergent trends in agricultural decarbonization; lagging agricultural countries increasing emissions; inability of economies to maintain decarbonization pace (economic constraints, technological barriers, policy/regulatory issues, misunderstanding of climate neutrality goals).
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: The results of the study reveal an inertial and divergent trend of the agricultural economy towards decarbonisation, which slows down the overall movement towards “net zero” due to the presence of clubs of lagging agricultural countries that increase emissions in violation of international agreements. ... The EU is the most prominent example of accelerated climate convergence, but markers of its weakening in recent years are identified due to the inability of economies to maintain the pace of decarbonisation caused by economic constraints, technological barriers, policy and regulatory issues, and misunderstandings of climate neutrality goals.

"This research employs both qualitative and quantitative analyses to assess convergence measures in the agricultural sector’s carbon emissions." "The results of the study reveal an inertial and divergent trend of the agricultural economy towards decarbonisation, which slows down the overall movement towards “net zero” due to the presence of clubs of lagging agricultural countries that increase emissions in violation of international agreements." "The long-term decarbonisation capacity of the agricultural sector is the key trigger for a country to make a positive contribution to the global convergence towards climate neutrality."
Approach-sourcematerial: Methodology / approach. This research employs both qualitative and quantitative analyses to assess convergence measures in the agricultural sector’s carbon emissions. The quantitative component relies on the sigma and beta convergence models to evaluate international convergence in carbon emissions CO2 dynamics. Additionally, statistical indicators of variation, skewness, Gini and Theil indices were calculated for delineating global smoothness and the concentration of agricultural emissions.
Methodology: Employs both qualitative and quantitative analyses, using sigma and beta convergence models to assess international convergence in agricultural carbon emissions (CO2) dynamics, covering 194 countries globally and focusing on the EU27, UK, and Ukraine from 1972-2022, examining key UN conferences and agreements; also calculates statistical indicators like variation, skewness, Gini, and Theil indices.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Methodology / approach. This research employs both qualitative and quantitative analyses to assess convergence measures in the agricultural sector’s carbon emissions. The quantitative component relies on the sigma and beta convergence models to evaluate international convergence in carbon emissions CO2 dynamics. The dimensions of modeling are as follows: global sample within 194 countries with reliable emissions data; European Union, focusing on convergence within the EU27 member states, the United Kingdom, and Ukraine. The time period covers open data from 1972–2022. The dynamics of sigma and beta convergence is determined for the points, namely UN Stockholm Conference (1972), UN Rio Conference (1992), Kyoto Protocol (entry into force in 2005), Paris Agreement (2015). Additionally, statistical indicators of variation, skewness, Gini and Theil indices were calculated for delineating global smoothness and the concentration of agricultural emissions.
Highlights: Agricultural decarbonization shows an inertial and divergent trend globally, with lagging countries hindering "net zero" goals; recent emission skewness reduction indicates increased decarbonization efforts, though insufficient; the EU exemplifies climate convergence, but faces weakening due to economic and technological constraints; long-term decarbonization capacity is crucial for global convergence, driven by factors like proactive climate agreement compliance, technology sharing, and digital agriculture.
Highlights-sourcematerial: The results of the study reveal an inertial and divergent trend of the agricultural economy towards decarbonisation, which slows down the overall movement towards “net zero” due to the presence of clubs of lagging agricultural countries that increase emissions in violation of international agreements. The reduction in emissions skewness in recent years shows that more countries are “pulling” decarbonisation due to their high capacity to move towards net zero, but this is not enough. The EU is the most prominent example of accelerated climate convergence, but markers of its weakening in recent years are identified due to the inability of economies to maintain the pace of decarbonisation caused by economic constraints, technological barriers, policy and regulatory issues, and misunderstandings of climate neutrality goals. It is shown that the long-term decarbonisation capacity of the agricultural sector is the key trigger for a country to make a positive contribution to the global convergence towards climate neutrality. Factors in this process include proactive compliance with global climate agreements, technology sharing and cooperation, digitalisation and smart agriculture, and green financing and investment.

Factors in this process include proactive compliance with global climate agreements, technology sharing and cooperation, digitalisation and smart agriculture, and green financing and investment."

Paper Title: Modelling the combined impact of sea level rise, land subsidence, and tropical cyclones in compound flooding of coastal cities
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased flood inundation, land submergence, amplified by SLR and LS, particularly severe with high subsidence rates.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Compound flooding results in a negative effect, creating challenges for decision-making and coastal management... our findings indicated that the highest subsidence rate exceeds −20 mm/yr... the most severely affected by LS is projected to become submerged and completely inundated by 2100. We found that the combined impact of SLR and LS significantly amplified flood inundation and SLR is the primary amplification factor of flooding, projected to account for 46.2% by 2100.
Drivers of Risk: Sea-level rise (SLR), tropical cyclones (TCs), land subsidence (LS); SLR primary amplification factor; Compound flooding.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "Low-lying coastal areas are threatened worldwide by compound flooding effects, including sea-level rise (SLR), frequent tropical cyclones (TCs), and accelerated land subsidence (LS)." ... "We found that the combined impact of SLR and LS significantly amplified flood inundation and SLR is the primary amplification factor of flooding, projected to account for 46.2% by 2100." ... "the combined impact of flood hazards" ... "interactions among multi-driver factors of compound flooding"
Category-sourcematerial: To comprehend the precise consequences and the combined effect of LS and SLR on coastal cities and to inform management scientifically, we initially chose historical TCs (TC6311 and TC1415) that resulted in exceptionally high surges and significant losses as reference scenarios for simulating potential compound events. We then utilized a hydrodynamic model, which combines TCs, LS, and SLR, to simulate the combined impact of flood hazards.
Approach-sourcematerial: We then utilized a hydrodynamic model, which combines TCs, LS, and SLR, to simulate the combined impact of flood hazards.
Methodology: A hydrodynamic model was used to simulate the combined impact of tropical cyclones (TCs), land subsidence (LS), and sea-level rise (SLR) on coastal flooding, using historical TCs as reference scenarios and quantifying the individual and combined contributions of LS and SLR to flood inundation.
Methodology-sourcematerial: To comprehend the precise consequences and the combined effect of LS and SLR on coastal cities and to inform management scientifically, we initially chose historical TCs (TC6311 and TC1415) that resulted in exceptionally high surges and significant losses as reference scenarios for simulating potential compound events. Highlights: Compound flooding, exacerbated by land subsidence, significantly amplifies flood inundation in coastal areas, with sea-level rise being the primary driver of this amplification; a framework for assessing these interacting factors can aid future risk management.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "Compound flooding results in a negative effect...the combined impact of SLR and LS significantly amplified flood inundation and SLR is the primary amplification factor of flooding...The framework established in this study facilitates the quantitative assessment of the interactions among multi-driver factors of compound flooding, thereby serving as a supplementary and guiding tool for future risk management."

Paper Title: Forecasting the effect of extreme sea-level rise on financial market risk
Outcome(s) of Interest: Climate risk premium and cost (positive and negative effects) on financial and economic sectors.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: The main evidence shows that the different regional extreme MSLR measures forecast opposite effects, positive and negative, on financial and economic sectors over time, showing climate risk premium and cost, respectively.
Drivers of Risk: Sea-level rise; Extreme sea-level events; Regional MSLR (US, Europe, Australia); Climate risk (physical); Financial market risk.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Sea-level rise is one of the major risks of climate change. Based on the global and regional mean sea level rise (MSLR)... Furthermore, we analyze the connection between different regional extreme MSLR measures (US, Europe, and Australia), used as proxies of climate risk, and financial market risk in several sectors.
Category-sourcematerial: Based on the global and regional mean sea level rise (MSLR), we propose extreme sea-level value at rise (ExSLVaR) and extreme sea-level expected rise (ExSLER) measures to forecast extreme MSLR calculated for the 10-day time series from eight seas/oceans from December 1992 to October 2020. Furthermore, we analyze the connection between different regional extreme MSLR measures (US, Europe, and Australia), used as proxies of climate risk, and financial market risk in several sectors.
Approach-sourcematerial: Based on the global and regional mean sea level rise (MSLR), we propose extreme sea-level value at rise (ExSLVaR) and extreme sea-level expected rise (ExSLER) measures to forecast extreme MSLR calculated for the 10-day time series from eight seas/oceans from December 1992 to October 2020. Furthermore, we analyze the connection between different regional extreme MSLR measures (US, Europe, and Australia), used as proxies of climate risk, and financial market risk in several sectors.
Methodology: The study proposes 'extreme sea-level value at rise' (ExSLVaR) and 'extreme sea-level expected rise' (ExSLER) measures, calculated from 10-day time series data (Dec 1992-Oct 2020) from eight seas/oceans, to forecast extreme mean sea level rise (MSLR).
Methodology-sourcematerial: Based on the global and regional mean sea level rise (MSLR), we propose extreme sea-level value at rise (ExSLVaR) and extreme sea-level expected rise (ExSLER) measures to forecast extreme MSLR calculated for the 10-day time series from eight seas/oceans from December 1992 to October 2020.
Highlights: Extreme sea-level rise measures (ExSLVaR and ExSLER) forecast both positive (climate risk premium in insurance) and negative (risk cost in oil & gas) impacts on financial sectors, indicating the need for mitigation strategies in policies, regulations, and investment portfolios.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "The main evidence shows that the different regional extreme MSLR measures forecast opposite effects, positive and negative, on financial and economic sectors over time, showing climate risk premium and cost, respectively. Specifically, the insurance sector presents the highest risk premium, and the oil and gas sector the highest risk cost. These measures are relevant for policymakers, regulators, and investors seeking strategies to mitigate future physical and transition risk while accounting for such risk in policies, regulations, and portfolio allocation."
More Notes: Extreme sea-level rise forecasts, measured as ExSLVaR and ExSLER using 1992-2020 data, are used to analyze the impact of climate risk on financial markets, revealing both risk premiums (e.g., in insurance) and risk costs (e.g., in oil and gas) across different sectors and regions, with implications for policymakers and investors.
More Notes-sourcematerial: The abstract discusses the development of ExSLVaR and ExSLER measures to forecast extreme sea-level rise, analyzes their connection with financial market risks across sectors and regions, and highlights the effects on sector-specific risk premiums and costs, noting the insurance and oil and gas sectors specifically.
Hazard definition-sourcematerial: Sea-level rise is one of the major risks of climate change. Based on the global and regional mean sea level rise (MSLR), we propose extreme sea-level value at rise (ExSLVaR) and extreme sea-level expected rise (ExSLER) measures to forecast extreme MSLR...
Impact persistence-sourcematerial: The abstract focuses on forecasting extreme sea-level rise and its impact on financial sectors. It does not discuss the persistence of impacts, adaptation strategies, or permanent changes.
Space disaggregation-sourcematerial: Based on the global and regional mean sea level rise (MSLR), we propose extreme sea-level value at rise (ExSLVaR) and extreme sea-level expected rise (ExSLER) measures to forecast extreme MSLR calculated for the 10-day time series from eight seas/oceans from December 1992 to October 2020. Furthermore, we analyze the connection between different regional extreme MSLR measures (US, Europe, and Australia), used as proxies of climate risk, and financial market risk in several sectors.

It doesn't mention interactions between different climate impacts; rather, it explores the relationship between a single climate impact (sea-level rise) and economic sectors.
