Paper Title: Daily DeepCropNet: A hierarchical deep learning approach with daily time series of vegetation indices and climatic variables for corn yield estimation
Outcome(s) of Interest: Accurate corn yield estimation; improved accuracy under normal and extreme climate conditions; potential for capturing short-duration stresses; identification of key growth stage (silking).
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: The DDCN model provided the highest accuracy for end-of-season yield estimation under normal climate conditions in 2018–2020 (RMSE = 0.86 Mg ha−1) and extreme heat and drought conditions in 2012 (RMSE = 1.17 Mg ha−1)...Further analyses indicated that the DDCN model had the potential for capturing the short-duration stresses...We also found that the DDCN model identified the silking phase as the key growth stage...
Drivers of Risk: Climate variability, extreme heat, drought, short-duration stresses, temporal fluctuations, vegetation indices.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: The DDCN model provided the highest accuracy for end-of-season yield estimation under normal climate conditions in 2018–2020 (RMSE = 0.86 Mg ha−1) and extreme heat and drought conditions in 2012 (RMSE = 1.17 Mg ha−1). Further analyses indicated that the DDCN model had the potential for capturing the short-duration stresses. The input analysis showed that the daily time series of climatic variables provided more temporal fluctuations than weekly aggregated inputs. By evaluating the contributions of different variables, we found that vegetation indices yield higher impact than climatic variables for yield estimation under both normal and stressful climate conditions.
Category-sourcematerial: Accurate large-scale crop yield estimation under climate variability is essential to understanding the dynamics of global food security... a hierarchical deep learning model, named Daily DeepCropNet (DDCN), has been developed to process daily time series of satellite-derived vegetation indices and climatic variables for corn yield estimation... The experiment was conducted at county level over the US Corn Belt from 2006 to 2020... the DDCN model provided the highest accuracy for end-of-season yield estimation...
Approach-sourcematerial: "In this study, a hierarchical deep learning model, named Daily DeepCropNet (DDCN), has been developed to process daily time series of satellite-derived vegetation indices and climatic variables for corn yield estimation. The DDCN model was a three-level pyramid structure that incorporated the corn phenology durations to extract crop growth patterns from the daily inputs. The Transformer and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) were implemented as a temporal filter in each level to capture the short-term crop growth patterns."
Methodology: A hierarchical deep learning model (Daily DeepCropNet or DDCN) was developed, utilizing a three-level pyramid structure incorporating corn phenology durations to process daily time series of satellite-derived vegetation indices and climatic variables for corn yield estimation; the model employs Transformer and LSTM as temporal filters at each level to capture short-term crop growth patterns and hierarchically transfers patterns from weekly to growing season aggregation to learn long-term dependencies.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "In this study, a hierarchical deep learning model, named Daily DeepCropNet (DDCN), has been developed to process daily time series of satellite-derived vegetation indices and climatic variables for corn yield estimation. The DDCN model was a three-level pyramid structure that incorporated the corn phenology durations to extract crop growth patterns from the daily inputs. The Transformer and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) were implemented as a temporal filter in each level to capture the short-term crop growth patterns. The temporal patterns were hierarchically transferred from weekly to growing season aggregation to learn long-term dependency."
Highlights: A hierarchical deep learning model (DDCN) effectively estimates corn yield using daily time series data, outperforming LSTM, Transformer, and Random Forest models, particularly in normal and extreme climate conditions, by capturing short-duration stresses and identifying the silking phase as key; vegetation indices are more impactful than climatic variables for yield estimation.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "The DDCN model provided the highest accuracy for end-of-season yield estimation under normal climate conditions in 2018–2020 (RMSE = 0.86 Mg ha−1) and extreme heat and drought conditions in 2012 (RMSE = 1.17 Mg ha−1)...Further analyses indicated that the DDCN model had the potential for capturing the short-duration stresses...We also found that the DDCN model identified the silking phase as the key growth stage and achieved higher accuracy than other models for in-season yield estimation...vegetation indices yield higher impact than climatic variables for yield estimation under both normal and stressful climate conditions."
More Notes: A hierarchical deep learning model (DDCN) using daily satellite and climate data accurately estimates corn yield in the US Corn Belt, even under extreme conditions, outperforming other models and highlighting the importance of vegetation indices and the silking phase for accurate in-season predictions.
More Notes-sourcematerial: "a hierarchical deep learning model, named Daily DeepCropNet (DDCN), has been developed to process daily time series of satellite-derived vegetation indices and climatic variables for corn yield estimation...The DDCN model provided the highest accuracy for end-of-season yield estimation under normal climate conditions in 2018–2020 (RMSE = 0.86 Mg ha−1) and extreme heat and drought conditions in 2012 (RMSE = 1.17 Mg ha−1)...the DDCN model identified the silking phase as the key growth stage and achieved higher accuracy than other models for in-season yield estimation...vegetation indices yield higher impact than climatic variables for yield estimation under both normal and stressful climate conditions."
Hazard definition-sourcematerial: The DDCN model provided the highest accuracy for end-of-season yield estimation under normal climate conditions in 2018–2020 (RMSE = 0.86 Mg ha−1) and extreme heat and drought conditions in 2012 (RMSE = 1.17 Mg ha−1).
Impact persistence-sourcematerial: "The temporal patterns were hierarchically transferred from weekly to growing season aggregation to learn long-term dependency." and "Further analyses indicated that the DDCN model had the potential for capturing the short-duration stresses."
Space disaggregation-sourcematerial: The experiment was conducted at county level over the US Corn Belt from 2006 to 2020.
Impact interactions-sourcematerial: The abstract focuses on developing a deep learning model (DDCN) to improve corn yield estimation by processing daily time series data of vegetation indices and climatic variables. It primarily addresses the challenge of capturing short-term and long-term crop responses to climate variability, particularly under normal and stressful conditions like heat and drought. There is no mention of interactions between different impact types (e.g., simultaneous heat and drought events exacerbating impacts), spatial interactions (e.g., effects in one region influencing another), or sectoral interactions (e.g., impacts on agriculture affecting water resources or energy).
Climate features-sourcematerial: There is no mention of tipping points or climate complexities.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is not directly addressed in the abstract. The focus is on improving crop yield estimation under climate variability, particularly extreme events, using a deep learning model (DDCN). The model's ability to capture short-duration stresses and identify key growth stages could *potentially* inform adaptation strategies, but this is not explicitly stated.
Adaptation considered-sourcematerial: "Accurate large-scale crop yield estimation under climate variability is essential to understanding the dynamics of global food security...The DDCN model provided the highest accuracy for end-of-season yield estimation under normal climate conditions...and extreme heat and drought conditions...Further analyses indicated that the DDCN model had the potential for capturing the short-duration stresses...The DDCN model could be further integrated with the process-based model to achieve the fusion of knowledge and data for explainable AI in agricultural system modeling."

Paper Title: UQAM-TCW: A Global Hybrid Tropical Cyclone Wind Model Based Upon Statistical and Coupled Climate Models
Outcome(s) of Interest: Quantifying financial impacts, storm simulation, risk assessment, cyclogenesis, wind structure, ENSO effects, return periods, hazard maps, climate and financial applications.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: It is therefore of high societal value to synthetically simulate TC tracks and winds to assess potential impacts along with their probability distributions for example, land use planning and financial risk management...The model includes frequency and location of cyclogenesis, full trajectories with maximum sustained winds and the entire wind structure along each track for the six typical cyclogenesis basins...Being an important driver of TCs globally, we also integrate ENSO effects in key components of the model...As an application of the model, we show global hazard maps for direct and indirect hits expressed in terms of return periods...The global TC model can be of interest to climate and environmental scientists, economists and financial risk managers.
Drivers of Risk: ENSO effects; Cyclogenesis frequency and location; TC trajectories and intensity (maximum sustained winds); Wind structure; Historical risk features; Climate model large ensemble.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Integrating statistical and physical methods, the model is probabilistic and consistent with the physics of how TCs develop. The model includes frequency and location of cyclogenesis, full trajectories with maximum sustained winds and the entire wind structure along each track for the six typical cyclogenesis basins from IBTrACS. Being an important driver of TCs globally, we also integrate ENSO effects in key components of the model. ...the model melds statistical modeling, to capture historical risk features, with a climate model large ensemble, to generate large samples of physically coherent TC seasons.
Category-sourcematerial: Tropical cyclones (TCs) are among the most destructive natural hazards and yet, quantifying their financial impacts remains a significant methodological challenge. ... As an application of the model, we show global hazard maps for direct and indirect hits expressed in terms of return periods.
Approach-sourcematerial: "We present a global TC model (the UQAM-TCW model thereafter) that melds statistical modeling, to capture historical risk features, with a climate model large ensemble, to generate large samples of physically coherent TC seasons. Integrating statistical and physical methods, the model is probabilistic and consistent with the physics of how TCs develop."
Methodology: The UQAM-TCW model generates synthetic tropical cyclone (TC) tracks by integrating statistical modeling of historical risk features with a climate model large ensemble to produce physically coherent TC seasons, including cyclogenesis frequency and location, full trajectories with maximum sustained winds and wind structure, and incorporating ENSO effects.
Methodology-sourcematerial: The UQAM-TCW model melds statistical modeling, to capture historical risk features, with a climate model large ensemble, to generate large samples of physically coherent TC seasons. The model includes frequency and location of cyclogenesis, full trajectories with maximum sustained winds and the entire wind structure along each track for the six typical cyclogenesis basins from IBTrACS. ... We also integrate ENSO effects in key components of the model.
Highlights: Presents a global tropical cyclone model (UQAM-TCW) that integrates statistical modeling with climate model ensembles to generate physically coherent TC seasons, incorporating ENSO effects and providing hazard maps for direct and indirect hits expressed in terms of return periods.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Tropical cyclones (TCs) are among the most destructive natural hazards and yet, quantifying their financial impacts remains a significant methodological challenge...We present a global TC model (the UQAM-TCW model thereafter) that melds statistical modeling, to capture historical risk features, with a climate model large ensemble, to generate large samples of physically coherent TC seasons...Being an important driver of TCs globally, we also integrate ENSO effects in key components of the model...As an application of the model, we show global hazard maps for direct and indirect hits expressed in terms of return periods.
More Notes: The UQAM-TCW global TC model integrates statistical and physical methodologies to simulate tropical cyclone (TC) tracks and winds, incorporating ENSO effects and providing probabilistic hazard maps useful for climate impact assessments and financial risk analysis.
More Notes-sourcematerial: "We present a global TC model (the UQAM-TCW model thereafter) that melds statistical modeling, to capture historical risk features, with a climate model large ensemble, to generate large samples of physically coherent TC seasons...Being an important driver of TCs globally, we also integrate ENSO effects in key components of the model...As an application of the model, we show global hazard maps for direct and indirect hits expressed in terms of return periods. The global TC model can be of interest to climate and environmental scientists, economists and financial risk managers."

Paper Title: Hydroponics: Exploring innovative sustainable technologies and applications across crop production, with Emphasis on potato mini-tuber cultivation
Outcome(s) of Interest: Resilient potato production; mitigates land suitability loss; high-efficiency mini tuber production; sustainable food supply; water/energy/space savings.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Hydroponics reduces dependency on agricultural land and pesticides, mitigating negative effects of extreme weather events; a promising solution to the massive production of potato mini tuber at high efficiency; enduring significant reforms in soilless food production systems can address food supply; hydroponics system provides better yield and crop productivity by saving water, energy, and space.
Drivers of Risk: Climate change, land suitability loss, yield loss; drought; unpredictable weather; contaminated water; nutrient imbalances (K); root rot; technical failure; algal growth; harsh environments; system failure (water, nutrients).
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Climatic changes predicted to cause loss in land suitability for potato production, leading to yield loss. Traditional soil-based agriculture faces challenges from drought, unpredictable weather, and contaminated water sources. Nutrient imbalances may occur in closed hydroponic systems if not maintained appropriately. Specifically, plants absorb significant potassium (K) amount from the nutrient solution, resulting in a disproportion in the solution's potassium content. The primary limitations of the ebb and flow system include root rot and crop loss due to technical failure, and its susceptibility to algal growth. The system addresses challenging potato cultivation environments such as deserts, cold steep terrains, and coastal regions. Harsh environments such as hilly areas too high to cultivate, concrete school playgrounds, and arctic settlements. Since the hydroponics system utilizes only water and nutrient solution without the involvement of soil, any failure or problem in the nutrient distribution, water pump, or nozzle clogging will lead to rapid death of the growing plants.

The study examined electrical conductivity on nutrient intake and growth, assessing optimal electrical conductivities and planting densities for different potato cultivars.

Paper Title: Snow Water Equivalent Monitoring—A Review of Large-Scale Remote Sensing Applications
Outcome(s) of Interest: Changes in snow patterns; impacts on water management, agriculture, winter tourism, environmental stability, migration, cultural shifts; SWE decrease in Northern Hemisphere.

Approach-sourcematerial: This review explores the potential of remote sensing in monitoring snow water equivalent (SWE) on a large scale, analyzing 164 selected publications from 2000 to 2023. Categorized by methodology and content, the analysis reveals a growing interest in the topic, with a concentration of research in North America and China. Methodologically, there is a shift from passive microwave (PMW) inversion algorithms to artificial intelligence (AI), particularly the Random Forest (RF) and neural network (NN) approaches.
Methodology: Methodologically, research on snow water equivalent (SWE) is shifting from passive microwave (PMW) inversion algorithms to artificial intelligence (AI), particularly Random Forest (RF) and neural network (NN) approaches, with most studies integrating PMW data with auxiliary information.

Highlights: Remote sensing of snow water equivalent (SWE) is increasingly used, with a methodological shift towards AI like Random Forest and neural networks, predominantly in North America and China; long-term studies suggest a general SWE decrease in the Northern Hemisphere with regional variations, and future research should address PMW data issues, downsampling, interdisciplinary studies, and forecasting.

Finally, the review suggests potential future SWE research directions such as addressing PMW data issues, downsampling for detailed analyses, conducting interdisciplinary studies, and incorporating forecasting to enable more widespread applications.

Impact persistence-sourcematerial: Snow plays a crucial role in the global water cycle, providing water to over 20% of the world’s population and serving as a vital component for flora, fauna, and climate regulation. Changes in snow patterns due to global warming have far-reaching impacts on water management, agriculture, and other economic sectors such as winter tourism. Additionally, they have implications for environmental stability, prompting migration and cultural shifts in snow-dependent communities. Accurate information on snow and its variables is, thus, essential for both scientific understanding and societal planning. This review explores the potential of remote sensing in monitoring snow water equivalent (SWE) on a large scale, analyzing 164 selected publications from 2000 to 2023. Categorized by methodology and content, the analysis reveals a growing interest in the topic, with a concentration of research in North America and China. Methodologically, there is a shift from passive microwave (PMW) inversion algorithms to artificial intelligence (AI), particularly the Random Forest (RF) and neural network (NN) approaches. A majority of studies integrate PMW data with auxiliary information, focusing thematically on remote sensing and snow research, with limited incorporation into broader environmental contexts. Long-term studies (>30 years) suggest a general decrease in SWE in the Northern Hemisphere, though regional and seasonal variations exist. Finally, the review suggests potential future SWE research directions such as addressing PMW data issues, downsampling for detailed analyses, conducting interdisciplinary studies, and incorporating forecasting to enable more widespread applications. © 2024 by the authors.

Paper Title: Growth impact of climate change and response policies: The advanced climate change long-term (ACCL) model
Outcome(s) of Interest: GDP damage due to climate change; GDP impact of mitigation measures; Total Factor Productivity (TFP) impact.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: This paper provides a tool to build climate change scenarios to forecast Gross Domestic Product (GDP), modelling both GDP damage due to climate change and the GDP impact of mitigating measures...Compared to other models, it provides a comprehensive modelisation of Total Factor Productivity (TFP), which is the most significant determinant of the GDP projected path.

Approach-sourcematerial: This paper provides a tool to build climate change scenarios to forecast Gross Domestic Product (GDP), modelling both GDP damage due to climate change and the GDP impact of mitigating measures. It adopts a supply-side, long-term view, with 2060 and 2100 horizons. It is a global projection tool (30 countries/regions)... Compared to other models, it provides a comprehensive modelisation of Total Factor Productivity (TFP), which is the most significant determinant of the GDP projected path.
Methodology: The paper employs a global projection tool modeling GDP impacts due to climate change and mitigation via a supply-side, long-term approach with horizons in 2060 and 2100, considering five energy types by CO2 emissions and providing comprehensive TFP modelization, allowing calibration with estimated or literature-based defaults, and contrasting energy policy scenarios to advocate for strategic long-term policy frameworks.
Methodology-sourcematerial: This paper provides a tool to build climate change scenarios to forecast Gross Domestic Product (GDP), modelling both GDP damage due to climate change and the GDP impact of mitigating measures. It adopts a supply-side, long-term view, with 2060 and 2100 horizons. It is a global projection tool (30 countries/regions), with assumptions and results both at the world and the country/regional level. Five different types of energy inputs are taken into account according to their CO2 emission factors. Full calibration is possible at each stage, with estimated or literature-based default parameters. Compared to other models, it provides a comprehensive modelisation of Total Factor Productivity (TFP)... We present simulation results of different energy policy scenarios.
Highlights: Develops a global projection tool to forecast GDP impacts from climate change (damage and mitigation) for 30 countries/regions, focusing on Total Factor Productivity, energy inputs, and long-term (2060, 2100) supply-side economics, highlighting the need for long-run policy frameworks and international cooperation.
Highlights-sourcematerial: The paper provides a tool to build climate change scenarios to forecast Gross Domestic Product (GDP), modelling both GDP damage due to climate change and the GDP impact of mitigating measures. It adopts a supply-side, long-term view, with 2060 and 2100 horizons. It is a global projection tool (30 countries/regions), with assumptions and results both at the world and the country/regional level. Five different types of energy inputs are taken into account according to their CO2 emission factors. Compared to other models, it provides a comprehensive modelisation of Total Factor Productivity (TFP), which is the most significant determinant of the GDP projected path. We present simulation results of different energy policy scenarios. They illustrate both the tragedy of the horizon and the tragedy of the commons, which call for a policy framework that adequately integrates a long run perspective, through a low-enough discount rate and an effective intergenerational solidarity as well as international cooperation.

Paper Title: Turning climate justice into practice? Channeling loss and damage funding through national social protection systems in climate-vulnerable countries
Outcome(s) of Interest: Efficient distribution of Loss and Damage funding through social protection systems; safeguarding development gains; addressing noneconomic loss and damage (NELD); dismantling soft adaptation barriers; fostering climate resilience; aligning with human rights, climate justice, and Common But Differentiated Responsibilities.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: With rapid-onset climate impacts intensifying and slow-onset impacts further manifesting, loss and damage from climate change is already occurring. Thus, quick, effective, and transparent distribution of upcoming funds is necessary. As increasing climate impacts threaten to reverse development gains of the last decades, climate-sensitive social protection has received increasing attention for its potential to address climate impacts and to strengthen the adaptive capacity and resilience of climate-vulnerable populations. This review article explores the prospects of channeling Loss and Damage funding through existing national social protection systems and highlights how this approach can efficiently contribute to safeguarding development gains, including previously overlooked aspects such as noneconomic loss and damage (NELD), while also dismantling soft adaptation barriers and thus fostering climate resilience in the long term. Although we identify barriers, including gaps in coverage of social protection systems both between and within countries, we argue that channeling some L&D funding through social protection systems aligns with core human rights and climate justice agendas, as well as the principle of Common But Differentiated Responsibilities and Respective Capabilities.
Drivers of Risk: Rapid-onset and slow-onset climate impacts; insufficient climate finance reaching affected communities; buckling humanitarian aid and development systems; reversal of development gains.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: With rapid-onset climate impacts intensifying and slow-onset impacts further manifesting, loss and damage from climate change is already occurring. Thus, quick, effective, and transparent distribution of upcoming funds is necessary. Currently, only a tiny fraction of existing sources of climate finance reaches affected communities, commonly marked by high levels of poverty and low levels of adaptive capacity. Similarly, donor-based global humanitarian aid and development systems are buckling under the weight of increasing demand. As increasing climate impacts threaten to reverse development gains of the last decades...

Paper Title: Long-term trajectory of ozone impact on maize and soybean yields in the United States: A 40-year spatial-temporal analysis
Outcome(s) of Interest: Ozone-induced yield losses in maize and soybean; spatial-temporal variability; economic losses; drought year peaks; regional hotspots; hydrothermal factor modulation.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "ozone pollution has led to average yield losses of −3.5% for maize and −6.1% for soybean, translating into an annual economic loss of approximately $2.6 billion...distinct peaks of abnormally high yield reduction rates in drought years...Significant spatial heterogeneity was detected in ozone impacts...with ozone damage hotspots located in the Southeast Region and the Mississippi River Basin...hydrothermal factors modulate crop responses to ozone"
Drivers of Risk: Ozone pollution (1981-2021); Spatial-temporal variability; Drought years; Hydrothermal factors (temperature, precipitation); Regional hotspots (Southeast, Mississippi River Basin).
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "ozone's influence on agricultural production exhibits marked fluctuations and differs significantly across various geographical locations"; "distinct peaks of abnormally high yield reduction rates in drought years"; "Significant spatial heterogeneity was detected in ozone impacts across the study area, with ozone damage hotspots located in the Southeast Region and the Mississippi River Basin for maize and soybean, respectively."; "hydrothermal factors modulate crop responses to ozone, with maize showing an inverted U-shaped yield loss trend with temperature increases, while soybean demonstrated an upward trend. Both crops experienced amplified ozone-induced yield losses with rising precipitation."
Category-sourcematerial: Our results revealed that over the past four decades, ozone pollution has led to average yield losses of −3.5% for maize and −6.1% for soybean, translating into an annual economic loss of approximately $2.6 billion.
Approach-sourcematerial: To address this issue, we conducted a comprehensive assessment of the spatial-temporal variability of ozone impacts on maize and soybean yields in the United States (1981–2021) using a geographically and temporally weighted regression (GTWR) model.
Methodology: A geographically and temporally weighted regression (GTWR) model was used to assess the spatial-temporal variability of ozone impacts on maize and soybean yields in the United States from 1981–2021.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "To address this issue, we conducted a comprehensive assessment of the spatial-temporal variability of ozone impacts on maize and soybean yields in the United States (1981–2021) using a geographically and temporally weighted regression (GTWR) model."
Highlights: Ozone pollution from 1981-2021 caused average yield losses of -3.5% for maize and -6.1% for soybean in the US, costing $2.6 billion annually, with abnormally high yield reduction rates during drought years, spatial hotspots in the Southeast and Mississippi River Basin respectively, and hydrothermal factors modulating crop responses, necessitating spatiotemporal variability in assessments.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Our results revealed that over the past four decades, ozone pollution has led to average yield losses of −3.5% for maize and −6.1% for soybean, translating into an annual economic loss of approximately $2.6 billion. Interestingly, despite an overall downward trend in ozone impacts on crop yields following the implementation of stringent ozone emission control measures in 1997, our study identified distinct peaks of abnormally high yield reduction rates in drought years. Significant spatial heterogeneity was detected in ozone impacts across the study area, with ozone damage hotspots located in the Southeast Region and the Mississippi River Basin for maize and soybean, respectively. Furthermore, we discovered that hydrothermal factors modulate crop responses to ozone, with maize showing an inverted U-shaped yield loss trend with temperature increases, while soybean demonstrated an upward trend. Both crops experienced amplified ozone-induced yield losses with rising precipitation. Overall, our study highlights the necessity of incorporating spatiotemporal variability into assessments of crop yield losses attributable to ozone pollution.
More Notes: Ozone pollution in the US from 1981-2021 caused significant maize and soybean yield losses, averaging -3.5% and -6.1% respectively, costing $2.6 billion annually, with spatial heterogeneity showing hotspots in the Southeast (maize) and Mississippi River Basin (soybean); while emission controls reduced overall impact, drought years saw abnormally high yield reduction rates, and hydrothermal factors (temperature, precipitation) modulated crop responses to ozone.

Paper Title: Future fire-PM2.5 mortality varies depending on climate and socioeconomic changes
Outcome(s) of Interest: Global mortality associated with fire-PM2.5, current and projected, under various climate and socioeconomic scenarios.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "Here, we estimated the mortality associated to fire-PM2.5 (fire-PM2.5 mortality) for the present period (2010s) and then projected the mortality for the 2050s and 2090s under 10 scenarios created by combining two Representative Concentration Pathways (RCP2.6 or 6.0) with five Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSP1-5)."
Drivers of Risk: Climate change (drier/warmer weather), socioeconomic pathways (population growth, technical development), fire emissions, baseline mortality rate.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "…under the coexisting impacts of climate and socioeconomic changes…decreases in fire emissions and baseline mortality rate…scenario of high population growth and low technical development (SSP3) together with severe global warming (RCP6.0) would lead to an increase in the fire-PM2.5 mortality rate…due to increased fire under drier and warmer weather conditions."
Category-sourcematerial: Fire emits hazardous air pollutants, the most dominant of which is fine particulate matter of diameter ⩽2.5 μm (PM2.5)...Here, we estimated the mortality associated to fire-PM2.5 (fire-PM2.5 mortality) for the present period (2010s) and then projected the mortality for the 2050s and 2090s...the fire-PM2.5 mortality rate was projected to decrease under most scenarios because of decreases in fire emissions and baseline mortality rate...increased fire under drier and warmer weather conditions. Stratification of countries by gross domestic product indicated the need for adaptation efforts in the highest-income countries to avoid future increases of mortality associated with fire-PM2.5
Approach-sourcematerial: We used the Li-Park fire model in combination with a chemical transport model and health risk assessment to calculate fire-PM2.5 mortality.

Paper Title: Long-term croplands water productivity in response to management and climate in the Western US Corn Belt
Outcome(s) of Interest: Water productivity (WP) differences between irrigated and rainfed systems; Impact of water input, vapor pressure deficit, and soil water content on yield and WP.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "Our results showed significant differences (p < 0.05) in WP between irrigated and rainfed sites...Water input (mm, sum of precipitation and irrigation) was the main management factor in rainfed maize WP (R=0.67, p = 0.05) and Y (R=0.79, p < 0.05). Vapor pressure deficit was negatively correlated with Y in rainfed maize (R=−0.72, p < 0.05) and therefore was considered a determinant in WP (R=−0.7, p < 0.05). For soybean, soil water content had the highest correlations with Y and WP (irrigated: R=−0.77; rainfed: R=0.49, only significant in irrigated sites)."
Drivers of Risk: Global population growth, water scarcity, climate variability, water input, vapor pressure deficit (VPD), soil water content (SWC).

Page 3: Global population growth and water scarcity are raising concerns about agricultural systems’ ability to meet future food, fuel, feed, and fiber demands. 
Page 4: Drivers of Risk: "We also analyzed the correlations between Y, ET and WP and the selected climatic and water-related variables to investigate the effect of inter-annual climate variability on WP and determine the drivers of its temporal variability."
Page 10: "To study the possible effects of climate variability on Y, ET, and WP...WI showed the highest correlations with WP (R=0.67, p = 0.05, rainfed site)..."
Page 12: Drivers of Risk: In this study VPD was one of the main drivers of maize’s WP by reducing Y and consequently WP particularly in the rainfed site...In contrast, we found that SWC was determinant in soybean’s Y in both irrigated and rainfed systems.
Category-sourcematerial: The study evaluates yield, evapotranspiration, and water productivity for maize and soybean across different irrigation conditions... Assesses yield, evapotranspiration, and water productivity for maize and soybean across different irrigation conditions... Evaluates changes in water productivity (WP) of maize and soybean under different irrigation and rotation systems... This study evaluates water productivity (WP), yield (Y), and evapotranspiration (ET) in maize and soybean cropping systems in the Midwestern U.S... The findings of this study provide valuable insights into the relationship between WP, Y, and ET with management and climate variability in three major cropping systems in the midwestern U.S... Long-term croplands water productivity in response to management and climate... The primary objective of this study was to quantify and assess the differences in WP, Y, and ET among three major cropping systems in the Eastern Nebraska US Corn Belt..., and to investigate their response to management and interannual climate and water variability... We hypothesized that higher WP will be observed in irrigated sites due to increased Y resulting from enhanced availability of water and nutrients through irrigation and crop rotation...  We found that irrigated maize systems had the highest WP among the studied cropping systems with irrigated maize in rotation being the most efficient system... This study revealed important insights related to management of maize and soybean cropping systems in the midwestern US. We found that Y is the main driver of the year-to-year variations in WP... highlights: We hypothesized that higher WP will be observed in irrigated sites due to increased Y resulting from enhanced availability of water and nutrients through irrigation and crop rotation... In contrast, the rainfed site is expected to exhibit more efficient water use under limited water conditions through stomatal closure... but this may reduce crop photosynthetic activity and ultimately Y, contributing to lower WP... For maize, the average annual Y was 1.11 kg m-2 in the irrigated continuous site and increased to 1.17 kg m-2 (5.6%) under irrigated rotation... in general, irrigated crops had a higher annual Y, ET, and WP compared to rainfed crops... For soybean, while the difference in Y was significant, differences in ET and WP were non-significant between irrigated and rainfed rotations.
Approach-sourcematerial: In this study, we used data collected over a 20-year period (2001–2020) to investigate the interannual variability in yield (Y), crop evapotranspiration (ET), and water productivity (WP, ratio of Y and ET), and their response to management and climate in three major cropping systems located in Eastern Nebraska: irrigated continuous maize, irrigated maize-soybean rotation, and rainfed maize-soybean rotation... Eddy Covariance (EC) flux measurements are used to estimate crop evapotranspiration (ET) between crop emergence and harvest... Crop productivity and ecosystem respiration are assessed using gross primary production derived from net ecosystem CO2 exchange and ecosystem respiration, with statistical analysis including ANOVA, Tukey's test, and PCA to analyze the data between 2001 and 2014... The analysis uses principal component analysis (PCA) and correlation analysis, focusing on two crop types and different cropping systems... Field scale and long-term observational data from ENREEC sites in the Midwestern US. PCA model used to analyze climate variables, yield, and evapotranspiration.
Methodology: Long-term (2001-2020) data from three Eastern Nebraska sites (irrigated continuous maize, irrigated maize-soybean rotation, and rainfed maize-soybean rotation) were used to investigate yield (Y), crop evapotranspiration (ET), and water productivity (WP).
Methodology-sourcematerial: Page 3: "In this study, we used data collected over a 20-year period (2001–2020) to investigate the interannual variability in yield (Y), crop evapotranspiration (ET), and water productivity (WP, ratio of Y and ET), and their response to management and climate in three major cropping systems located in Eastern Nebraska: irrigated continuous maize, irrigated maize-soybean rotation, and rainfed maize-soybean rotation."; Page 4: "To accomplish this, we used data from three proximate monitored sites in Eastern Nebraska (US) with similar climate and edaphic properties."; Page 11: "...three major cropping systems in the midwestern of the US: irrigated continuous maize, irrigated maize-soybean rotation, and rainfed maize-soybean rotation."
Highlights: Yield is the main driver of year-to-year WP variations; VPD is the most important determinant of maize WP affecting yield; SWC is determinant in soybean's yield in both irrigated and rainfed systems.
Highlights-sourcematerial: In this study VPD was one of the main drivers of maize’s WP by reducing Y and consequently WP particularly in the rainfed site. VPD was the most important determinant of maize WP, with the highest effect on Y (correlation R=0.7, p < 0.05), SWC showed the highest correlations with WP in irrigated (R=0.77, p < 0.05) and rainfed (R=0.49, p > 0.05) soybean systems. We found that Y is the main driver of the year-to-year variations in WP.
More Notes: The study highlights the significance of considering climate variability and management practices, particularly the absence of replicates due to the cost of experiments, when evaluating water productivity, with results from Eastern Nebraska showing irrigated maize in rotation had higher water productivity than rainfed systems, strongly influenced by yield rather than evapotranspiration.
More Notes-sourcematerial: "Despite the complexity of interpreting results attributed to the absence of replicates within years which is mainly due to the elevated cost of maintaining such experiment and the complexity of the used techniques...Our results indicated that irrigated maize in rotation had the highest WP (2.23 kg m-3) compared to...rainfed maize in rotation (1.82 kg m-3), with significant differences only observed between irrigated and rainfed systems...The results suggest that Y is more determinant in the year-to-year changes to WP than ET...This study highlights the importance of climate variability and management in determining crops WP, especially in rainfed systems."
Hazard definition-sourcematerial: The study used data collected over a 20-year period (2001–2020) to investigate the interannual variability in yield (Y), crop evapotranspiration (ET), and water productivity (WP, ratio of Y and ET), and their response to management and climate in three major cropping systems located in Eastern Nebraska...Vapor pressure deficit was negatively correlated with Y in rainfed maize (R=−0.72, p <0.05) and therefore was considered a determinant in WP (R=−0.7, p <0.05).
Impact persistence-sourcematerial: The study uses long-term data (2001-2020) from three monitored sites in Eastern Nebraska (both irrigated and rainfed systems) along with Eddy Covariance measurements for data collection, and it calculates WP based on dry grain yield per unit of crop ET... We also analyzed the correlations between Y, ET and WP and the selected climatic and water-related variables to investigate the effect of inter-annual climate variability on WP and determine the drivers of its temporal variability... This study highlights the importance of climate variability and management in determining crops WP. The results of the PCA model showed that the five inter-related climatic variables, along with Y and ET, explained most (up to 73.1%) of the variance under the different cropping systems analyzed... Our analysis also demonstrated a clear distinction between irrigated and rainfed cropping systems, and similarities between continuous and rotation maize systems. We identified Y, WI, VPD and TA as the primary factors affecting the temporal variability of maize WP in both irrigated and rainfed systems... This study revealed important insights related to management of maize and soybean cropping systems in the midwestern US. We found that Y is the main driver of the year-to-year variations in WP. VPD was the most important determinant of maize WP, with the highest effect on Y.

Focus on three cropping systems: irrigated continuous maize, irrigated maize-soybean rotation, and rainfed maize-soybean rotation.
Impact interactions-sourcematerial: The study focuses on the relationship between water productivity (WP), yield (Y), and evapotranspiration (ET) in different cropping systems and how these are affected by management and climate variability, but it does not explicitly consider interactions between multiple climate impacts or sectors. While the analysis includes climate variables like vapor pressure deficit (VPD) and soil water content (SWC), as well as management practices, it emphasizes correlations within the agricultural sector rather than interactions across different sectors or spatial interactions. This is supported by statements on Pages 10-12 which focus on correlations and factors affecting WP, Y, and ET within specific cropping systems and climate variables. Additionally, the paper uses statistical methods like PCA to assess variance but does not mention assessing interactions between multiple climate impacts or across sectors.
Economic dynamics-sourcematerial: Global population growth and water scarcity are raising concerns about agricultural systems' ability to meet future food, fuel, feed, and fiber demands. Water productivity (WP), the ratio of crop production to water use, is crucial for assessing agricultural resilience and sustainability... In this study, we used data collected over a 20-year period (2001–2020) to investigate the interannual variability in yield (Y), crop evapotranspiration (ET), and water productivity (WP, ratio of Y and ET), and their response to management and climate in three major cropping systems located in Eastern Nebraska: irrigated continuous maize, irrigated maize-soybean rotation, and rainfed maize-soybean rotation... Evaluation overview: The primary objective of this study was to quantify and assess the differences in WP, Y, and ET among three major cropping systems in the Eastern Nebraska US Corn Belt..., and to investigate their response to management and interannual climate and water variability... Methodology: To accomplish this, we used data from three proximate monitored sites in Eastern Nebraska (US) with similar climate and edaphic properties. We calculated the Y, ET and WP in the three cropping systems and estimated changes related to management and crop rotation. We also analyzed the correlations between Y, ET and WP and the selected climatic and water-related variables.

The study highlights the importance of water input (WI) in rainfed maize and the negative impact of vapor pressure deficit (VPD) on rainfed maize yield and WP. For soybean, soil water content (SWC) is shown to be a key factor. The research identifies management strategies (irrigation, crop rotation) that can enhance WP and resilience in the US Corn Belt given different climate conditions.

These findings can aid in formulating strategies to enhance water productivity and resilience in the US Corn Belt." & "We hypothesized that higher WP will be observed in irrigated sites due to increased Y resulting from enhanced availability of water and nutrients through irrigation and crop rotation...In contrast, the rainfed site is expected to exhibit more efficient water use under limited water conditions through stomatal closure... but this may reduce crop photosynthetic activity and ultimately Y, contributing to lower WP." & "This study highlights the importance of climate variability and management in determining crops WP...We identified Y, WI, VPD and TA as the primary factors affecting the temporal variability of maize WP in both irrigated and rainfed systems. In rainfed maize systems, the positive correlation between Y and WI suggests that increasing water availability can improve crop productivity, while higher VPD and TA increase the atmospheric water demand and can result in stomatal closure, reducing plant carbon uptake through photosynthesis and Y."

Paper Title: Disentangling the drivers of future Antarctic ice loss with a historically calibrated ice-sheet model
Outcome(s) of Interest: Sea-level rise; Antarctic ice sheet collapse; West Antarctic ice sheet; Thwaites Glacier retreat.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "Antarctic contribution to global mean sea-level rise by 2100 of 6 (-8 to 15)cm under a low-emission scenario and 5.5 (-10 to 16)cm under a very high-emission scenario... oceanic and atmospheric drivers are projected to result in a complete collapse of the West Antarctic ice sheet along with significant grounding-line retreat in the marine basins of the East Antarctic ice sheet, leading to a median global mean sea-level rise of 2.75 (6.95)m by 2300 (3000)...mass loss is likely to remain confined to the Amundsen Sea Embayment, where present-day climate conditions seem sufficient to commit to a continuous retreat of Thwaites Glacier."
Drivers of Risk: Oceanic forcing (sub-shelf melting, temperature/salinity), atmospheric forcing (temperature, snowfall, runoff), surface mass balance (SMB), melt-elevation feedback, ice discharge (calving), ice-ocean interactions, hydrofracturing, present-day climate uncertainties, CMIP6 model uncertainties.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Page 1: Sub-shelf melting, ice discharge, surface mass balance, ocean temperature, atmospheric temperature, snowfall, surface runoff, melt-elevation feedback. Page 5: Uncertainty in the representation of present-day Antarctic climate (SMB and air temperature), uncertainty in ocean present-day temperature and salinity fields, uncertainty in imposed climate forcing (using a subset of CMIP6 climate models), melt-elevation feedback, surface melt and runoff, ice–ocean interactions (i.e., sub-shelf melting), and the effective ice–ocean heat flux. Page 6: The analysis considers the impact of atmospheric and oceanic forcings and surface melt-driven hydrofracturing of ice shelves. Page 9: Factors such as surface mass balance, sub-shelf melt fluxes, and dynamic ice loss (calving fluxes) are evaluated as drivers of sea-level rise. Page 14: The influences of oceanic and atmospheric forcings, melt-elevation feedback, and ice-shelf hydrofracturing on Antarctic contributions to GMSLR are assessed. Page 21: The drivers of risk include sub-shelf melt, calving, surface mass balance fluxes, atmospheric warming, and surface runoff.
Category-sourcematerial: We use an observationally calibrated ice-sheet model to investigate the future trajectory of the Antarctic ice sheet related to uncertainties in the future balance between sub-shelf melting and ice discharge, on the one hand, and the surface mass balance, on the other. Our ensemble of simulations, forced by a panel of CMIP6 climate models, suggests that the ocean will be the primary driver of short-term Antarctic mass loss, initiating ice loss in West Antarctica already during this century. The atmosphere initially plays a mitigating role through increased snowfall, leading to an Antarctic contribution to global mean sea-level rise by 2100 of 6 (-8 to 15) cm under a low-emissions scenario and 5.5 (-10 to 16) cm under a very high-emissions scenario. However, under the very high-emission pathway, the influence of the atmosphere shifts beyond the end of the century, becoming an amplifying driver of mass loss as the ice sheet’s surface mass balance decreases.
Approach-sourcematerial: The analysis is performed using the Kori-ULB ice-sheet model, an ensemble of simulations accounting for ice–ocean and ice–atmosphere interactions, with Bayesian calibration based on past mass balance estimates.
Methodology: The study uses the Kori-ULB ice-sheet model, forced by a panel of CMIP6 climate models, with Bayesian calibration based on past mass balance estimates from the IMBIE project. A 100-member perturbed parameter ensemble is generated using Latin hypercube sampling of nine key parameters governing ice-ocean-atmosphere interactions, considering both discrete (initial state, climate forcing) and continuous parameters. Historical hindcast simulations (1950-2014) are performed, and the ensemble is applied for SSP1-2.6, SSP5-8.5, and constant present-day climate conditions. Structural error is estimated by multiplying the observational error by a factor of 10. Gaps in oceanic forcing data are filled using a methodology following Kreuzer et al. (2021). A PDD algorithm calculates surface mass balance, and the PICO model is used for sub-shelf melt parameterization. Analysis extends to 2300 and 3000 for sea-level rise projections.
Methodology-sourcematerial:
Page 1: Observationally-calibrated ice-sheet model, forced by a panel of CMIP6 climate models. Analysis extends to 2300 and 3000 for sea-level rise projections.
Page 3: The analysis is performed using the Kori-ULB ice-sheet model, an ensemble of simulations accounting for ice–ocean and ice–atmosphere interactions, with Bayesian calibration based on past mass balance estimates.
Page 4: Gaps in oceanic forcing data on the continental shelf are filled using a methodology following Kreuzer et al. (2021), utilizing anomalies added to reference climatologies for the ice-sheet model.
Page 5: A perturbed parameter ensemble including nine key parameters governing processes at the ice–ocean–atmosphere boundary is designed. Latin hypercube sampling is used to create 100 distinct parameter vectors. The ensemble includes discrete inputs (initial state and climate forcing uncertainties) and continuous parameters (ice–atmosphere and ice–ocean interactions). Historical hindcast simulations (1950-2014) are performed, and the 100-member ensemble is applied for both socio-economic pathways and under constant present-day climate conditions. Additional projections were produced under SSP5-8.5.
Page 6: Structural error is estimated by multiplying the observational error by a factor of 10.
Page 8: The analysis uses a Bayesian calibration of an ensemble with observational constraints from the IMBIE project, focusing on historical trends in the Antarctic ice sheet's mass change and its drivers.
Page 17: Projected Antarctic mass loss under SSP5-8.5 is mainly driven by the combined effects of oceanic forcing and the melt-elevation feedback. Hydrofracturing produces higher median rates of contribution to GMSLR.
Highlights: The paper designed a parameter space to explore the uncertainty in ice-ocean and ice-atmosphere interactions by ensuring parameter ranges are as wide as physically plausible.
Highlights-sourcematerial:
Page 5: The paper designs a parameter space in order for parameter ranges to be as wide as physically plausible to explore the uncertainty in ice-ocean and ice-atmosphere interactions
- "Overall, we have designed our parameter space in order for parameter ranges to be as wide as physically plausible"
More Notes: The study focuses on Antarctic ice sheets and uses CMIP6 models MRI-ESM2-0, IPSL-CM6A-LR, CESM2-WACCM, and UKESM1-0-LL for climate forcing.
More Notes-sourcematerial: "The study focuses on Antarctic ice sheets." "we use a subset of CMIP6 climate models (GCM): MRI-ESM2-0, IPSL-CM6A-LR, CESM2-WACCM and UKESM1-0-LL"
Hazard definition-sourcematerial: The analysis extends to 2300 and 3000 for sea-level rise projections.
Impact persistence-sourcematerial: Overall, future Antarctic mass changes will likely be characterised by a transition in the primary driver of mass loss, shifting from an ocean-driven to an atmospheric-driven contribution of the AIS to GMSLR. The timing of this transition will be dictated by the trajectories of future atmospheric warming.
Space disaggregation-sourcematerial: Relatively coarse spatial resolution of 16 km is used, which may overlook smaller bedrock irregularities and pinning points.
Impact interactions-sourcematerial: The paper analyzes interactions between ice-ocean and ice-atmosphere processes and their sequential influence on Antarctic mass loss. The analysis is performed using a perturbed parameter ensemble that includes parameters governing processes at the ice-ocean-atmosphere boundary (Page 5). It explores the evolution of Antarctic mass loss driven by oceanic forcing initially and atmospheric forcing later, indicating a sequential transition in the main drivers of mass loss (Pages 13 and 15). The study highlights that "initially triggered by the oceanic forcing, mass loss is then further accelerated by the melt-elevation feedback as near-surface warming increases" (Page 25), supporting the notion of sequential interactions.
Economic dynamics-sourcematerial: Page 5: After the 1950-2014 historical hindcast simulations, the 100-member ensemble is applied for both socio-economic pathways and under constant present-day (1995-2014 average) climate conditions.
Climate features-sourcematerial:
Our ensemble of simulations, forced by a panel of CMIP6 climate models, suggests that the ocean will be the primary driver of short-term Antarctic mass loss, initiating ice loss in West Antarctica already during this century. The atmosphere initially plays a mitigating role through increased snowfall... However, under the very high-emission pathway, the influence of the atmosphere shifts beyond the end of the century, becoming an amplifying driver of mass loss... We show that this transition occurs when Antarctic near-surface warming exceeds a critical threshold of +7.5°C, at which the increase in surface runoff outweighs the increase in snow accumulation, a signal that is amplified by the melt–elevation feedback. Therefore, under the very high-emission scenario, oceanic and atmospheric drivers are projected to result in a complete collapse of the West Antarctic ice sheet along with significant grounding-line retreat...
Page 25: ... The decrease in SMB is associated with a strong increase in surface runoff with warming air temperatures, a signal significantly amplified by the melt-elevation feedback. More generally, this decrease in SMB seems to start when Antarctic near-surface warming exceeds +7.5°C, the threshold at which the increase in surface runoff outweighs the increase in snow accumulation. Reaching such a threshold may therefore be delayed under more sustainable socio-economic pathways. If this threshold is not crossed, i.e., under the socio-economic pathway SSP1-2.6, Antarctic mass loss would likely remain limited...
Page 22: ... our results support the findings from Kittel et al. (2021), suggesting the existence of a threshold at +7.5◦C in near-surface warming over Antarctica (which may already be reached by the end of this century under SSP5-8.5; Figure S6). This threshold results in reduced SMB for the grounded ice sheet due to a significant increase in surface runoff. ...
Adaptation considered: Adaptation strategies aren't explicitly modeled. The study projects sea-level rise under different emission scenarios (SSP1-2.6 and SSP5-8.5), effectively exploring the impact of mitigation. It also analyzes the influence of oceanic and atmospheric forcings, including melt-elevation feedback and ice shelf hydrofracturing, on ice sheet mass balance and sea-level rise, providing insights into factors influencing the magnitude of potential impacts.
Adaptation considered-sourcematerial: Pages 1, 14, 15, 16, 17, 25: We use an observationally-calibrated ice-sheet model to investigate the future trajectory of the Antarctic ice sheet...Under a more sustainable socio-economic pathway... separating the respective influences of the oceanic and atmospheric forcings and of the melt-elevation feedback, and including the influence of ice-shelf hydrofracturing...Contribution of atmospheric and oceanic forcing...sensitivity analyses confirm that projected Antarctic mass loss during the 21st century is primarily driven by the ocean... Project Antarctic mass loss under SSP5-8.5 is mainly driven by the combined effects of oceanic forcing and the melt-elevation feedback...Reaching such a threshold may therefore be delayed under more sustainable socio-economic pathways.

Paper Title: Prediction of the potential distribution area of Spodoptera frugiperda and its parasitic wasp, Trichogramma pretiosum
Outcome(s) of Interest: Potential distribution areas of Spodoptera frugiperda and Trichogramma pretiosum under current and future climatic conditions; feasibility of introducing T. pretiosum to control S. frugiperda based on potential global distribution.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: To predict the trends of S. frugiperda and its parasitoid wasp Trichogramma pretiosum in their habitats under current and future climatic conditions, based on MaxEnt model and geographic distribution data of their historical occurrence, we project the feasibility of introducing T. pretiosum to control S. frugiperda by evaluating on their potential global distribution.
Drivers of Risk: Climate change, pest distribution, greenhouse gas concentration.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "To predict the trends of S. frugiperda and its parasitoid wasp Trichogramma pretiosum in their habitats under current and future climatic conditions, based on MaxEnt model and geographic distribution data of their historical occurrence, we project the feasibility of introducing T. pretiosum to control S. frugiperda by evaluating on their potential global distribution...under the current greenhouse gas concentration, the potential distribution area of S. frugiperda is concentrated in 50° N-30° S, with a total area of 1.74 × 106km2"
Category-sourcematerial: Spodoptera frugiperda is a migratory agricultural pest with fast-spreading speed, long migration distance, and wide host range, which seriously threatens the safety of economic crops.
Approach-sourcematerial: "based on MaxEnt model and geographic distribution data of their historical occurrence, we project the feasibility of introducing T. pretiosum to control S. frugiperda by evaluating on their potential global distribution."
Methodology: MaxEnt modeling was used with historical occurrence data to project the potential global distribution of Spodoptera frugiperda and its parasitoid wasp Trichogramma pretiosum under current and future climatic conditions to assess the feasibility of using T. pretiosum for biocontrol.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "based on MaxEnt model and geographic distribution data of their historical occurrence, we project the feasibility of introducing T. pretiosum to control S. frugiperda by evaluating on their potential global distribution...under current and future climatic conditions"

Paper Title: Strong increase in mortality attributable to ozone pollution under a climate change and demographic scenario
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased global ozone-related mortality, specifically respiratory, due to climate change, population growth/aging, and ozone trends.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "global mortality attributable to ozone"; "ozone-related mortality on a global scale up to 2090"; "global ozone-related mortality by 2090 will amount to 2.79 M [95% CI 0.97 M-5.23 M] to 3.12 M [95% CI 1.11 M-5.75 M] per year, approximately ninefold that of the 327 K [95% CI 103 K-652 K] deaths per year in 2000"; "Climate change alone may lead to an increase of ozone-related mortality in 2090 between 42 K [95% CI −37 K-122 K] and 217 K [95% CI 68 K-367 K] per year"; "Population growth and ageing are associated with an increase in global ozone-related mortality by a factor of 5.34, while the increase by ozone trends alone ranges between factors of 1.48 and 1.7."
Drivers of Risk: Climate change, population growth & aging, ozone trends (pollution emissions).
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "Pollution emissions, demographic, and climate changes are expected to drive future ozone-related mortality... Climate change alone may lead to an increase of ozone-related mortality... Population growth and ageing are associated with an increase in global ozone-related mortality...the increase by ozone trends alone ranges between factors of 1.48 and 1.7."
Category-sourcematerial: Long-term exposure to ambient ozone (O3) is associated with excess respiratory mortality. Pollution emissions, demographic, and climate changes are expected to drive future ozone-related mortality. ... We estimated ozone-related mortality on a global scale up to 2090... Climate change alone may lead to an increase of ozone-related mortality in 2090... Ambient ozone pollution under the high-emissions SSP3-7.0 scenario is projected to become a significant human health risk factor.
Approach-sourcematerial: Here, we assess global mortality attributable to ozone according to an Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) Shared Socioeconomic Pathway (SSP) scenario applied in Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 6 (CMIP6) models, projecting a temperature increase of about 3.6 °C by the end of the century. We estimated ozone-related mortality on a global scale up to 2090 following the Global Burden of Disease (GBD) 2019 approach, using bias-corrected simulations from three CMIP6 Earth System Models (ESMs) under the SSP3-7.0 emissions scenario.
Methodology: Ozone-related mortality was estimated globally up to 2090, following the Global Burden of Disease (GBD) 2019 approach, using bias-corrected simulations from three CMIP6 Earth System Models (ESMs) under the SSP3-7.0 emissions scenario.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Here, we assess global mortality attributable to ozone according to an Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) Shared Socioeconomic Pathway (SSP) scenario applied in Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 6 (CMIP6) models... We estimated ozone-related mortality on a global scale up to 2090 following the Global Burden of Disease (GBD) 2019 approach, using bias-corrected simulations from three CMIP6 Earth System Models (ESMs) under the SSP3-7.0 emissions scenario.
Highlights: Global ozone-related mortality is projected to increase ninefold by 2090 under a high-emissions scenario (SSP3-7.0), with climate change contributing 42K-217K deaths annually; population growth and aging will increase mortality by a factor of 5.34, while ozone trends alone will increase it by a factor of 1.48-1.7, but improved living conditions and healthcare could mitigate these impacts.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "Based on the three ESMs simulations, global ozone-related mortality by 2090 will amount to 2.79 M [95% CI 0.97 M-5.23 M] to 3.12 M [95% CI 1.11 M-5.75 M] per year, approximately ninefold that of the 327 K [95% CI 103 K-652 K] deaths per year in 2000. Climate change alone may lead to an increase of ozone-related mortality in 2090 between 42 K [95% CI −37 K-122 K] and 217 K [95% CI 68 K-367 K] per year. Population growth and ageing are associated with an increase in global ozone-related mortality by a factor of 5.34, while the increase by ozone trends alone ranges between factors of 1.48 and 1.7. Yet, optimizing living conditions and healthcare standards worldwide to the optimal ones today (application of minimum baseline mortality rates) will help mitigate the adverse consequences associated with population growth and ageing, and ozone increases caused by pollution emissions and climate change."

Paper Title: Plant-Pathogen interactions and global food security
Outcome(s) of Interest: Large-scale crop losses (pre/postharvest), reduced productivity and commercial value, threat to global food security, food availability targets thwarted.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Annually, large-scale losses occur in various types of plant cultures worldwide, both in the field (preharvest) and during storage (postharvest), affecting productivity and reducing the product's commercial value. On the other hand, climate change and the emergence of new diseases thwart food availability targets, destroying large areas used for planting food crops and posing a serious threat to global food security.
Drivers of Risk: Climate change, plant pathogens (pre- and post-harvest), new disease emergence, agrochemical use (resistance, environmental hazards).
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "climate change and the emergence of new diseases thwart food availability targets...Agrochemicals were largely used to enhance yields and protect crops from diseases and pests. However, the chemical approach is environmentally unfriendly, induces resistance in plant pathogens, creates environmental hazards, and, most importantly, accumulates chemicals in agricultural products."
Category-sourcematerial: The increase in agricultural production is essential to sustain the continuing population growth...climate change and the emergence of new diseases thwart food availability targets, destroying large areas used for planting food crops and posing a serious threat to global food security.
Methodology: Innovative molecular detection tools, remote sensing, and biological practices are employed for rapid disease diagnosis, long-term health monitoring, and ecofriendly disease management.
Methodology-sourcematerial: The use of more innovative molecular detection tools and remote sensing has enabled fast and on-site diagnosis of plant diseases and the ability to monitor the long-term health condition of plants. ... various biological practices are now available that are ecofriendly, cost-effective, and can be used in the field.
Highlights: Climate change and plant pathogens threaten food security by impacting agricultural productivity. Innovative molecular detection tools and remote sensing aid in disease monitoring, while biological practices offer eco-friendly alternatives to harmful agrochemicals. Key challenges hindering food security are also addressed.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "climate change and the emergence of new diseases thwart food availability targets, destroying large areas used for planting food crops and posing a serious threat to global food security...The use of more innovative molecular detection tools and remote sensing has enabled fast and on-site diagnosis of plant diseases and the ability to monitor the long-term health condition of plants...various biological practices are now available that are ecofriendly, cost-effective, and can be used in the field...Finally, particular attention is given to the current challenges that impede food security."

Paper Title: Economic conditions, climate change and housing affordability across US states: the mediating role of interest rates in a panel data analysis
Outcome(s) of Interest: Housing affordability is affected by both economic conditions and climate change.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "The consequences of climate change, such as extreme weather events can worsen the housing crisis by reducing the supply of affordable housing and driving up costs...This paper is to investigate how in addition to economic conditions, climate change affect the affordability of housing in the USA..."
Drivers of Risk: Climate change (extreme weather events), economic conditions (interest rates), regional temperature (hottest vs. coldest states).
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "The consequences of climate change, such as extreme weather events can worsen the housing crisis...economic conditions...interest rates...This study also divided the states into two regions, the hottest and coldest, to examine differences in housing affordability."
Category-sourcematerial: The consequences of climate change, such as extreme weather events can worsen the housing crisis by reducing the supply of affordable housing and driving up costs.
Approach-sourcematerial: Design/methodology/approach: This study used a panel autoregressive distributed lag model to estimate short- and long-term effects...
Methodology: A panel autoregressive distributed lag model was used to estimate short- and long-term effects on state-level housing affordability, analyzing economic periods before and after the global financial crisis, and dividing states into the hottest and coldest regions to examine differences.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Design/methodology/approach: This study used a panel autoregressive distributed lag model to estimate short- and long-term effects and analyzed economic periods before and after the global financial crisis. This study also divided the states into two regions, the hottest and coldest, to examine differences in housing affordability.
Highlights: Economic improvements increase housing affordability, especially in colder U.S. regions; climate change has a short-term positive effect on housing affordability; interest rates significantly mediate the relationship between economic conditions and housing affordability.
Highlights-sourcematerial: The study shows results that suggest better economic conditions lead to increased housing affordability, particularly in colder regions. Additionally, climate change positively affects housing affordability in the short term. Finally, this study confirms the important role of interest rates in the relationship between economic conditions and housing affordability.
More Notes: The study examines how climate change, alongside economic conditions, impacts housing affordability in the USA, using state-level data and a panel autoregressive distributed lag model, revealing that economic improvement increases housing affordability, climate change has a short-term positive effect, and interest rates significantly influence the economic-housing affordability relationship, especially in colder regions.

Additionally, climate change positively affects housing affordability in the short term.
