Paper Title: Preparing municipal water system planning for a changing climate: Integrating climate-sensitive demand estimates
Outcome(s) of Interest: Overestimation of imported water, misclassified vulnerabilities during supply limitations, greater sensitivity to changes in demand than supply.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Neglecting climatic influences on MWS demands, the model overestimates the volume of imported water by up to 50% and misclassifies vulnerabilities during supply-limiting conditions...the MWS exhibited an average threefold greater sensitivity to percent changes in demand relative to percent changes in supply.
Drivers of Risk: Climate change impacts, nonstationarity in supply/demand, inaccurate demand modeling, sensitivity to demand changes.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Seasonality and a changing climate exert strong influences on supply and demand in the western United States, challenging municipal water system (MWS) management. Although supply and demand exhibit characteristics of nonstationarity... Neglecting climatic influences on MWS demands, the model overestimates the volume of imported water by up to 50% and misclassifies vulnerabilities during supply-limiting conditions. Moreover, the MWS exhibited an average threefold greater sensitivity to percent changes in demand relative to percent changes in supply.
Category-sourcematerial: Seasonality and a changing climate exert strong influences on supply and demand in the western United States, challenging municipal water system (MWS) management. Given the projected impacts of climate change on water resources, we use the documented performance of a real-world MWS with a calibrated systems model to investigate how demands modeled with and without the influences of climate impact system vulnerability indicators...
Approach-sourcematerial: Given the projected impacts of climate change on water resources, we use the documented performance of a real-world MWS with a calibrated systems model to investigate how demands modeled with and without the influences of climate impact system vulnerability indicators...
Methodology: A calibrated systems model of a real-world municipal water system (MWS) was used to investigate how demands modeled with and without climate influences impact system vulnerability indicators, defined by exceedance of historical daily mean imported water.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "we use the documented performance of a real-world MWS with a calibrated systems model to investigate how demands modeled with and without the influences of climate impact system vulnerability indicators—determined by the exceedance of historical daily mean imported water—for MWS planning guidance."
Highlights: Econometric models often used to estimate water demands underestimate climate variability, which can lead to a 50% overestimation of imported water needs and misclassification of vulnerabilities; a climate-sensitive demand model reduces error to <3% and correctly categorizes vulnerabilities, highlighting the importance of accurate demand modeling for MWS performance, as demand variances have a threefold greater impact than supply variances.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "commonly used econometric-based models to estimate demands discount the influences of climate variability and trends"; "Neglecting climatic influences on MWS demands, the model overestimates the volume of imported water by up to 50% and misclassifies vulnerabilities"; "the climate-sensitive demand estimates reduced model error (i.e., <3% error) and correctly categorized vulnerabilities"; "the MWS exhibited an average threefold greater sensitivity to percent changes in demand relative to percent changes in supply."
More Notes: Climate-sensitive demand modeling is crucial for accurate municipal water system vulnerability assessments, as neglecting climatic influences can lead to significant overestimation of water imports and misclassification of vulnerabilities, with demand variations having a greater impact than supply variations.
More Notes-sourcematerial: "Neglecting climatic influences on MWS demands, the model overestimates the volume of imported water by up to 50% and misclassifies vulnerabilities during supply-limiting conditions...the MWS exhibited an average threefold greater sensitivity to percent changes in demand relative to percent changes in supply."
Hazard definition-sourcematerial: Although supply and demand exhibit characteristics of nonstationarity, the commonly used econometric-based models to estimate demands discount the influences of climate variability and trends in seasonal MWS vulnerability assessments. Given the projected impacts of climate change on water resources, we use the documented performance of a real-world MWS with a calibrated systems model to investigate how demands modeled with and without the influences of climate impact system vulnerability indicators...
Impact persistence-sourcematerial: There is no explicit mention of persistence in impacts in the abstract. The abstract focuses on the impact of climate variability and trends on water supply and demand and how neglecting these influences can lead to errors in modeling and vulnerability assessments.
Space disaggregation-sourcematerial: "the documented performance of a real-world MWS with a calibrated systems model"
Impact interactions-sourcematerial: The sensitivity to variances in demand emphasizes the need to account for factors influencing supply and demand when investigating the impacts of a changing climate, suggesting future research to examine the coupled influences of modeled supply and demand accuracy on MWS performance.
Economic dynamics-sourcematerial: Although supply and demand exhibit characteristics of nonstationarity, the commonly used econometric-based models to estimate demands discount the influences of climate variability and trends in seasonal MWS vulnerability assessments.
Climate features-sourcematerial: The abstract focuses on the impact of climate change on water supply and demand in municipal water systems, specifically addressing nonstationarity, climate variability, and trends. It does not mention tipping points or other climate complexities.
Adaptation considered: The study considers adaptation indirectly by emphasizing the importance of accurately modeling climate-sensitive water demands in municipal water systems (MWS). The research highlights how neglecting climate influences on demand leads to inaccurate vulnerability assessments and overestimation of imported water needs. By demonstrating the significant sensitivity of MWS to changes in demand, the study suggests that future research should focus on improving the accuracy of both supply and demand models for better MWS planning and management in a changing climate. This implies that improved modeling can inform adaptation strategies.
Adaptation considered-sourcematerial: "Given the projected impacts of climate change on water resources, we use the documented performance of a real-world MWS...to investigate how demands modeled with and without the influences of climate impact system vulnerability indicators...Neglecting climatic influences on MWS demands, the model overestimates the volume of imported water by up to 50% and misclassifies vulnerabilities...The climate-sensitive demand estimates reduced model error...Moreover, the MWS exhibited an average threefold greater sensitivity to percent changes in demand relative to percent changes in supply...suggesting future research to examine the coupled influences of modeled supply and demand accuracy on MWS performance."

Paper Title: Orchards management under changing climate
Outcome(s) of Interest: Reduced agricultural yields and quality; altered blossoming and fruiting; water management difficulties; increased adverse weather threats; threats to orchard survival; economic sustainability challenges.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: As a result of climate change, the global agriculture industry is presently facing unprecedented challenges, particularly with respect to orchards. Orchards are currently experiencing a broad range of climatic changes, both rapidly and continuously. The effects on agricultural yields and quality as a result of the alteration in blossoming and fruiting formation brought about by increasing temperatures. Along with difficult water management, the increasing rate of adverse weather conditions puts orchards’ survival under threat. The improvements are presently being experienced across the world’s orchard sector, needing prompt action by farmers to ensure continued food production and economic sustainability.
Drivers of Risk: Increasing temperatures, altered blossoming/fruiting, difficult water management, adverse weather conditions, pests and diseases.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "increasing temperatures," "alteration in blossoming and fruiting formation," "difficult water management," "increasing rate of adverse weather conditions," "efficient pest and disease management in a setting that is consistently changing."
Category-sourcematerial: As a result of climate change, the global agriculture industry is presently facing unprecedented challenges, particularly with respect to orchards...The effects on agricultural yields and quality as a result of the alteration in blossoming and fruiting formation brought about by increasing temperatures.
Approach-sourcematerial: The abstract discusses the challenges faced by orchards due to climate change and the need for adaptation through modern innovations like sensors and data analytics, effective irrigation systems, and collaboration among farmers, academics, and legislative bodies. However, it does not specify any particular modeling or analysis approach used in the research study, such as econometric, macro-model, or stylised models.
Methodology: The paper advocates for a multidimensional approach to orchard management that includes selecting climate-compatible crop types, utilizing real-time environmental monitoring via sensors and data analytics, implementing effective irrigation systems, prioritizing soil health, and employing efficient pest and disease management.
Methodology-sourcematerial: By choosing crop kinds that are especially compatible with the new climate, orchards can adapt to changing environmental conditions. Cultivators are able to actively monitor environmental conditions in real time using modern innovations like sensors and data analytics, enabling them to make more accurate and informed management decisions. Orchards need to put effective irrigation systems in place and give soil health care first priority if they want to make sure they have sufficient supplies of water while saving precious resources. Additionally, the general health and stability of orchards depend on efficient pest and disease management in a setting that is consistently changing.
Highlights: Climate change is significantly impacting orchards globally, threatening yields and economic sustainability through altered blossoming/fruiting, water management issues, and increased adverse weather. Adaptation strategies include selecting climate-compatible crop types, utilizing data analytics for informed decisions, implementing efficient irrigation and soil health practices, and employing effective pest/disease management. Collaboration between farmers, academics, and policymakers is crucial for developing climate-resilient policies and ensuring the long-term viability of orchards.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "As a result of climate change, the global agriculture industry is presently facing unprecedented challenges, particularly with respect to orchards...The effects on agricultural yields and quality as a result of the alteration in blossoming and fruiting formation brought about by increasing temperatures...difficult water management, the increasing rate of adverse weather conditions puts orchards’ survival under threat...Climate change resistance and adaptation are essential for the long-term viability of orchards, calling for a multidimensional approach...By choosing crop kinds that are especially compatible with the new climate, orchards can adapt to changing environmental conditions...Cultivators are able to actively monitor environmental conditions in real time using modern innovations like sensors and data analytics, enabling them to make more accurate and informed management decisions...Orchards need to put effective irrigation systems in place and give soil health care first priority...Additionally, the general health and stability of orchards depend on efficient pest and disease management...working together will be necessary...Farmers, academics, and legislative bodies must collaborate to create effective policies that enhance orchard resiliency...International assistance is also necessary...guarantee the long-term viability of orchards in a dynamic and changing global setting."

Paper Title: Comparative chromatin dynamics reveal differential thermal tolerance mechanisms between two congeneric oyster species
Outcome(s) of Interest: Thermal tolerance, apoptosis pathways, transcriptional regulation, heat stress response, oyster species adaptation, epigenetics
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "The comparative analysis showed that the inhibitor of apoptosis protein (IAP) family and its associated apoptosis pathways were the major divergent pathway between the two species...C. angulata activated the genes related to anti-apoptosis, DNA damage repair, and fatty acid synthesis...These findings suggest that C. angulata may have evolved a more centralized and energy-efficient transcriptional regulatory network at the genomic level, thereby facilitating its long-term adaptation to relatively high temperature environments...the key transcription factor Hsf1, involved in heat shock responses, may contribute to the differential heat response patterns in C. gigas and C. angulata through divergent expression levels of its alternatively spliced isoforms (Hsf1a and Hsf1d)...Our work will contribute to predicting the adaptive potential of oysters and other marine invertebrates in facing future global warming..."
Drivers of Risk: High-temperature stress; global warming; species-specific thermal tolerance; apoptosis pathways; transcription factor Hsf1; differential expression of genes related to heat shock proteins, anti-apoptosis, DNA damage repair, and fatty acid synthesis.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Oysters, being globally farmed bivalve of significant economic and ecological value, are increasingly confronted with heat stress induced by global warming... The comparative analysis showed that the inhibitor of apoptosis protein (IAP) family and its associated apoptosis pathways were the major divergent pathway between the two species... The promoter regions of differentially expressed genes in C. gigas under heat stress were found to be enriched with motifs such as Foxp1, Foxk2, Myc, Cebpd, Foxo3, and Hsf1 motifs and expressed genes related to molecular chaperones, including heat shock protein family. And C. angulata activated the genes related to anti-apoptosis, DNA damage repair, and fatty acid synthesis... key transcription factor Hsf1, involved in heat shock responses, may contribute to the differential heat response patterns in C. gigas and C. angulata through divergent expression levels of its alternatively spliced isoforms (Hsf1a and Hsf1d).
Category-sourcematerial: Oysters, being globally farmed bivalve of significant economic and ecological value...the adaptive potential of oysters and other marine invertebrates in facing future global warming, and provide a theoretical foundation for genetic improvement of resistance traits and sustainable development of oyster industry.
Approach-sourcematerial: Using ATAC-Seq and RNA-Seq to reveal the differential thermal tolerance mechanisms at the chromatin dynamics level in response to high-temperature stress.
Methodology: ATAC-Seq and RNA-Seq were used to compare chromatin dynamics and gene expression in Crassostrea gigas (thermosensitive) and Crassostrea angulata (thermotolerant) oysters under heat stress to reveal differential thermal tolerance mechanisms.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "In this study, we conducted a comparative analysis between two closely related oyster species (the relatively thermosensitive Crassostrea gigas and the relatively thermotolerant Crassostrea angulata) using ATAC-Seq and RNA-Seq to reveal the differential thermal tolerance mechanisms at the chromatin dynamics level in response to high-temperature stress."
Highlights: Oysters' thermal tolerance mechanisms differ between species: Crassostrea angulata activates anti-apoptosis, DNA repair, and fatty acid synthesis, while C. gigas relies on heat shock proteins regulated by transcription factors like Hsf1, suggesting divergent evolutionary adaptation to high temperatures.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "comparative analysis between two closely related oyster species (the relatively thermosensitive Crassostrea gigas and the relatively thermotolerant Crassostrea angulata)...C. angulata activated the genes related to anti-apoptosis, DNA damage repair, and fatty acid synthesis...C. gigas...expressed genes related to molecular chaperones, including heat shock protein family...key transcription factor Hsf1, involved in heat shock responses, may contribute to the differential heat response patterns...through divergent expression levels"
More Notes: Oysters face increasing heat stress due to global warming, and this study reveals epigenetic mechanisms behind differential heat tolerance in two oyster species, highlighting key transcription factors and pathways that could inform strategies for oyster conservation and aquaculture.
More Notes-sourcematerial: Oysters, being globally farmed bivalve of significant economic and ecological value, are increasingly confronted with heat stress induced by global warming...Our work will contribute to predicting the adaptive potential of oysters and other marine invertebrates in facing future global warming, and provide a theoretical foundation for genetic improvement of resistance traits and sustainable development of oyster industry.

It does not mention interactions between climate impacts, spatial considerations, different sectors, or sequential events related to climate impacts.

It explores genetic differences that may facilitate long-term adaptation to high temperatures. Findings may help predict adaptive potential and inform genetic improvement strategies.

Paper Title: Future Climate Change in the Northern Bering Sea
Outcome(s) of Interest: Ecosystem reorganization; negative impacts on economic and subsistence food resources; radically low sea ice events (1-3 per decade); need for community planning.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: The Bering Sea is undergoing major changes from increasing winter temperatures to the north, extreme minimum sea-ice years in 2018 and 2019, through to an ecosystem reorganisation and negative impacts on communities' economic and subsistence food resources. ... Of critical importance is the probable reoccurrence of low Bering Sea sea-ice years over the next decades and related ecosystem impacts. We propose that radically low sea ice will have a frequency of one to three 2018-like low sea-ice events per decade in the coming two decades... Communities need to plan for a response to intermittent occurrence of 2018-like extreme sea-ice loss and their ecosystem impacts over the coming decades.
Drivers of Risk: Global warming background; weakened Alaskan Arctic Front (AAF); positive feedback processes (sea-ice loss, warmer air/sea, wavy jet stream, southerly winds); westward shift of Aleutian Low; increasing Arctic temperatures.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: The Bering Sea is undergoing major changes from increasing winter temperatures to the north...These events have emerged under a global warming background, with positive feedback processes through a weakened atmospheric Alaskan Arctic Front (AAF) that promotes a self-reinforcing process of sea-ice loss, warmer air and sea temperatures, a wavy jet stream, and southerly winds...Arctic temperatures to the north are increasing, weakening their contribution to the AAF...A weakened AAF and low sea-ice years needs the winter Aleutian low pressure system to be far to the west of its average position, with southerly rather than northeasterly winds, warm years and low sea-ice extent.
Category-sourcematerial: "ecosystem reorganisation and negative impacts on communities' economic and subsistence food resources...Of critical importance is the probable reoccurrence of low Bering Sea sea-ice years over the next decades and related ecosystem impacts...Communities need to plan for a response to intermittent occurrence of 2018-like extreme sea-ice loss and their ecosystem impacts over the coming decades."
Approach-sourcematerial: Based on a historical meteorological analysis and ensemble climate model projections.
Methodology: Historical meteorological analysis and ensemble climate model projections were used to estimate the future frequency of extreme low sea-ice events in the Bering Sea.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "We propose that radically low sea ice will have a frequency of one to three 2018-like low sea-ice events per decade in the coming two decades, based on a historical meteorological analysis and ensemble climate model projections."
Highlights: The Bering Sea is undergoing major changes due to warming, including extreme sea-ice loss and ecosystem reorganization, exacerbated by a weakened Alaskan Arctic Front. While interannual variability exists, radically low sea ice is projected to occur one to three times per decade in the coming two decades, requiring communities to plan for these intermittent extreme events.
Highlights-sourcematerial: The Bering Sea is undergoing major changes from increasing winter temperatures to the north, extreme minimum sea-ice years in 2018 and 2019, through to an ecosystem reorganisation and negative impacts on communities' economic and subsistence food resources. ... positive feedback processes through a weakened atmospheric Alaskan Arctic Front (AAF) ... Of critical importance is the probable reoccurrence of low Bering Sea sea-ice years over the next decades and related ecosystem impacts. We propose that radically low sea ice will have a frequency of one to three 2018-like low sea-ice events per decade in the coming two decades ... Communities need to plan for a response to intermittent occurrence of 2018-like extreme sea-ice loss and their ecosystem impacts over the coming decades.
More Notes: The Bering Sea is experiencing major changes due to warming, including extreme sea-ice loss, ecosystem reorganization, and impacts on communities. A weakened atmospheric Alaskan Arctic Front (AAF) exacerbates sea-ice loss, but interannual variability, particularly the Aleutian Low, can temporarily support the AAF and restore sea-ice. Radically low sea ice is projected to occur frequently (1-3 events per decade) in the coming decades, requiring communities to plan for extreme sea-ice loss and related ecosystem impacts.
More Notes-sourcematerial: The Bering Sea is undergoing major changes from increasing winter temperatures to the north, extreme minimum sea-ice years in 2018 and 2019, through to an ecosystem reorganisation and negative impacts on communities' economic and subsistence food resources...positive feedback processes through a weakened atmospheric Alaskan Arctic Front (AAF) that promotes a self-reinforcing process of sea-ice loss... Interannual variability is still important: during 2021–2024 the Aleutian Low-pressure system was regionally dominant in supporting a strong AAF, and sea-ice conditions were observed close to the climatological mean... Of critical importance is the probable reoccurrence of low Bering Sea sea-ice years over the next decades and related ecosystem impacts. We propose that radically low sea ice will have a frequency of one to three 2018-like low sea-ice events per decade in the coming two decades...Communities need to plan for a response to intermittent occurrence of 2018-like extreme sea-ice loss and their ecosystem impacts over the coming decades.

Climate features-sourcematerial: These events have emerged under a global warming background, with positive feedback processes through a weakened atmospheric Alaskan Arctic Front (AAF) that promotes a self-reinforcing process of sea-ice loss, warmer air and sea temperatures, a wavy jet stream, and southerly winds. Before 2017, the AAF, consisting of cold dry air mass to the north and moist relatively warm air mass to the south, was a barrier to northward movement of storms, keeping the northern Bering/Chukchi seas with a cold Arctic climate. That historical situation is ending.

Paper Title: Unintended consequences of modifying coastal river systems
Outcome(s) of Interest: San Bernard River mouth closure; Sediment clogging; Reduced river discharge; GIWW conduit for flow; Wave-transported sediment; Altered sediment/flow pathways; Hazards; Costly dredging projects.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "closure of the San Bernard River mouth, located on the central coast of Texas, which was clogged with sediments by the 1990s...GIWW was the main conduit for river flow...reducing discharge in the terminal limb of the river...river mouth became clogged with wave-transported sediment...With a limited connection to the sea, altered sediment and flow pathways have led to numerous hazards and costly corrective dredging projects surpassing $12 million to date."
Drivers of Risk: Channel modification projects (Brazos River diversion, GIWW construction); Reduced river discharge; Wave-transported sediment; Altered sediment and flow pathways.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Coastal infrastructure projects, particularly the modification of coastal river channels...the diversion of the Brazos River channel (1929) and the construction of the Gulf Intracoastal Waterway (GIWW) (1940s)...the GIWW was the main conduit for river flow...reducing discharge in the terminal limb of the river...the river mouth became clogged with wave-transported sediment...altered sediment and flow pathways have led to numerous hazards...
Category-sourcematerial: Due to reduced flow, the river mouth became clogged with wave-transported sediment supplied by the still-adjusting Brazos River which had been diverted to within 6 km of the San Bernard River. With a limited connection to the sea, altered sediment and flow pathways have led to numerous hazards and costly corrective dredging projects surpassing $12 million to date.
Approach-sourcematerial: The objective of this study was to a) document the delayed geomorphic response to the projects using a GIS analysis of historical maps and aerial imagery, and b) provide a snapshot of altered flow pathways in the area using measurements collected in situ.
Methodology: GIS analysis of historical maps and aerial imagery documented the geomorphic response to river channel modification projects; in-situ measurements captured altered flow pathways.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "The objective of this study was to a) document the delayed geomorphic response to the projects using a GIS analysis of historical maps and aerial imagery, and b) provide a snapshot of altered flow pathways in the area using measurements collected in situ."
Highlights: Coastal infrastructure projects like river channel modifications can have delayed and unintended geomorphic consequences, such as the San Bernard River mouth closure in Texas due to reduced flow caused by the Gulf Intracoastal Waterway and sediment from the Brazos River diversion, leading to hazards and costly dredging.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Coastal infrastructure projects, particularly the modification of coastal river channels, can lead to unintended geomorphic changes years or decades later. The closure of the San Bernard River mouth was clogged with sediments by the 1990s due to the diversion of the Brazos River channel and the construction of the Gulf Intracoastal Waterway, which bisects the San Bernard River, reducing discharge. The altered sediment and flow pathways have led to hazards and costly dredging projects.
More Notes: Coastal infrastructure projects like river channel modifications can have unintended long-term geomorphic consequences, such as the San Bernard River mouth closure due to altered sediment flow from the Brazos River diversion and GIWW construction, leading to hazards and costly dredging.

Paper Title: A probable framework for flood-induced risk evaluation for water supply, sanitation and hygiene
Outcome(s) of Interest: Compromised water quality/access, disrupted sanitation, increased waterborne diseases, disproportionate impact on vulnerable communities.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Key findings indicate that floods significantly compromise water quality and access, disrupt sanitation services, and increase the prevalence of waterborne diseases, disproportionately affecting vulnerable communities.
Drivers of Risk: Hydrological: flood extent/intensity (historical data/climate projections); WASH Infrastructure Vulnerability; Socio-economic: community health/livelihoods.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: hydrological modeling to predict flood extents and intensities using historical data and climate projections...GIS-based tools to map vulnerable WASH infrastructure and populations. Socio-economic analysis assesses the impact on community health and livelihoods.
Category-sourcematerial: Floods pose a significant global threat, impacting over 250 million people annually... This study presents a comprehensive framework for evaluating flood-induced risks to WASH systems... Key findings indicate that floods significantly compromise water quality and access, disrupt sanitation services, and increase the prevalence of waterborne diseases...
Approach-sourcematerial: "This study presents a comprehensive framework for evaluating flood-induced risks to WASH systems, integrating hydrological modeling, risk assessment methodologies, and socio-economic analysis to identify and mitigate vulnerabilities." "The framework employs a multi-step approach, starting with hydrological simulations to predict flood extents and intensities using historical data and climate projections. A detailed risk assessment follows, utilizing GIS-based tools to map vulnerable WASH infrastructure and populations. Socio-economic analysis assesses the impact on community health and livelihoods."
Methodology: The methodology involves a comprehensive framework integrating hydrological modeling to predict flood extents and intensities, a risk assessment using GIS-based tools to map vulnerable WASH infrastructure, and socio-economic analysis to evaluate community health and livelihoods, culminating in a decision-support system for prioritizing interventions.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "The framework employs a multi-step approach, starting with hydrological simulations to predict flood extents and intensities using historical data and climate projections. A detailed risk assessment follows, utilizing GIS-based tools to map vulnerable WASH infrastructure and populations. Socio-economic analysis assesses the impact on community health and livelihoods. The framework includes a decision-support system to prioritize interventions based on risk severity and resource availability."
Highlights: The study highlights a comprehensive framework for evaluating flood-induced risks to WASH systems, combining hydrological modeling, risk assessment, and socio-economic analysis to identify vulnerabilities and prioritize interventions to enhance community preparedness and resilience against health risks from floods.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Floods pose a significant global threat, impacting over 250 million people annually...This study presents a comprehensive framework for evaluating flood-induced risks to WASH systems, integrating hydrological modeling, risk assessment methodologies, and socio-economic analysis to identify and mitigate vulnerabilities...The framework includes a decision-support system to prioritize interventions based on risk severity and resource availability...For practitioners and policymakers, the framework provides a robust tool for designing resilient WASH systems, enhancing community preparedness, and reducing flood-related health risks.
More Notes: Floods threaten WASH systems globally, contaminating water, destroying sanitation, disrupting hygiene, and increasing waterborne diseases; a framework integrates hydrological modeling, risk assessment, and socio-economic analysis to mitigate vulnerabilities and inform resilient WASH system design.
More Notes-sourcematerial: Floods pose a significant global threat, impacting over 250 million people annually and causing extensive damage to infrastructure and public health systems. In the Water Supply, Sanitation, and Hygiene (WASH) sector, floods can lead to water contamination, the destruction of sanitation facilities, and the disruption of hygiene practices, thereby increasing the risk of waterborne diseases, which account for approximately 3.4 million deaths each year. This study presents a comprehensive framework for evaluating flood-induced risks to WASH systems, integrating hydrological modeling, risk assessment methodologies, and socio-economic analysis to identify and mitigate vulnerabilities. The framework includes a decision-support system to prioritize interventions based on risk severity and resource availability. The research outcomes offer substantial benefits to both society and the academic community. For practitioners and policymakers, the framework provides a robust tool for designing resilient WASH systems, enhancing community preparedness, and reducing flood-related health risks.

Paper Title: Impact of heat stress on poultry production: Feed intake, water homeostasis, gut integrity, and meat quality
Outcome(s) of Interest: Disrupted production sustainability; impaired water homeostasis, gut integrity, and meat quality; economic losses; welfare concerns.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Heat stress has a negative effect on poultry by disrupting production sustainability, water homeostasis, gut integrity, and meat quality, leading to economic losses and welfare concerns.
Drivers of Risk: Heat stress (HS), high environmental temperatures, climate change.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Page 1: "Heat stress caused by climate change is a major challenge due to rising global temperatures."; Page 2: "Heat stress (HS) is the main driver of risk affecting poultry production."; Page 3: "Heat stress driven by high environmental temperatures is the primary risk affecting poultry health and metabolism."; Page 6: "As climate change continues to drive higher temperatures and more frequent HS events..."
Category-sourcematerial: Heat stress (HS) is a major challenge to the poultry industry ... Heat stress has a negative effect on poultry by disrupting production sustainability, water homeostasis, gut integrity, and meat quality ... this article emphasizes the importance of adopting sustainable and innovative practices to enhance poultry farming’s long-term viability ... Heat stress (HS) impacts poultry production by reducing growth, reproductive efficiency, egg production, and overall production sustainability.
Approach-sourcematerial: Experiments involve exposure to controlled heat stress environments to observe physiological and performance impacts on broiler chickens.
Methodology: Experiments exposing broiler chickens to controlled heat stress environments are conducted to observe impacts on physiological performance, blood chemistry, mRNA expression, and behavior, aiming to understand molecular and inflammatory responses, as well as oxidative status, to devise mitigation strategies.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Beckford, R. C., et al., 2020. Effects of heat stress on performance, blood chemistry, and hypothalamic and pituitary mRNA expression in broiler chickens. Bohler, M., et al., 2020. Highlights: Sprinkler cooling systems can reduce core body temperature in heat-stressed broilers.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Moreover, sprinkler cooling systems have been observed to reduce core body temperature in broilers caused by HS (Liang et al., 2020). This is an innovative mitigation strategy that might be very beneficial for poultry farming.
More Notes: Heat stress mitigation strategies for poultry include selective breeding for heat-tolerant lines, nutritional interventions (e.g., vitamin E, betaine, resveratrol, antioxidants), improved housing conditions, and precision farming technologies to maintain production sustainability and meat quality.

Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Such profound alteration of the global climate is exacerbating the effect of direct human pressure on biodiversity. Paper Title: Land-use competition in 1.5°C climate stabilization: is there enough land for all potential needs?
Outcome(s) of Interest: Land use sufficiency under 1.5°C scenario with proper policies; Increased food consumption; 2.5-3.5 Gha for NBS (3-6 Gt CO2/year); 0.4-0.6 Gha for energy (bioenergy, wind, solar); Requires effective policies and long-term commitments.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Using the MIT Integrated Global System (IGSM) framework we analyze land-use competition in a 1.5°C climate stabilization scenario...we find that to address the numerous trade-offs, effective approaches to nature-based solutions (NBS) and agriculture practices are essential...global land is sufficient to provide increased consumption of food per capita (without large diet changes) over the century while also utilizing 2.5–3.5 billion hectares (Gha) of land for NBS practices that provide a carbon sink of 3–6 gigatonnes (Gt) of CO2 per year as well as 0.4–0.6 Gha of land for energy production—0.2–0.3 Gha for 50–65 exajoules (EJ) per year of bioenergy and 0.2–0.35 Gha for 300–600 EJ/year of wind and solar power generation...attaining this outcome, capable of delivering a 1.5°C future, requires effective policies and measures at national and global levels that promote efficient land use for food, energy and nature (including carbon sequestration) and ensure long-term commitments by decision makers from governments and industry in order to realize the benefits of climate change mitigation.
Drivers of Risk: Land-use competition; bioenergy demand; natural sink demand; sustainable farming/food production needs; ineffective policies; lack of long-term commitments.

Paper Title: County-scale flood risk assessment of properties and associated population in the United States
Outcome(s) of Interest: Flood risk, county scale, eastern/southern U.S., coastal areas, heightened risk, robust response, structural measures, non-structural measures, emergency measures, Social Vulnerability Index, vulnerability indicators, tailored policies
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Flood risk was assessed at the county scale in the United States, focusing on properties and associated populations. The results indicate that counties in the eastern and southern regions of the United States, along with those in the coastal areas, face a heightened risk. This is primarily due to a larger population and a more significant concentration of properties with high hazard scores. However, our analysis also showed that the counties in these regions have very robust responses, including structural, non-structural, and emergency measures, subduing the risk associated with flooding. We also compared our maps with the National Risk Index (NRI), revealing a reasonable correlation between the Social Vulnerability Index (SoVI) and our vulnerability assessment. A notable distinction between the SoVI and our proposed vulnerability framework was our inclusion of unique vulnerability indicators referring to social, ecological, economic, and health factors specific to flooding, suggesting that vulnerability can vary depending on the type of natural hazard. A county-scale approach like this will make policymakers aware of the existing flood risks, which can help make tailored, effective, and locally relevant policies.
Drivers of Risk: Hazard, Exposure, Vulnerability, Population Density, Property Concentration, Flood-prone Regions

This is primarily due to a larger population and a more significant concentration of properties with high hazard scores...inclusion of unique vulnerability indicators referring to social, ecological, economic, and health factors specific to flooding"

A county-scale approach like this will make policymakers aware of the existing flood risks, which can help make tailored, effective, and locally relevant policies.

Additionally, the response was added as a core component to the flood risk framework."

A Self-Organizing Map (SOM) was implemented to carry out unsupervised clustering, which captured the interactions within vulnerability and overall risk components.

Paper Title: Phenologically Corrected Crop Condition Mapping and Assessment with Vegetation Index Time Series
Outcome(s) of Interest: Crop condition assessment, yield prediction, within-season crop monitoring.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "method could facilitate crop yield prediction as well as providing within-season crop condition monitoring information at high spatial and temporal resolution" and "assess crop condition across years at the same growth stages"
Drivers of Risk: Yearly differences in planting dates and climate conditions.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "However, due to the yearly differences in planting dates and climate conditions, averaging VI on the same calendar day may not reflect the general crop condition at the same growth stages."
Category-sourcematerial: Vegetation index (VI) time series from remotely sensed data have been used to assess the crop condition by comparing the current year's conditions with the averaged VI time series over multiple years...In this study, phenologically corrected Enhanced Vegetation Index (EVI2) was generated to assess crop condition across years at the same growth stages...The validation of the assessed crop conditions is performed on the National Agricultural Statistics Service (NASS) Crop Progress & Condition (CPC) gridded layers...Further evaluation of yield anomaly estimates with and without phenological correction indicates that the method could facilitate crop yield prediction as well as providing within-season crop condition monitoring information at high spatial and temporal resolution.
Approach-sourcematerial: "Vegetation index (VI) time series from remotely sensed data have been used to assess the crop condition...phenologically corrected Enhanced Vegetation Index (EVI2) was generated to assess crop condition across years at the same growth stages...Crop emergence dates were first generated using the within-season emergence (WISE) algorithm...Comparisons based on growth degree days (GDD) instead of calendar days were also carried out to reflect the variance in crop development rate."
Methodology: Phenologically corrected Enhanced Vegetation Index (EVI2) generated using the WISE algorithm on Landsat and Sentinel-2 data (2018-2022) was used to align and average EVI2 time series based on crop emergence dates to create a baseline of normal crop condition; crop conditions from other years were then compared to this baseline, with comparisons also made using growth degree days (GDD); validation was performed using NASS Crop Progress & Condition (CPC) gridded layers.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "phenologically corrected Enhanced Vegetation Index (EVI2) was generated to assess crop condition across years at the same growth stages. Crop emergence dates were first generated using the within-season emergence (WISE) algorithm based on routine harmonized Landsat and Sentinel-2 data covering central Iowa. Different years of EVI2 time series from 20182022, generated from the Harmonized Landsat-Sentinel (HLS) dataset were aligned and then averaged based on the crop emergence dates as a baseline of normal crop condition. Then, the crop conditions from other years are assessed are compared with respect to this baseline. Comparisons based on growth degree days (GDD) instead of calendar days were also carried out to reflect the variance in crop development rate. The validation of the assessed crop conditions is performed on the National Agricultural Statistics Service (NASS) Crop Progress & Condition (CPC) gridded layers."
Highlights: Phenologically corrected vegetation index (EVI2), aligned by crop emergence dates and growth degree days (GDD), improves crop condition assessment compared to calendar-day averaging, validated against NASS Crop Progress & Condition data, and shows potential for enhanced yield prediction and within-season monitoring.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Vegetation index (VI) time series from remotely sensed data have been used to assess the crop condition by comparing the current year's conditions with the averaged VI time series over multiple years... due to the yearly differences in planting dates and climate conditions, averaging VI on the same calendar day may not reflect the general crop condition at the same growth stages... phenologically corrected Enhanced Vegetation Index (EVI2) was generated... Crop emergence dates... alignment based on the crop emergence dates as a baseline... Comparisons based on growth degree days (GDD) instead of calendar days were also carried out... validation of the assessed crop conditions is performed on the National Agricultural Statistics Service (NASS) Crop Progress & Condition (CPC) gridded layers... assessment results indicated that they are consistent with the CPC layers... evaluation of yield anomaly estimates... method could facilitate crop yield prediction as well as providing within-season crop condition monitoring.
More Notes: This study uses phenologically corrected vegetation indices to assess crop condition by aligning and comparing multi-year EVI2 time series based on crop emergence dates and growth degree days, validated against NASS data, to improve crop condition monitoring and yield prediction.
More Notes-sourcematerial: Vegetation index (VI) time series from remotely sensed data have been used to assess the crop condition... due to the yearly differences in planting dates and climate conditions, averaging VI on the same calendar day may not reflect the general crop condition at the same growth stages... phenologically corrected Enhanced Vegetation Index (EVI2) was generated to assess crop condition across years at the same growth stages... Comparisons based on growth degree days (GDD) instead of calendar days were also carried out... The validation of the assessed crop conditions is performed on the National Agricultural Statistics Service (NASS) Crop Progress & Condition (CPC) gridded layers... the method could facilitate crop yield prediction as well as providing within-season crop condition monitoring information at high spatial and temporal resolution.

Paper Title: Northern sea route: present climate and future projections

Implementing wide-ranging adaptation measures to reduce the deaths and economic costs of extreme heat waves, droughts, wildfires, hurricanes, and floods Recognizing the unusually rapid warming in the Arctic, which is disrupting weather patterns in the northern hemisphere and currents in the Atlantic Ocean, causing world-wide sea-level rise, and also causing methane leaks in the Arctic region—leaksthat could eventually lead to irreversible global warming consequences. The book examines the national and local governmental policies—and business practices—that are needed in sector-specific chapters. Indeed, a central message is that understanding the issues posed by climate change requires no less than an understanding of climate science, micro-and macro-economics, technologies for mitigation and adaptation measures, as well as politics and law at many governmental levels from local to global.

Paper Title: Machine Learning Algorithm and Multi Sensor Suite for Orbital Power Line Management and Wildfire Prediction
Outcome(s) of Interest: Reduced power-line ignited wildfires; improved energy company maintenance; early responder warnings.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Such a project would help to better inform energy companies of their power lines that are at higher risk of igniting fires to help them focus their maintenance efforts, as well as provide a warning to early responders.
Drivers of Risk: Wildfires, Climate Change, Year-Round Fire Season, Power Line Sparks, Vegetation Contact, Electrical Faults, Increased Power Lines.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Wildfires are a persistent problem across the United States and worldwide, causing devastating damage to land, infrastructure and loss of life. Climate change has turned the once few month long “fire season” to a year-round phenomenon, with a consistent rise in the acreage burned per year. Of the human-driven causes, power line sparked fires may be the most preventable with active monitoring and prediction. Fires ignited by power lines result from vegetation coming into contact with conductors. This can happen when lines droop in high heat and swing into each other or nearby vegetation, when electrical faults cause arcs, or when lines fall to the ground. Although these anomalous scenarios are planned for with designs and regulations, the uncontrolled growth of climate change and increased quantity of power lines makes improved management and monitoring tools necessary for power companies and first responders to handle the problem on a large scale.
Category-sourcematerial: Wildfires are a persistent problem across the United States and worldwide, causing devastating damage to land... Climate change has turned the once few month long “fire season” to a year-round phenomenon, with a consistent rise in the acreage burned per year... Wildfire prediction algorithms currently do not account for power lines themselves... This report will initiate the necessary steps to develop a wildfire predictive algorithm and a remote sensing mission to provide the inputs for it.
Approach-sourcematerial: We propose a continually updating algorithm, designed to pinpoint high-risk areas within power line corridors to identify potential faults and ignition points. ... These readings would be utilized to develop a live-updating fire prediction algorithm. ... We propose an algorithm that takes in inputs from a monitoring mission, including RF emissions and vegetation height, as well as inputs from other sources, such as weather, terrain, and historical fire data.
Methodology: A wildfire prediction algorithm will be developed, incorporating inputs from a proposed satellite platform (RF emission change detection, stereo vegetation height), suborbital platforms (detailed RF data), weather, terrain, and historical fire data, to pinpoint high-risk areas within power line corridors for potential fault and ignition identification.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "We propose a continually updating algorithm, designed to pinpoint high-risk areas within power line corridors to identify potential faults and ignition points...These sources will be primarily found on a proposed satellite platform, as it offers repeated measurements of high risk areas. Such a satellite could measure and monitor RF emissions for changes, take stereo images of vegetation to determine changes in height...change detection could be used in conjunction with a suborbital platforms, such as a drones, to collect more detailed data. These readings would be utilized to develop a live-updating fire prediction algorithm...We propose an algorithm that takes in inputs from a monitoring mission, including RF emissions and vegetation height, as well as inputs from other sources, such as weather, terrain, and historical fire data."
Highlights: Climate change extends fire season year-round in the U.S., increasing power line-induced wildfires; a proposed satellite-based algorithm aims to predict and manage these risks by monitoring RF emissions and vegetation, assisting energy companies and first responders.

Paper Title: Important considerations in machine learning-based landslide susceptibility assessment under future climate conditions
Outcome(s) of Interest: Landslide susceptibility mapping; economic losses and casualties; model performance; valid predictions under projected climate conditions.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Rainfall-induced landslides have caused a large amount of economic losses and casualties over the years...landslide susceptibility over regions of interest...improve the model performance with the imbalanced dataset...the predictions remain valid for the projected climate conditions...landslide susceptibility maps in California, USA are generated using the developed model.
Drivers of Risk: Rainfall; Landslide conditioning factors; Climate change.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "Rainfall-induced landslides have caused a large amount of economic losses and casualties over the years... selection of landslide conditioning factors... extrapolation problems for landslide prediction under future conditions need to be carefully addressed."
Category-sourcematerial: Rainfall-induced landslides have caused a large amount of economic losses and casualties over the years. Machine learning techniques have been widely applied in recent years to assess landslide susceptibility over regions of interest. ... As a case study, landslide susceptibility maps in California, USA are generated using the developed model and are compared with the historical California landslide catalog. These results suggest that the developed model can be of great significance in global landslide susceptibility mapping under climate change scenarios.
Approach-sourcematerial: Machine learning techniques have been widely applied in recent years to assess landslide susceptibility over regions of interest. ... In this work, we introduce methodologies to address these challenges using XGBoost to train the landslide prediction model. Data resampling techniques are adopted to improve the model performance with the imbalanced dataset. ... the extrapolation performance of the XGBoost model is evaluated, showing that the predictions remain valid for the projected climate conditions.
Methodology: XGBoost is used to train a landslide prediction model, addressing data imbalance with resampling techniques like SMOTE combined with gridded hyperspace sampling; model performance is evaluated using various metrics, and extrapolation performance is assessed for projected climate conditions, demonstrated by generating landslide susceptibility maps for California.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "In this work, we introduce methodologies to address these challenges using XGBoost to train the landslide prediction model. Data resampling techniques are adopted to improve the model performance with the imbalanced dataset. Various models are trained and their performances are evaluated using a combination of different metrics. The results show that synthetic minority oversampling technique combined with the proposed gridded hyperspace sampling technique performs better than the other imbalance learning techniques with XGBoost. Subsequently, the extrapolation performance of the XGBoost model is evaluated...As a case study, landslide susceptibility maps in California, USA are generated using the developed model..."
Highlights: Addresses challenges in machine learning-based landslide susceptibility mapping, including class imbalance, factor selection, and extrapolation, using XGBoost and data resampling; demonstrates improved performance and validity under projected climate conditions for California, suggesting potential for global applications.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Rainfall-induced landslides have caused a large amount of economic losses and casualties over the years... a number of challenges limit the reliability and performance of machine learning-based landslide models. Paper Title: Seasonal dynamics of global marine heatwaves over the last four decades
Outcome(s) of Interest: Devastating impacts on marine ecosystem services and socioeconomic systems; economic and ecological losses.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Marine heatwaves (MHWs), prolonged periods of abnormally high sea temperature, have greater devastating impacts on marine ecosystem services and socioeconomic systems than gradual long-term ocean warming...Understanding the seasonality of MHWs can help better formulate corresponding policies to reduce economic and ecological losses caused by these events...
Drivers of Risk: Air-sea heat flux, ENSO (Equatorial Pacific & W. Equatorial Indian Ocean), latitude (mid-high latitudes see increased interseasonal differences).
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "The interseasonal difference is predominantly driven by air-sea heat flux, rather than oceanic horizontal advection and vertical process...In the Equatorial Pacific and Western Equatorial Indian Ocean, intense MHWs are primarily exacerbated by the El Niño-Southern Oscillation (ENSO), which also determines interseasonal variations in MHWs...An increase in these interseasonal differences is observed in mid and high latitudes, with a significant increase in the warm season and a weaker trend in the cold season."
Category-sourcematerial: prolonged periods of abnormally high sea temperature, have greater devastating impacts on marine ecosystem services and socioeconomic systems than gradual long-term ocean warming. Typically, the highest cumulative intensity, characterizing total impacts on ecosystems, occurs during the local warm seasons in most oceans, leading to a significant interseasonal difference between warm and cold seasons. Understanding the seasonality of MHWs can help better formulate corresponding policies to reduce economic and ecological losses caused by these events and can improve the accuracy of future predictions.
Approach-sourcematerial: Using satellite-derived daily sea surface temperature (SST) data from 1982 to 2022, this work reveals a strong seasonality in MHWs. The interseasonal difference is predominantly driven by air-sea heat flux, rather than oceanic horizontal advection and vertical process.
Methodology: Utilizes satellite-derived daily sea surface temperature (SST) data from 1982 to 2022 to analyze interseasonal variations of marine heatwaves.

More Notes: MHWs exhibit strong seasonality, with peak intensity during warm seasons, driven primarily by air-sea heat flux, and influenced by ENSO in the Equatorial Pacific and Western Equatorial Indian Ocean, highlighting the need for seasonal awareness in policy and prediction.

Paper Title: Global warming’s grip on agriculture: Strategies for sustainable production amidst climate change using regression based prediction
Outcome(s) of Interest: Climate impacts on food production (rice, wheat, livestock, milk, eggs, vegetables, fruits); direct correlation between climate variables and production levels; uneven distribution of impacts (developing countries more vulnerable); increased uncertainty in food supply; affects market stability; food safety concerns.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: This study examines the impact of various climate variables—temperature, humidity, precipitation, and soil moisture—on the primary production of essential foods such as rice, wheat, livestock, milk, eggs, vegetables, and fruits...The findings reveal a direct correlation between climate variables and the production levels of vegetables and other food items, highlighting the immediate effects of climatic changes on agriculture. The study also points out the uneven distribution of these climate impacts, with developing countries facing more severe challenges...This uneven impact contributes to increased uncertainty in food supply and affects market stability. Furthermore, concerns about food safety are intensifying under the influence of climate change...This research underscores a complex landscape where the risks and benefits of climate change on food production are not uniformly distributed...

Paper Title: A Review on the Impact of Thermal Stress on Fish Biochemistry
Outcome(s) of Interest: Reduced growth, reproductive impairments, heightened infection susceptibility, mortality, oxidative stress, cell death, organ failure.

Category-sourcematerial: Fish are an important resource for humans, providing food, economic support, and ecological services. However, rising global temperatures and subsequent increases in their habitat water temperature, pose a significant challenge.

Methodology-sourcematerial: We conducted a systematic review to understand the biochemical responses of thermal stress on fish.
Highlights: Rising water temperatures due to climate change induce thermal stress in fish, triggering biochemical responses from neurotransmitter and hormonal levels to organism-wide effects, impacting growth, reproduction, immunity, and survival; fish employ coping mechanisms at molecular levels, but prolonged stress leads to oxidative stress, cell death, and mortality, highlighting the need for further research to inform mitigation strategies.

Paper Title: Temperature Thresholds of Crustaceans in the Age of Climate Change
Outcome(s) of Interest: Collapse of cod fishery, potential lobster fishery collapse, economic consequences, impacts on crustacean fisheries, socioeconomic impacts on shellfish industry.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "This unprecedented ocean warming is contributing to the collapse of the fishery for cod in the region, and similar impacts on the lobster fishery are likely with severe economic consequences...the socioeconomic impacts of climate change (temperature and ocean acidification) on the shellfish industry in Maritime Canada...have been modeled at a fine scale...all Canadian provinces are vulnerable to significant impacts by climate change-caused shifts in crustacean fishery, illustrating the connection between temperature (climate change), crustaceans, and economics."
Drivers of Risk: Greenhouse gas emissions; ocean warming; marine heatwaves (frequency, duration); regional differences (Gulf of Maine - geography, ocean currents, warming rate); warming scenarios; temperature-related changes.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Since the Industrial Revolution, increases in greenhouse gas emissions have not only led to atmospheric warming but also to the warming of the oceans and even marine heat waves (IPCC, 2019; Laufkötter et al., 2020). The frequency and duration of these marine heat waves are increasing globally, with a continuing increase expected (Oliver et al., 2018). There are some regional differences in ocean warming, but all oceans will be affected to some degree (Laufkötter et al., 2020). One of the most pronounced regional differences can be seen in the Gulf of Maine on the northeast coast of the United States. Due to its shallow, semi-enclosed geography, and the respective ocean currents, this body of water is currently warming faster than 99% of the world’s oceans... By 2050 the Gulf of Maine is expected to warm by 1.1°C-2.4°C at the surface and 1.5-2.1°C at the bottom, depending on which warming scenario is applied to the modeling (Brickman et al., 2021)...temperature-related changes will have significant impacts on local and global economies.

Approach-sourcematerial: The source material describes the warming scenarios and modeling applied to the Gulf of Maine, indicating an approach specific to oceanographic and climate modeling, such as "depending on which warming scenario is applied to the modeling" and references modeling socioeconomic impacts like in "the socioeconomic impacts of climate change... have been modeled at a fine scale (Wilson et al., 2020)." These aspects do not directly fall under Econometric, Macro-model, or Stylised categories, hence classified as [Other].
Methodology: Modeling of future ocean warming scenarios was applied to estimate temperature changes in the Gulf of Maine by 2050, and fine-scale modeling was used to assess the socioeconomic impacts of climate change (temperature and ocean acidification) on the shellfish industry in Maritime Canada.

Paper Title: Exposure of Global Rail and Road Infrastructures in Future Record-Breaking Climate Extremes
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased exposure of rail and road infrastructure to extreme heat, thaw-freezing, drought, and heavy rain leading to interruptive losses and additional maintenance costs.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Transport infrastructures face threats under climate change, with extreme heat and thaw-freezing posing great risks. Expected annual exposures of these hazards are multiple times the average of eight hazards, with rising compound events increasing exposure. Mitigation is possible with sustainable pathways, but unavoidable exposure to multiple hazards exists. Adaptation strategies are recommended to avoid losses from record-breaking events.
Drivers of Risk: Extreme temperature, thaw-freezing index ratio, drought, heavy rain compound events, emissions growth, radiative forcing pathways.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "...higher extreme heat and increasing thaw-freezing index ratio pose great threats...the exposure rapidly increases due to sharply increasing drought and heavy rain compound events as emissions growth and development accelerate...Sustainable and lower radiative forcing pathways would contribute to the exposure mitigation..."
Category-sourcematerial: Transport infrastructures built on historical experience are expected to face multiple threats under climate change, especially the continuous interruptive losses and additional maintenance costs caused by more intense or frequent record-breaking extreme climate events...In this study, we investigated the change in the exposure of global rail and road infrastructures to eight record-breaking meteorological hazards using the latest Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 6 (CMIP6) climate data, including extreme temperature and extreme precipitation...Moreover, the exposure rapidly increases due to sharply increasing drought and heavy rain compound events as emissions growth and development accelerate...the fact that most areas will still be affected by multiple hazards is probably unavoidable.
Approach-sourcematerial: "In this study, we investigated the change in the exposure of global rail and road infrastructures to eight record-breaking meteorological hazards using the latest Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 6 (CMIP6) climate data, including extreme temperature and extreme precipitation."
Methodology: The study assessed the changing exposure of global rail and road infrastructures to eight record-breaking meteorological hazards (including extreme temperature and precipitation) using CMIP6 climate data.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "In this study, we investigated the change in the exposure of global rail and road infrastructures to eight record-breaking meteorological hazards using the latest Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 6 (CMIP6) climate data, including extreme temperature and extreme precipitation."
Highlights: Global transport infrastructures are increasingly threatened by record-breaking extreme climate events, with extreme heat and changing thaw-freezing indices posing major risks, while sustainable pathways could mitigate exposures by up to 52%, though adaptation alongside emission reductions remains crucial.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "higher extreme heat and increasing thaw-freezing index ratio pose great threats to global rail and road infrastructures...Sustainable and lower radiative forcing pathways would contribute to the exposure mitigation, with the peak exposure of eight hazards under the SSP1-2.6 scenarios averaging 52% lower...However, the fact that most areas will still be affected by multiple hazards is probably unavoidable. Thus, in parallel with global efforts to reduce greenhouse gas emissions, we recommend that the transport sector incorporate locally appropriate climate change adaptation strategies..."
More Notes: Global rail and road infrastructure face increased exposure to record-breaking extreme heat, thaw-freezing cycles, drought, and heavy rain compound events, necessitating local adaptation strategies alongside emissions reductions.

Paper Title: Evaluating the values and limitations for coral and oyster reefs in coastal disaster risk reduction: a literature review
Outcome(s) of Interest: Coastal disaster risk reduction; economic valuation of reefs; climate change impacts on reefs; socioeconomic disparity; climate-susceptible livelihoods.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "values and limitations of coral and oyster reefs in coastal disaster risk reduction," "economic valuation of oyster reefs in coastal disaster risk reduction is in its infancy," "ongoing and projected global warming circumstances will put coral reefs more at risk than oyster reefs," "severity of the associated consequences for humans will depend on socioeconomic disparity," "Individuals who rely on climate-susceptible livelihoods will suffer the most."
Drivers of Risk: Climate change (global warming), socioeconomic disparity, poor governance, climate-susceptible livelihoods, human influences.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "Due to the lower threshold of environmental tolerance of corals, the ongoing and projected global warming circumstances will put coral reefs more at risk than oyster reefs. The severity of the associated consequences for humans will depend on socioeconomic disparity and poor governance among coastal communities. Individuals who rely on climate-susceptible livelihoods will suffer the most...simulation and modelling studies on the reactions of corals and oyster reefs to short, medium, and long-term projected climate change and human influences are necessary."
Category-sourcematerial: To comprehend and advance this field, we used a detailed review approach to examine 84 relevant previous papers to provide a comprehensive overview of the existing state of knowledge of the values and limitations of coral and oyster reefs in coastal disaster risk reduction.
Approach-sourcematerial: To comprehend and advance this field, we used a detailed review approach to examine 84 relevant previous papers to provide a comprehensive overview of the existing state of knowledge of the values and limitations of coral and oyster reefs in coastal disaster risk reduction.
Methodology: A detailed review approach was utilized to examine 84 relevant previous papers to provide a comprehensive overview of the existing state of knowledge on the values and limitations of coral and oyster reefs in coastal disaster risk reduction.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Previous researchers have made efforts to link the limitations and values of coral and oyster reefs to coastal defence... we used a detailed review approach to examine 84 relevant previous papers to provide a comprehensive overview of the existing state of knowledge of the values and limitations of coral and oyster reefs in coastal disaster risk reduction.
Highlights: Economic valuation of oyster reefs for coastal defense is underdeveloped, coral reefs face greater climate change risks than oyster reefs, and socioeconomic disparity exacerbates human vulnerability; collaborative studies and climate change simulations are needed.
Highlights-sourcematerial: The results show that the literature on the economic valuation of oyster reefs in coastal disaster risk reduction is in its infancy and therefore needs more attention. Due to the lower threshold of environmental tolerance of corals, the ongoing and projected global warming circumstances will put coral reefs more at risk than oyster reefs. The severity of the associated consequences for humans will depend on socioeconomic disparity and poor governance among coastal communities. Individuals who rely on climate-susceptible livelihoods will suffer the most. Additionally, simulation and modelling studies on the reactions of corals and oyster reefs to short, medium, and long-term projected climate change and human influences are necessary.
More Notes: Coral and oyster reefs provide coastal defense, but oyster reef valuation requires more study; coral reefs are more vulnerable to climate change than oyster reefs, disproportionately impacting vulnerable coastal communities, necessitating collaborative studies with local governments and modeling of reef responses to climate change.

Paper Title: Habitability of low-lying socio-ecological systems under a changing climate
Outcome(s) of Interest: Societies' livelihoods, health, well-being, cultures threatened; loss of habitability (land, water, food, infrastructure, economy) due to irreversibility, thresholds, and cascading effects.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Climate change will push the planet worryingly close to its boundaries... climate change does (and will) severely affect societies’ livelihoods, health, well-being, and cultures... habitability in terms of five habitability pillars: land, freshwater, food, settlement and infrastructure, and economic and subsistence activities... severe climate risks: the point of irreversibility of changes, physical and socio-ecological thresholds, and cascading effects across various habitability dimensions.
Drivers of Risk: Climate change, low-lying coastal socio-ecological systems (LCS), habitability pillars (land, freshwater, food, settlement/infrastructure, economic/subsistence activities), social/cultural factors, irreversibility, thresholds, cascading effects.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "Climate change will push the planet worryingly close to its boundaries... This paper discusses the “severe climate risks” concept... Focusing on low-lying coastal socio-ecological systems (LCS)... we argue for a more place- and people-based framing relating to “habitability under a changing climate.” We summarize habitability in terms of five habitability pillars: land, freshwater, food, settlement and infrastructure, and economic and subsistence activities; we acknowledge social and cultural factors... Building on the IPCC AR6 framing of severe climate risks, we discuss three key parameters describing severe climate risks in LCS: the point of irreversibility of changes, physical and socio-ecological thresholds, and cascading effects across various habitability dimensions."
Category-sourcematerial: Focusing on low-lying coastal socio-ecological systems (LCS)...we argue for a more place- and people-based framing relating to “habitability under a changing climate.”...We further develop the habitability framing and examine climate risk to future human health and habitability for three climate “hotspot” archetypes (arctic coasts, atoll islands, densely populated urban areas)...Building on the IPCC AR6 framing of severe climate risks, we discuss three key parameters describing severe climate risks in LCS...We also highlight the variability of severe risk conditions both between coastal archetypes and within each of them.
Approach-sourcematerial: "This paper discusses the “severe climate risks” concept developed under Working Group II’s contribution to the Fifth and Sixth Assessment Reports of the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC, AR5, and AR6)." "Building on the IPCC AR6 framing of severe climate risks, we discuss three key parameters describing severe climate risks in LCS: the point of irreversibility of changes, physical and socio-ecological thresholds, and cascading effects across various habitability dimensions." "Further work should consist of refining the case study framing to find the right balance between capturing context-specificities through real-world local case studies and commonalities derived from more generic archetypes."
Methodology: The study develops a place- and people-based "habitability under a changing climate" framing, using five habitability pillars (land, freshwater, food, settlement & infrastructure, economic & subsistence activities) with underlying social and cultural factors; it then examines climate risk to future human health and habitability for three climate "hotspot" archetypes (arctic coasts, atoll islands, densely populated urban areas), discusses irreversibility, thresholds, and cascading effects, and highlights variability in severe risk conditions between and within coastal archetypes, advocating for refining case studies and identifying methods to assess irreversibility, thresholds, and cascading effects.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Focusing on low-lying coastal socio-ecological systems (LCS) and acknowledging that attempts to define “severe” climate risk have been problematic at the level of global syntheses, we argue for a more place- and people-based framing relating to “habitability under a changing climate.” We summarize habitability in terms of five habitability pillars: land, freshwater, food, settlement and infrastructure, and economic and subsistence activities; we acknowledge social and cultural factors (including perceptions, values, governance arrangements, human agency, power structures) as critical underlying factors rather than as separate pillars. We further develop the habitability framing and examine climate risk to future human health and habitability for three climate “hotspot” archetypes (arctic coasts, atoll islands, densely populated urban areas). Building on the IPCC AR6 framing of severe climate risks, we discuss three key parameters describing severe climate risks in LCS: the point of irreversibility of changes, physical and socio-ecological thresholds, and cascading effects across various habitability dimensions. We also highlight the variability of severe risk conditions both between coastal archetypes and within each of them. Further work should consist of refining the case study framing to find the right balance between capturing context-specificities through real-world local case studies and commonalities derived from more generic archetypes. In addition, there is a need to identify appropriate methods to assess irreversibility, thresholds, and cascading effects, and thus severe climate risks to habitability.
Highlights: Focuses on severe climate risks in low-lying coastal socio-ecological systems (LCS) using a habitability framing based on five pillars: land, freshwater, food, settlement/infrastructure, and economic/subsistence activities, considering social and cultural factors; examines risks to health and habitability in Arctic coasts, atoll islands, and urban areas; discusses irreversibility, thresholds, and cascading effects; and emphasizes the variability of risks and the need for refined case studies and methods for assessing severe climate risks.

Further work should consist of refining the case study framing to find the right balance between capturing context-specificities through real-world local case studies and commonalities derived from more generic archetypes. In addition, there is a need to identify appropriate methods to assess irreversibility, thresholds, and cascading effects, and thus severe climate risks to habitability."
