Paper Title: Urban growth modeling for the assessment of future climate and disaster risks: approaches, gaps and needs
Outcome(s) of Interest: Future risk trajectories, risk-informed planning, adaptation decisions.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: A detailed understanding of whether, where and how cities are growing within or into hazard-prone areas is an urgent prerequisite for assessing future risk trajectories, risk-informed planning, and adaptation decisions.
Drivers of Risk: Urbanization, climate change, hazard exposure, socio-economic changes, urban growth.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Urban climate-related disaster risks are set to rise, driven by the interaction of two global megatrends: urbanization and climate change. [...] This analysis has been mostly neglected to date, as most climate change and disaster risk research has focused on the assessment of future hazard trends but less on the assessment of how socio-economic changes affect future hazard exposure. Urban growth and expansion modeling provide a powerful tool, given that urban growth is a major driver of future disaster risk in cities.
Category-sourcematerial: Urban climate-related disaster risks are set to rise, driven by the interaction of two global megatrends: urbanization and climate change...a major driver of future disaster risk in cities...future-oriented urban risk assessment and the planning of adaptation measures.
Approach-sourcematerial: "Urban growth and expansion modeling provide a powerful tool, given that urban growth is a major driver of future disaster risk in cities. The paper reviews the achievements lately made in urban growth and exposure modeling and assesses how they can be applied in the context of future-oriented urban risk assessment and the planning of adaptation measures."
Methodology: Urban growth and expansion modeling is identified as a powerful tool for urban risk assessment, highlighting persistent methodological challenges including integrating urban morphology, long-term time horizons, feedbacks between urbanization and hazard trends, integrating future urban growth drivers and adaptation responses, feedbacks between adaptation and urbanization, and utilizing a common scenario framework.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Urban growth and expansion modeling provide a powerful tool, given that urban growth is a major driver of future disaster risk in cities...It also analyses which methodological challenges persist in urban growth and exposure modeling and how they might be overcome. These points pertain particularly to the need to consider and integrate (1) urban morphology patterns and potential linkages to exposure as well as vulnerability, (2) long-term time horizons to consider long-term developments, (3) feedbacks between urbanization trajectories and hazard trends, (4) the integration of future urban growth drivers and adaptation responses, (5) feedbacks between adaptation and urbanization, and (6) scenarios, which are developed within a commonly defined scenario framework.
Highlights: Urban climate-related disaster risks are increasing due to urbanization and climate change; understanding urban growth in hazard-prone areas is crucial for risk assessment and adaptation; urban growth and exposure modeling is a useful tool; key challenges include integrating urban morphology, long-term time horizons, feedbacks between urbanization and hazards, growth drivers, adaptation responses, and commonly defined scenarios.

Paper Title: Forecasting freshwater cyanobacterial harmful algal blooms for Sentinel-3 satellite resolved U.S. lakes and reservoirs
Outcome(s) of Interest: Near-term prediction of cyanoHAB occurrence, exceeding WHO Alert Level 1 (>12 μg L−1 chlorophyll-a with cyanobacteria dominance).
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Freshwater cyanoHABs may grow to excessive concentrations and cause human, animal, and environmental health concerns in lakes and reservoirs. Knowledge of the timing and location of cyanoHAB events is important for water quality management of recreational and drinking water systems. An Integrated Nested Laplace Approximation (INLA) hierarchical Bayesian spatiotemporal model was applied to forecast World Health Organization (WHO) recreation Alert Level 1 exceedance >12 μg L−1 chlorophyll-a with cyanobacteria dominance for 2192 satellite resolved lakes in the United States across nine climate zones.
Drivers of Risk: Water temperature, precipitation, lake geomorphology, cyanobacterial growth.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Relevant predictors included water surface temperature, precipitation, and lake geomorphology...Predictors were limited to data sources relevant to cyanobacterial growth...
Category-sourcematerial: Freshwater cyanoHABs may grow to excessive concentrations and cause human, animal, and environmental health concerns in lakes and reservoirs. Knowledge of the timing and location of cyanoHAB events is important for water quality management of recreational and drinking water systems.
Approach-sourcematerial: Weekly cyanobacteria abundance was quantified from the Ocean and Land Colour Instrument (OLCI) onboard the Sentinel-3 satellite as the response variable. An Integrated Nested Laplace Approximation (INLA) hierarchical Bayesian spatiotemporal model was applied to forecast World Health Organization (WHO) recreation Alert Level 1 exceedance >12 μg L−1 chlorophyll-a with cyanobacteria dominance for 2192 satellite resolved lakes in the United States across nine climate zones. The INLA model was compared against support vector classifier and random forest machine learning models; and Dense Neural Network, Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), Recurrent Neural Network (RNN), and Gneural Network (GNU) neural network models.
Methodology: A hierarchical Bayesian spatiotemporal model (INLA) was developed to forecast exceedance of WHO Alert Level 1 for cyanobacterial blooms in 2192 U.S. lakes using satellite-derived cyanobacteria abundance, water surface temperature, precipitation, and lake geomorphology as predictors; model performance was compared to machine learning and neural network models, with INLA demonstrating superior accuracy.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "An Integrated Nested Laplace Approximation (INLA) hierarchical Bayesian spatiotemporal model was applied to forecast World Health Organization (WHO) recreation Alert Level 1 exceedance >12 μg L−1 chlorophyll-a with cyanobacteria dominance for 2192 satellite resolved lakes in the United States across nine climate zones...Predictors were limited to data sources relevant to cyanobacterial growth, readily available on a weekly basis, and at the national scale for operational forecasting. Relevant predictors included water surface temperature, precipitation, and lake geomorphology...The INLA model was compared against support vector classifier and random forest machine learning models; and Dense Neural Network, Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), Recurrent Neural Network (RNN), and Gneural Network (GNU) neural network models...Overall, the INLA model outperformed the machine learning and neural network models..."
Highlights: The INLA model accurately forecasts cyanoHAB events in U.S. lakes using satellite data and environmental predictors, outperforming other models with 90% accuracy, useful for water quality management.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "This forecasting approach may be useful for water managers and associated public health managers to predict near-term future high-risk cyanobacterial harmful algal blooms (cyanoHAB) occurrence...The INLA model outperformed the machine learning and neural network models with prediction accuracy of 90% with 88% sensitivity, 91% specificity, and 49% precision...Knowledge of the timing and location of cyanoHAB events is important for water quality management of recreational and drinking water systems."
More Notes: A spatiotemporal Bayesian model using satellite data, water temperature, precipitation, and lake geomorphology can forecast harmful algal blooms across the US with 90% accuracy, outperforming machine learning and neural network models.

Relevant predictors included water surface temperature, precipitation, and lake geomorphology.

Paper Title: Climate attribution time series track the evolution of human influence on North Pacific sea surface temperature
Outcome(s) of Interest: Human influence on regional ocean temperatures; extreme SST values; negative warming effects on sockeye fishery catches.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "...human influence on regional ocean temperatures could first be detected in the 1970s and grew until 2014-2020 temperatures showed overwhelming evidence of human contribution...Extreme regional SST values that were expected every 1000-10 000 years in the preindustrial climate are expected every 5-40 years in the current climate...We link negative warming effects on sockeye fishery catches to increasing human influence on regional temperatures..."
Drivers of Risk: Human influence, North Pacific sea surface temperature, anthropogenic warming, ecosystem services, sockeye salmon productivity, extreme SST anomalies, shifting baseline syndrome, Bayesian attribution, climate models, RR and FAR values.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Page 1: "The analysis highlights human influence on North Pacific sea surface temperatures as the main driver of risk with implications for ecosystem services."; Page 2: "Human influence on climate and ocean warming affecting SST."; Page 4: "Ocean temperature (three-year running mean SST) is identified as a key driver affecting salmon productivity."; Page 5: "Human influence on climate change is identified as a risk impacting sea surface temperature anomalies."; Page 6: "The shocks or changes impacting the outcomes include anthropogenic influence leading to increased frequency and intensity of SST anomalies."; Page 7: "Changes in sea surface temperature, particularly the increase beginning from the late 1990s to early 2000s, are driving changes in risk."; Page 8: "Increases in sea surface temperature and anthropogenic warming affecting sockeye salmon catch anomalies.", "The gradual increase in human influence documented by our attribution time series illustrates the ‘shifting baseline’ syndrome."; Page 9: "Increased North Pacific temperatures, particularly SST extremes, are driving changes in sockeye salmon productivity.", "Our results also show that the evidence for human influence on North Pacific temperatures is consistently stronger for three-year running mean SST than for annual SST values."; Page 15: "Human-induced global warming and prolonged marine heatwaves affecting marine ecosystems."
Category-sourcematerial: Page 1: We use the Gulf of Alaska sockeye salmon fishery to show how attribution timeseries may be used to contextualize the impacts of human-induced ocean warming on ecosystem services. We link negative warming effects on sockeye fishery catches to increasing human influence on regional temperatures (increasing FAR values), and we find that sockeye salmon migrating to sea in years with the strongest evidence for human effects on temperature (FAR ≥ 0.98) produce catches 1.4 standard deviations below the long-term log mean.
Approach-sourcematerial:
Page 1: The study uses Bayesian estimates of the Fraction of Attributable Risk (FAR) and Risk Ratio (RR) derived from 23 global climate models to track the growth of human influence on ocean temperatures over the past seven decades (1950–2022).
Page 2: Analysis performed using Fraction of Attributable Risk (FAR) and Risk Ratio (RR) statistics, applied to SST observations from 1950-2022, using 23 CMIP6 models and pre-industrial baseline comparisons.
Page 4: Bayesian regression models are used to confirm the negative relationship between ocean warming and sockeye productivity, weighted by the average year of ocean entry for each harvest year. We use the Gulf of Alaska commercial sockeye salmon (Oncorhynchus nerka) fishery as a case study to show how attribution time series can contextualize the human role in observed climate change impacts on ecosystem services.
Page 10: The analysis uses Bayesian smoothed regression to estimate warming and defines 'present' conditions relative to North Pacific warming for a 15-year window centered on the observation year. CMIP6 models are used with a model weighting approach based on skill and independence, considering bias, variability, autocorrelation, and the trend in SST.
Methodology: The study employs Bayesian estimates of Fraction of Attributable Risk (FAR) and Risk Ratio (RR) derived from 23 CMIP6 global climate models to assess human influence on sea surface temperatures (SST) in five regional North Pacific ecosystems from 1950-2022, using pre-industrial baselines. Model selection incorporates weighting criteria based on SST bias, trend, interannual variability, and autocorrelation. Bayesian regression models confirm the negative relationship between ocean warming and sockeye productivity and relate fishery harvest to FAR values. Extreme event return times are evaluated.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Page 1: "Using Bayesian estimates of the Fraction of Attributable Risk (FAR) and Risk Ratio (RR) derived from 23 global climate models..."; Page 2: "Analysis performed using Fraction of Attributable Risk (FAR) and Risk Ratio (RR) statistics, applied to SST observations from 1950-2022, using 23 CMIP6 models and pre-industrial baseline comparisons."; Page 3: "Model selection and evaluation incorporate four weighting criteria: SST bias, trend, interannual variability, and autocorrelation."; Page 4: "Bayesian regression models are used to (1) confirm the negative relationship between ocean warming and sockeye productivity and (2) relate fishery harvest for salmon entering the ocean to fraction of attributable risk (FAR) values..."; Page 3: "Extreme event return times are evaluated to assess the likelihood of extreme climate events."
Highlights: Human influence on North Pacific sea surface temperatures (SST) is overwhelming and detectable from the 1970s, rising significantly by 2014-2020 with critical implications for ecosystems, notably the Gulf of Alaska sockeye salmon fishery. Regional temperatures now largely reflect anthropogenic activity, with FAR values exceeding 0.98 across all five regions. Extreme SST values that were rare preindustrial events are now frequent. Notably, Northern regions like the Eastern Bering Sea show high attributable risks and are highly affected, pointing to critical adaptation needs for fisheries.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Attribution time series track the evolution of human influence on sea surface temperature, showing overwhelming evidence of human contribution by 2014–2020. For the entire North Pacific, FAR and RR values show that temperatures have reached levels that were likely impossible in the preindustrial climate. Regional results indicate the strongest evidence for human influence in the northernmost ecosystems (Eastern Bering Sea and Gulf of Alaska), though all regions showed FAR values > 0.98 for at least one year. Extreme regional SST values that were expected every 1000–10000 years in the preindustrial climate are expected every 5–40 years in the current climate. Ocean warming has profound consequences for fisheries production and other critical ecosystem services globally. All five regions, plus the overall North Pacific, have reached temperatures that can be attributed to human activities with high confidence (i.e. FAR > 0.98). The strongest evidence for anthropogenic influence is seen in the northernmost region (Eastern Bering Sea).
More Notes: Presents a particular adaptation challenge due to a persistent trend...reaching levels indicated by RR values.
More Notes-sourcematerial: Page 8: More Notes: "Presents a particular adaptation challenge due to a persistent trend...reaching levels indicated by RR values."
Hazard definition-sourcematerial: Page 1: We apply climate attribution techniques to sea surface temperature time series from five regional North Pacific ecosystems to track the growth in human influence on ocean temperatures over the past seven decades (1950–2022).
Impact persistence-sourcematerial: Our results indicate that deleterious temperatures should be expected as often as not in the current climate, and that temperatures that appear necessary for healthy catches will become rare once North Pacific warming exceeds 1.5 °C....The gradual increase in human influence documented by our attribution time series illustrates the ‘shifting baseline’ syndrome....Our results also show that the evidence for human influence on North Pacific temperatures is consistently stronger for three-year running mean SST than for annual SST values...But this result also suggests an important ecological insight... fish populations are typically sensitive to climate at multiple life history stages, suggesting they are more responsive to multi-year perturbations than shorter events.

Extreme regional SST values that were expected every 1000–10000 years in the preindustrial climate are expected every 5–40 years in the current climate...relating declining productivity of fish stocks to increasing human influence over regional temperatures may provide information about declining fisheries sustainability, a critical adaptation challenge for stakeholders...our estimates show negligible uncertainty for regional FAR time series from roughly the 1990s onwards, indicating that model uncertainty plays little role in our conclusions regarding anthropogenic influence on SST in recent decades...CMIP6 model outputs for calculating FAR and RR values were weighted...rewards model skill and independence...North Pacific RR values have exceeded 10 5 for both annual and three-year mean SST; Eastern Bering Sea RR values have exceeded 10 4...The strongest evidence for anthropogenic influence is seen in the northernmost region (Eastern Bering Sea)...attribution timeseries for the North Pacific Ocean and regional ecosystems: multi-model estimates of the Risk Ratio (RR) for annual and three-year running mean sea surface temperature (SST) with 95% credible intervals, 1950–2022. "Our results also show that the evidence for human influence on North Pacific temperatures is consistently stronger for three-year running mean SST than for annual SST values...But this result also suggests an important ecological insight... fish populations are typically sensitive to climate at multiple life history stages, suggesting they are more responsive to multi-year perturbations than shorter events."

"At-sea distribution data suggest that warming since 2014 may be approaching a tipping point at which the envelope of preferred temperatures for sockeye salmon will no longer be available in the Gulf of Alaska, particularly in winter."

Adaptation considered: The study uses the Gulf of Alaska sockeye salmon fishery as a case study, linking negative impacts on catches to increasing human influence on regional temperatures. It proposes that attribution statistics in ecosystem models may help inform adaptation decision-making. The analysis acknowledges that warming since 2014 might be approaching a tipping point for sockeye salmon, and that temperatures necessary for healthy catches may become rare with further warming.
Adaptation considered-sourcematerial: "We use the Gulf of Alaska sockeye salmon fishery to show how attribution timeseries may be used to contextualize the impacts of human-induced ocean warming on ecosystem services... Our results also show that the evidence for human influence on North Pacific temperatures is consistently stronger for three-year running mean SST than for annual SST values... fish populations are typically sensitive to climate at multiple life history stages... At-sea distribution data suggest that warming since 2014 may be approaching a tipping point at which the envelope of preferred temperatures for sockeye salmon will no longer be available in the Gulf of Alaska, particularly in winter... Our results indicate that deleterious temperatures should be expected as often as not in the current climate, and that temperatures that appear necessary for healthy catches will become rare once North Pacific warming exceeds 1.5 °C... We propose that using an attribution statistic as an explanatory variable in an ecosystem services model, rather than SST or a heatwave index, may provide valuable context for adaptation decision-making."

Paper Title: Archived DNA reveals marine heatwave-associated shifts in fish assemblages
Outcome(s) of Interest: Tropicalization, mesopelagic species increase, temperate species decline, fisheries assemblage shifts, ecosystem impacts, species-specific abundance estimates.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Analysis combining microscopic observations with environmental DNA (eDNA) metabarcoding...captures major and sometimes unexpected changes to fish assemblages...during and after the 2014–2016 Pacific Marine Heatwave. Joint modeling efforts reveal patterns of tropicalization with increases in southern, mesopelagic species and associated declines in commercially important temperate fish species (e.g., North Pacific Hake [Merluccius productus] and Pacific Sardine [Sardinops sagax]). Data show shifts in fisheries assemblages (e.g., Northern Anchovy, Engraulis mordax) even after the return to average water temperatures, corroborating ecosystem impacts...yield higher-resolution, species-specific quantitative abundance estimates.
Drivers of Risk: Marine heatwave, tropicalization, temperature increase.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Marine heatwaves can drive large-scale shifts in marine ecosystems...during and after the 2014–2016 Pacific Marine Heatwave. Joint modeling efforts reveal patterns of tropicalization with increases in southern, mesopelagic species...even after the return to average water temperatures...
Category-sourcematerial: Data show shifts in fisheries assemblages (e.g., Northern Anchovy, Engraulis mordax) even after the return to average water temperatures, corroborating ecosystem impacts found through multiple traditional surveys of this study area.
Approach-sourcematerial: Joint modeling efforts reveal patterns of tropicalization...Our innovative approach of metabarcoding preservative eDNA coupled with quantitative modeling leverages the taxonomic breadth and resolution of DNA sequences combined with microscopy-derived ichthyoplankton identification to yield higher-resolution, species-specific quantitative abundance estimates.
Methodology: The study combined microscopic observations of ichthyoplankton with environmental DNA (eDNA) metabarcoding of ethanol preservative from a 23-year time series to analyze fish assemblage changes; quantitative modeling then leveraged DNA sequence data and microscopy-derived ichthyoplankton identification to estimate species-specific abundance.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Analysis combining microscopic observations with environmental DNA (eDNA) metabarcoding of the ethanol preservative of an ichthyoplankton biorepository spanning a 23 years time series captures major and sometimes unexpected changes to fish assemblages...Our innovative approach of metabarcoding preservative eDNA coupled with quantitative modeling leverages the taxonomic breadth and resolution of DNA sequences combined with microscopy-derived ichthyoplankton identification to yield higher-resolution, species-specific quantitative abundance estimates.
Highlights: Marine heatwaves caused tropicalization of fish assemblages in the California Current, with increases in southern, mesopelagic species and declines in temperate commercial fish, even after temperatures normalized; eDNA metabarcoding of ichthyoplankton biorepositories offers a high-resolution method for reconstructing historical assemblage dynamics.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Marine heatwaves can drive large-scale shifts in marine ecosystems... Analysis combining microscopic observations with environmental DNA (eDNA) metabarcoding... captures major and sometimes unexpected changes to fish assemblages in the California Current Large Marine Ecosystem during and after the 2014–2016 Pacific Marine Heatwave. Joint modeling efforts reveal patterns of tropicalization with increases in southern, mesopelagic species and associated declines in commercially important temperate fish species... Data show shifts in fisheries assemblages... even after the return to average water temperatures... Our innovative approach of metabarcoding preservative eDNA coupled with quantitative modeling leverages the taxonomic breadth and resolution of DNA sequences combined with microscopy-derived ichthyoplankton identification to yield higher-resolution, species-specific quantitative abundance estimates. This work opens the door to economically reconstruct the historical dynamics of assemblages from modern and archived samples worldwide.
More Notes: The 2014-2016 Pacific Marine Heatwave caused tropicalization of California Current fish assemblages, with southern and mesopelagic species increasing while commercially important temperate species declined, and these shifts persisted even after water temperatures normalized.
More Notes-sourcematerial: "Analysis combining microscopic observations with environmental DNA (eDNA) metabarcoding of the ethanol preservative of an ichthyoplankton biorepository spanning a 23 years time series captures major and sometimes unexpected changes to fish assemblages in the California Current Large Marine Ecosystem during and after the 2014–2016 Pacific Marine Heatwave. Joint modeling efforts reveal patterns of tropicalization with increases in southern, mesopelagic species and associated declines in commercially important temperate fish species (e.g., North Pacific Hake [Merluccius productus] and Pacific Sardine [Sardinops sagax]). Data show shifts in fisheries assemblages (e.g., Northern Anchovy, Engraulis mordax) even after the return to average water temperatures, corroborating ecosystem impacts found through multiple traditional surveys of this study area."
Hazard definition-sourcematerial: Marine heatwaves can drive large-scale shifts in marine ecosystems...the 2014–2016 Pacific Marine Heatwave.
Impact persistence-sourcematerial: Data show shifts in fisheries assemblages (e.g., Northern Anchovy, Engraulis mordax) even after the return to average water temperatures, corroborating ecosystem impacts found through multiple traditional surveys of this study area.
Space disaggregation-sourcematerial: "fish assemblages in the California Current Large Marine Ecosystem"
Impact interactions-sourcematerial: The abstract focuses on species assemblage shifts due to marine heatwaves, specifically highlighting the increase in southern species and decline in temperate species. It does not explicitly mention or imply any interactions *between* different climate impacts, focusing instead on the direct impact of the heatwave on the ecosystem.
Economic dynamics-sourcematerial: There is no mention of economic factors, economic modeling, or how the economy might dynamically respond to these ecological changes.
Climate features-sourcematerial: Data show shifts in fisheries assemblages (e.g., Northern Anchovy, Engraulis mordax) even after the return to average water temperatures, corroborating ecosystem impacts found through multiple traditional surveys of this study area.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is considered indirectly, as the paper focuses on understanding shifts in marine ecosystems due to marine heatwaves, highlighting changes in fish assemblages that could inform fisheries management. No specific adaptation strategies are discussed, but the research approach could contribute to adaptation planning by providing detailed data on species dynamics and ecosystem changes.
Adaptation considered-sourcematerial: Analysis combining microscopic observations with environmental DNA (eDNA) metabarcoding ... captures major and sometimes unexpected changes to fish assemblages in the California Current Large Marine Ecosystem during and after the 2014–2016 Pacific Marine Heatwave. Joint modeling efforts reveal patterns of tropicalization with increases in southern, mesopelagic species and associated declines in commercially important temperate fish species. ... Data show shifts in fisheries assemblages ... even after the return to average water temperatures, corroborating ecosystem impacts found through multiple traditional surveys of this study area.

Paper Title: Integration as Adaptation: Advancing Research and Practice for Inclusive Climate Receiving Communities
Outcome(s) of Interest: Effective integration of newcomers into receiving communities.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Based on our review, we found that migration is only adaptation when newcomers are effectively integrated into receiving communities.
Drivers of Risk: Longer term climatic shifts, highly visible disasters.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: In North America, highly visible disasters have already caused populations to relocate, but historically, larger scale migration has occurred in response to longer term climatic shifts.
Category-sourcematerial: To lay the groundwork for this emerging area of research and practice, we draw from adjacent areas of planning. We examine current research on immigrant integration and amenity migration, recent media coverage of climate destinations, research on shifts in physical livability, and the emerging body of work on receiving communities.
Approach-sourcematerial: "We examine current research on immigrant integration and amenity migration, recent media coverage of climate destinations, research on shifts in physical livability, and the emerging body of work on receiving communities."
Methodology: Draws from research on immigrant integration and amenity migration, media coverage of climate destinations, research on shifts in physical livability, and emerging work on receiving communities, confined geographically to North America and focused on recent research.
Methodology-sourcematerial: We draw from adjacent areas of planning. We examine current research on immigrant integration and amenity migration, recent media coverage of climate destinations, research on shifts in physical livability, and the emerging body of work on receiving communities. Limitations to our study include a focus on recent research and confining the geographical scope to North America.
Highlights: Climate-related internal relocation is transforming the Global North, particularly North America, requiring planners to reconcile social inclusion with economic development, meet the distinct needs of newcomers (which may exacerbate existing resident needs), and pursue long-term planning for deconcentration through participatory processes.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "Despite early focus on the Global South, it has become clear that the Global North will be transformed through climate-related internal relocation as well...As relocation to destinations spanning small towns to metropolitan gateways accelerates, planning can play a central role in shaping how these places accommodate newcomers and prepare for longer term urban restructuring...migration is only adaptation when newcomers are effectively integrated into receiving communities. This can occur through reconciling social inclusion with economic development, meeting the distinct needs of newcomers that may also exacerbate the needs of current residents, and pursuing long-term planning for deconcentration...planners should focus on reconciling social inclusion and economic development and engaging newcomers and existing residents in participatory processes to develop physical and social resilience."
More Notes: Climate-related relocation in North America requires planning that integrates newcomers into receiving communities through social inclusion, economic development, and participatory processes to build physical and social resilience.
More Notes-sourcematerial: "As climate-related migration becomes part of adaptation practice, planners should focus on reconciling social inclusion and economic development and engaging newcomers and existing residents in participatory processes to develop physical and social resilience." ; "Based on our review, we found that migration is only adaptation when newcomers are effectively integrated into receiving communities. This can occur through reconciling social inclusion with economic development, meeting the distinct needs of newcomers that may also exacerbate the needs of current residents, and pursuing long-term planning for deconcentration."
Hazard definition-sourcematerial: The abstract discusses climate-related internal relocation in North America driven by both "highly visible disasters" and "longer term climatic shifts," but does not specify which hazards were explicitly analyzed in the paper. It focuses on the migration and planning aspects rather than detailing the specific climate hazards themselves.
Impact persistence-sourcematerial: Based on our review, we found that migration is only adaptation when newcomers are effectively integrated into receiving communities. This can occur through reconciling social inclusion with economic development, meeting the distinct needs of newcomers that may also exacerbate the needs of current residents, and pursuing long-term planning for deconcentration.
Space disaggregation-sourcematerial: Despite early focus on the Global South, it has become clear that the Global North will be transformed through climate-related internal relocation as well. In North America, highly visible disasters have already caused populations to relocate... Limitations to our study include a focus on recent research and confining the geographical scope to North America.
Impact interactions-sourcematerial: The abstract focuses on climate-related migration and the integration of newcomers into receiving communities in North America. It discusses social inclusion, economic development, and long-term planning for resilience, but it doesn't mention interactions between different types of direct climate impacts.
Economic dynamics-sourcematerial: Problem, research strategy, and findings: Despite early focus on the Global South, it has become clear that the Global North will be transformed through climate-related internal relocation as well. ... As relocation to destinations spanning small towns to metropolitan gateways accelerates, planning can play a central role in shaping how these places accommodate newcomers and prepare for longer term urban restructuring. ... Based on our review, we found that migration is only adaptation when newcomers are effectively integrated into receiving communities. This can occur through reconciling social inclusion with economic development, meeting the distinct needs of newcomers that may also exacerbate the needs of current residents, and pursuing long-term planning for deconcentration.
Climate features-sourcematerial: The abstract focuses on climate-related migration and adaptation strategies for receiving communities in North America, with no mention of tipping points or other climate complexities.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is considered through the lens of climate-related migration and relocation to new communities. Effective integration of newcomers is key for migration to be a successful adaptation strategy. Planning can play a central role in shaping how destinations accommodate newcomers and prepare for longer-term urban restructuring. Focus is on social inclusion, economic development, meeting distinct needs of newcomers (that may exacerbate needs of current residents), and long-term planning for deconcentration as important facets of adaptation in receiving communities.

Paper Title: Drought responses of traditional and modern wheats in different phenological stages
Outcome(s) of Interest: Reduced yield; altered physio-morphological and quality traits; landraces show drought resilience.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Drought treatments reduced yield and some physio-morphological traits and increased quality traits...Modern wheat cultivars had higher grain yields compared to landraces, but some landraces had a lower yield loss compared to modern wheats under drought stress.
Drivers of Risk: Drought, changing climatic conditions, seasonal drought (pre-heading, post-heading), reduced yield, varying responses between landraces and modern varieties.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "Drought is one of the most pressing issues in many regions worldwide...The genotypes were subjected to seasonal drought treatments under rain shelters, including pre-heading drought (T1), post-heading drought (T2), irrigation based on long-term precipitation (T3), and seasonal precipitation and supplemental irrigation (T4)...Drought treatments reduced yield and some physio-morphological traits and increased quality traits...Pre-heading drought treatments had the greatest effect on yield and physio-morphological traits while post-heading drought treatments had the greatest effect on quality traits...Modern wheat cultivars had higher grain yields compared to landraces, but some landraces had a lower yield loss compared to modern wheats under drought stress."
Category-sourcematerial: "Selecting wheat genotypes adapted to changing climatic conditions is crucial for sustainable agricultural production, economic development, and food security."
Approach-sourcematerial: The genotypes were subjected to seasonal drought treatments under rain shelters, including pre-heading drought (T1), post-heading drought (T2), irrigation based on long-term precipitation (T3), and seasonal precipitation and supplemental irrigation (T4). Grain yield and physio-morphological and quality traits of the genotypes under seasonal drought and irrigated conditions were evaluated.
Methodology: 156 bread wheat genotypes (landraces from Turkey, Iran, Afghanistan, and modern varieties) were evaluated under rain shelters with pre-heading drought, post-heading drought, irrigation based on long-term precipitation, and seasonal precipitation with supplemental irrigation. Grain yield, physio-morphological, and quality traits were assessed under these conditions.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "In this study, 156 bread wheat genotypes, including landraces from Turkey, Iran, and Afghanistan... and high-yielding modern wheat varieties... were evaluated. The genotypes were subjected to seasonal drought treatments under rain shelters, including pre-heading drought (T1), post-heading drought (T2), irrigation based on long-term precipitation (T3), and seasonal precipitation and supplemental irrigation (T4). Grain yield and physio-morphological and quality traits of the genotypes under seasonal drought and irrigated conditions were evaluated."
Highlights: Wheat landraces from Turkey, Iran, and Afghanistan show potential for breeding drought-tolerant varieties due to lower yield loss under drought stress compared to modern cultivars, despite modern cultivars generally having higher yields; landraces also exhibited advantageous traits like higher protein content and greater biomass under drought.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "Modern wheat cultivars had higher grain yields compared to landraces, but some landraces had a lower yield loss compared to modern wheats under drought stress. The higher tillering rate of the landraces resulted in greater biomass production...Landraces had higher protein content in all treatments...given that different drought scenarios will be encountered in the future, landraces should be effectively used in the breeding programs to develop seasonal drought-tolerant modern or climate-resilient wheat varieties."
More Notes: Landraces of wheat from Turkey, Iran, and Afghanistan show promise for drought tolerance breeding programs despite lower overall yield compared to modern varieties, exhibiting less yield loss under drought and higher protein content.
More Notes-sourcematerial: The study found that modern wheat cultivars had higher grain yields compared to landraces, but some landraces had a lower yield loss compared to modern wheats under drought stress. The higher tillering rate of the landraces resulted in greater biomass production, taller plant height, and peduncle length. Landraces had higher protein content in all treatments, whereas modern wheats had higher protein content in T2 treatments due to the selection pressure that has been ongoing for years.
Hazard definition-sourcematerial: Drought is one of the most pressing issues in many regions worldwide. ... The genotypes were subjected to seasonal drought treatments under rain shelters, including pre-heading drought (T1), post-heading drought (T2), irrigation based on long-term precipitation (T3), and seasonal precipitation and supplemental irrigation (T4).
Impact persistence-sourcematerial: The abstract focuses on evaluating wheat genotypes' responses to seasonal drought treatments and identifying traits that contribute to drought tolerance. It doesn't address the persistence of climate impacts beyond a single season or consider long-term adaptation strategies.
Space disaggregation-sourcematerial: In this study, 156 bread wheat genotypes, including landraces from Turkey, Iran, and Afghanistan, which have adapted to changing climatic conditions for thousands of years, and high-yielding modern wheat varieties, including advanced breeding lines and cultivars were evaluated.
Impact interactions-sourcematerial: The abstract focuses on the individual effects of different drought treatments (pre-heading and post-heading) on various wheat genotypes. It does not discuss interactions between different climate impacts or how these impacts might affect different sectors or locations simultaneously.
Climate features-sourcematerial: The abstract focuses on drought tolerance in wheat genotypes and does not mention climate tipping points or other complex climate phenomena.
Adaptation considered: The study implicitly considers adaptation by evaluating landraces (ancient wheat varieties) known to have adapted to past climatic conditions and comparing them to modern varieties under drought conditions. It suggests using these landraces in breeding programs to develop drought-tolerant modern wheat, thus explicitly aiming to develop adaptive capacity.
Adaptation considered-sourcematerial: "landraces from Turkey, Iran, and Afghanistan, which have adapted to changing climatic conditions for thousands of years," "Modern wheat cultivars had higher grain yields compared to landraces, but some landraces had a lower yield loss compared to modern wheats under drought stress," "landraces should be effectively used in the breeding programs to develop seasonal drought-tolerant modern or climate-resilient wheat varieties."

Paper Title: Diverse pathways for climate resilience in marine fishery systems
Outcome(s) of Interest: Climate-resilient fisheries, ecological assets & community strength, economic assets & effective governance.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "We found population abundance, learning capacity, and responsive governance were the most important attributes for conferring resilience, with ecosystem connectivity, place attachment, and accountable governance scoring the strongest across the climate-resilient fisheries...We identified five fishery archetypes to guide stakeholders as they set long-term goals and prioritize actions to improve resilience. Lastly, we found evidence for two pathways to resilience: (1) building ecological assets and strengthening communities, which we observed in rural and small-scale fisheries, and (2) building economic assets and improving effective governance, which was demonstrated in urban and wealthy fisheries."
Drivers of Risk: Climate change impacts; ecological and social dimensions; lack of concrete examples; climate stressor; inhibiting resilience.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "Both the ecological and social dimensions of fisheries are being affected by climate change." "Concrete examples that explicitly link these attributes to social-ecological outcomes are lacking." "Systematically assessed to understand how attributes enable or inhibit resilience to a given climate stressor."
Category-sourcematerial: Both the ecological and social dimensions of fisheries are being affected by climate change...the case studies were systematically assessed to understand how attributes enable or inhibit resilience to a given climate stressor...We identified five fishery archetypes to guide stakeholders as they set long-term goals and prioritize actions to improve resilience.
Approach-sourcematerial: "Using a novel framework for evaluating 38 resilience attributes, the case studies were systematically assessed to understand how attributes enable or inhibit resilience to a given climate stressor." and "We found population abundance, learning capacity, and responsive governance were the most important attributes for conferring resilience...We used these responses to develop an attribute typology that describes robust sources of resilience, actionable priority attributes and attributes that are case specific or require research."
Methodology: A novel framework was used to evaluate 38 resilience attributes across 18 case studies, spanning ecological, socio-economic, governance and geographic contexts, to understand how these attributes enable or inhibit resilience to climate stressors in fisheries.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "We assembled 18 case studies spanning ecological, socio-economic, governance and geographic contexts. Using a novel framework for evaluating 38 resilience attributes, the case studies were systematically assessed to understand how attributes enable or inhibit resilience to a given climate stressor."
Highlights: Climate resilience in fisheries depends on population abundance, learning capacity, and responsive governance; ecosystem connectivity, place attachment, and accountable governance are also strong contributing factors; two pathways to resilience exist: building ecological assets and strengthening communities (rural/small-scale) or building economic assets and improving effective governance (urban/wealthy).
Highlights-sourcematerial: Both the ecological and social dimensions of fisheries are being affected by climate change... To better understand climate resilience, we assembled 18 case studies... We found population abundance, learning capacity, and responsive governance were the most important attributes for conferring resilience, with ecosystem connectivity, place attachment, and accountable governance scoring the strongest across the climate-resilient fisheries... Lastly, we found evidence for two pathways to resilience: (1) building ecological assets and strengthening communities, which we observed in rural and small-scale fisheries, and (2) building economic assets and improving effective governance, which was demonstrated in urban and wealthy fisheries.
More Notes: Climate change impacts on fisheries require holistic resilience-building; this study identifies key resilience attributes, pathways (ecological/community vs. economic/governance), and archetypes to guide stakeholders in improving climate resilience in fishery systems.

Paper Title: Climatological occurrences of hail and tornadoes associated with mesoscale convective systems in the United States
Outcome(s) of Interest: Hail, tornadoes, property damage, economic loss, supercells, mesoscale convective systems, hazard events, severe weather, seasonal variability, regional variability, EF scale intensity, synoptic forcing.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Hail and tornadoes are hazardous weather events responsible for significant property damage and economic loss worldwide. The most devastating occurrences of hail and tornadoes are commonly produced by supercells in the United States. On average, 8 %–17 % of hail events and 17 %–32 % of tornado events are associated with MCSs, depending on the criteria used to define MCSs. The highest total and MCS-associated hazard events occur from March to May, while the highest MCS-associated portion (23 % for hail and 45 % for tornadoes) is observed in winter (December–February) due to the dominance of MCSs caused by strong synoptic forcing. As hailstone size increases, the fraction associated with MCS decreases, but there is an increasing trend for tornado severity from EF0 to EF3 (Enhanced Fujita Scale).
Drivers of Risk: MCS association, seasonality (March-May peak for total events, winter peak for MCS-associated portion), synoptic forcing in winter, tornado severity (EF0-EF3 increasing association with MCS), supercells embedded within MCS for violent tornadoes.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "The highest total and MCS-associated hazard events occur from March to May, while the highest MCS-associated portion (23 % for hail and 45 % for tornadoes) is observed in winter (December–February) due to the dominance of MCSs caused by strong synoptic forcing. As hailstone size increases, the fraction associated with MCS decreases, but there is an increasing trend for tornado severity from EF0 to EF3 (Enhanced Fujita Scale). Violent tornadoes at EF4 and EF5 associated with MCSs were also observed, which are generated by supercells embedded within MCSs."

Paper Title: Forecasting global spread of invasive pests and pathogens through international trade
Outcome(s) of Interest: Forecasted spread of non-native plant pests; identification of bridgehead populations; quantification of transport pathway risk levels.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "...forecast the spread of non-native plant pests... ...historical, known spotted lanternfly introductions to identify potential bridgehead populations that may contribute to global spread... ...quantifying transport pathways risk levels..."
Drivers of Risk: Climate suitability, host availability, propagule pressure, international trade networks, bridgehead effects.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "...core drivers of biological invasions—climate suitability, host availability, and propagule pressure—quantified through open, globally available databases...", "...complex transmission networks arising from bridgehead population establishment.", "...often as an unintended consequence of international trade..."
Category-sourcematerial: threaten biodiversity, ecosystem function, food security, and economic livelihoods...safeguard plant health, agriculture, and natural resources.
Approach-sourcematerial: This paper presents a flexible spatiotemporal stochastic network model called PoPS (Pest or Pathogen Spread) Global that couples international trade networks with core drivers of biological invasions—climate suitability, host availability, and propagule pressure—quantified through open, globally available databases to forecast the spread of non-native plant pests.
Methodology: A spatiotemporal stochastic network model (PoPS Global) couples international trade networks with climate suitability, host availability, and propagule pressure to forecast the spread of non-native plant pests, calibrated using historical data of pest introductions (e.g., spotted lanternfly) to identify potential bridgehead populations.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "This paper presents a flexible spatiotemporal stochastic network model called PoPS (Pest or Pathogen Spread) Global that couples international trade networks with core drivers of biological invasions—climate suitability, host availability, and propagule pressure—quantified through open, globally available databases to forecast the spread of non-native plant pests." "The model was calibrated with historical, known spotted lanternfly introductions to identify potential bridgehead populations that may contribute to global spread."
Highlights: A spatiotemporal stochastic network model (PoPS Global) forecasts the spread of non-native plant pests by integrating international trade networks, climate suitability, host availability, and propagule pressure, enabling proactive responses and resource allocation for plant health, agriculture, and natural resource protection.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "This paper presents a flexible spatiotemporal stochastic network model called PoPS (Pest or Pathogen Spread) Global that couples international trade networks with core drivers of biological invasions—climate suitability, host availability, and propagule pressure—quantified through open, globally available databases to forecast the spread of non-native plant pests...The modular design of the framework makes it adaptable for various pests capable of dispersing via human-mediated pathways, supports proactive responses to emerging pests when limited data are available, and enables forecasts at different spatial and temporal resolutions...This global view of phytosanitary pandemics provides crucial information for anticipating biological invasions, quantifying transport pathways risk levels, and allocating resources to safeguard plant health, agriculture, and natural resources."
More Notes: Non-native plant pests and pathogens, spread through international trade and exacerbated by bridgehead effects, pose a threat to biodiversity, ecosystems, food security, and economies; the PoPS Global model forecasts their spread by integrating trade networks, climate suitability, host availability, and propagule pressure, demonstrated with the spotted lanternfly case study, providing insights for biosecurity and resource allocation.

We demonstrate the framework using a case study of the invasive planthopper spotted lanternfly (Lycorma delicatula)... This global view of phytosanitary pandemics provides crucial information for anticipating biological invasions, quantifying transport pathways risk levels, and allocating resources to safeguard plant health, agriculture, and natural resources.

Paper Title: The social costs of tropical cyclones
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased global social cost of carbon (SCC).
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "accounting for TC impacts substantially increases the global SCC by more than 20%; median global SCC increases from US$ 173 to US$ 212 per tonne of CO2 under a middle-of-the-road future emission and socioeconomic development scenario"
Drivers of Risk: Tropical cyclones, socioeconomic factors, emission scenarios, temperature changes, climate pathways, global warming, economic vulnerability, atmospheric circulation, extreme weather events.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Tropical cyclones (TCs) are among the most harmful extreme weather events. They affect on average 20.4 million people annually, and they caused mean direct annual economic losses of US$ 51.5 billion averaged over the last decade... Accounting for different emission (Representative Concentration Pathways (RCPs)), socioeconomic development (Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSPs)), and economic discounting scenario combinations thereby allows for a systematic assessment of compounding uncertainties... The primary factors impacting outcomes are tropical cyclones, changes in global mean temperature (GMT), atmospheric circulation changes, and socioeconomic variables such as population development... The TC-SCC of a country is determined by its exposure and economic vulnerability to TCs... ﬂoods and droughts cause direct damages that are comparable to TC damages globally... For each GCM-RCP-region combination, we then compute the mean frequency and intensity of landfalling TCs ...
Category-sourcematerial: Tropical cyclones (TCs) are among the most harmful extreme weather events. They affect on average 20.4 million people annually, and they caused mean direct annual economic losses of US$ 51.5 billion averaged over the last decade. ... Yet, these long-term effects are not accounted for in current estimates of the social cost of carbon (SCC)... to quantify the country-level SCC induced by the persistent growth effects of damaging TCs. ... For 37 out of the 41 TC-affected countries considered, we ﬁ nd growth losses to robustly increase with GMT... This clearly illustrates that growth losses do not depend on the development level but rather on country-speciﬁ c characteristics. Thus, also growth in high-income countries is negatively affected by TCs suggesting that, even in this country group, historically implemented adaptation measures may not sufﬁ ce to compensate for climate change-induced loss increases in the future. ... Uncertainties with regard to changes of the predictor (national shares of affected people) along the RCP pathways...
Approach-sourcematerial: Page 2: "We employ a three-way fixed effects panel model with annual national shares of people exposed to TCs for different lag-years as exogenous predictors introduced in ref. 2."; Page 4: "...using a mixed-effects modeling approach (Methods)."; Page 9: "To estimate the national GDPpc growth responses over the period 1981–2015, we use a three-way ﬁxed effects panel model ..."; Page 10: "...using the mixed-effects model...we run two OLS regressions..."
Methodology: The study employs a modeling framework with historical growth responses to TC strikes, probabilistic event-based projections, and temperature-dependent damage functions, using a three-way fixed effects panel model, mixed-effects modeling, OLS regressions, and multiple scenarios (RCPs, SSPs, discount rates) with methods like maximum entropy bootstrapping.
Methodology-sourcematerial: We present a modeling framework to quantify future TC damages that comprises three components. First, we estimate the historical growth responses of countries to TC strikes...Second, we generate probabilistic, event-based projections of TC damages...Third, we derive event-based, and country-specific temperature-dependent damage functions... We employ a three-way fixed effects panel model... (Page 2); We use the 'no further climate change' baseline scenario...derive event-based, country-level temperature-dependent damage functions...using a mixed-effects modeling approach (Page 4); Estimation of temperature-dependent damage functions...regressing the growth rate changes...mixed-effects model...we run two OLS regressions... (Page 10); We combine process-based modeling of the population exposure to TCs with an empirical growth model (Page 6).
Highlights: Accounting for TC damages could substantially increase the SCC, suggesting climate policy benefits are currently underestimated, and may help prevent a critical lack of climate action.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "We ﬁnd that accounting for TC impacts substantially increases the global SCC by more than 20%; median global SCC increases from US$ 173 to US$ 212 per tonne of CO2 under a middle-of-the-road future emission and socioeconomic development scenario." (Page 1); "Adequately accounting for the damages of extreme weather events in policy evaluation may therefore help to prevent a critical lack of climate action." (Page 1); "For 37 out of the 41 TC-affected countries considered, we ﬁnd growth losses to robustly increase with GMT...This clearly illustrates that growth losses do not depend on the development level but rather on country-speciﬁ c characteristics." (Page 4); "The substantial increase in SCC by 22.2% at the global level and by 44.4% in TC-affected countries that we observe when explicitly accounting for TC impacts and their persistence in the economic system" (Page 8)
More Notes: The analysis accounts for the persistence of damages in the economic system, and the preferred SCC estimate of US$/tCO2 212 (66% CI: US$/tCO2 138–318) is somewhat lower than recent meta-analyses because the approach explicitly only resolves the impacts from annual temperatures and TCs. Discounting rates are a primary source of uncertainty.
More Notes-sourcematerial: In addition, we here account for the persistence of damages in the economic system, building on earlier works. Our preferred estimate is with US $/tCO2 212 (66% CI: US$/tCO2 138–318) somewhat lower than the median estimate of the meta-analysis for 2018–2022. This is not surprising since we only resolve the impacts from annual temperatures and TCs, explicitly... Discounting represents the primary source of uncertainty...

Impact persistence-sourcematerial: Page 1: "We here derive temperature-dependent damage functions for 41 TC-affected countries to quantify the country-level SCC induced by the persistent growth effects of damaging TCs."; Page 2: "First, we estimate the historical growth responses of countries to TC strikes accounting for the persistence of damages in the economic system."; Page 8: "...by deriving empirical country-level temperature dependent-damage functions accounting for the persistence of damages in the economic system..."; Page 8: "The substantial increase in SCC by 22.2% at the global level and by 44.4% in TC-affected countries that we observe when explicitly accounting for TC impacts and their persistence in the economic system, suggests that a more explicit representation of TCs could drive up previous SCC estimates..."

Impact interactions-sourcematerial: We here present a modeling framework to quantify future TC damages that comprises three components. First, we estimate the historical growth responses of countries to TC strikes accounting for the persistence of damages in the economic system. Second, we generate probabilistic, event-based projections of TC damages by projecting the historical growth losses along a large set of synthetic time series of TCs with landfalls generated with a TC emulator. Third, we derive event-based, and country-specific temperature-dependent damage functions that account for the persistence of damages in the economic system from the growth losses calculated along the different scenario combinations. (Page 2)

Paper Title: Breaking Boundaries in Wind Engineering: LSU WISE Open-Jet Facility Revolutionizes Solar Panel and Building Design

Through controlled wind conditions, researchers can gain invaluable insights into the behaviour and performance of these critical infrastructures. ...Open-jet testing, introduced as an advanced tool, overcomes turbulence modelling constraints, providing a more accurate representation of real-world conditions. The LSU WISE open-jet facility produces complete turbulence at a large scale, eliminating the need for corrections accompanied by partial turbulence simulation. ...They emphasize the superiority of large-scale open-jet testing, underscoring its critical role in designing resilient structures.
Highlights: LSU's WISE open-jet testing facility enhances structural resilience to windstorms by accurately simulating real-world conditions, revealing that BIPV integration may not require additional reinforcement (reducing uplift forces by 45-63%), challenging Reynolds number insensitivity assumptions for sharp-cornered structures, and demonstrating the underestimation of peak loads in small-scale testing, leading to improved high-rise building design.
Highlights-sourcematerial: The LSU WISE open-jet facility produces complete turbulence at a large scale, eliminating the need for corrections accompanied by partial turbulence simulation. Integrating photovoltaic panels with gable-roofed buildings may not require additional structural reinforcement, with a reduction in wind uplift forces by 45–63%. Contrary to long-held beliefs, the findings challenge the notion that wind loads on structures with sharp corners are insensitive to Reynolds number. Open-jet testing produces higher peak pressures, providing real-world justification for actual damage in high-rise buildings.

These strategic choices are the coping mechanisms banks can take to survive while affecting the local competitive lending market...Results suggest that community banks do employ the selected strategic choices to a certain degree...studies on whether these institutions engage in risk-reducing management strategies have been limited...the survivorship of firms under extreme weather poses an essential question about the local economy's health."
Drivers of Risk: Flood events; climate change; weather shocks; lack of guidance/assessment tools; regulatory concerns.

Highlights: Community banks employ some strategies to manage flood risk (increased capital, portfolio diversification), but the scale is insufficient to fully mitigate the risk; branch expansion is not used as a strategy, highlighting the need for better guidance and risk assessment tools to encourage proactive climate risk management.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Results suggest that community banks do employ the selected strategic choices to a certain degree, as it is found that there is an increase in the core capital that absorbs shocks and portfolio diversification. Paper Title: Biochar-integrated reactive filtration of wastewater for P removal and recovery, micropollutant catalytic oxidation, and negative CO2e: Life cycle assessment and techno-economic analysis
Outcome(s) of Interest: Carbon-negative water treatment, pollutant removal, carbon sequestration, carbon-neutral cost, performance standard.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "LCA results indicated that the Fe-CatOx-BC-RF pilot system is a BC dose-dependent carbon-negative technology at −1.21 kg CO2e/m3...demonstrating potential as a biochar dose-dependent negative emissions technology...carbon negativity to become a water treatment performance standard...carbon sequestration by reducing the carbon footprint of advanced water treatment...forecasted carbon-neutral process cost with low P and micropollutant removal"
Drivers of Risk: Scale of treatment, biochar dose, carbon accounting method.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: LCA results indicated that the Fe-CatOx-BC-RF pilot system is a BC dose-dependent carbon-negative technology at −1.21 kg CO2e/m3, where biochar addition constitutes a −1.53 kg/m3 CO2e beneficial impact to the process. ... Using the C100 100-year carbon accounting approach rather than Cnet reduces these CO2e metrics for the process by about 25%. ... TEA... shows that at scale, the mean cost for treating 1130 m3/day (0.3 MGD) WRRF secondary influent water with Fe-CatOx-BC-RF using the C100 metric is US$0.18 ± US$0.01/m3 to achieve overall process carbon neutrality. Using the same BC dose in an estimation of a 3780 m3/day (1 MGD) Fe-CatOx-BC-RF facility, the carbon neutral cost of treatment is reduced further to US$0.08 ± $0.01 with added BC accounting for US$0.03/m3.
Category-sourcematerial: "Life cycle assessment (LCA) and techno-economic analysis (TEA) models are developed for a tertiary wastewater treatment system...the overall process changed from 0.02 kg CO2e/m3 to a carbon negative −1.41 kg CO2e/m3, demonstrating potential as a biochar dose-dependent negative emissions technology."
Approach-sourcematerial: Life cycle assessment (LCA) and techno-economic analysis (TEA) models are developed...In this work, we conduct an ISO-compliant LCA...A stochastic TEA for the cost of water treatment...
Methodology: Life cycle assessment (LCA) and techno-economic analysis (TEA) models, compliant with ISO standards, were developed for a tertiary wastewater treatment system using biochar-integrated reactive filtration (RF) with and without iron-ozone catalytic oxidation (CatOx); LCA was performed on a 49.2 m3/day pilot system and a modeled 1130 m3/day WRRF system using a biochar addition rate of 0.45 g/L to quantify environmental impacts, while TEA used Monte Carlo stochastic modeling to determine the cost of water treatment for various scales.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Life cycle assessment (LCA) and techno-economic analysis (TEA) models are developed for a tertiary wastewater treatment system that employs a biochar-integrated reactive filtration (RF) approach...In this work, we conduct an ISO-compliant LCA of a 49.2 m3/day (9 gpm) field pilot-scale Fe-CatOx-BC-RF system and a 1130 m3/day (0.3 MGD) water resource recovery facility (WRRF)-installed RF system, modeled with BC addition at the same rate of 0.45 g/L to quantify their environmental impacts...Techno-economic analysis (TEA) of this biochar catalytic oxidation reactive filtration process using Monte Carlo stochastic modeling shows a forecasted carbon-neutral process cost...
Highlights: Biochar-integrated reactive filtration for tertiary wastewater treatment can achieve carbon-negative global warming potential (-1.21 kg CO2e/m3) while removing 90-99% of total phosphorus and >90% of detected micropollutants; the technology aids in long-term carbon sequestration, potentially enabling carbon-neutral or carbon-negative water treatment with forecasted carbon-neutral process costs as low as $0.11/m3 for a 3780-m3/day facility.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Life cycle assessment (LCA) of a pilot scale tertiary biochar water treatment process with or without catalytic ozonation at a WRRF shows a carbon negative global warming potential of −1.21-kg CO2e/m3 while removing 90%–99% TP and >90% of detected micropollutants. Biochar-integrated reactive filtration use can aid in long-term carbon sequestration by reducing the carbon footprint of advanced water treatment in a dose-dependent manner, allowing an overall carbon-neutral or carbon-negative process. Techno-economic analysis (TEA) of this biochar catalytic oxidation reactive filtration process using Monte Carlo stochastic modeling shows a forecasted carbon-neutral process cost with low P and micropollutant removal as US$0.11/m3 ± 0.01 for a 3780-m3/day (1 MGD) scale installation with BC cost at US$0.03/m3 of that total.
More Notes: Biochar-integrated reactive filtration for tertiary wastewater treatment can achieve carbon-negative global warming potential while removing phosphorus and micropollutants, with techno-economic analysis indicating a pathway to carbon-neutral treatment costs at larger scales.
More Notes-sourcematerial: "LCA results indicated that the Fe-CatOx-BC-RF pilot system is a BC dose-dependent carbon-negative technology at −1.21 kg CO2e/m3...For the WRRF-installed RF system, modeled with the same rate of BC addition, the overall process changed from 0.02 kg CO2e/m3 to a carbon negative −1.41 kg CO2e/m3...TEA...shows that at scale, the mean cost...with Fe-CatOx-BC-RF using the C100 metric is US$0.18 ± US$0.01/m3 to achieve overall process carbon neutrality...Life cycle assessment (LCA) of a pilot scale tertiary biochar water treatment process...shows a carbon negative global warming potential of −1.21-kg CO2e/m3 while removing 90%–99% TP and >90% of detected micropollutants."

Paper Title: Simulating synergistic effects of climate change and conservation practices on greenhouse gas emissions and crop growth in long-term maize cropping systems
Outcome(s) of Interest: GHG emissions (CO2, N2O) increase, GWP increase, maize yield decrease, total biomass decrease, SOC increase.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "Under future climatic conditions, CO2 and N2O emissions increased significantly — 19.4 % ± 5.8 % and 26.6 % ± 8.9 %, respectively — compared to those under historical baseline conditions. Likewise, the GWP increased by 19.8 ± 5.8 %. Although rising [CO2]atm afforded limited benefits in terms of crop photosynthesis rates, rising future temperatures shortened crop growth cycles, resulting in a net decrease of 9.1 % ± 1.9 % in maize yield and 4.2 % ± 1.6 % in total biomass. SOC saw a net increase of 4.8 % ± 0.4 % under the future (vs. baseline) climate."
Drivers of Risk: Temperature increase, precipitation changes, elevated atmospheric CO2, shortened crop cycles.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Compared to other climatic factors (e.g., shortwave radiation, wind run, and relative humidity), temperature, precipitation, and [CO2]atm played more important roles for future carbon dioxide (CO2) and nitrous oxide (N2O) emissions, global warming potential (GWP), soil organic carbon (SOC), crop yield, and total crop biomass...rising future temperatures shortened crop growth cycles...
Category-sourcematerial: Understanding the impacts of future climate change and long-term agronomic practices on environmental quality and agricultural productivity is critical to the development of sustainable agronomic management approaches. ... To project potential future (2065–2084) climatic conditions for a field under a long-term maize cropping system situated in Nebraska (USA), ... the effects of four long-term conservation practices were simulated ... for future carbon dioxide (CO2) and nitrous oxide (N2O) emissions, global warming potential (GWP), soil organic carbon (SOC), crop yield, and total crop biomass. ... Although rising [CO2]atm afforded limited benefits in terms of crop photosynthesis rates, rising future temperatures shortened crop growth cycles, resulting in a net decrease of 9.1 % ± 1.9 % in maize yield and 4.2 % ± 1.6 % in total biomass. SOC saw a net increase of 4.8 % ± 0.4 % under the future (vs. baseline) climate. ... Whereas crop yield and biomass were not significantly affected by residue or tillage management practices. The simulation method provided valuable evidence for management decisions to assess the synergistic effects of climate change and long-term agronomic practices on the environment and agricultural productivity.
Approach-sourcematerial: To project potential future (2065–2084) climatic conditions...12 different combinations of regional climate models × global climate models (RCMs-GCMs) were generated...Then, employing a well-calibrated instance of the Root Zone Water Quality Model (RZWQM2), the effects of four long-term conservation practices were simulated under the 12 RCMs-GCMs.
Methodology: Future climate conditions (2065-2084) for a Nebraska maize field were projected using 12 RCM-GCM combinations under RCP8.5 and elevated CO2; the Root Zone Water Quality Model (RZWQM2) was then used to simulate the effects of four long-term conservation practices under these climate projections.
Methodology-sourcematerial: To project potential future (2065–2084) climatic conditions for a field under a long-term maize cropping system situated in Nebraska (USA), 12 different combinations of regional climate models × global climate models (RCMs-GCMs) were generated under representative concentration pathway 8.5 (RCP8.5) and a heightened atmospheric carbon dioxide concentration ([CO2]atm = 714.1 ppm). Then, employing a well-calibrated instance of the Root Zone Water Quality Model (RZWQM2), the effects of four long-term conservation practices were simulated under the 12 RCMs-GCMs.
Highlights: Future climate conditions (RCP8.5) are projected to increase CO2 and N2O emissions, and global warming potential in Nebraska maize cropping systems, while decreasing maize yield and total biomass despite a rise in soil organic carbon; residue retention with tillage is predicted to increase CO2 and N2O emissions compared to residue removal with no-till, but crop yield and biomass were not significantly affected by these residue or tillage management practices.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Compared to other climatic factors (e.g., shortwave radiation, wind run, and relative humidity), temperature, precipitation, and [CO2]atm played more important roles for future carbon dioxide (CO2) and nitrous oxide (N2O) emissions, global warming potential (GWP), soil organic carbon (SOC), crop yield, and total crop biomass...Under future climatic conditions, CO2 and N2O emissions increased significantly — 19.4 % ± 5.8 % and 26.6 % ± 8.9 %, respectively — compared to those under historical baseline conditions. Likewise, the GWP increased by 19.8 ± 5.8 %. Although rising [CO2]atm afforded limited benefits in terms of crop photosynthesis rates, rising future temperatures shortened crop growth cycles, resulting in a net decrease of 9.1 % ± 1.9 % in maize yield and 4.2 % ± 1.6 % in total biomass. SOC saw a net increase of 4.8 % ± 0.4 % under the future (vs. baseline) climate. Compared to residue removal with no-till treatment, annual CO2 and N2O emissions in the long-term maize cropping system were predicted to increase by 26.8 % and 27.9 % between 2065 and 2084 under residue retention with tillage treatment, respectively. Whereas crop yield and biomass were not significantly affected by residue or tillage management practices.
More Notes: Future climate change and long-term conservation practices in Nebraska's maize cropping systems under RCP8.5, with elevated [CO2]atm, are projected to increase CO2 and N2O emissions, GWP, and SOC, but reduce maize yield and total biomass; management practices like residue retention with tillage exacerbate emissions without significantly affecting crop yield and biomass, suggesting a need for investigation of other RCPs and practices for sustainable agricultural production.

Likewise, the GWP increased by 19.8 ± 5.8 %. Although rising [CO2]atm afforded limited benefits in terms of crop photosynthesis rates, rising future temperatures shortened crop growth cycles, resulting in a net decrease of 9.1 % ± 1.9 % in maize yield and 4.2 % ± 1.6 % in total biomass. SOC saw a net increase of 4.8 % ± 0.4 % under the future (vs. baseline) climate. Compared to residue removal with no-till treatment, annual CO2 and N2O emissions in the long-term maize cropping system were predicted to increase by 26.8 % and 27.9 % between 2065 and 2084 under residue retention with tillage treatment, respectively. Whereas crop yield and biomass were not significantly affected by residue or tillage management practices. Further investigation is needed on the effects of other climate RCPs and agronomic management for sustainable agricultural production.
