Paper Title: Invasive hematophagous arthropods and associated diseases in a changing world
Outcome(s) of Interest: Biological invasions, vector-borne diseases, climate change, human health impacts, ecological and genetic drivers, urbanization, invasive arthropods, disease outbreaks, surveillance, One Health.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "Biological invasions have increased significantly...Hematophagous arthropods can be vectors of infectious and potentially lethal pathogens...constituting a growing threat to humans...major vector-borne diseases...expanding in a world dominated by climate change, land-use change...impacts on ecosystems and human health...Burgeoning urbanization, climate change and the urban heat island effect are likely to interact to favor invasive hematophagous arthropods and the diseases they can vector...novel disease outbreaks...ecological risks...disease management...One Health approach."
Drivers of Risk: Climate change, globalization, international trade & transport, urbanization, land-use change, human population movement.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Page 2: "Biological invasions, particularly of invasive hematophagous arthropods, driven by international trade, climate change, land-use change, and urbanization."; Page 3: "Ongoing climate change may assist the invasion rates of alien species..."; Page 4: "Ongoing globalization and climate change are expected to drive and accelerate the proliferations of invasive hematophagous arthropods..."; Page 6: "Human population movements, human trade, plane travel, ship/sea transport are drivers of introduction..."; Page 7: "The Belt and Road Initiative proposed by China may promote further invasions by hematophagous arthropods."; Page 8: "Climate change increases the number of favorable thermal niches for hematophagous arthropod invasions..."; Page 9: "Increasing rates of sea level rise potentially expanding coastal marsh habitats..."; Page 10: "Anthropogenic climate change is identified as a driver promoting geographic expansion..."; Page 11: "Climate change projected to increase the area occupied by arthropod invaders, urbanization providing habitats..."; Page 12: "Urbanization, agricultural practices, light pollution, and pesticide use affecting mosquito populations."; Page 13: "...predicted to further increase with climate change... shipping and air traffic, the transport of vertebrates... and worn tires as prominent factors..."; Page 14: "accelerated worldwide urbanization and interconnectivity, which could further support mosquito proliferation and adaptations to human-modified environments."
Category-sourcematerial: Economic impacts of invasive hematophagous arthropods, including nuisance biting affecting recreational and real estate values.
Approach-sourcematerial: Use of modeling study with logistic response curves.
Methodology: Recent methodologies include data collection applications and near-infrared spectroscopy (NIRS) for monitoring mosquito invasions, along with modeling studies using logistic response curves to assess economic impacts of management delays.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "Recent data collection applications (e.g. Mosquito Alert, http://www.mosquitoalert.com; GLOBE Observer, https://observer.globe.gov/; Invasive Mosquito Project, http://www.citizenscience.us/imp/) involving cutting-edge technology have been implemented to monitor mosquitoes and models have been developed to partly automate invader identifications [73]. Recently, the near-infrared spectroscopy (NIRS) technique has been successfully used to detect Zika and chikungunya infection in dead Ae. aegypti female mosquitoes [74], providing a rapid and cost-effective arbovirus surveillance tool with high accuracy levels (> 90%)." "A recent modeling study using logistic response curves to resemble impact dynamics showed that for the Aedes genus, current management delays of 55 years have led to an additional cost of US$ 4.57 billion that could have been avoided, showing the crucial importance of acting early against future invasions and other global changes [68, 121]."
Highlights: Review constructs a timeline of past disease vector invasions across hematophagous arthropod groups, considering individual species and socioeconomic drivers.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Page 4: Highlights: "In doing so, we construct a timeline of past disease vector invasions across hematophagous arthropod groups, considering individual species and socioeconomic drivers."
More Notes: The review emphasizes integrative management strategies addressing invasion dynamics, ecological and socioeconomic impacts, global drivers, and social dimensions across taxa, spotlighting collaboration opportunities.
More Notes-sourcematerial: Our review provides a broader, overarching perspective that spans invasion dynamics, ecological and socioeconomic impacts, global change drivers and wider social and cultural dimensions in an integrative way across multiple taxa...highlight opportunities for collaboration among the health sector and environment researchers, alongside engagement with policymakers and citizens.
Hazard definition-sourcematerial: Climate warming may have a major role in fostering hematophagous arthropod invasions by increasing the number of favorable thermal niches for their development, including towards formerly cold and restrictive environments, such as at higher latitudes and altitudes... With climate change projections over the next 3 decades likely to increase the area occupied by current arthropod invaders by 18%, the need for better biosecurity control to mitigate hematophagous arthropod nuisance biting and disease has never been stronger. Rapid response to eradicate invaders post-invasion as well as the implementation of effective biosecurity control pre-invasion (i.e. biosurveillance and intensive monitoring) are up to ten times cheaper for mosquito-borne diseases than waiting and paying for accrued damages.

The use of modeling studies with logistic response curves showcases the importance of early action in mitigating economic and epidemiological impacts, with citizen science and advanced data collection applications aiding in real-time monitoring and identification of invasions. Although not labeled directly as 'adaptation,' these approaches indicate a substantial focus on preemptive and responsive measures as part of adaptive management strategies.
Adaptation considered-sourcematerial: "Rapid response to eradicate invaders post-invasion as well as the implementation of effective biosecurity control pre-invasion (i.e. biosurveillance and intensive monitoring) are up to ten times cheaper for mosquito-borne diseases than waiting and paying for accrued damages [120]. A recent modeling study using logistic response curves to resemble impact dynamics showed that for the Aedes genus, current management delays of 55 years have led to an additional cost of US$ 4.57 billion that could have been avoided, showing the crucial importance of acting early against future invasions and other global changes [68, 121]. Recent data collection applications (e.g. Mosquito Alert, http://www.mosquitoalert.com; GLOBE Observer, https://observer.globe.gov/; Invasive Mosquito Project, http://www.citizenscience.us/imp/) involving cutting-edge technology have been implemented to monitor mosquitoes and models have been developed to partly automate invader identifications [73]."

Paper Title: Accounting for forest fire risks: global insights for climate change mitigation
Outcome(s) of Interest: Forest fire risk premia size, impact on carbon credit/offset break-even price, spatial and inter-temporal heterogeneity of forest fires, importance of incorporating fire risk into carbon credit/offset programs.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Fire is an important risk in global forest loss... Forest fire risks will increase with climate change... Our estimates of forest fire risk premia better account for the risk of forest burning... Our results show the following: (1) forest fire risk premia can be much larger than the historical forest area burned; (2) for some countries, forest fire risk premia have a large impact on the relative country-level break-even price of carbon credits or offsets; (3) a large spatial and inter-temporal heterogeneity of forest fires across countries between 2001 and 2020; and (4) the importance of properly incorporating forest fire risk premia into carbon credits/offset programs.
Drivers of Risk: Climate change, forest fire risk, inter-temporal variability, spatial heterogeneity, carbon credits, carbon offsets.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "Forest fire risks will increase with climate change in some locations...a large spatial and inter-temporal heterogeneity of forest fires across countries between 2001 and 2020...the importance of properly incorporating forest fire risk premia into carbon credits/offset programs...heterogeneity in fire risk premia across 10 Canadian provinces."
Category-sourcematerial: Fire is an important risk in global forest loss...Forest fire risks will increase with climate change in some locations...To quantify the importance of forest fire risks, we undertook a global study...Our estimates of forest fire risk premia better account for the risk of forest burning...
Approach-sourcematerial: Our estimates of forest fire risk premia better account for the risk of forest burning that would be additional to the risk-free and break-even price of credits or offsets to promote carbon sequestration and storage in forests.
Methodology: A global study was undertaken using economic datasets and global remote sensing data from 2001 to 2020 to quantify individual country fire risks and estimate forest fire risk premia.
Methodology-sourcematerial: combining economic datasets and global remote sensing data from 2001 to 2020. Our estimates of forest fire risk premia better account for the risk of forest burning that would be additional to the risk-free and break-even price of credits or offsets to promote carbon sequestration and storage in forests.
Highlights: Forest fire risk premia can significantly exceed historical burned areas, substantially impact carbon credit/offset break-even prices in some countries, exhibit substantial spatial and temporal heterogeneity globally (2001-2020), and require proper incorporation into carbon credit/offset programs, with notable sub-national variations demonstrated across Canadian provinces.
Highlights-sourcematerial: (1) forest fire risk premia can be much larger than the historical forest area burned; (2) for some countries, forest fire risk premia have a large impact on the relative country-level break-even price of carbon credits or offsets; (3) a large spatial and inter-temporal heterogeneity of forest fires across countries between 2001 and 2020; and (4) the importance of properly incorporating forest fire risk premia into carbon credits/offset programs. As part of our analysis, and to emphasise the possible sub-national scale differences, our results highlight the heterogeneity in fire risk premia across 10 Canadian provinces.
More Notes: Forest fire risk premia, which significantly impact carbon credit pricing, exhibit substantial spatial and inter-temporal variability across countries and even within sub-national regions like Canadian provinces.
More Notes-sourcematerial: "forest fire risk premia can be much larger than the historical forest area burned; (2) for some countries, forest fire risk premia have a large impact on the relative country-level break-even price of carbon credits or offsets; (3) a large spatial and inter-temporal heterogeneity of forest fires across countries between 2001 and 2020; and (4) the importance of properly incorporating forest fire risk premia into carbon credits/offset programs. As part of our analysis, and to emphasise the possible sub-national scale differences, our results highlight the heterogeneity in fire risk premia across 10 Canadian provinces."

Impact persistence-sourcematerial: The abstract mentions "inter-temporal heterogeneity of forest fires across countries between 2001 and 2020" and that existing estimates don't fully account for "how these risks change inter-temporally."
Space disaggregation-sourcematerial: To quantify the importance of forest fire risks, we undertook a global study of individual country fire risks...
Economic dynamics-sourcematerial: The abstract focuses on quantifying forest fire risks and their impact on carbon credit pricing, emphasizing spatial and inter-temporal heterogeneity. It does not explicitly mention modeling or considering the dynamic nature of the economy.
Climate features-sourcematerial: Fire is an important risk in global forest loss and contributed 20% to 25% of the global anthropogenic greenhouse gas emissions between 1997 and 2016. Forest fire risks will increase with climate change in some locations, but existing estimates of the costs of using forests for climate mitigation do not yet fully account for these risks or how these risks change inter-temporally. To quantify the importance of forest fire risks, we undertook a global study of individual country fire risks, combining economic datasets and global remote sensing data from 2001 to 2020. Our estimates of forest fire risk premia better account for the risk of forest burning that would be additional to the risk-free and break-even price of credits or offsets to promote carbon sequestration and storage in forests. Our results show the following: (1) forest fire risk premia can be much larger than the historical forest area burned; (2) for some countries, forest fire risk premia have a large impact on the relative country-level break-even price of carbon credits or offsets; (3) a large spatial and inter-temporal heterogeneity of forest fires across countries between 2001 and 2020; and (4) the importance of properly incorporating forest fire risk premia into carbon credits/offset programs. As part of our analysis, and to emphasise the possible sub-national scale differences, our results highlight the heterogeneity in fire risk premia across 10 Canadian provinces. © 2023, The Author(s).
Adaptation considered: Adaptation isn't explicitly considered in the abstract. The focus is on quantifying forest fire risk for carbon credit/offset programs, not on how to adapt to those risks.
Adaptation considered-sourcematerial: "Forest fire risks will increase with climate change in some locations, but existing estimates of the costs of using forests for climate mitigation do not yet fully account for these risks or how these risks change inter-temporally. To quantify the importance of forest fire risks, we undertook a global study of individual country fire risks...Our estimates of forest fire risk premia better account for the risk of forest burning that would be additional to the risk-free and break-even price of credits or offsets to promote carbon sequestration and storage in forests."

Paper Title: Assessing coastline recession for adaptation planning: sea level rise versus storm erosion
Outcome(s) of Interest: End-century coastline recession; SLR substantially increases recession; Wave climate marginal impact; Storm erosion vs. SLR dominance depends on beach type and risk tolerance; Moderate risk aversion (temporary structures): SLR dominant; High risk aversion (infrastructure): Storm erosion dominant.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: The Sixth Assessment report (AR6) of the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) states with high confidence that most sandy coasts around the world will experience an increase in coastal erosion over the twenty-first century... Results show that SLR substantially increases the projected end-century recession at both types of coasts and that projected changes in the wave climate have only a marginal impact... the dominance of storm erosion over SLR (and vice versa) on total recession by 2100 depends on both the type of the beach and the risk tolerance levels... For moderately risk-averse decisions... additional erosion due to SLR can be considered as the dominant driver of end-century recession at both types of beaches. However, for more risk-averse decisions... storm erosion becomes the dominant process.
Drivers of Risk: SLR, storm erosion, risk tolerance, beach type, process dominance ratio.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "To adequately inform adaptation measures, it is necessary to have a good understanding of the relative importance of the physical processes driving coastline recession, as well as of linkages between consideration (or not) of certain processes and the level of risk tolerance...Results show that SLR substantially increases the projected end-century recession at both types of coasts and that projected changes in the wave climate have only a marginal impact...the dominance of storm erosion over SLR (and vice versa) on total recession by 2100 depends on both the type of the beach and the risk tolerance levels."
Category-sourcematerial: The Sixth Assessment report (AR6) of the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) states with high confidence that most sandy coasts around the world will experience an increase in coastal erosion over the twenty-first century. ... Here, we apply the multi-scale Probabilistic Coastline Recession (PCR) model to two end-member sandy coastal types (swell dominated and storm dominated), to investigate where and when coastline recession projections are dominated by the differential contributions from Sea Level Rise (SLR) and storm erosion.
Approach-sourcematerial: Here, we apply the multi-scale Probabilistic Coastline Recession (PCR) model to two end-member sandy coastal types (swell dominated and storm dominated), to investigate where and when coastline recession projections are dominated by the differential contributions from Sea Level Rise (SLR) and storm erosion. An analysis of the Process Dominance Ratio (PDR), introduced here, shows that the dominance of storm erosion over SLR (and vice versa) on total recession by 2100 depends on both the type of the beach and the risk tolerance levels.
Methodology: The multi-scale Probabilistic Coastline Recession (PCR) model is applied to swell-dominated and storm-dominated sandy coasts to investigate the contributions of Sea Level Rise (SLR) and storm erosion to coastline recession projections; a Process Dominance Ratio (PDR) is introduced to analyze the dominance of storm erosion versus SLR on total recession by 2100.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Here, we apply the multi-scale Probabilistic Coastline Recession (PCR) model to two end-member sandy coastal types (swell dominated and storm dominated), to investigate where and when coastline recession projections are dominated by the differential contributions from Sea Level Rise (SLR) and storm erosion. ... An analysis of the Process Dominance Ratio (PDR), introduced here, shows that the dominance of storm erosion over SLR (and vice versa) on total recession by 2100 depends on both the type of the beach and the risk tolerance levels.
Highlights: SLR significantly increases projected coastline recession by 2100 for both swell- and storm-dominated coasts, while wave climate changes have a marginal impact; the dominance of storm erosion versus SLR depends on beach type and risk tolerance: SLR dominates for moderately risk-averse decisions, whereas storm erosion dominates for more risk-averse decisions.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Results show that SLR substantially increases the projected end-century recession at both types of coasts and that projected changes in the wave climate have only a marginal impact. An analysis of the Process Dominance Ratio (PDR), introduced here, shows that the dominance of storm erosion over SLR (and vice versa) on total recession by 2100 depends on both the type of the beach and the risk tolerance levels. For moderately risk-averse decisions (i.e. decisions accounting only for high exceedance probability recessions and hence do not account for very high amounts of potential recession—for example, the placement of temporary summer beach cabins), additional erosion due to SLR can be considered as the dominant driver of end-century recession at both types of beaches. However, for more risk-averse decisions that would typically account for higher potential recession (i.e. lower exceedance probability recessions), such as the placement of coastal infrastructure, multi-storey apartment buildings etc., storm erosion becomes the dominant process.
More Notes: The Probabilistic Coastline Recession model highlights that sea level rise significantly increases end-century recession at both swell and storm dominated coasts, while wave climate changes minimally impact recession; storm erosion becomes the primary driver of total recession for high-risk-averse decisions, essential for informed adaptation strategies in coastal management.
More Notes-sourcematerial: "Results show that SLR substantially increases the projected end-century recession at both types of coasts and that projected changes in the wave climate have only a marginal impact. ... For more risk-averse decisions that would typically account for higher potential recession (i.e. lower exceedance probability recessions), such as the placement of coastal infrastructure, multi-storey apartment buildings etc., storm erosion becomes the dominant process. The results of this study provide new insights on which physical processes need to be considered when and where in terms of numerical modelling efforts needed for supporting different management decisions, potentially enabling more streamlined and comprehensive assessments of the efficacy of coastal adaptation measures."
Hazard definition-sourcematerial: Results show that SLR substantially increases the projected end-century recession at both types of coasts...storm erosion becomes the dominant process.
Impact persistence-sourcematerial: The Sixth Assessment report (AR6) of the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) states with high confidence that most sandy coasts around the world will experience an increase in coastal erosion over the twenty-first century. An increase in long term coastal erosion (coastline recession) along sandy coasts can translate into massive socio-economic impacts, unless appropriate adaptation measures are implemented in the next few decades.
Space disaggregation-sourcematerial: "we apply the multi-scale Probabilistic Coastline Recession (PCR) model to two end-member sandy coastal types (swell dominated and storm dominated)"
Impact interactions-sourcematerial: The abstract focuses on the individual contributions of Sea Level Rise (SLR) and storm erosion to coastline recession, rather than how these impacts might interact with each other or with other sectors. It analyzes their relative dominance under different risk tolerance levels, but not synergistic or cascading effects.
Economic dynamics-sourcematerial: Here, we apply the multi-scale Probabilistic Coastline Recession (PCR) model to two end-member sandy coastal types (swell dominated and storm dominated), to investigate where and when coastline recession projections are dominated by the differential contributions from Sea Level Rise (SLR) and storm erosion. Results show that SLR substantially increases the projected end-century recession at both types of coasts and that projected changes in the wave climate have only a marginal impact. An analysis of the Process Dominance Ratio (PDR), introduced here, shows that the dominance of storm erosion over SLR (and vice versa) on total recession by 2100 depends on both the type of the beach and the risk tolerance levels.
Climate features-sourcematerial: The Sixth Assessment report (AR6) of the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) states with high confidence that most sandy coasts around the world will experience an increase in coastal erosion over the twenty-first century.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is mentioned as crucial to mitigate socio-economic impacts from increased coastal erosion. The study aims to inform adaptation measures by understanding the relative importance of physical processes driving coastline recession and their link to risk tolerance. The findings can help streamline assessments of coastal adaptation measures efficacy by identifying which processes (SLR vs. storm erosion) are dominant under different risk tolerance levels for various coastal management decisions.
Adaptation considered-sourcematerial: "An increase in long term coastal erosion (coastline recession) along sandy coasts can translate into massive socio-economic impacts, unless appropriate adaptation measures are implemented in the next few decades. To adequately inform adaptation measures, it is necessary to have a good understanding of the relative importance of the physical processes driving coastline recession...The results of this study provide new insights on which physical processes need to be considered when and where in terms of numerical modelling efforts needed for supporting different management decisions, potentially enabling more streamlined and comprehensive assessments of the efficacy of coastal adaptation measures."

Stresses caused by climate change, such as rising and hotter seas, increased droughts and floods, and acrid waters, threaten the world's most populated areas and aquatic ecosystems. Although local human activities have influenced coastal zones for millennia, it is still unclear how these impacts and stresses from climate change may combine to endanger coastal ecosystems.

To generate the data, we couple the Global Change Analysis Model (GCAM) with a land use spatial downscaling model (Demeter), a global hydrologic framework (Xanthos), and a water withdrawal downscaling model (Tethys).

Paper Title: Global warming increases risk from compound dry-hot events to human and agricultural systems
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased risk to human and agricultural systems (populations and cropland) globally, particularly in North America, Europe, Africa, Oceania (population) and northern South America, Europe, southeast China (cropland).
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Results showed a widespread increase in the risk to human and agricultural systems (represented by the populations and cropland) under the two scenarios (SSP2-4.5 and SSP5-8.5) across the globe, particularly in North America, Europe, Africa and Oceania for population and northern South America, large parts of Europe, and southeast China for cropland.
Drivers of Risk: Increase in hazards primarily contribute to increased population risk.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: The increase in hazards was shown to primarily contribute to the increased population risk.
Category-sourcematerial: Owing to their potentially amplified impacts, compound dry-hot events (CDHEs) can pose serious risk to human life and ecosystems...This study assessed the risk from CDHEs based on model simulations from Coupled Model Intercomparison Project 6 (CMIP6) models for different future periods...Results showed a widespread increase in the risk to human and agricultural systems (represented by the populations and cropland) under the two scenarios (SSP2-4.5 and SSP5-8.5) across the globe...The increase in hazards was shown to primarily contribute to the increased population risk.
Approach-sourcematerial: This study assessed the risk from CDHEs based on model simulations from Coupled Model Intercomparison Project 6 (CMIP6) models for different future periods, including near term (2021–2040), mid-term (2041–2060) and long term (2081–2100) periods, under two different Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSPs) scenarios. Using cropland and population as exposure indicators, the risk from CDHEs to human and agricultural systems was investigated by combing the hazard (the frequency or duration of CDHEs) and vulnerability indicators (GDP and irrigation fraction).
Methodology: Risk from compound dry-hot events (CDHEs) was assessed using Coupled Model Intercomparison Project 6 (CMIP6) model simulations for near-term (2021-2040), mid-term (2041-2060), and long-term (2081-2100) periods under SSP2-4.5 and SSP5-8.5 scenarios, combining hazard (frequency/duration of CDHEs), exposure (cropland and population), and vulnerability (GDP and irrigation fraction) indicators to investigate risk to human and agricultural systems globally.
Methodology-sourcematerial: This study assessed the risk from CDHEs based on model simulations from Coupled Model Intercomparison Project 6 (CMIP6) models for different future periods, including near term (2021–2040), mid-term (2041–2060) and long term (2081–2100) periods, under two different Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSPs) scenarios. Using cropland and population as exposure indicators, the risk from CDHEs to human and agricultural systems was investigated by combing the hazard (the frequency or duration of CDHEs) and vulnerability indicators (GDP and irrigation fraction).
Highlights: CDHE risk to human and agricultural systems, assessed using CMIP6 models, shows widespread increases under SSP2-4.5 and SSP5-8.5, particularly in North America, Europe, Africa and Oceania (population) and northern South America, Europe, and southeast China (cropland), with hazards primarily driving increased population risk; risk to human (cropland) systems increase by 136%, 213%, 207% (102%, 161% and 225%) in the near, mid, and long term, respectively.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Owing to their potentially amplified impacts, compound dry-hot events (CDHEs) can pose serious risk to human life and ecosystems...This study assessed the risk from CDHEs based on model simulations from Coupled Model Intercomparison Project 6 (CMIP6) models...under two different Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSPs) scenarios...Using cropland and population as exposure indicators, the risk from CDHEs to human and agricultural systems was investigated...Results showed a widespread increase in the risk to human and agricultural systems (represented by the populations and cropland) under the two scenarios (SSP2-4.5 and SSP5-8.5) across the globe, particularly in North America, Europe, Africa and Oceania for population and northern South America, large parts of Europe, and southeast China for cropland. The median values of relative changes in risk to human (cropland) systems were 136%, 213% and 207% (102%, 161% and 225%) for the near term, mid-term and long term, respectively. The increase in hazards was shown to primarily contribute to the increased population risk.
More Notes: Compound dry-hot events (CDHEs) will likely increase risk to human and agricultural systems globally, especially in North America, Europe, Africa, Oceania, and parts of South America and China, with hazard increases primarily driving increased population risk.

The increase in hazards was shown to primarily contribute to the increased population risk.

Paper Title: Integrated drought vulnerability and risk assessment for future scenarios: An indicator based analysis
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased global population at risk of drought; Varying increases in drought hazard across 58% of global land; Decreases in drought vulnerability across 50-80% of global land; Disproportionate impact on arid/semi-arid regions (Africa, Central Asia, West Asia, Australia); Increased drought vulnerability/risk in Amazon, central/western U.S.; Decreased drought vulnerability/risk in eastern China, southern India, Japan, Korea, Southeast Asia, northern Europe, South America (excluding Amazon); Reduced vulnerability but increased drought risk in eastern U.S.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: The study finds increased future population at risk of drought by 21.96% - 25.95% in the 2030s and 36.64% - 45.40% in the 2050s, driven by rapid population growth and changes in drought hazard and vulnerability, with 58% of global land facing increased drought hazard and 50% - 80% seeing decreases in drought vulnerability. Most arid/semi-arid regions, like Africa, Central Asia, West Asia, and Australia, face significant increases in drought hazard, vulnerability, and risk. In contrast, humid/semi-humid regions, including the Amazon and central/western U.S., experience increased drought vulnerability/risk, while regions like eastern China, southern India, Japan, Korea, Southeast Asia, and northern Europe see decreases. The eastern U.S. faces reduced vulnerability but increased drought risk.
Drivers of Risk: Rapid population growth; changes in drought hazard; changes in drought vulnerability; socioeconomic development; emission scenarios.

Paper Title: Root zone soil moisture in over 25% of global land permanently beyond pre-industrial variability as early as 2050 without climate policy
Outcome(s) of Interest: Regional shifts in root zone soil moisture beyond pre-industrial variability envelopes, with implications for agriculture and Earth system functions; Increased frequency and severity of permanent dry and wet departures across various regions, dependent on SSP scenario; Widespread soil moisture departures, especially under high emission scenarios, underscoring the need for mitigation.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "Here, we analysed root zone soil moisture departures from the pre-industrial climate variability for a multi-model ensemble of 14 Earth system models (ESMs) in the Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 6 (CMIP6) in four climate scenarios as defined by the shared socioeconomic pathways (SSPs) SSP1-2.6, SSP2-4.5, SSP3-7.0, and SSP5-8.5 between 2021 and 2100...We defined 'permanent departures'when a region's soil moisture exits the regional variability envelope of the pre-industrial climate and does not fall back into the range covered by the baseline envelope until 2100...Permanent dry departures (i.e. lower soil moisture than pre-industrial variability) were found to be most pronounced in Central America, southern Africa, the Mediterranean region, and most of South America, whereas permanent wet departures are most pronounced in south-eastern South America, northern Africa, and southern Asia...By 2100, the occurrence of permanent departures in SSP1-2.6 increases to 34% and, in SSP3-7.0, to 45%. Our findings underscore the importance of mitigation to avoid further degrading the Earth system functions upheld by soil moisture."
Drivers of Risk: Root zone soil moisture, pre-industrial baseline, green water planetary boundary, SSP scenarios, climate models, anthropogenic warming, land system change, regional warming, drying and wetting trends, water balance terms, emissions, adaptation, mitigation.

Approach-sourcematerial: Uses total soil moisture content from 14 different ESMs based on CMIP6 output to compare soil moisture content between 2021-2100 for different SSPs with a PiControl baseline to determine when the deviation of total soil moisture content from the baseline becomes permanent.
Methodology: The study analyzes total soil moisture content from 14 CMIP6-based ESMs (2021-2100) under SSP1-2.6, SSP2-4.5, SSP3-7.0, and SSP5-8.5, comparing them to a PiControl baseline across 43 IPCC climate reference regions to determine when soil moisture deviations become permanent, defining permanence as no return to the pre-industrial variability envelope by 2100; the analysis assesses yearly and monthly (12-month rolling mean) timescales, examining the frequency of models showing permanent departures.

Highlights: Root zone soil moisture departures serve as an early indicator of imminent long-term shifts in regional water cycles; the study emphasizes the critical importance of identifying the time when these permanent departures occur.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Root zone soil moisture departures act as an early warning for regions that are projected to have a permanently different water cycle in the coming decades. (Page 3) The study emphasizes time of emergence as a critical point for permanent wet or dry departures in soil moisture. (Page 4)
More Notes: The study determines future root zone soil moisture states (2021-2100) using CMIP6 models, comparing SSP scenarios to a pre-industrial control, aggregated to IPCC WGI climate reference regions to avoid downscaling issues; identifies permanent departures from baseline variability, quantifying affected global land area; notes inconsistencies in departure patterns, particularly in Asia; highlights spatial variability with decreased (red) and increased (blue) soil moisture relative to a PiControl baseline; model spread reflects different hypotheses; model limitations and uncertainties exist due to land-atmosphere interactions and prescribed land cover changes; emission trajectories are critical in determining regional drying/wetting patterns.

The analyses were done for 43 ice-free climate reference regions used by the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC).
Impact interactions-sourcematerial: In this study, we analysed the future (2021–2100) changes in total soil moisture in different IPCC reference regions with respect to the pre-industrial baseline (1850-like conditions). Four different scenarios (SSP1–2.6, SSP2–4.5, SSP3–7.0, and SSP5–8.5) from 14 ESMs in CMIP6 were studied. We evaluated different aspects: the deviation of total soil moisture from the PiControl scenario, permanent departures beyond the PiControl variability and the time of emergence of those permanent departures, on both yearly and monthly scales. Despite some ESM disagreement, we found several clear and consistent signals.

Adaptation considered: Adaptation considerations are acknowledged through the recognition of significant model uncertainties, particularly around the lack of observational data for validation and the prescribed land cover changes in ESMs, suggesting adaptive land management, such as improved vegetation models, could provide better insights. The study underscores the need for adaptation due to anticipated permanent soil moisture changes across different regions, even under SSP1–2.6, highlighting the role of mitigation and adaptation in managing new hydrological realities spurred by anthropogenic warming and land system changes captured in SSP scenarios.
Adaptation considered-sourcematerial: ESMs have particularly high uncertainties in certain regions...with conflicting departure signals among ESMs in each SSP scenario." The paper identifies model limitations and uncertainties in predicting soil moisture changes due to misrepresented land-atmosphere interactions and prescribed land cover changes: "Thus, given the ESM limitations in such regions and the lack of observational in situ data for validation, more research is needed...the land cover and its change in most ESMs are prescribed...hence, the root zone soil moisture may undergo dynamics due to land cover adaptation and land cover change...that may be better captured by more sophisticated vegetation models." "Even in the SSP1–2.6 trajectory in which the most vigorous climate mitigation efforts take place, the total soil moisture content in 33% of the ice-free land surface area is anticipated to permanently depart from the pre-industrial variability by the end of the 21st century...Hence, even with global mitigation efforts, these results call for swift adaptation to the new hydrological situation in several regions around the globe...pointing to the critical role of mitigation."

Paper Title: Spatiotemporal variations of global land surface wind speed and wind power energy density from 1950 to 2021; [1950-2021 年全球陆表风速及发电风能时空变化]
Outcome(s) of Interest: Global land surface wind speed and wind power energy density changes with significant regional, seasonal, and continental variations.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: (1) The global mean land surface wind speed increases slightly at an overall rate of 0.020 m/(s· 10a) from 1950 to 2021. (2) The surface wind speed changes on a global scale exhibit significant spatial differences... (3) As the seasonal changes, the land surface wind speed in winter season shows the significant changes... (4) The global wind power energy density has slightly increased, with an average growth rate of 3.017 W/(m2· 10a)... There were significant regional, seasonal, and monthly differences in global land surface wind speed changes.
Drivers of Risk: Climate change impacts; wind speed changes; regional, seasonal, and monthly differences; decreasing wind speeds in some regions (e.g., Asia); spatial differences (Africa, Antarctica).
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: (1) The global mean land surface wind speed increases slightly at an overall rate of 0.020 m/(s· 10a) from 1950 to 2021. (2) The surface wind speed changes on a global scale exhibit significant spatial differences, especially remarkable in Africa and Antarctica in comparison with other continents. (3) As the seasonal changes, the land surface wind speed in winter season shows the significant changes, with a changing rate of 0.033 m/(s· 10a)... Asia stands out as the only continent with an overall decrease, while the remaining four continents show minor changes.
Category-sourcematerial: [Objective] Climate change directly affects global wind speed changes and the efficiency of wind energy resource utilization... (4) The global wind power energy density has slightly increased, with an average growth rate of 3.017 W/(m2· 10a). The increase was most significant in January and December of the year, reaching 7.086 W/(m2· 10a) and 6.660 W/(m2· 10a), respectively.
Approach-sourcematerial: Using the ERA5-Land grid reanalysis wind speed dataset at the 0.1° resolution from the European Center for Medium Range Weather Forecasts (ECMWF) and methods such as least squares, Theil-Sen slope estimation, and MK trend test to study the annual, seasonal, and monthly average wind speed trends and the evolution of suitable wind power areas over the past 72 years from 1950 to 2021 worldwide.
Methodology: The ERA5-Land reanalysis wind speed dataset (0.1° resolution, 1950-2021) was analyzed using least squares, Theil-Sen slope estimation, and the Mann-Kendall trend test to study annual, seasonal, and monthly wind speed trends and changes in suitable wind power areas.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Using the ERA5-Land grid reanalysis wind speed dataset at the 0.1° resolution from the European Center for Medium Range Weather Forecasts (ECMWF) and methods such as least squares, Theil-Sen slope estimation, and MK trend test to study the annual, seasonal, and monthly average wind speed trends and the evolution of suitable wind power areas over the past 72 years from 1950 to 2021 worldwide.
Highlights: Global land surface wind speeds have slightly increased overall from 1950-2021, but with significant regional, seasonal, and monthly variations; wind power energy density has also slightly increased globally, with Antarctica and Africa showing the most significant increases, while Asia shows a decrease.
Highlights-sourcematerial: (1) The global mean land surface wind speed increases slightly at an overall rate of 0.020 m/(s· 10a) from 1950 to 2021. (2) The surface wind speed changes on a global scale exhibit significant spatial differences, especially remarkable in Africa and Antarctica in comparison with other continents. (3) As the seasonal changes, the land surface wind speed in winter season shows the significant changes, with a changing rate of 0.033 m/(s· 10a).... The monthly average wind speed change rate and range in Antarctica are relatively large.... (4) The global wind power energy density has slightly increased, with an average growth rate of 3.017 W/(m2· 10a). Antarctica and Africa lead in increasing wind power density for electricity generation, at 14.107 W/(m2· 10a) and 4.652 W/(m2· 10a) respectively. Asia stands out as the only continent with an overall decrease, while the remaining four continents show minor changes.
More Notes: Global land surface wind speeds have shown slight increases overall from 1950-2021, with significant regional, seasonal, and monthly variations; wind power density has also generally increased, although Asia shows a decrease.

There were significant regional, seasonal, and monthly differences in global land surface wind speed changes. ... (4) The global wind power energy density has slightly increased, with an average growth rate of 3.017 W/(m2· 10a). ... Asia stands out as the only continent with an overall decrease, while the remaining four continents show minor changes.

It focuses on analyzing historical changes and spatial and temporal variability in wind speed, as well as its implications for wind energy. There is no mention of whether these impacts are considered permanent, temporary, or adaptive.

[Methods] Using the ERA5-Land grid reanalysis wind speed dataset at the 0.1° resolution from the European Center for Medium Range Weather Forecasts (ECMWF) and methods such as least squares, Theil-Sen slope estimation, and MK trend test to study the annual, seasonal, and monthly average wind speed trends and the evolution of suitable wind power areas over the past 72 years from 1950 to 2021 worldwide. [Results] (1) The global mean land surface wind speed increases slightly at an overall rate of 0.020 m/(s· 10a) from 1950 to 2021...[Conclusion] There were significant regional, seasonal, and monthly differences in global land surface wind speed changes.

Adaptation considered: The paper acknowledges the necessity for ongoing dynamic monitoring and detailed analysis of wind speed and wind energy density changes as a means to support climate change response, resource management, and ecological protection. This implies adaptation considerations by highlighting the importance of tracking these changes for efficient resource utilization and sustainable development. However, the abstract does not provide specific adaptation strategies or discuss the extent of adaptation measures implemented.

In order to accurately support climate change response, resources and ecological protection, and efficient development, dynamic monitoring and detailed analysis of changes in land surface wind speed and wind energy density in each region is still needed."

Paper Title: Anthropogenic influence on extremes and risk hotspots
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased risk of extreme temperature/rainfall; 94%/72% global population affected; 97%/76% global GDP affected (2018 vs. 1961-1990).
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: We show that the risk from extreme temperature and rainfall events has severely increased for most regions worldwide...increased anthropogenic forcings are mostly responsible for increased risk to extreme temperature/precipitation affecting 94%/72% of global population and 97%/76% of global GDP relative to the baseline period 1961–1990.
Drivers of Risk: Anthropogenic forcing (main driver), natural forcing (amplified by anthropogenic), increased risk of extreme events.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Anthropogenic forcing has been the main driver of such increases and its effects amplify those of natural forcing.
Category-sourcematerial: We show that the risk from extreme temperature and rainfall events has severely increased for most regions worldwide. In some areas the probabilities of occurrence of extreme temperatures and precipitation have increased at least fivefold and twofold, respectively.
Approach-sourcematerial: Here, we provide spatial and temporal observation-based analyses of the role of natural and anthropogenic factors, using state-of-the-art time series methods.
Methodology: Uses observation-based spatial and temporal analyses with state-of-the-art time series methods to assess the roles of natural and anthropogenic factors in extreme temperature and rainfall events.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "Here, we provide spatial and temporal observation-based analyses of the role of natural and anthropogenic factors, using state-of-the-art time series methods."
Highlights: Anthropogenic forcing significantly increases the risk of extreme temperature and rainfall events globally, amplifying natural forcing effects, with increased risks disproportionately impacting regions with high population and GDP.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "We show that the risk from extreme temperature and rainfall events has severely increased for most regions worldwide...Anthropogenic forcing has been the main driver of such increases and its effects amplify those of natural forcing...We also identify risk hotspots defined as regions for which increased risk of extreme events and high exposure in terms of either high Gross Domestic Product (GDP) or large population are both present...increased anthropogenic forcings are mostly responsible for increased risk to extreme temperature/precipitation affecting 94%/72% of global population and 97%/76% of global GDP"
More Notes: Anthropogenic forcing significantly increases the risk of extreme temperature and rainfall events globally, affecting a large proportion of the population and GDP.
More Notes-sourcematerial: "We show that the risk from extreme temperature and rainfall events has severely increased for most regions worldwide...Anthropogenic forcing has been the main driver of such increases...For the year 2018, increased anthropogenic forcings are mostly responsible for increased risk to extreme temperature/precipitation affecting 94%/72% of global population and 97%/76% of global GDP."
Hazard definition-sourcematerial: "Record-breaking temperature and precipitation events have been studied using event-attribution techniques. ... We show that the risk from extreme temperature and rainfall events has severely increased for most regions worldwide."
Impact persistence-sourcematerial: The abstract focuses on the frequency and magnitude of climate extremes, the role of natural and anthropogenic factors in driving these extremes, and the increased risk to population and GDP. It doesn't explicitly address the persistence of impacts or how impacts might change over time due to adaptation or other factors.
Space disaggregation-sourcematerial: "We show that the risk from extreme temperature and rainfall events has severely increased for most regions worldwide." and "In some areas the probabilities of occurrence of extreme temperatures and precipitation have increased at least fivefold and twofold, respectively." and "We also identify risk hotspots defined as regions for which increased risk of extreme events and high exposure in terms of either high Gross Domestic Product (GDP) or large population are both present."
Impact interactions-sourcematerial: No specific text justifies N/A, but the absence of discussion of interactions between climate impacts is the justification.
Economic dynamics-sourcematerial: The abstract focuses on attributing changes in extreme event probabilities to natural and anthropogenic forcings and identifying "risk hotspots" based on current GDP and population exposure. There is no mention of economic dynamics, changes in economic activity over time, or adaptive behaviors.
Climate features-sourcematerial: The abstract focuses on the attribution of extreme temperature and precipitation events to anthropogenic and natural forcing, analyzing the increased risk and identifying hotspots based on GDP and population exposure. There is no mention of tipping points or other complex climate phenomena beyond extreme events.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is not explicitly considered in this abstract. The focus is on attribution of extreme events to anthropogenic forcing and the identification of risk hotspots based on exposure (GDP/population) and increased risk. No adaptive measures are mentioned.
Adaptation considered-sourcematerial: "We also identify risk hotspots defined as regions for which increased risk of extreme events and high exposure in terms of either high Gross Domestic Product (GDP) or large population are both present."

Paper Title: Designing for resilience and sustainability: An integrated systems approach
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased resilience, profitability, shareholder value, environmental and social sustainability, climate-resilient design.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "By designing both facilities and supply chain processes for increased flexibility, visibility, and agility, organizations can improve their resilience to unforeseen disruptions, thus contributing to profitability and shareholder value. Moreover, resilience often enhances long-term environmental and social sustainability...climate change scenarios were used in an integrated modeling approach to enable climate-resilient design for two industrial processes-heat exchange and urea manufacturing."
Drivers of Risk: Turbulent change, gradual stresses, sudden shocks, unforeseen disruptions, climate change scenarios.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Resilience is defined as the capacity for complex systems to survive, adapt, and grow in the face of turbulent change, including gradual stresses and sudden shocks...organizations can improve their resilience to unforeseen disruptions...climate change scenarios were used in an integrated modeling approach to enable climate-resilient design.
Category-sourcematerial: "climate change scenarios were used in an integrated modeling approach to enable climate-resilient design for two industrial processes-heat exchange and urea manufacturing."
Approach-sourcematerial: "network analysis was used to quantify the contribution of pollination services to economic performance" and "climate change scenarios were used in an integrated modeling approach to enable climate-resilient design for two industrial processes-heat exchange and urea manufacturing."
Methodology: A comprehensive protocol for designing resilient and sustainable systems has been developed, incorporating life cycle-based tools for quantifying benefits, broadening system boundaries to include ecosystem services, and using methods like network analysis to quantify dependencies (e.g., pollination services) and integrated modeling with climate change scenarios for climate-resilient design.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Through collaboration with global manufacturing firms, a comprehensive protocol has emerged for design of resilient and sustainable systems, along with life cycle-based tools for quantifying the resulting benefits. These methods broaden the system boundary to account for the hidden role of ecosystem services in enterprise operations. For instance, network analysis was used to quantify the contribution of pollination services to economic performance, revealing the indirect dependencies of many industrial sectors on natural pollinators. In another application, climate change scenarios were used in an integrated modeling approach to enable climate-resilient design for two industrial processes-heat exchange and urea manufacturing.
Highlights: Resilience strategies enhance business risk management by improving flexibility, visibility, and agility in facilities and supply chains, contributing to profitability, sustainability, and consideration of ecosystem services in enterprise operations, including climate change scenarios for resilient design.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Resilience is defined as the capacity for complex systems to survive, adapt, and grow in the face of turbulent change, including gradual stresses and sudden shocks...By designing both facilities and supply chain processes for increased flexibility, visibility, and agility, organizations can improve their resilience to unforeseen disruptions, thus contributing to profitability and shareholder value...resilience often enhances long-term environmental and social sustainability...These methods broaden the system boundary to account for the hidden role of ecosystem services in enterprise operations...climate change scenarios were used in an integrated modeling approach to enable climate-resilient design for two industrial processes-heat exchange and urea manufacturing.
More Notes: Resilience strategies, including flexibility, visibility, and agility in facilities and supply chains, can improve business profitability, shareholder value, and long-term environmental and social sustainability, particularly through circular economies and consideration of ecosystem services, with methods and tools available for quantification, including climate-resilient design.
More Notes-sourcematerial: "Resilience is defined as the capacity for complex systems to survive, adapt, and grow in the face of turbulent change, including gradual stresses and sudden shocks...By designing both facilities and supply chain processes for increased flexibility, visibility, and agility, organizations can improve their resilience to unforeseen disruptions, thus contributing to profitability and shareholder value. Moreover, resilience often enhances long-term environmental and social sustainability, as in the case of "circular" economic systems that strive to eliminate waste...These methods broaden the system boundary to account for the hidden role of ecosystem services in enterprise operations...climate change scenarios were used in an integrated modeling approach to enable climate-resilient design..."

Paper Title: Advancing the estimation of future climate impacts within the United States
Outcome(s) of Interest: Net national damages, human health impacts, mortality, temperature changes, air pollution, regional variation, societal inequalities, premature mortality, net present damages, greenhouse gas emissions
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Results from FrEDI show that net national damages increase overtime, with mean climate-driven damages estimated to reach USD 2.9 trillion (95 % confidence interval (CI): USD 510 billion to USD 12 trillion) annually by 2090...the majority of annual long-Term (e.g., 2090) damages are associated with climate change impacts to human health, including mortality attributable to climate-driven changes in temperature and air pollution (O3 and PM2.5) exposure...Regional results also show that annual long-Term climate-driven damages vary geographically...Climate change impacts may also broaden existing societal inequalities, with, for example, Black or African Americans being disproportionately affected by additional premature mortality from changes in air quality...FrEDI projections are extended through 2300 to estimate the net present climate-driven damages within US borders from marginal changes in greenhouse gas emissions.
Drivers of Risk: Temperature, sea level rise, air quality (O3, PM2.5), wildfires, dust, high-tide flooding, tropical storms, Valley fever.

Page 3: Shocks or changes impacting the outcomes include changes in global mean surface temperature and global mean sea level rise. Page 4: Impacts are driven by changes in CONUS temperature and global mean sea level rise. Page 6: The damages are linked to climate-driven changes in temperature, air quality, Valley fever, dust, wildfire smoke, and crime incidents. Page 7: The key drivers of risk identified include climate-driven changes in temperature, air quality, tropical storm frequency and wind strength, high-tide flooding, wildfire smoke exposure, southwestern dust exposure, and Valley fever incidence. Page 8: Impacts driven by factors like temperature increase, air quality deterioration, and increased incidents of wildfires. Page 9: Key drivers of risk include changes in temperature, climate-driven air quality changes, high-tide flooding, and wildfires. Page 10: Shocks impacting outcomes include temperature, wildfire, agriculture, labor, air quality, wind damage, and transportation & high tide flooding. Page 11: Changes in air quality impact mortality rates and labor hours lost. Page 12: Primary driver of risk evaluated is temperature changes which lead to premature mortality. Page 14: Changes in crop yields (wheat, cotton, soybean, maize), coastal property value, power sector costs, transmission/distribution infrastructure damage, hot and cold temperatures, high-tide flooding, riverine flooding, work hours lost, thermally available habitat for commercial fish species, ozone and fine particulate matter exposure, property and violent crimes, temperature-induced track buckling, road surface quality, fine and coarse dust particle exposure, hurricane wind damage, urban drainage infrastructure needs, water quality changes, wildfire emissions, alpine recreation revenue, and Valley Fever. Highlights: The study examines climate change impacts on the U.S. and finds that long-term damages are predominantly health-related, with temperature-induced premature mortality being the largest factor, financially estimated at USD 2.3 trillion annually by 2090. The Southeast faces the highest per capita damages. Climate impacts significantly affect racial and vulnerable socioeconomic groups, exacerbating inequalities. The report highlights that proactive adaptation can alleviate future damages and reveals how different sectors (e.g., coastal properties, labor) exhibit increasing damage trends. Finally, adaptation potential varies, and benefits from greenhouse gas mitigation underscore the importance of climate action.

Page 16: Following Melvin et al. (2017): reactive adaptation, where decision makers respond to climate change impacts by repairing damaged infrastructure but do not take actions to prevent or mitigate future climate change impacts ... and proactive adaptation, where decision makers take adaptive action with the goal of preventing infrastructure repair costs associated with future climate change impacts.

Climate features-sourcematerial:
FrEDI also has the capability to investigate adaptation options in select sectors. Available adaptation options reflect the treatment of adaptation in the underlying sectoral studies. For most of these studies, because the implicit or explicit impact response functions are calibrated to historical or current data, historically practiced adaptation or hazard avoidance actions are “baked in”, while enhanced adaptation action or new (currently unknown) technologies are not considered.

FrEDI is extended by linearly extrapolating its sector-specific, temperature-binned damage functions to account for the full range of temperature scenarios derived from the RFF-SP emission scenarios run through FaIR (some of which have degrees of warming above 10◦C).

These sectors exhibit consistent upward trends across most graphs, indicating worsening impacts of climate change. Some sectors show discontinuities at temperature bin boundaries or thresholds (e.g., coastal properties after sea levels breach 100cm).

FrEDI assumes a linear rate of change in damages equal to the change in damages from 5 to 6◦. Adjustment factors developed from two constant population scenarios where per capita damage rates from 2090 are applied for 2090–2300 (Adjustment2b).

Since this work incorporates multiple disciplines, emission projections, climate modeling, impact modeling, and economic communities, it has the potential to be a useful tool in bridging the research gap between these communities and helping to address some of the omitted climate change risks currently within this field.

Due to FrEDI’s flexible framework, it allows for the model to be continually updated as studies of impacts on new sectors or updates to outdated sectoral studies become available.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is limitedly incorporated in the analysis, with scenarios such as reactive adaptation, reasonably anticipated adaptation, and no additional adaptation being considered for some sectors. Future enhanced adaptation or new technologies are not included. The FrEDI model does take into account historically practiced adaptation actions in its damage estimates, specifically reflecting these in some infrastructure sectors. However, many sectors' damages do not incorporate future adaptive measures, and the study emphasizes findings without significant future adaptation or enhanced adaptive measures. Different adaptation scenarios are explicitly considered for infrastructure costs, but overall assumptions suggest adaptation has the potential to both exacerbate and mitigate climate-driven impacts, with the latter being more frequent.
Adaptation considered-sourcematerial:
Page 5: This analysis groups mean damages from each of 20 FrEDI sectors into six topical categories and uses the default FrEDI adaptation assumptions of “reactive”, “reasonably anticipated adaptation”, or “no additional adaptation” (see Table A3) for each sector.

Page 13: FrEDI also has the additional capability to investigate some of these adaptation options in select sectors by reflecting the treatment in the underlying sector studies. FrEDI maintains adaptation assumptions from the underlying studies that form the basis of FrEDI’s temperature-driven sectoral damage functions. For most of these studies, because the implicit or explicit impact response function is calibrated to historical or current data, this means that historically practiced adaptation or hazard avoidance actions are “baked in” – but enhanced adaptation action or new (currently unknown) technologies are not considered.

Page 8: Historical data suggest that most of our response to sea level rise thus far is in between reactive adaptation and no adaptation. Notably, the largest impact sector in this study, temperature-related mortality, does not include assumptions about future adaptation.

Page 16: For infrastructure sectors (i.e., rail, roads, electricity transmission and distribution infrastructure; coastal properties; and transportation impacts from high-tide flooding), a no additional adaptation approach to infrastructure management does not incorporate climate change risks.

Page 16: Following Melvin et al. (2017): reactive adaptation, where decision makers respond to climate change impacts by repairing damaged infrastructure but do not take actions to prevent or mitigate future climate change impacts ... and proactive adaptation, where decision makers take adaptive action with the goal of preventing infrastructure repair costs associated with future climate change impacts.

Page 15: Temporal trends are a function of the underlying temperature (or sea level rise) binned damage functions and sector-specific scalars.

Conversely, NOx and SO2 began declining recently due to stringent controls in major contributors such as China, a trend expected to persist. Paper Title: Successful Integration of UAV Aeromagnetic Mapping with Terrestrial Methane Emissions Surveys in Orphaned Well Remediation
Outcome(s) of Interest: Methane emissions contributing to greenhouse gas concentrations and exacerbating climate change.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: methane emissions from orphaned wells contribute to global greenhouse gas concentrations and without proper mitigation, these emissions contribute to and further exacerbate global climate change processes.
Drivers of Risk: Orphaned/unregulated oil & gas wells; uncontrolled methane emissions; lack of effective identification methodology for heavy emitters; limited remediation resources.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "Orphaned oil and gas wells represent an important environmental and economic development concern...environmental impact of orphaned wells is largely associated with uncontrolled emissions of both fluids and gasses, most notably atmospheric methane...Often neglected in regulatory oversight, methane emissions from orphaned wells contribute to global greenhouse gas concentrations...An inherent challenge of remediation efforts targeting orphaned wells is the consistent observation that only a fraction of located and identified wells produce the majority of methane emissions, yet no methodology exists to effectively separate out heavy emitters...In a regulatory environment where resources dedicated to wellsite remediation are limited..."
Category-sourcematerial: Orphaned oil and gas wells represent an important environmental and economic development concern in areas where historical energy exploration and production activity pre-dated regulations on well abandonment documentation practices...the environmental impact of orphaned wells is largely associated with uncontrolled emissions of both fluids and gasses, most notably atmospheric methane...methane emissions from orphaned wells contribute to global greenhouse gas concentrations and without proper mitigation, these emissions contribute to and further exacerbate global climate change processes.
Approach-sourcematerial: We demonstrate that emitting wells have certain defined characteristics largely pertaining to the presence and integrity of metal casing to the surface, which can be distinguished as an organized high intensity magnetic anomaly in low-altitude UAV-based aeromagnetic surveys. In this paper, we present a database of identified and ground-truthed wellsites correlated to their magnetic signatures and provide a roadmap to initial prioritization of wellsites for subsequent remediation activities that can be implemented in complex environments where other survey options are not feasible.
Methodology: UAV-based aeromagnetic surveys can identify high intensity magnetic anomalies associated with emitting orphaned oil and gas wells, characterized by the presence and integrity of metal casing to the surface, allowing for prioritization of wellsites for remediation based on magnetic signatures.
Methodology-sourcematerial: emitting wells have certain defined characteristics largely pertaining to the presence and integrity of metal casing to the surface, which can be distinguished as an organized high intensity magnetic anomaly in low-altitude UAV-based aeromagnetic surveys. ... present a database of identified and ground-truthed wellsites correlated to their magnetic signatures and provide a roadmap to initial prioritization of wellsites for subsequent remediation activities ... propose implementing this methodology as a key element of a targeted approach to wellsite prioritization for subsequent remediation activity.
Highlights: Orphaned oil and gas wells contribute to methane emissions and climate change, and a methodology using UAV-based aeromagnetic surveys allows efficient identification of high-emission wells for prioritized remediation.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Orphaned oil and gas wells represent an important environmental and economic development concern...while the environmental impact of orphaned wells is largely associated with uncontrolled emissions of both fluids and gasses, most notably atmospheric methane...methane emissions from orphaned wells contribute to global greenhouse gas concentrations...only a fraction of located and identified wells produce the majority of methane emissions...emitting wells have certain defined characteristics...which can be distinguished as an organized high intensity magnetic anomaly in low-altitude UAV-based aeromagnetic surveys...propose implementing this methodology as a key element of a targeted approach to wellsite prioritization for subsequent remediation activity.

Paper Title: Erratum: Globally unequal effect of extreme heat on economic growth (Science Advances DOI: 10.1126/sciadv.add3726)
Outcome(s) of Interest: Economic loss; GDP impact; income inequality; extreme heat.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "Cumulative 1992–2013 losses from anthropogenic extreme heat likely fall between $16 trillion and $50 trillion globally. Losses amount to 8% of Gross Domestic Product per capita per year for regions in the bottom income decile, but only 3.5% for regions in the top income decile." "GDPpc is >7% per year lower than it would have been without anthropogenic effects on extreme heat in tropical countries such as Brazil, Venezuela, and Mali." "In high-latitude nations such as Canada and Finland, anthropogenic extreme heat changes have depressed GDPpc by ~3% per year." "Cumulative global losses due to extreme heat average more than $33 trillion in 2013." "For example, regions in the lowest income decile have experienced losses of 8% of GDPpc per year, compared to 3.5% in the top income decile."
Drivers of Risk: Anthropogenic extreme heat (Tx5d), average temperatures, temperature variability, regional average temperatures, tropical regions.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Page 1: "Increased extreme heat events driven by anthropogenic global warming."; Page 2: "Extreme heat, specifically the intensity of the most extreme heat wave each year, impacts economic growth in different regions based on their average temperatures."; Page 3: "Extreme heat and temperature variability are key drivers impacting growth and economic outcomes."; Page 4: "Anthropogenic increases in extreme heat (Tx5d) and changes in average temperatures are the primary drivers of risk."; Page 5: "Changes in extreme heat intensity, specifically relating to anthropogenic changes in Tx5d, affect economic growth and GDPpc."; Page 6: "Anthropogenic increases in extreme heat intensity are identified as the primary driver of economic impacts."; Page 7: "Changes in average temperatures and extreme heat intensity due to anthropogenic climate change."; Page 8: "Extreme heat is the primary driver of economic and adaptive resource considerations."; Page 10: "The impact of anthropogenic changes in extreme temperatures (Tx5d) on growth."
Category-sourcematerial: Economic growth is evaluated in relation to extreme heat impacts, particularly in poorer, tropical regions; The outcome being evaluated is the economic growth impact of extreme heat in various regions; Economic production and income trajectories are the outcomes being evaluated; The paper evaluates the economic impact of anthropogenic changes in extreme heat represented by changes in GDP per capita (GDPpc) across regions.
Approach-sourcematerial: Page 1: The analysis employs subnational economic data, extreme heat metrics (like Tx5d), and climate models to assess economic impacts. Page 4: The study uses a distributed lag model applied to observed and counterfactual Tx5d values, derived from CMIP6 climate model experiments, along with observed and counterfactual average temperatures. Page 5: The analysis involves simulating counterfactual regional GDPpc using observed and modeled temperature extremes, applying econometric models, and conducting a Monte Carlo analysis to quantify uncertainty. Page 9: Panel regression with fixed effects is used to model economic growth as a function of extreme high temperatures, temperature variability, mean temperature, region-specific time-invariant characteristics, and common global time-varying factors.
Methodology: The study quantifies the economic impact of extreme heat on global economic growth using subnational economic data, extreme heat metrics (Tx5d), and climate models (CMIP6). It models economic growth as a function of extreme high temperatures, temperature variability, mean temperature, region-specific time-invariant characteristics, and common global time-varying factors using panel regression with fixed effects. A distributed lag model assesses the persistence of extreme heat impacts. Counterfactual Tx5d values from climate models are used to isolate the contribution of anthropogenic warming. A statistical model predicts regional GDPpc where data is limited, and Monte Carlo analysis and bootstrapping quantify uncertainty.

The study uses a distributed lag model applied to observed and counterfactual Tx5d values, derived from CMIP6 climate model experiments, along with observed and counterfactual average temperatures. A statistical model is used to infer regional GDPpc time series where data is unavailable (1992–2013). (Page 4)
