More Notes-sourcematerial: Of particular note, there is new information about the way in which exposure to UV radiation modulates the immune system, causing both harms and benefits for health.
Hazard definition-sourcematerial: This assessment by the Environmental Effects Assessment Panel (EEAP) of the Montreal Protocol under the United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP) evaluates the effects of ultraviolet (UV) radiation on human health within the context of the Montreal Protocol and its Amendments. We assess work published since our last comprehensive assessment in 2018. Over the last four years gains have been made in knowledge of the links between sun exposure and health outcomes, mechanisms, and estimates of disease burden, including economic impacts. Of particular note, there is new information about the way in which exposure to UV radiation modulates the immune system, causing both harms and benefits for health. The burden of skin cancer remains high, with many lives lost to melanoma and many more people treated for keratinocyte cancer, but it has been estimated that the Montreal Protocol will prevent 11 million cases of melanoma and 432 million cases of keratinocyte cancer that would otherwise have occurred in the United States in people born between 1890 and 2100. While the incidence of skin cancer continues to rise, rates have stabilised in younger populations in some countries. Mortality has also plateaued, partly due to the use of systemic therapies for advanced disease. However, these therapies are very expensive, contributing to the extremely high economic burden of skin cancer, and emphasising the importance and comparative cost-effectiveness of prevention. Photodermatoses, inflammatory skin conditions induced by exposure to UV radiation, can have a marked detrimental impact on the quality of life of sufferers. More information is emerging about their potential link with commonly used drugs, particularly anti-hypertensives. The eyes are also harmed by over-exposure to UV radiation. The incidence of cataract and pterygium is continuing to rise, and there is now evidence of a link between intraocular melanoma and sun exposure. It has been estimated that the Montreal Protocol will prevent 63 million cases of cataract that would otherwise have occurred in the United States in people born between 1890 and 2100. Despite the clearly established harms, exposure to UV radiation also has benefits for human health. While the best recognised benefit is production of vitamin D, beneficial effects mediated by factors other than vitamin D are emerging. For both sun exposure and vitamin D, there is increasingly convincing evidence of a positive role in diseases related to immune function, including both autoimmune diseases and infection. With its influence on the intensity of UV radiation and global warming, the Montreal Protocol has, and will have, both direct and indirect effects on human health, potentially changing the balance of the risks and benefits of spending time outdoors.

Paper Title: The impact of global warming on obesity
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased obesity rates, especially in temperate zones. Worldwide increase of 79.7 million obese adults (12.3%) per 1°C increase in annual mean temperature.

The estimates suggest that a 1°C increase in the annual mean temperature would result in a worldwide increase in obese adults of 79.7 million, or 12.3%.

Hazard definition-sourcematerial:
Our study differs from Trentinaglia et al. (2021) in at least four major aspects. First, we examine the effect of global warming on obesity (defined as a BMI exceeding 30 kg/m2), while they focused on the effect of global warming on BMI... Second, we are able to identify both the short-run and long-run effects of warming based on different model settings, while they only examined the short-run effects... Finally, our study additionally examines the potential channels through which global warming affects obesity. To the best of our knowledge, our study is the first to directly examine the overall impact of warming on obesity based on long-run global data and to evaluate the potential of long-run adaptation in offsetting this impact.

Our primary analysis uses data from 1975 to 2016 for 152 countries (listed in Table A.1) with a population size larger than half a million in 2000...The climate data is obtained from the Climatic Research Unit...We calculate country-level climate measures as the population-weighted average across all climate grids within each country.

"The baseline model includes eight control variables (i.e., log GDP per capita and its square, annual total precipitation and its square, wet day frequency, frost day frequency, cloud cover percentage, and vapor pressure)" "temperature fluctuations are random and thus not influenced by any confounding factors" "Our analysis focuses on climate change predictions from 2016 to 2050."

Evaluation overview: "capture the causal effect of temperature on obesity." Methodology: "The baseline model employs a J-degree temperature polynomial to capture the well-documented nonlinear effect of temperature ... we expand model (1) to incorporate the interaction between income and the temperature polynomial."

Source material: 4.1 Impact on obesity; temperature coefﬁcient α′j is estimated by comparing the same country over different 30-year periods, capturing the impact of warming over 30 years; We then infer the effect of adaptation by comparing estimates from the two models...the short-run model relies on year-to-year temperature variations to identify the short-term effect, while the long-run model relies on 30-year temperature trends to identify the long-term effect...

Page 26: Source material here. Figure 14 presents the predicted country-level impact of global warming on obesity by the year 2050. These predictions are generated by combining the estimated country-level marginal effects with the projected temperature changes for each country from 2016 to 2050...Specifically, the predictions are made through three steps. Firstly, we estimate the nonlinear marginal effects of temperature on obesity using a third-degree version of model (1)...Panel B presents the predicted impact based on the long-run model. It indicates that global warming is likely to increase obesity in most countries from temperate zones while reducing obesity in most countries from subtropical and tropical zones...The country-level projected warming used in these calculations is presented in Appendix Figure A.3. For a comprehensive view, Appendix Figures A.4 and A.5 depict the predicted impacts under the low emissions scenario SSP1-1.9 and the high emissions scenario SSP5-8.5, respectively.

The impact of global warming on obesity, subsequently impacting human capital and economic performance. Our study aims to address this gap in the literature. We estimate the causal effect of global warming on obesity using plausibly exogenous year-to-year temperature fluctuations. The estimates suggest that global warming significantly increased obesity in most countries, with the exception of a small number of tropical countries. We estimate that a 1°C rise in annual mean temperature would increase the number of obese adults worldwide by 12.3%. Moreover, we identify substantial heterogeneity in the impact across countries in various regions and with differing income levels, age structures, and education levels. Finally, we infer the effect of long-term adaptation by comparing the baseline model with a long-difference model. Our findings indicate that long-term adaptation may not significantly mitigate the impact of global warming on obesity in temperate zones. We conclude by highlighting two limitations of this study. First, due to the lack of long-term sub-country data on obesity for most countries, we are unable to provide a within-country analysis with a reasonably large sample size... this study focuses explicitly on the effect of global warming on obesity."
Impact persistence-sourcematerial: Page 3: To infer the effect of long-term adaptation, we also estimate a “long-difference” model. The estimates suggest that global warming significantly increased obesity rates in countries within temperate zones while reducing obesity only in tropical countries. However, the long-term model suggests a larger, rather than smaller, impact of warming on obesity in temperate zones, indicating that adaptation cannot significantly offset the impact of warming on obesity.
Space disaggregation-sourcematerial: Our primary analysis uses data from 1975 to 2016 for 152 countries (listed in Table A.1) with a population size larger than half a million in 2000...The climate data is obtained from the Climatic Research Unit...We calculate country-level climate measures as the population-weighted average across all climate grids within each country.
Impact interactions-sourcematerial: Page 28: Global warming tends to reduce income in countries from the tropical and temperate zones (Dell et al. 2012; Burke et al. 2015). Lower income may have a limited impact on obesity in the short run if individuals can smooth consumption by using savings and borrowing. However, lower income is likely to reduce obesity in poor countries in the long run when consumption smoothing is infeasible. The second factor is the cumulative effect over time. If the impact of warming on obesity accumulates over time, the long-run impact could be larger than the short-run impact.
Economic dynamics: Adaptive (reflecting shifts in economic behavior in response to impacts)

Second, we are able to identify both the short-run and long-run effects of warming based on different model settings... Finally, our study additionally examines the potential channels through which global warming affects obesity. To the best of our knowledge, our study is the first to directly examine the overall impact of warming on obesity based on long-run global data and to evaluate the potential of long-run adaptation in offsetting this impact.
Climate features-sourcematerial: There is no mention of climate tipping points, abrupt changes, or other complex climate system dynamics.
Adaptation considered: Long-term adaptation is assessed using a "long-difference" model, estimating the effects of long-term temperature trends. The study compares short-run and long-run models, attributing differences to potential adaptation. Findings suggest that adaptation may not significantly mitigate global warming's impact on obesity in temperate zones, as the long-run model indicates a larger positive impact compared to the short-run model. Factors contributing to discrepancies include income variation and cumulative effects over time, suggesting adaptation's limited role in reducing obesity impacts. The analysis focuses on income, consumption patterns, and different regional impacts, emphasizing variability in adaptation across countries.
Adaptation considered-sourcematerial: Page 3: "To infer the effect of long-term adaptation, we also estimate a “long-difference” model... However, the long-term model suggests a larger, rather than smaller, impact of warming on obesity in temperate zones, indicating that adaptation cannot significantly offset the impact of warming on obesity."; Page 10: "We then infer the effect of adaptation by comparing estimates from the two models...the short-run model relies on year-to-year temperature variations to identify the short-term effect, while the long-run model relies on 30-year temperature trends to identify the long-term effect."; Page 28: "Given that individuals are more likely to adapt to long-term trends than to short-term ﬂuctuations, the difference between these two models reﬂects the effect of adaptation... It reveals that the long-run model predicts a more positive impact in temperate zones but a more negative impact in subtropical and tropical zones. This ﬁnding suggests that long-term adaptation does not signiﬁcantly reduce the impact of warming on obesity... the more negative impact in subtropical and tropical zones likely reﬂects that warming reduces income and, consequently, long-term consumption in these economically disadvantaged areas." ; Page 29: "Finally, we infer the effect of long-term adaptation by comparing the baseline model with a long-difference model. Our findings indicate that long-term adaptation may not significantly mitigate the impact of global warming on obesity in temperate zones."

Paper Title: Viticultural Manipulation and New Technologies to Address Environmental Challenges Caused by Climate Change
Outcome(s) of Interest: Disruption of grapevine growth, production, and wine quality; Cost-effectiveness and growth of the wine industry in different wine regions.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Climate change is a critical challenge for the global grape and wine industry, as it can disrupt grapevine growth, production, and wine quality. Climate change could influence the cost-effectiveness and growth of the wine industry in different wine regions since grapevine development is deeply dependent on weather (short-term) and climate (long-term) conditions.
Drivers of Risk: Climate change; grapevine growth disruption; production impact; wine quality degradation; weather dependence; climate dependence; pest and disease pressure; crop load influence; grape and wine composition alteration.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Climate change is a critical challenge for the global grape and wine industry, as it can disrupt grapevine growth, production, and wine quality. Climate change could influence the cost-effectiveness and growth of the wine industry in different wine regions since grapevine development is deeply dependent on weather (short-term) and climate (long-term) conditions. This review article addresses the impact of climate change on grapevines, such as vine phenology, pest and disease pressure, crop load, and grape and wine composition.
Category-sourcematerial: Climate change is a critical challenge for the global grape and wine industry, as it can disrupt grapevine growth, production, and wine quality.
Approach-sourcematerial: This review article addresses the impact of climate change on grapevines, such as vine phenology, pest and disease pressure, crop load, and grape and wine composition. It also reviews recent advances in the areas of viticultural manipulation and relevant technologies to potentially reduce the impact of climate change and help growers improve grape quality. Remote sensing is used for vineyard microclimate monitoring; thermal sensors combined with UAVs, aircraft, or satellites are used for water management; soil electrical conductivity sensors have been developed for soil mapping. Viticultural manipulations, such as regulated deficit irrigation for water use efficiency and berry-ripening delay for growing quality fruit, are also discussed.
Methodology: The review examines the use of remote sensing (thermal sensors with UAVs, aircraft, or satellites for water management; soil electrical conductivity sensors for soil mapping) and viticultural manipulations like regulated deficit irrigation and berry-ripening delay to address climate change impacts on grapevines.

Paper Title: Sustainable intensification with irrigation raises farm profit despite climate emergency
Outcome(s) of Interest: Farm profitability; crop productivity; water use; economic risk; impact of climate crisis; adaptation strategies.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "Without adaptation, the climate crisis detrimentally impacted on farm profitability due to the combination of increased evaporative demand and increased drought frequency." "Whole-farm intensification—via greater irrigated land use, incorporation of rice, cotton and maize and increased nitrogen fertiliser application—was the only adaptation capable of raising farm productivity under future climates." "Diversification through incorporation of grain legumes into crop rotations significantly improved profitability under historical climates; however, profitability of this adaptation declined under future climates." "Simplified systems reduced economic risk but also had lower long-term economic returns." "(1) When assessing whole-farm profit, metrics matter: Diversified systems generally had higher profitability than Intensified systems per unit water, but not per unit land area; (2) gravity-based irrigation infrastructure required the most water, followed by sprinkler systems, whereas Drip irrigation used the least water; (3) whole-farm agronomic adaptation through management and crop genotype had greater impact on productivity compared with changes in irrigation infrastructure; and (4) only whole-farm intensification was able to raise profitability under future climates."
Drivers of Risk: Climate crisis, increased evaporative demand, increased drought frequency, profitability decline, irrigation infrastructure, farm intensification, whole-farm adaptation
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Without adaptation, the climate crisis detrimentally impacted on farm profitability due to the combination of increased evaporative demand and increased drought frequency. Diversification through incorporation of grain legumes into crop rotations significantly improved profitability under historical climates; however, profitability of this adaptation declined under future climates. Simplified systems reduced economic risk but also had lower long-term economic returns. (2) gravity-based irrigation infrastructure required the most water, followed by sprinkler systems, whereas Drip irrigation used the least water; (4) only whole-farm intensification was able to raise profitability under future climates.
Category-sourcematerial: Societal Impact Statement: Despite comprising a small proportion of global agricultural land use, irrigated agriculture is enormously important to the global agricultural economy. Burgeoning food demand driven by population growth—together with reduced food supply caused by the climate crisis—is polarising the existing tension between water used for agricultural production versus that required for environmental conservation. We show that sustainable intensification via more diverse crop rotations, more efficient water application infrastructure and greater farm area under irrigation is conducive to greater farm business profitability under future climates.
Approach-sourcematerial: Here, we demonstrate how a new framework—WaterCan Profit—can be used to elicit such complexity using an irrigated case study farm with four whole-farm adaptation scenarios (Baseline, Diversified, Intensified and Simplified) with four types of irrigated infrastructure (Gravity, Pipe & Riser, Pivot and Drip).
Methodology: The study uses a "WaterCan Profit" framework to analyze the impacts of four whole-farm adaptation scenarios (Baseline, Diversified, Intensified, Simplified) with four irrigation infrastructure types (Gravity, Pipe & Riser, Pivot, Drip) on an irrigated case study farm under future climates, considering land-use changes in space and time.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Research aimed at improving crop productivity often does not account for the complexity of real farms underpinned by land-use changes in space and time. Here, we demonstrate how a new framework—WaterCan Profit—can be used to elicit such complexity using an irrigated case study farm with four whole-farm adaptation scenarios (Baseline, Diversified, Intensified and Simplified) with four types of irrigated infrastructure (Gravity, Pipe & Riser, Pivot and Drip).
Highlights: Whole-farm profit metrics are key to assessing adaptation strategies; gravity irrigation uses the most water, followed by sprinklers, with drip irrigation using the least; agronomic adaptation has a greater impact on productivity than changes in irrigation infrastructure; and only whole-farm intensification can raise profitability under future climates.
Highlights-sourcematerial: (1) When assessing whole-farm profit, metrics matter: Diversified systems generally had higher profitability than Intensified systems per unit water, but not per unit land area; (2) gravity-based irrigation infrastructure required the most water, followed by sprinkler systems, whereas Drip irrigation used the least water; (3) whole-farm agronomic adaptation through management and crop genotype had greater impact on productivity compared with changes in irrigation infrastructure; and (4) only whole-farm intensification was able to raise profitability under future climates.
More Notes: Whole-farm intensification (more irrigation, diverse crops, and increased fertilizer) was the only adaptation strategy that increased farm profitability under future climate scenarios, while diversification was more profitable historically but declined in the future.
More Notes-sourcematerial: "Without adaptation, the climate crisis detrimentally impacted on farm profitability...Whole-farm intensification...was the only adaptation capable of raising farm productivity under future climates...Diversification through incorporation of grain legumes into crop rotations significantly improved profitability under historical climates; however, profitability of this adaptation declined under future climates."

Without adaptation, the climate crisis detrimentally impacted on farm profitability due to the combination of increased evaporative demand and increased drought frequency.
Economic dynamics-sourcematerial: Summary: Research aimed at improving crop productivity often does not account for the complexity of real farms underpinned by land-use changes in space and time. Here, we demonstrate how a new framework—WaterCan Profit—can be used to elicit such complexity using an irrigated case study farm with four whole-farm adaptation scenarios (Baseline, Diversified, Intensified and Simplified) with four types of irrigated infrastructure (Gravity, Pipe & Riser, Pivot and Drip).
Climate features-sourcematerial: The abstract focuses on farm-level adaptations to climate change and does not mention tipping points or other large-scale climate complexities.
Adaptation considered: The study uses four whole-farm adaptation scenarios: Baseline, Diversified, Intensified, and Simplified. Intensification (greater irrigated land use, incorporation of new crops, increased nitrogen) was the only adaptation raising farm productivity under future climates. Diversification improved profitability historically, but declined in the future. Simplified systems reduced risk but also returns. Management and crop genotype changes had a greater impact than irrigation infrastructure changes.
Adaptation considered-sourcematerial: We demonstrate how a new framework—WaterCan Profit—can be used to elicit such complexity using an irrigated case study farm with four whole-farm adaptation scenarios (Baseline, Diversified, Intensified and Simplified) with four types of irrigated infrastructure (Gravity, Pipe & Riser, Pivot and Drip). Whole-farm intensification—via greater irrigated land use, incorporation of rice, cotton and maize and increased nitrogen fertiliser application—was the only adaptation capable of raising farm productivity under future climates. Diversification through incorporation of grain legumes into crop rotations significantly improved profitability under historical climates; however, profitability of this adaptation declined under future climates. Simplified systems reduced economic risk but also had lower long-term economic returns. Whole-farm agronomic adaptation through management and crop genotype had greater impact on productivity compared with changes in irrigation infrastructure.

Paper Title: Effects of shade and deficit irrigation on maize growth and development in fixed and dynamic AgriVoltaic systems
Outcome(s) of Interest: Decreased leaf area index, total dry matter, and grain yield; Reduced irrigation inputs; Increased phyllochron; Delayed phenology; Reduced stomatal conductance, net assimilation of CO2, and leaf temperature.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: We showed that maize crop responded to both independent and combined stresses (shade and water deficit), with a significant decrease in leaf area index, total dry matter and grain yield. Concerning water use, we showed the potential of AV to reduce irrigation inputs (by up to 19–47% compared to unshaded plots) via reduced soil water depletion and reference evapotranspiration. The crop development was impacted by shade by increasing phyllochron and causing a generalized delay in phenology. At a finer temporal scale, we concluded that maize leaves react to shade by reducing stomatal conductance, net assimilation of CO2 and leaf temperature in a correlated way to radiation, opening the possibility to use this behavior to optimize water use and shading strategies.
Drivers of Risk: Climate change, water scarcity, excessive radiation, shade from AgriVoltaics (AV) panels, water deficit.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Maize production is essential for global food security...the projected effects of climate change are likely to decrease drastically water availability for crops in many regions, affecting yield...protecting plants from excessive radiation...shade from panels may also affect crop development and production...maize crop responded to both independent and combined stresses (shade and water deficit), with a significant decrease in leaf area index, total dry matter and grain yield.
Category-sourcematerial: Maize production is essential for global food security and represents a major supply in several value chains. However, the projected effects of climate change are likely to decrease drastically water availability for crops in many regions, affecting yield. AgriVoltaics (AV) systems are an innovative solution that may improve maize resilience in water-scarce regions mainly by protecting plants from excessive radiation and by reducing irrigation needs. However, shade from panels may also affect crop development and production.
Approach-sourcematerial: This study addresses the interplay between radiation transmission, crop development and irrigation needs of maize cropping in field conditions, by the description of crop development dynamics, distinguishing between fixed and dynamic panels.
Methodology: The study analyzed maize crop development dynamics under AgriVoltaic (AV) systems (fixed and dynamic panels) in field conditions, focusing on radiation transmission, crop development, and irrigation needs, assessing the individual and combined effects of shade and water deficit on leaf area index, total dry matter, and grain yield, while also measuring stomatal conductance, net assimilation of CO2, and leaf temperature to understand water use optimization potential.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "This study addresses the interplay between radiation transmission, crop development and irrigation needs of maize cropping in field conditions, by the description of crop development dynamics, distinguishing between fixed and dynamic panels. We showed that maize crop responded to both independent and combined stresses (shade and water deficit), with a significant decrease in leaf area index, total dry matter and grain yield. Concerning water use, we showed the potential of AV to reduce irrigation inputs (by up to 19–47% compared to unshaded plots) via reduced soil water depletion and reference evapotranspiration. The crop development was impacted by shade by increasing phyllochron and causing a generalized delay in phenology. At a finer temporal scale, we concluded that maize leaves react to shade by reducing stomatal conductance, net assimilation of CO2 and leaf temperature in a correlated way to radiation, opening the possibility to use this behavior to optimize water use and shading strategies."
Highlights: AgriVoltaics (AV) can improve maize resilience in water-scarce regions by reducing irrigation needs (19-47%) through decreased soil water depletion and reference evapotranspiration, though shade from panels can negatively impact crop development, decreasing leaf area index, total dry matter, and grain yield; dynamic AV systems show promise in reducing spatial heterogeneities in soil water depletion compared to fixed AV.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "AgriVoltaics (AV) systems are an innovative solution that may improve maize resilience in water-scarce regions mainly by protecting plants from excessive radiation and by reducing irrigation needs...we showed the potential of AV to reduce irrigation inputs (by up to 19–47% compared to unshaded plots) via reduced soil water depletion and reference evapotranspiration...the crop development was impacted by shade by increasing phyllochron and causing a generalized delay in phenology...with a significant decrease in leaf area index, total dry matter and grain yield...dynamic AV showed their ability to reduce the spatial heterogeneities in soil water depletion, showing the importance of controlled shade strategies in AV systems concerning water use."

Paper Title: Hydrodynamic impacts on algal blooms in reservoirs and bloom mitigation using reservoir operation strategies: A review
Outcome(s) of Interest: Reduced algal blooms; improved water quality.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Inducing unfavorable hydrodynamic environments for algal growth through reservoir operations is considered an efficient, low-cost, and clean method for controlling algal blooms in reservoirs. Enhancing direct hydrodynamic effects (including flow velocity, water turbulence, and shear stress) could inhibit algal growth. Hypolimnetic withdrawal might be a long-term prevention strategy for controlling algal blooms because it removes the internal nutrient load and alters water stratification, while epilimnetic withdrawal, which flushes away algae in upper layers, may be an emergency measure.
Drivers of Risk: Global warming, eutrophication, hydrodynamic conditions, nutrient distribution, water retention, stratification, light distribution.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Algal blooms that have substantial adverse effects are becoming increasingly prevalent in reservoirs, especially under global warming and eutrophication conditions...intensifying indirect hydrodynamic effects (including water retention, stratification, nutrient distribution, and light distribution) could promote algal blooms in reservoirs.
Category-sourcematerial: Algal blooms that have substantial adverse effects are becoming increasingly prevalent in reservoirs, especially under global warming and eutrophication conditions. ...enhancing direct hydrodynamic effects (including flow velocity, water turbulence, and shear stress) could inhibit algal growth, while intensifying indirect hydrodynamic effects (including water retention, stratification, nutrient distribution, and light distribution) could promote algal blooms in reservoirs. ...hypolimnetic withdrawal might be a long-term prevention strategy for controlling algal blooms because it removes the internal nutrient load and alters water stratification...
Approach-sourcematerial: The author first reviewed relevant literature and concluded that enhancing direct hydrodynamic effects (including flow velocity, water turbulence, and shear stress) could inhibit algal growth, while intensifying indirect hydrodynamic effects (including water retention, stratification, nutrient distribution, and light distribution) could promote algal blooms in reservoirs. The author then classified reservoir operation strategies into basic reservoir operation, selective withdrawal operation, and multi-objective optimal operation and pointed out that the latter could be key to practical engineering applications.
Methodology: The methodology involved reviewing literature to understand the impact of hydrodynamic conditions on algal blooms, classifying reservoir operation strategies into three types to inhibit or promote algal growth, and analyzing strategies like hypolimnetic and epilimnetic withdrawal for their potential in controlling the blooms.
Methodology-sourcematerial: The author first reviewed relevant literature and concluded that enhancing direct hydrodynamic effects ... could inhibit algal growth, while intensifying indirect hydrodynamic effects ... could promote algal blooms in reservoirs. Moreover, hypolimnetic withdrawal might be a long-term prevention strategy for controlling algal blooms ... while epilimnetic withdrawal, which flushes away algae in upper layers, may be an emergency measure.
Highlights: Direct hydrodynamic effects (flow velocity, turbulence, shear stress) inhibit algal growth, while indirect effects (water retention, stratification, nutrient/light distribution) promote it; multi-objective optimal reservoir operation, especially hypolimnetic withdrawal, offers long-term algal bloom control by removing nutrients and altering stratification.

Paper Title: Challenges and opportunities for biochar to promote circular economy and carbon neutrality
Outcome(s) of Interest: Biochar, carbon neutrality, circular economy, CO2 sequestration, emissions reduction, soil health, agricultural productivity, biomass management, UN SDGs.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Biochar...sequester carbon in soil and to remediate aquatic environment...soil conditioner...biochar offers opportunity through a circular economy...progress toward low-emissions materials accelerates their advance towards net-zero emissions...strengthening biomass management through CE and resource recovery paradigms...promote net zero emissions in its applications...close the loops by implementing the circularity of biomass resources...biomass conversion into biochar can promote net zero emissions and CE in the framework of the UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)...biochar application as soil enrichment offsets 12% of CO2 emissions by land use annually...improve its health and agricultural productivity, while minimizing about 1/8 of CO2 emissions...sequester CO2 in the long-term and prevent the release of carbon back into the atmosphere after its decomposition...This practice could sequester 2.5 gigatons (Gt) of CO2 annually...promote carbon neutrality and CE applications.
Drivers of Risk: Unused biomass; Soil degradation; CO2 emissions from land use; Decomposition of organic matter releasing carbon.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Biochar application as soil enrichment offsets 12% of CO2 emissions by land use annually. Paper Title: Quantifying the effects of vegetation productivity and drought scenarios on livestock production decisions and income
Outcome(s) of Interest: Significant economic losses at the ranch level (up to 32.1% in net income over 40 years) due to drought linked to climate change factors (temperature, vapor pressure deficit, precipitation).
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Our findings indicate forage productivity is tightly linked to mean annual temperature, vapor pressure deficit, and precipitation, and both drought scenarios resulted in significant economic effects at the ranch level (i.e., up to 32.1% losses in net income over a 40-year timespan).
Drivers of Risk: Mean annual temperature, vapor pressure deficit, precipitation.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Our findings indicate forage productivity is tightly linked to mean annual temperature, vapor pressure deficit, and precipitation...
Category-sourcematerial: • The amount of available summer forage for livestock is becoming more uncertain under a changing climate. • Remote sensing estimates of herbaceous biomass production are becoming more readily available for managers and scientists, making assessments of available forage across large regions possible. • We coupled remotely sensed forage estimates with a ranch-level economic model to assess the effects of drought (short and long term) on several key economic factors in the future. • Our findings indicate forage productivity is tightly linked to mean annual temperature, vapor pressure deficit, and precipitation, and both drought scenarios resulted in significant economic effects at the ranch level (i.e., up to 32.1% losses in net income over a 40-year timespan).
Approach-sourcematerial: We coupled remotely sensed forage estimates with a ranch-level economic model to assess the effects of drought (short and long term) on several key economic factors in the future.
Methodology: Remotely sensed forage estimates were coupled with a ranch-level economic model to assess the effects of drought (short and long term) on key economic factors.

Highlights: Changing climate impacts summer forage availability for livestock, and drought scenarios significantly affect ranch-level economics (up to 32.1% net income losses); coupling remote sensing data with economic models improves understanding and adaptation strategies for rangeland management.
Highlights-sourcematerial: • The amount of available summer forage for livestock is becoming more uncertain under a changing climate. • Our findings indicate forage productivity is tightly linked to mean annual temperature, vapor pressure deficit, and precipitation, and both drought scenarios resulted in significant economic effects at the ranch level (i.e., up to 32.1% losses in net income over a 40-year timespan). • Even though our established drought scenarios are hypothetical, the coupling of remote sensing data with economic models further increases the understanding of the effects of a changing climate on rangeland productivity and can ultimately improve implementation of adaptive rangeland management strategies.

Paper Title: Regulating the time of the crop model clock: A data assimilation framework for regions with high phenological heterogeneity
Outcome(s) of Interest: Improved yield estimation, especially in low-yield plots, through assimilation of phenological data; further improvements with simultaneous assimilation of canopy cover, biomass, and phenology.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Results: In the real-world paddy rice case, assimilating phenology with the proposed strategies significantly improved yield estimation in low-yield plots (less than 4 ton/ha) compared to assimilating canopy cover (CC) alone, with an R2 increase from 0.07 to 0.48. Assimilating CC, biomass and phenology simultaneously produced the best yield estimation for all plots, with R2 = 0.57 and RMSE = 1.00 ton/ha.
Drivers of Risk: Mismatch between simulated and observed phenology; phenological heterogeneity; disturbance of model clock.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "the mismatch between simulated and observed phenology significantly deteriorates the performance of crop growth modeling...where the phenological heterogeneity was high even when climate condition was relatively uniform...a considerable proportion of model parameters and variables are entangled with phenology, therefore simply assimilating phenological measurements could disturb the model clock."
Category-sourcematerial: "in crop growth data assimilation systems...","...AquaCrop model to simulate crop growth...","...yield estimation..."
Approach-sourcematerial: The framework used an open-source version of the AquaCrop model to simulate crop growth and used the ensemble Kalman filter (EnKF) to assimilate observations sequentially. A parameter refresh method was proposed to restore the phenological consistency of model parameters after updating the phenology state. Assimilation strategies with different observation types and compositions of state vectors were designed after a global sensitivity analysis of model parameters. These strategies were evaluated through the Observing System Simulation Experiments (OSSE), and the selected strategies were tested in a real-world case.
Methodology: An open-source AquaCrop model and the ensemble Kalman filter (EnKF) were used, incorporating a parameter refresh method to maintain phenological consistency after state updates. Assimilation strategies based on global sensitivity analysis were designed and evaluated through Observing System Simulation Experiments (OSSE) and a real-world case study, with different observation types and state vector compositions.
Methodology-sourcematerial: the framework used an open-source version of the AquaCrop model to simulate crop growth and used the ensemble Kalman filter (EnKF) to assimilate observations sequentially. A parameter refresh method was proposed to restore the phenological consistency of model parameters after updating the phenology state. Assimilation strategies with different observation types and compositions of state vectors were designed after a global sensitivity analysis of model parameters. These strategies were evaluated through the Observing System Simulation Experiments (OSSE), and the selected strategies were tested in a real-world case.
Highlights: Assimilation of phenological data within a dynamically consistent framework significantly improves crop yield estimation, especially in areas with high phenological variability, demonstrating the potential for improved regional crop modeling and yield forecasting.
Highlights-sourcematerial: The abstract states that assimilating phenological measurements with a parameter refresh method and specific assimilation strategies resolved the phenological mismatch and improved yield estimates, as evidenced by results showing an R2 increase from 0.07 to 0.48 in low-yield plots and an overall R2 = 0.57 with RMSE = 1.00 ton/ha for all plots. It concludes that this improved yield estimation is significant when phenological heterogeneity is high, highlighting the framework's potential for regional crop modeling and forecasting.
More Notes: Assimilating phenological data, particularly in heterogeneous smallholder fields, significantly improves crop yield estimation, especially in low-yield plots, by addressing phenological mismatches and maintaining model clock consistency, offering potential for regional in-season crop modeling and yield forecasting.

Paper Title: Future global socioeconomic risk changes to rainstorms based on the different return periods of CMIP6
Outcome(s) of Interest: Extreme precipitation, rainstorm risk, socioeconomic impact, regional variations, future scenarios, intensity increase, return periods, global assessment, climate models, disaster prevention.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Based on the future daily precipitation simulation data of 24 global climate models of the Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSP) 2-4.5 in the sixth phase of the Coupled Model Intercomparison Program (CMIP6), the reanalysis precipitation data of ERA5, and the prediction of population and gross domestic product (GDP), this study evaluated the socioeconomic risk of future global rainstorms at four return periods (5 years, 10 years, 20 years, 50 years) and their changes relative to historical periods. The main conclusions are as follows:... 1) Under the future scenarios, the rainstorm intensity in Oceania is the highest and that in Europe is the lowest. With the increase of the return period, the areas where rainstorms occur continue to expand, and the intensity of rainstorms is also projected to increase.... 3) The regions with the most obvious increase of rainstorm risk in the future are concentrated in the southern and eastern coastal areas of Asia, the central, northern, and southeastern areas of Africa, and the eastern, western, and southern coastal areas of North America.... The study can provide a certain theoretical support for disaster prevention and mitigation in areas affected by extreme precipitation.
Drivers of Risk: Rainstorm intensity, population change, GDP change.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "The factors that are most related to risk changes at the four return periods differ in the 10 countries with significant changes in risk. The risk change of Russia has the greatest correlation with the change of rainstorm intensity. The risk changes of the United States, Brazil, India, Mexico, the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Argentina, and Australia have the greatest correlation with the change of population. The risk changes of Canada and China have the greatest correlation with the change of GDP."
Category-sourcematerial: Based on the future daily precipitation simulation data of 24 global climate models of the Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSP) 2-4.5 in the sixth phase of the Coupled Model Intercomparison Program (CMIP6), the reanalysis precipitation data of ERA5, and the prediction of population and gross domestic product (GDP), this study evaluated the socioeconomic risk of future global rainstorms at four return periods (5 years, 10 years, 20 years, 50 years) and their changes relative to historical periods.
Approach-sourcematerial: Based on the future daily precipitation simulation data of 24 global climate models of the Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSP) 2-4.5 in the sixth phase of the Coupled Model Intercomparison Program (CMIP6), the reanalysis precipitation data of ERA5, and the prediction of population and gross domestic product (GDP), this study evaluated the socioeconomic risk of future global rainstorms at four return periods (5 years, 10 years, 20 years, 50 years) and their changes relative to historical periods.
Methodology: Utilizes daily precipitation simulations from 24 CMIP6 global climate models (SSP2-4.5), ERA5 reanalysis data, and population/GDP projections to evaluate socioeconomic risk of global rainstorms at 5, 10, 20, and 50-year return periods, and their changes relative to historical periods.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Based on the future daily precipitation simulation data of 24 global climate models of the Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSP) 2-4.5 in the sixth phase of the Coupled Model Intercomparison Program (CMIP6), the reanalysis precipitation data of ERA5, and the prediction of population and gross domestic product (GDP), this study evaluated the socioeconomic risk of future global rainstorms at four return periods (5 years, 10 years, 20 years, 50 years) and their changes relative to historical periods.
Highlights: Future rainstorm intensity is projected to be highest in Oceania and lowest in Europe, increasing with return period. Asia and Africa face the highest rainstorm risk, while Europe and Oceania face the lowest. Risk increases most notably in coastal Asia, central/northern/southeastern Africa, and coastal North America, with risk reductions mainly in central/southern Europe, northwestern/eastern Africa, and northern South America. The factors most related to risk changes vary by country: rainstorm intensity for Russia, population for the US, Brazil, India, Mexico, DR Congo, Argentina, and Australia, and GDP for Canada and China.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Based on the future daily precipitation simulation data of 24 global climate models of SSP 2-4.5 in CMIP6, ERA5 reanalysis data, and predictions of population and GDP, this study evaluated the socioeconomic risk of global rainstorms. Conclusions include: 1) Rainstorm intensity is highest in Oceania and lowest in Europe, with both intensity and occurrence areas increasing with return period; 2) Rainstorm risk is lowest in Europe and Oceania, highest in Asia and Africa; 3) Notable future risk increases are in southern/eastern Asia, various African regions, and coastal North America, while risk reductions are in parts of Europe, Africa, and South America; 4) Risk change factors differ by country, correlated with rainstorm intensity for Russia, population for the US, Brazil, India, Mexico, DR Congo, Argentina, and Australia, and GDP for Canada and China.

Paper Title: Temperature, storage, and natural gas futures prices
Outcome(s) of Interest: Changes in NG storage, futures prices, and variance.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "...important for explaining weekly changes in NG in storage and daily changes in NG futures prices and their variance."
Drivers of Risk: Temperature shocks (HDD, CDD based on hourly data), oil futures prices and volatilities, storage announcements.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Previous literature suggests that daily changes in US natural gas (NG) futures prices and their variance can be explained by changes in oil futures prices and volatilities, storage announcements, and temperature shocks. These studies measure temperature shocks using the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration definitions of heating degree day (HDD) and cooling degree day (CDD)... Our results show that the proposed measures of HDD and CDD and temperature changes are important for explaining weekly changes in NG in storage and daily changes in NG futures prices and their variance.
Category-sourcematerial: Previous literature suggests that daily changes in US natural gas (NG) futures prices and their variance can be explained by ... temperature shocks. ... Our results show that the proposed measures of HDD and CDD and temperature changes are important for explaining weekly changes in NG in storage and daily changes in NG futures prices and their variance.
Approach-sourcematerial: "measure expected values of variables via econometric models instead of historical averages" and "Our results show that the proposed measures of HDD and CDD and temperature changes are important for explaining weekly changes in NG in storage and daily changes in NG futures prices and their variance."
Methodology: The methodology involves using hourly temperatures to better capture deviations from comfort levels, temperature variability, and thermal inertia, and incorporates economically relevant weights and econometric models for national average temperature shocks and expected values, enhancing the analysis of natural gas futures prices and storage.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Previous literature suggests that daily changes in US natural gas (NG) futures prices and their variance can be explained by changes in oil futures prices and volatilities, storage announcements, and temperature shocks... We show how hourly temperatures can be used to better measure how long as well as how much temperatures depart above and/or below the “comfort level” within a day, the effects of temperature variability within a day, and the effects of thermal inertia...Another innovation is that we use more economically relevant weights to convert regional temperature shocks to national averages, and measure expected values of variables via econometric models instead of historical averages.
Highlights: Hourly temperature data and economically relevant weighting improve the measurement of temperature shocks on natural gas markets compared to traditional heating/cooling degree days.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Previous literature suggests that daily changes in US natural gas (NG) futures prices and their variance can be explained by changes in oil futures prices and volatilities, storage announcements, and temperature shocks. These studies measure temperature shocks using the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration definitions of heating degree day (HDD) and cooling degree day (CDD). We show how hourly temperatures can be used to better measure how long as well as how much temperatures depart above and/or below the “comfort level” within a day, the effects of temperature variability within a day, and the effects of thermal inertia. Hourly temperatures also allow us to construct measures of extreme temperatures that better match the timing of release of other market information. Another innovation is that we use more economically relevant weights to convert regional temperature shocks to national averages.
More Notes: Improved measures of heating/cooling degree days using hourly temperatures, economically relevant weighting, and econometric modeling better explain changes in natural gas storage and futures prices.
More Notes-sourcematerial: "We show how hourly temperatures can be used to better measure how long as well as how much temperatures depart above and/or below the “comfort level” within a day... Another innovation is that we use more economically relevant weights to convert regional temperature shocks to national averages, and measure expected values of variables via econometric models instead of historical averages. Our results show that the proposed measures of HDD and CDD and temperature changes are important for explaining weekly changes in NG in storage and daily changes in NG futures prices and their variance."

Paper Title: No-till imparts yield stability and greater cumulative yield under variable weather conditions in the southeastern USA piedmont
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased maize yields (42-93%) with no-till/conservation tillage; Reduced maize yield variation (10-32%) with no-till in low soil moisture; Increased probability of high maize/soybean yields (90th percentile) with no-till (15%/10%).
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Yield and weather data from a 28-year tillage study in the southeastern U.S. piedmont region were analyzed... No-till and conservation tillage increased maize yields 42–93% and no-till decreased coefficient of variation of maize yields when soil moisture was low by 10–32%... the probability of reaching the 90th yield percentile was greater in no-till than conventional tillage in both maize and soybean, by 15% and 10%, respectively.
Drivers of Risk: Soil moisture; temperature; surface residue management; lack of soil health differentiation.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Growing seasons were grouped by soil moisture and temperature during crop growth stages...No-till and conservation tillage increased maize yields 42–93% and no-till decreased coefficient of variation of maize yields when soil moisture was low by 10–32%...Results suggest that surface residue management may be an important driver of system performance, possibly more so than overall soil health...Previous reports from the site have likewise indicated little differentiation in soil health between tillage systems over the life of the study.
Category-sourcematerial: Yield and weather data from a 28-year tillage study in the southeastern U.S. piedmont region were analyzed to determine the effect of various conservation tillage practices on maize and soybean productivity and stability under a variety of growth conditions. ... No-till and conservation tillage increased maize yields 42–93% and no-till decreased coefficient of variation of maize yields when soil moisture was low by 10–32%, but had a less pronounced effect on soybean yields. ... Results suggest that surface residue management may be an important driver of system performance, possibly more so than overall soil health.
Approach-sourcematerial: Yield and weather data from a 28-year tillage study in the southeastern U.S. piedmont region were analyzed to determine the effect of various conservation tillage practices on maize and soybean productivity and stability under a variety of growth conditions. Growing seasons were grouped by soil moisture and temperature during crop growth stages, and mean crop yields and yield coefficient of variation for the tillage treatments were calculated within the year clusters. Probability density estimates were also used to predict the likelihood of obtaining yields at low and high percentiles.
Methodology: Analysis of 28-year yield and weather data from a southeastern U.S. tillage study; growing seasons grouped by soil moisture and temperature during crop growth stages; calculation of mean crop yields and yield coefficient of variation for tillage treatments within year clusters; probability density estimates used to predict likelihood of obtaining yields at low and high percentiles.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Yield and weather data from a 28-year tillage study in the southeastern U.S. piedmont region were analyzed to determine the effect of various conservation tillage practices on maize and soybean productivity and stability under a variety of growth conditions. Growing seasons were grouped by soil moisture and temperature during crop growth stages, and mean crop yields and yield coefficient of variation for the tillage treatments were calculated within the year clusters. Probability density estimates were also used to predict the likelihood of obtaining yields at low and high percentiles.
Highlights: No-till and conservation tillage significantly increased maize yields and stability under low soil moisture conditions in the southeastern U.S. piedmont, while both maize and soybean had a higher probability of reaching high yield percentiles with no-till compared to conventional tillage, suggesting surface residue management is key.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Yield and weather data from a 28-year tillage study in the southeastern U.S. piedmont region were analyzed ... No-till and conservation tillage increased maize yields 42–93% and no-till decreased coefficient of variation of maize yields when soil moisture was low by 10–32% ... the probability of reaching the 90th yield percentile was greater in no-till than conventional tillage in both maize and soybean, by 15% and 10%, respectively ... Results suggest that surface residue management may be an important driver of system performance.
More Notes: In a long-term study in the southeastern U.S., no-till and conservation tillage generally increased maize yields, especially under low soil moisture, and improved the probability of achieving high yields for both maize and soybean, suggesting surface residue management's importance.
More Notes-sourcematerial: Yield and weather data from a 28-year tillage study in the southeastern U.S. piedmont region were analyzed to determine the effect of various conservation tillage practices on maize and soybean productivity and stability... No-till and conservation tillage increased maize yields 42–93% and no-till decreased coefficient of variation of maize yields when soil moisture was low by 10–32%... However, the probability of reaching the 90th yield percentile was greater in no-till than conventional tillage in both maize and soybean, by 15% and 10%, respectively... Results suggest that surface residue management may be an important driver of system performance, possibly more so than overall soil health.

Paper Title: For whom the bell tolls: Climate change and income inequality
Outcome(s) of Interest: Rising income inequality, particularly in developing countries.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "We find that an increase in climate change vulnerability is positively associated with rising income inequality...While climate change vulnerability has no statistically significant effect on income distribution in advanced economies, the coefficient on climate change vulnerability is seven times greater and statistically highly significant in the case of developing countries..."
Drivers of Risk: Climate change vulnerability; weaker adaptation/mitigation capacity in developing countries.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: We find that an increase in climate change vulnerability is positively associated with rising income inequality... the coefficient on climate change vulnerability is seven times greater and statistically highly significant in the case of developing countries due largely to weaker capacity for climate change adaptation and mitigation.
Category-sourcematerial: The paper discusses the impact of climate change on income inequality rather than focusing on one of the specific categories listed. It highlights the association between climate change vulnerability and rising income inequality, with a particular focus on the difference between advanced economies and developing countries. This topic does not fall neatly into one of the specified categories like agriculture, energy, or health impacts, and is instead more closely related to social and economic dimensions, making "Other" the most fitting classification.
Approach-sourcematerial: Using a large panel of 158 countries during the period 1995–2019...These findings are robust with alternative estimation methods and measures of income inequality.
Methodology: Employs a panel dataset of 158 countries from 1995-2019 to analyze the association between climate change vulnerability and income inequality.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "using a large panel of 158 countries during the period 1995–2019...we provide new evidence on the association between climate change and income inequality...splitting the sample into country groups..."
Highlights: Climate change vulnerability is linked to rising income inequality, particularly in developing countries where adaptation and mitigation capacities are weaker, while no significant impact is observed in advanced economies.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "We find that an increase in climate change vulnerability is positively associated with rising income inequality...While climate change vulnerability has no statistically significant effect on income distribution in advanced economies, the coefficient on climate change vulnerability is seven times greater and statistically highly significant in the case of developing countries due largely to weaker capacity for climate change adaptation and mitigation."
More Notes: Climate change vulnerability exacerbates income inequality, particularly in developing countries, due to their limited adaptation and mitigation capacities.

We find that an increase in climate change vulnerability is positively associated with rising income inequality.

Paper Title: Sources and uncertainties of future global drought risk with ISIMIP2b climate scenarios and socioeconomic indicators
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased drought risk in populous and agriculturally dependent regions; Agricultural land use is the largest contributor to global drought risk (except Africa, where population is the largest contributor).
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: The projections suggest that in addition to increasing drought hazards caused by climate change, populous regions, or areas heavily dependent on agriculture are at a higher risk than other regions because of high water consumption levels. The contributions analysis indicates that agricultural land use is the largest contributor to drought risk, except for Africa, where the population makes the largest contribution.
Drivers of Risk: Hazard (precipitation, evapotranspiration), Exposure (population, agricultural land), Vulnerability (GDP, water storage, water consumption).
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Drought hazard is evaluated using the standardized precipitation-evapotranspiration index. Drought exposure considers population and agricultural land use, and drought vulnerability accounts for gross domestic product, total water storage, and water consumption.
Category-sourcematerial: "This study aims to demonstrate projected future global drought risk, which is quantified based on indicators representing three risk components, namely, hazard, exposure, and vulnerability."
Approach-sourcematerial: This study aims to demonstrate projected future global drought risk, which is quantified based on indicators representing three risk components, namely, hazard, exposure, and vulnerability. Drought hazard is evaluated using the standardized precipitation-evapotranspiration index. Drought exposure considers population and agricultural land use, and drought vulnerability accounts for gross domestic product, total water storage, and water consumption. This global-scale study was conducted for the historical and future periods of 1975–2005 and 2070–2099, respectively, and employed three combined scenarios consisting of Representative Concentration Pathways (RCPs) and Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSPs) with datasets from the Inter-Sectoral Impact Model Intercomparison Project (ISIMIP2b).
Methodology: Drought risk was quantified using indicators for hazard (standardized precipitation-evapotranspiration index), exposure (population and agricultural land use), and vulnerability (gross domestic product, total water storage, and water consumption) for historical (1975-2005) and future (2070-2099) periods using ISIMIP2b datasets across three RCP/SSP scenarios.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "Drought risk is quantified based on indicators representing three risk components, namely, hazard, exposure, and vulnerability. Drought hazard is evaluated using the standardized precipitation-evapotranspiration index. Drought exposure considers population and agricultural land use, and drought vulnerability accounts for gross domestic product, total water storage, and water consumption. This global-scale study was conducted for the historical and future periods of 1975–2005 and 2070–2099, respectively, and employed three combined scenarios consisting of Representative Concentration Pathways (RCPs) and Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSPs) with datasets from the Inter-Sectoral Impact Model Intercomparison Project (ISIMIP2b)."
Highlights: Future drought risk is projected globally using hazard (precipitation-evapotranspiration index), exposure (population, agriculture), and vulnerability (GDP, water storage, consumption) indicators; populous and agriculturally dependent regions face higher risk due to high water consumption; agricultural land use is the largest contributor to drought risk globally except in Africa (population); model uncertainty dominates over RCP/SSP scenarios, with GCM uncertainty the most dominant.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Drought hazard is evaluated using the standardized precipitation-evapotranspiration index. Drought exposure considers population and agricultural land use, and drought vulnerability accounts for gross domestic product, total water storage, and water consumption...projections suggest that in addition to increasing drought hazards caused by climate change, populous regions, or areas heavily dependent on agriculture are at a higher risk than other regions because of high water consumption levels. The contributions analysis indicates that agricultural land use is the largest contributor to drought risk, except for Africa, where the population makes the largest contribution. Model uncertainty of the General Circulation Models (GCMs) and Hydrological Models (HMs) is dominant compared to the RCP and SSP scenarios, with uncertainty from the GCMs the most dominant.
More Notes: Projects increased global drought risk, particularly in populous and agricultural regions, with agricultural land use being the largest contributor to risk globally, except in Africa where population is the largest contributor; model uncertainty is dominant.

Paper Title: Radiative Effects and Costing Assessment of Arctic Sea Ice Albedo Changes
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased Arctic SIRF leads to warming equivalent to 34.5 Gt CO2 increase; loss of 24.4-48.8 trillion USD for climate regulation service (CRS); projected average annual cost of 6.7-13.3 trillion USD (2020-2100, RCP 8.5).
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Through model transformation, it is shown that the Arctic SIRF rising leads to global warming comparable to the effect of an increase of 34.5 Gt of CO2 in the atmosphere relative to pre-industrialization, and results in a loss of 24.4–48.8 trillion USD for climate regulation service (CRS). From 2020 to 2100, in the representative concentration pathway (RCP) 8.5, the Arctic SIRF is projected to increase by 0.31 (Formula presented.). Combined with the discount rate, the estimated average annual cost over the period ranges from 6.7–13.3 trillion USD.
Drivers of Risk: Arctic sea ice loss, decreased albedo, increased solar absorption, Arctic amplification, sea ice albedo radiative forcing, climate change, CO2 equivalent emissions, economic cost, global warming, Representative Concentration Pathway (RCP) 8.5.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: The rapid loss of Arctic Sea ice cover and thickness diminishes the surface albedo, which increases the ocean’s absorption of solar heat and exacerbates the Arctic amplification effect...the extent of summer sea ice is anticipated to decrease below 1 million km2 by the 2050s as a result of the extreme climate...past and future changes in sea ice albedo radiative forcing and the resulting economic cost remain to be explored...the evolution of Arctic sea ice radiative forcing (SIRF) from 1982 to 2100...SIRF is converted to CO2 equivalent emissions...the Arctic SIRF rising leads to global warming comparable to the effect of an increase of 34.5 Gt of CO2 in the atmosphere relative to pre-industrialization, and results in a loss of 24.4–48.8 trillion USD for climate regulation service (CRS)...From 2020 to 2100, in the representative concentration pathway (RCP) 8.5, the Arctic SIRF is projected to increase...the estimated average annual cost over the period ranges from 6.7–13.3 trillion USD.
Category-sourcematerial: The rapid loss of Arctic Sea ice cover and thickness diminishes the surface albedo, which increases the ocean’s absorption of solar heat and exacerbates the Arctic amplification effect.
Approach-sourcematerial: In this study, we first analyze the evolution of Arctic sea ice radiative forcing (SIRF) from 1982 to 2100 using a radiative kernel method based on albedo data from the Polar Pathfinder-Extent (APP-x) and Coupled Model Intercomparison Project 5 (CMIP5). Then, the SIRF is converted to CO2 equivalent emissions via the Dynamic Integrated Model of Climate and Economy (DICE) model. Finally, the associated costs are calculated using the substitute cost method, based on the social cost of carbon to achieve the Paris Agreement targets.
Methodology: Arctic sea ice radiative forcing (SIRF) from 1982-2100 is analyzed using a radiative kernel method based on albedo data from APP-x and CMIP5; SIRF is converted to CO2 equivalent emissions using the DICE model; associated costs are calculated using the substitute cost method, based on the social cost of carbon to achieve the Paris Agreement targets.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "we first analyze the evolution of Arctic sea ice radiative forcing (SIRF) from 1982 to 2100 using a radiative kernel method based on albedo data from the Polar Pathfinder-Extent (APP-x) and Coupled Model Intercomparison Project 5 (CMIP5). Then, the SIRF is converted to CO2 equivalent emissions via the Dynamic Integrated Model of Climate and Economy (DICE) model. Finally, the associated costs are calculated using the substitute cost method, based on the social cost of carbon to achieve the Paris Agreement targets."
Highlights: Arctic sea ice radiative forcing (SIRF) averaged -0.75 ± 0.1 from 1982-2020, increasing by 0.12; April-June SIRF accounts for 77% of the annual value, peaking at -3.2 in May; rising SIRF equates to a 34.5 Gt CO2 increase and a loss of 24.4-48.8 trillion USD for climate regulation; under RCP 8.5 from 2020-2100, SIRF is projected to increase by 0.31, with an estimated average annual cost of 6.7-13.3 trillion USD.
Highlights-sourcematerial: The results show that the average Arctic SIRF was −0.75 ± 0.1 between 1982 and 2020, and increased by 0.12 during this period. The SIRF in April–June accounts for nearly 77% of the average annual value, with a maximum absolute value of –3.2 in May. Through model transformation, it is shown that the Arctic SIRF rising leads to global warming comparable to the effect of an increase of 34.5 Gt of CO2 in the atmosphere relative to pre-industrialization, and results in a loss of 24.4–48.8 trillion USD for climate regulation service (CRS). From 2020 to 2100, in the representative concentration pathway (RCP) 8.5, the Arctic SIRF is projected to increase by 0.31. Combined with the discount rate, the estimated average annual cost over the period ranges from 6.7–13.3 trillion USD.
