Paper Title: How will United States commercial building energy use be impacted by IPCC climate scenarios?
Outcome(s) of Interest: Energy use impact, commercial buildings, 2030-2100, RCP scenarios, electricity and natural gas usage, US climate zones, temperature rise, net energy saver.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "The aim of this analysis is to understand how future climate scenarios will impact electricity and natural gas use of commercial buildings in the United States...This study analyzes this impact for 2030, 2045, and 2100 using Representative Concentration Pathways (RCP) scenarios...High performance computing resources use these FMYs to simulate commercial prototype buildings in every American Society of Heating, Refrigeration, and Air Conditioning Engineers (ASHRAE) climate zone of the United States (US)...This analysis quantifies the impact of climate change on source electrical and natural gas usage for commercial buildings in the United States over the next 80 years...While climate change may ultimately require adaptations of the built environment to withstand its effects and because the United States is a country that requires more heating than cooling, from a building energy perspective, climate change (average temperature rise) is a net energy saver for the United States."
Drivers of Risk: Climate change; Anthropogenically-forced weather shifts; RCP scenarios (2.6, 4.5, 6.0, 8.5); Increased electricity use in warmer climates; Decreased natural gas use in colder climates; Urban growth.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Climate change and anthropogenically-forced shift of weather in the future will impact energy use...This study analyzes this impact for 2030, 2045, and 2100 using Representative Concentration Pathways (RCP) scenarios defined in Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) Assessment Report 5. The large, gridded simulation of meteorological variables for RCPs 2.6, 4.5, 6.0, and 8.5 are selected...High performance computing resources use these FMYs to simulate commercial prototype buildings in every American Society of Heating, Refrigeration, and Air Conditioning Engineers (ASHRAE) climate zone of the United States (US)...When adjusted for anticipated urban growth by 2100, the predicted range is 65% increase (8.5) and 71% increase (2.6). Under a global temperature rise climate scenario, the warmest US climate zones will see a large increases in electricity use derived from space cooling while the coldest US climate zones will see significant decreases in natural gas use caused by the decrease in heating necessary.
Category-sourcematerial: The aim of this analysis is to understand how future climate scenarios will impact electricity and natural gas use of commercial buildings in the United States. This study analyzes this impact for 2030, 2045, and 2100 using Representative Concentration Pathways (RCP) scenarios defined in Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) Assessment Report 5. ... This analysis quantifies the impact of climate change on source electrical and natural gas usage for commercial buildings in the United States over the next 80 years. ... If US commercial floorspace remained constant, total energy use by 2100 is predicted between an 1.75% decrease under the greatest emission scenario (8.5) and a 1.76% increase under the lowest emission scenario (2.6). When adjusted for anticipated urban growth by 2100, the predicted range is 65% increase (8.5) and 71% increase (2.6). Under a global temperature rise climate scenario, the warmest US climate zones will see a large increases in electricity use derived from space cooling while the coldest US climate zones will see significant decreases in natural gas use caused by the decrease in heating necessary. ...from a building energy perspective, climate change (average temperature rise) is a net energy saver for the United States.
Approach-sourcematerial: This study analyzes this impact for 2030, 2045, and 2100 using Representative Concentration Pathways (RCP) scenarios defined in Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) Assessment Report 5. The large, gridded simulation of meteorological variables for RCPs 2.6, 4.5, 6.0, and 8.5 are selected and downscaled to make available hourly Future Meteorological Year (FMY) weather files for use and improvement in subsequent studies. High performance computing resources use these FMYs to simulate commercial prototype buildings in every American Society of Heating, Refrigeration, and Air Conditioning Engineers (ASHRAE) climate zone of the United States (US), and results are scaled to nation-wide energy use using conditioned floor area multipliers.
Methodology: Gridded meteorological variables for RCPs 2.6, 4.5, 6.0, and 8.5 are downscaled to create hourly Future Meteorological Year (FMY) weather files; these are used in high performance computing to simulate commercial prototype buildings in all ASHRAE climate zones of the US, and the results are scaled to nation-wide energy use using conditioned floor area multipliers, without speculating on future building or grid characteristics, to quantify the impact of climate change on source electrical and natural gas usage.
Methodology-sourcematerial: The large, gridded simulation of meteorological variables for RCPs 2.6, 4.5, 6.0, and 8.5 are selected and downscaled to make available hourly Future Meteorological Year (FMY) weather files for use and improvement in subsequent studies. High performance computing resources use these FMYs to simulate commercial prototype buildings in every American Society of Heating, Refrigeration, and Air Conditioning Engineers (ASHRAE) climate zone of the United States (US), and results are scaled to nation-wide energy use using conditioned floor area multipliers. The analysis is conducted without speculating the physical and performance traits of future buildings or the grid characteristics. This analysis quantifies the impact of climate change on source electrical and natural gas usage for commercial buildings in the United States over the next 80 years.
Highlights: Climate change impacts on U.S. commercial building energy use are analyzed for 2030, 2045, and 2100 under various RCP scenarios, showing potential energy use changes ranging from a slight decrease to an increase depending on the emission scenario and urban growth; warmer zones will likely see increased electricity use for cooling, while colder zones will see decreased natural gas use for heating, suggesting a net energy savings for the U.S. due to climate change.
Highlights-sourcematerial: The aim of this analysis is to understand how future climate scenarios will impact electricity and natural gas use of commercial buildings in the United States...This study analyzes this impact for 2030, 2045, and 2100 using Representative Concentration Pathways (RCP) scenarios...total energy use by 2100 is predicted between an 1.75% decrease under the greatest emission scenario (8.5) and a 1.76% increase under the lowest emission scenario (2.6)...When adjusted for anticipated urban growth by 2100, the predicted range is 65% increase (8.5) and 71% increase (2.6)...the warmest US climate zones will see a large increases in electricity use derived from space cooling while the coldest US climate zones will see significant decreases in natural gas use caused by the decrease in heating necessary...from a building energy perspective, climate change (average temperature rise) is a net energy saver for the United States.
More Notes: The study projects that by 2100, without considering building or grid traits changes, US commercial buildings' energy use could decrease by 1.75% under high emissions (RCP 8.5) and increase by 1.76% under low emissions (RCP 2.6) assuming static floorspace; however, accounting for urban growth, energy use might rise by 65% under RCP 8.5 and 71% under RCP 2.6, with increased electricity for cooling in warmer zones and reduced natural gas for heating in colder zones, ultimately suggesting a net energy saving due to climate-induced temperature rise.
More Notes-sourcematerial: Climate change and anthropogenically-forced shift of weather in the future will impact energy use and resilience of both the built environment and the electric grid. If US commercial floorspace remained constant, total energy use by 2100 is predicted between an 1.75% decrease under the greatest emission scenario (8.5) and a 1.76% increase under the lowest emission scenario (2.6). When adjusted for anticipated urban growth by 2100, the predicted range is 65% increase (8.5) and 71% increase (2.6). Under a global temperature rise climate scenario, the warmest US climate zones will see a large increases in electricity use derived from space cooling while the coldest US climate zones will see significant decreases in natural gas use caused by the decrease in heating necessary. While climate change may ultimately require adaptations of the built environment to withstand its effects and because the United States is a country that requires more heating than cooling, from a building energy perspective, climate change (average temperature rise) is a net energy saver for the United States.

Paper Title: Important wheat diseases in the US and their management in the 21st century
Outcome(s) of Interest: Wheat yield losses (20%), economic damage, plant mortality.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Each year, about 20% of wheat is lost due to diseases. These diseases badly impact the yield and cause mortality of the plants.
Drivers of Risk: Population growth, yield demand, climate adaptation, arable land competition, disease impact, abiotic stress, pathogen distribution.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Due to the rapid increase in population, wheat production needs to be increased by 50% by 2050 and this growth will be mainly based on yield increases, as there is strong competition for scarce productive arable land from other sectors. This increasing demand can be further achieved using sustainable approaches including integrated disease pest management, adaption to warmer climates, less use of water resources and increased frequency of abiotic stress tolerances. Out of 200 diseases of wheat, 50 cause economic losses and are widely distributed. Each year, about 20% of wheat is lost due to diseases.
Category-sourcematerial: "Wheat is a crop of historical significance... wheat production needs to be increased by 50% by 2050... This review focuses on important diseases of the wheat present in the United States... This study will be of utmost importance to wheat breeding programs throughout the world to breed for resistance under changing environmental conditions."
Approach-sourcematerial: The abstract is a review article summarizing information about wheat diseases, their impact, and management strategies. It doesn't describe a specific modeling or analysis approach.
Methodology: The review compiles comprehensive information on wheat diseases in the United States, including causal organisms, economic damage, symptoms, host range, favorable conditions, and disease management strategies, along with genetic and breeding efforts for disease control, QTLs, and identified genes.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "This review focuses on important diseases of the wheat present in the United States, with comprehensive information of causal organism, economic damage, symptoms and host range, favorable conditions, and disease management strategies. Furthermore, major genetic and breeding efforts to control and manage these diseases are discussed. A detailed description of all the QTLs, genes reported and cloned for these diseases are provided in this review."
Highlights: Wheat production needs to increase significantly by 2050, primarily through yield improvements and sustainable practices like integrated disease management and climate adaptation, as diseases currently cause 20% annual wheat losses in the United States; review provides detailed information on major U.S. wheat diseases, including causal organisms, economic impact, symptoms, favorable conditions, management strategies, and genetic/breeding efforts, aiming to aid breeding programs in developing disease resistance under changing environmental conditions.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "Due to the rapid increase in population, wheat production needs to be increased by 50% by 2050 and this growth will be mainly based on yield increases...Each year, about 20% of wheat is lost due to diseases...This review focuses on important diseases of the wheat present in the United States, with comprehensive information of causal organism, economic damage, symptoms and host range, favorable conditions, and disease management strategies. Furthermore, major genetic and breeding efforts to control and manage these diseases are discussed...This study will be of utmost importance to wheat breeding programs throughout the world to breed for resistance under changing environmental conditions."
More Notes: Wheat production needs to increase significantly by 2050, and diseases cause substantial yield losses annually, necessitating improved disease management and breeding for resistance, particularly in the context of changing environmental conditions in the United States.
More Notes-sourcematerial: "Due to the rapid increase in population, wheat production needs to be increased by 50% by 2050...Each year, about 20% of wheat is lost due to diseases...This review focuses on important diseases of the wheat present in the United States...This study will be of utmost importance to wheat breeding programs throughout the world to breed for resistance under changing environmental conditions."

Paper Title: Large scale seasonal forecasting of peak season algae metrics in the Midwest and Northeast U.S.
Outcome(s) of Interest: Peak season algae metrics (chlorophyll-a) magnitude, severity, and duration; Forecast skill.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "Forecasts are assessed for the magnitude, severity, and duration of seasonal chlorophyll concentrations."
Drivers of Risk: Pre-season local and global variables; sea surface temperature; pre-season chlorophyll-a.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Peak season algae conditions are influenced by an array of pre-season (spring and early summer) local and global scale variables; Regions of pre-season sea surface temperature, and pre-season chlorophyll-a demonstrate the most predictive power for peak season algae metrics...
Category-sourcematerial: In recent decades, many inland lakes have seen an increase in the prevalence of potentially harmful algae. ... Using the LAGOS-NE dataset, pre-season local and global drivers of peak-season algae metrics (represented by chlorophyll-a) are identified for 178 lakes across the Northeast and Midwest U.S. ... Forecasts are assessed for the magnitude, severity, and duration of seasonal chlorophyll concentrations.
Approach-sourcematerial: Using the LAGOS-NE dataset, pre-season local and global drivers of peak-season algae metrics (represented by chlorophyll-a) are identified for 178 lakes across the Northeast and Midwest U.S. from readily available gridded datasets. Forecasting models are built for each lake conditioned on relevant pre-season predictors. Forecasts are assessed for the magnitude, severity, and duration of seasonal chlorophyll concentrations.
Methodology: Pre-season local and global variables are identified using the LAGOS-NE dataset to build forecasting models for peak-season algae metrics (chlorophyll-a) in 178 lakes across the Northeast and Midwest U.S., focusing on magnitude, severity, and duration of seasonal chlorophyll concentrations and assessing model performance against climatology.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Using the LAGOS-NE dataset, pre-season local and global drivers of peak-season algae metrics (represented by chlorophyll-a) are identified for 178 lakes across the Northeast and Midwest U.S. from readily available gridded datasets. Forecasting models are built for each lake conditioned on relevant pre-season predictors. Forecasts are assessed for the magnitude, severity, and duration of seasonal chlorophyll concentrations. Regions of pre-season sea surface temperature, and pre-season chlorophyll-a demonstrate the most predictive power for peak season algae metrics, and resulting models show significant skill. Based on categorical forecast metrics, more than 70% of magnitude models and 90% of duration models outperform climatology.
Highlights: Pre-season sea surface temperature and chlorophyll-a are predictive of peak-season algae (chlorophyll-a) in Northeast and Midwest U.S. lakes; forecasts outperform climatology, especially for algae duration, and are most accurate for large, mesotrophic/oligotrophic lakes regarding high algae magnitude, potentially aiding lake managers in preparing for algae blooms.
Highlights-sourcematerial: In recent decades, many inland lakes have seen an increase in the prevalence of potentially harmful algae... Currently, little information regarding expected algae conditions is available prior to the peak season... Peak season algae conditions are influenced by... pre-season local and global scale variables... Using the LAGOS-NE dataset, pre-season local and global drivers of peak-season algae metrics... are identified for 178 lakes across the Northeast and Midwest U.S... Forecasts are assessed for the magnitude, severity, and duration of seasonal chlorophyll concentrations. Regions of pre-season sea surface temperature, and pre-season chlorophyll-a demonstrate the most predictive power... Based on categorical forecast metrics, more than 70% of magnitude models and 90% of duration models outperform climatology. Forecasts of high and severe algae magnitude perform best in large mesotrophic and oligotrophic lakes... The advance notice of elevated algae biomass provided by these models may allow lake managers to better prepare for challenges posed by algae during the high use season for inland lakes.
More Notes: Forecasts of algae blooms based on pre-season variables outperform climatology, especially for magnitude in larger lakes, potentially aiding lake managers.
More Notes-sourcematerial: "Based on categorical forecast metrics, more than 70% of magnitude models and 90% of duration models outperform climatology. Forecasts of high and severe algae magnitude perform best in large mesotrophic and oligotrophic lakes...The advance notice of elevated algae biomass provided by these models may allow lake managers to better prepare for challenges posed by algae during the high use season for inland lakes."
Hazard definition-sourcematerial: The paper focuses on forecasting peak season algae conditions in inland lakes, which are influenced by pre-season variables. It aims to provide advance notice of elevated algae biomass to help lake managers prepare for challenges during the high use season.
Impact persistence-sourcematerial: The advance notice of elevated algae biomass provided by these models may allow lake managers to better prepare for challenges posed by algae during the high use season for inland lakes.
Space disaggregation-sourcematerial: Using the LAGOS-NE dataset, pre-season local and global drivers of peak-season algae metrics (represented by chlorophyll-a) are identified for 178 lakes across the Northeast and Midwest U.S. from readily available gridded datasets. Forecasting models are built for each lake conditioned on relevant pre-season predictors.
Impact interactions-sourcematerial: The abstract focuses on identifying pre-season drivers of peak-season algae metrics, and building forecasting models based on these drivers. It does not mention any analysis of interactions between different climate impacts.
Economic dynamics-sourcematerial: There is no mention of economic activities, models, or considerations within the abstract.
Climate features-sourcematerial: N/A
Adaptation considered: The abstract suggests that forecasting algae blooms can help lake managers *prepare* for algae-related challenges, implying an adaptation strategy. The models provide advance notice of elevated algae, which would enable better management during the high-use season. The extent of adaptation is limited to preparation enabled by the forecast, though no details of how this preparation would occur is provided.
Adaptation considered-sourcematerial: The advance notice of elevated algae biomass provided by these models may allow lake managers to better prepare for challenges posed by algae during the high use season for inland lakes.

Drivers of Risk: Extreme events, climate change projections (higher magnitudes), life-cycle risk, infrastructure design/construction/maintenance.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "Many countries struggle to ensure the functioning of their infrastructure during or after extreme events. Furthermore, climate change (CC) projections suggest that future events might be of higher magnitudes. When considering the life-cycle of infrastructure, one of the most important aspects to consider is risk. Risk is first identified and then addressed during CI design, construction, and subsequent maintenance."
Category-sourcematerial: This study examines the currently available literature on infrastructure assessment tools and the impact of climate change on its maintenance costs. Critical infrastructure (CI) performance and protection is a global priority. Many countries struggle to ensure the functioning of their infrastructure during or after extreme events. Furthermore, climate change (CC) projections suggest that future events might be of higher magnitudes.

Methodology: The study reviews literature on infrastructure assessment tools and the impact of climate change on maintenance costs, focusing on risk identification and mitigation through cost-benefit analysis (CBA) to inform short- and long-term decision-making for climate change preparedness.

The objective of CBA is to find, from an economic standpoint, the best strategy to mitigate climate change by reducing the costs of preparedness and mitigation actions for CC. In conclusion, the framework aims for better decision making in both the short-term and the long-term."

Paper Title: Integrated fuzzy analytical bridge risk assessment: Multi-objective and multi-hazard framework
Outcome(s) of Interest: Reduced bridge infrastructure failure risk due to climate change.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: The paper discusses a framework that involves a multi-objective and multi-hazard risk assessment method, aimed at evaluating the impact of climate change on bridge structures. It emphasizes the significance of empowering practitioners and decision-makers to make informed decisions regarding the risk level to bridges caused by anticipated climatic factors.
Drivers of Risk: Severe weather events, aging infrastructure, inadequacy of historical data-based building codes/standards.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "severe weather events exacerbating the already- existing stress on aging infrastructure... primarily developed based on historical data, the risk of infrastructure failure due to climate change is growing..."
Category-sourcematerial: "This paper discusses the need for a multi-objective and multi- hazard risk assessment method to evaluate the impact of climate change on bridge struc- tures."
Approach-sourcematerial: "The framework proposed includes integrating the fuzzy analytical hierarchy process to create a risk assessment methodology that evaluates bridges structurally and socio- economically."
Methodology: Proposes a multi-objective and multi-hazard risk assessment method integrating the fuzzy analytical hierarchy process to evaluate bridge infrastructure structurally and socio-economically, aiming to empower practitioners and decision-makers in making informed decisions about climate-related risks.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "This paper discusses the need for a multi-objective and multi- hazard risk assessment method to evaluate the impact of climate change on bridge struc- tures. The framework proposed includes integrating the fuzzy analytical hierarchy process to create a risk assessment methodology that evaluates bridges structurally and socio- economically. The objective is to create a system that empowers practitioners and decision- makers to make well-informed decisions regarding the risk level to bridges caused by anticipated climatic factors."
Highlights: Climate change increasingly threatens infrastructure, particularly bridges, due to severe weather and aging infrastructure. Current building codes are insufficient, necessitating a multi-hazard and multi-objective risk assessment framework, incorporating fuzzy analytical hierarchy process, to aid decision-making and evaluate bridge risk structurally and socio-economically.

Paper Title: Decreased Aircraft Takeoff Performance under Global Warming
Outcome(s) of Interest: Reduced maximum takeoff weight (MTOW), increased takeoff distance, more weight-restriction days, potential impact on airlines' economic benefit and flight operations.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: The warming air leads to the MTOW reducing and takeoff distance increasing. Taking the Boeing 737–800 aircraft as an illustration, the number of weight-restriction days increases significantly across the airports, which can influence airlines’ economic benefit and flight operations in the future. It is also found that the takeoff distance does not change linearly with temperature, but shows a stronger increase with higher temperature.
Drivers of Risk: Global warming; increased frequency of extreme high temperatures.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: With global warming, extreme high temperatures become more frequent and are an important factor affecting aircrafts’ takeoff performance.
Category-sourcematerial: With global warming, extreme high temperatures become more frequent and are an important factor affecting aircrafts’ takeoff performance...the influence of increasing temperature on aircrafts’ maximum takeoff weight (MTOW) and takeoff distance is evaluated quantitatively in this study...the warming air leads to the MTOW reducing and takeoff distance increasing...the number of weight-restriction days increases significantly across the airports, which can influence airlines’ economic benefit and flight operations in the future.
Approach-sourcematerial: Using Community Earth System Model version 1 (CESM1) and Boeing Performance Software (BPS), the influence of increasing temperature on aircrafts’ maximum takeoff weight (MTOW) and takeoff distance is evaluated quantitatively in this study.
Methodology: The study uses Community Earth System Model version 1 (CESM1) and Boeing Performance Software (BPS) to quantitatively evaluate the influence of increasing temperature on aircrafts’ maximum takeoff weight (MTOW) and takeoff distance.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Using Community Earth System Model version 1 (CESM1) and Boeing Performance Software (BPS), the influence of increasing temperature on aircrafts’ maximum takeoff weight (MTOW) and takeoff distance is evaluated quantitatively in this study.
Highlights: Rising temperatures significantly impact aircraft takeoff performance, reducing maximum takeoff weight, increasing takeoff distance, and increasing weight restriction days, potentially affecting airline economics and operations. Takeoff distance increases non-linearly with temperature, requiring significantly longer runways at low-altitude airports in the future.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "extreme high temperatures become more frequent and are an important factor affecting aircrafts’ takeoff performance...the influence of increasing temperature on aircrafts’ maximum takeoff weight (MTOW) and takeoff distance is evaluated quantitatively...the warming air leads to the MTOW reducing and takeoff distance increasing...the number of weight-restriction days increases significantly across the airports, which can influence airlines’ economic benefit and flight operations in the future...the takeoff distance does not change linearly with temperature, but shows a stronger increase with higher temperature. The takeoff distance increases about 6.2% on average during 2071−2080 compared to 1991−2000 for the low-altitude airports, calling for an additional 113−222 m takeoff distance in future summers."
More Notes: Higher temperatures due to climate change will reduce aircraft takeoff performance, increasing weight restrictions and takeoff distances, particularly impacting low-altitude airports.
More Notes-sourcematerial: With global warming, extreme high temperatures become more frequent and are an important factor affecting aircrafts’ takeoff performance...the warming air leads to the MTOW reducing and takeoff distance increasing...the number of weight-restriction days increases significantly across the airports...the takeoff distance increases about 6.2% on average during 2071−2080 compared to 1991−2000 for the low-altitude airports.
Hazard definition-sourcematerial: The results show that the distribution of summer daily maximum temperature shifts obviously to higher temperature at all the chosen airports...The warming air leads to the MTOW reducing and takeoff distance increasing...The takeoff distance increases about 6.2% on average during 2071−2080 compared to 1991−2000 for the low-altitude airports, calling for an additional 113−222 m takeoff distance in future summers.
Impact persistence-sourcematerial: No discussion of persistence, adaptation, or permanent alterations.
Space disaggregation-sourcematerial: "at all the chosen airports and the variation in temperature is larger at four airports (Jinan, Shanghai, Lhasa, and Urumqi) during 2071−2080 under the RCP8.5 scenario than that in the historical run (1991−2000)."
Impact interactions-sourcematerial: No information to suggest any interactions were considered.

Climate features-sourcematerial: The study finds that the takeoff distance does not change linearly with temperature, but shows a stronger increase with higher temperature. The takeoff distance increases about 6.2% on average during 2071−2080 compared to 1991−2000 for the low-altitude airports, calling for an additional 113−222 m takeoff distance in future summers.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation considerations are implied in the context of addressing economic impacts and flight operations due to increased weight-restriction days and extended takeoff distances at warmer temperatures. No specific adaptation strategies are explicitly discussed, but the need to accommodate increased takeoff distances and possible operational adjustments is suggested as temperatures rise.

Paper Title: Using nontraditional water sources and implementing water conservation practices under changing climate
Outcome(s) of Interest: Decreased productive farms, irrigated areas, and crop yield due to freshwater shortage; need for alternative water sources (recycled water); demand for recycled water; adoption of water management strategies; water management strategy differences based on watershed characteristics.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Agriculture is the largest water-consuming sector on global level, and this consumption is about two-thirds of the entire freshwater consumption. With the change in climate, the requirement for irrigation water is increasing, though the availability of freshwater is decreasing accordingly. Shortage of freshwater sources leads to a decrease in the number of productive farms, irrigated areas, and crop yield. [...] To reduce the pressure on the freshwater sources and keep up the productivity of the farms, it is necessary to consider alternative sources of water. For this reason, the idea of using recycled water came forward. [...] Results indicate that differences in these watersheds dictate specific water management strategies based on the soils, land use, and demographics present in each watershed.
Drivers of Risk: Climate change, increasing irrigation water demand, decreasing freshwater availability, groundwater depletion, on-farm surface water limitations, off-farm water source constraints.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Agriculture is the largest water-consuming sector on global level, and this consumption is about two-thirds of the entire freshwater consumption. With the change in climate, the requirement for irrigation water is increasing, though the availability of freshwater is decreasing accordingly. Shortage of freshwater sources leads to a decrease in the number of productive farms, irrigated areas, and crop yield. In the US, the primary sources of irrigation are 1) groundwater from on-farm wells (often freshwater from the confined aquifers), 2) on-farm surface water, and 3) off-farm water from a variety of sources.
Category-sourcematerial: Agriculture is the largest water-consuming sector on global level, and this consumption is about two-thirds of the entire freshwater consumption. With the change in climate, the requirement for irrigation water is increasing, though the availability of freshwater is decreasing accordingly. Shortage of freshwater sources leads to a decrease in the number of productive farms, irrigated areas, and crop yield...To reduce the pressure on the freshwater sources and keep up the productivity of the farms, it is necessary to consider alternative sources of water.
Approach-sourcematerial: This presentation will cover the results of a hydrologic model (e.g., Soil Water Assessment Tool - SWAT) and the agent-based model to identify the need and challenges of using recycled water in Maryland... In this framework, the SWAT model is used to determine and forecast crop water requirements based on long-term climate variability, and the agent-based modeling system is used to investigate how the adoption of water management strategies emerges because of socio-economic factors and human-environment interactions, and how management policies influence this process.
Methodology: The study uses the Soil Water Assessment Tool (SWAT) to model and forecast crop water requirements based on climate variability, and agent-based modeling to investigate the adoption of water management strategies considering socio-economic factors and human-environment interactions.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "This presentation will cover the results of a hydrologic model (e.g., Soil Water Assessment Tool - SWAT) and the agent-based model to identify the need and challenges of using recycled water in Maryland, analyze the demand for recycled water use in irrigation, and develop a Diagnostic Decision Support System (DDSS) that helps provide alternative water management recommendations to the stakeholders. In this framework, the SWAT model is used to determine and forecast crop water requirements based on long-term climate variability, and the agent-based modeling system is used to investigate how the adoption of water management strategies emerges because of socio-economic factors and human-environment interactions, and how management policies influence this process."
Highlights: Agriculture faces increasing irrigation demands due to climate change and decreasing freshwater availability; recycled water, modern irrigation, BMPs, and drought-resistant crops are potential solutions; a hydrologic model (SWAT) and agent-based model are used to identify the need, challenges, and demand for recycled water in Maryland's Zekiah and Greensboro watersheds, considering socio-economic factors and human-environment interactions to develop water management recommendations.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Agriculture is the largest water-consuming sector... With the change in climate, the requirement for irrigation water is increasing... shortage of freshwater sources... alternative sources of water... recycled water... modern irrigation technologies... water-conserving Best Management Practices (BMPs)... drought-resistant crops... hydrologic model (e.g., Soil Water Assessment Tool - SWAT) and the agent-based model... need and challenges of using recycled water in Maryland... demand for recycled water... alternative water management recommendations... socio-economic factors and human-environment interactions... Zekiah Watershed and the Greensboro Watershed.
More Notes: Recycled water use for irrigation, along with demand management strategies and drought-resistant crops, can reduce pressure on freshwater resources, but faces challenges like water quality, social acceptance, and stakeholder conflicts; hydrologic and agent-based modeling in Maryland watersheds are being used to identify needs, analyze demand, and develop decision support systems for alternative water management.
More Notes-sourcematerial: "To reduce the pressure on the freshwater sources and keep up the productivity of the farms, it is necessary to consider alternative sources of water. For this reason, the idea of using recycled water came forward... Despite a lot of benefits, wastewater use has some challenges e.g., recycled water quality, social acceptance, and conflicts between different stakeholders... This presentation will cover the results of a hydrologic model (e.g., Soil Water Assessment Tool - SWAT) and the agent-based model to identify the need and challenges of using recycled water in Maryland, analyze the demand for recycled water use in irrigation, and develop a Diagnostic Decision Support System (DDSS) that helps provide alternative water management recommendations to the stakeholders."
Hazard definition-sourcematerial: With the change in climate, the requirement for irrigation water is increasing, though the availability of freshwater is decreasing accordingly. Shortage of freshwater sources leads to a decrease in the number of productive farms, irrigated areas, and crop yield.
Impact persistence-sourcematerial: The abstract mentions using a hydrologic model to forecast crop water requirements based on long-term climate variability and developing water management strategies based on socio-economic factors, indicating that some consideration of changing impacts over time (adaptive) is involved. However, the core topic is about the current use of alternative water sources and management strategies rather than persistent impacts themselves.
Space disaggregation-sourcematerial: For the initial analysis, two different watersheds have been chosen in Maryland. These two watersheds are the Zekiah Watershed and the Greensboro Watershed. Although both watersheds are within the Coastal Plain Physiographic region and drain to Chesapeake Bay, they are different in demography, land use, and water demand. Results indicate that differences in these watersheds dictate specific water management strategies based on the soils, land use, and demographics present in each watershed.

Paper Title: CLIMATE CHANGE IN A SEMI-ARID ENVIRONMENT: EFFECTS ON CROP ROTATION WITH DAIRY MANURE APPLICATIONS
Outcome(s) of Interest: Crop yield changes (wheat & barley increase, potato & sugarbeet decrease); Nitrogen mineralization, N seepage, and nitrous oxide emissions minimally impacted by climate change.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "Spring wheat yield increased by 22% and 16% for manure and fertilizer treatments, respectively, compared to the baseline scenario...potato tuber yield decreased by 65% and 60% in the manure and fertilizer treatments, respectively...Spring barley produced a 33% higher yield with increased temperature and CO2...Sugarbeet yields decreased by 16% and 18% for manure and fertilizer treatments, respectively...Nitrogen mineralization, N seepage from the profile, and nitrous oxide emissions were strongly influenced by the manure applications, and there was little simulated impact of climate change on these processes."
Drivers of Risk: Climate change, increased temperature, elevated CO2, water availability, irrigation requirements.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "The impacts of climate change on cropping systems and the availability of water for irrigation would be a serious challenge... The RZWQM2 simulations used bias-corrected and spatially disaggregated projections from the World Climate Research Program's coupled model inter-comparison project phase 5 to generate 40 GCM projections for the time from 2071-2099... The 28-yr scenarios were designed to simulate the impact of temperature and CO2 regimes on crop production..."
Category-sourcematerial: Agricultural crops grown in the irrigated semi-arid region of southern Idaho account for almost two-thirds of the median household income in the region. The impacts of climate change on cropping systems and the availability of water for irrigation would be a serious challenge for the state's economic dependence on agriculture. The objective of the study was to simulate the future impact of climate change on a crop rotation of spring wheat-potato-spring barley-sugarbeet grown in the semi-arid region of southern Idaho using conventional management practices and a high dairy manure application. Results were compared to a baseline scenario of conventional management practices, historical weather data, and ambient CO2. Spring wheat yield increased by 22% and 16% for manure and fertilizer treatments, respectively, compared to the baseline scenario. Using the same comparison, potato tuber yield decreased by 65% and 60% in the manure and fertilizer treatments, respectively, for the highest temperature and CO2 increase scenarios. Spring barley produced a 33% higher yield with increased temperature and CO2. However, yield decreased when temperature increased, but CO2 remained unchanged. Sugarbeet yields decreased by 16% and 18% for manure and fertilizer treatments, respectively, compared to the baseline scenario. These simulation results indicate that genetic enhancements or alternative management will be needed to maintain potato and sugar beet production levels in semi-arid areas, while spring barley and wheat yields may increase, assuming adequate irrigation water supplies are available.
Approach-sourcematerial: The Root Zone Water Quality Model (RZWQM2) simulations used bias-corrected and spatially disaggregated projections from the World Climate Research Program's coupled model inter-comparison project phase 5 to generate 40 GCM projections for the time from 2071-2099.
Methodology: RZWQM2 simulations using bias-corrected and spatially disaggregated projections from 40 GCMs (2071-2099) under high temperature and CO2 regimes were used to assess climate change impacts on a spring wheat-potato-spring barley-sugarbeet rotation in southern Idaho, comparing results to a baseline scenario of historical weather and ambient CO2, and validating the model with field experiment data from conventional fertilizer and heavy dairy manure applications.
Methodology-sourcematerial: The Root Zone Water Quality Model (RZWQM2) simulations used bias-corrected and spatially disaggregated projections from the World Climate Research Program's coupled model inter-comparison project phase 5 to generate 40 GCM projections for the time from 2071-2099. The 28-yr scenarios were designed to simulate the impact of temperature and CO2 regimes on crop production...Data from a field experiment in southern Idaho with conventional fertilizer practices and annual applications of 52 Mg ha-1 dairy manure with a crop rotation of spring wheat-potato-spring barley-sugarbeet were used in the RZWQM2 simulations. Results were compared to a baseline scenario of conventional management practices, historical weather data, and ambient CO2.
Highlights: Projected climate change impacts on southern Idaho crop yields (2071-2099) vary: spring wheat and barley yields may increase, while potato and sugar beet yields are likely to decrease, necessitating genetic enhancements or alternative management strategies to maintain production.

Spring barley produced a 33% higher yield with increased temperature and CO2... Sugarbeet yields decreased by 16% and 18% for manure and fertilizer treatments, respectively, compared to the baseline scenario... these simulation results indicate that genetic enhancements or alternative management will be needed to maintain potato and sugar beet production levels in semi-arid areas, while spring barley and wheat yields may increase, assuming adequate irrigation water supplies are available.
More Notes: Simulations project that future climate change in southern Idaho may increase spring wheat and barley yields but significantly decrease potato and sugar beet yields, necessitating genetic improvements or alternative management strategies to maintain current production levels for the latter two crops.

Paper Title: Dynamic geospatial modeling of mycotoxin contamination of corn in Illinois: unveiling critical factors and predictive insights with machine learning
Outcome(s) of Interest: Aflatoxin (AFL) >20 ppb, Fumonisin (FUM) >5 ppm corn contamination in Illinois.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Historical aflatoxin (AFL) and fumonisin (FUM) mycotoxin contamination data of corn, daily weather data, satellite data, dynamic geospatial soil properties, and land usage parameters were modeled to identify factors significantly contributing to the outbreaks of mycotoxin contamination of corn grown in Illinois (IL), AFL >20 ppb, and FUM >5 ppm.
Drivers of Risk: Temperature and precipitation prior to sowing; aflatoxin risk index (ARI) in January, March, July, and November; corn and wheat NDVI; soil characteristics (calcium carbonate, soil moisture, available water-holding capacity, clay percentage).
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: GBM identified temperature and precipitation prior to sowing as significant influential factors contributing to high AFL and FUM contamination. AFL-GBM showed that a higher aflatoxin risk index (ARI) in January, March, July, and November led to higher AFL contamination in the southern regions of IL. Higher values of corn-specific normalized difference vegetation index (NDVI) in July led to lower AFL contamination in Central and Southern IL, while higher wheat-specific NDVI values in February led to higher AFL. FUM-GBM showed that temperature in July and October, precipitation in February, and NDVI values in March are positively correlated with high contamination throughout IL. Furthermore, the dynamic geospatial models showed that soil characteristics were correlated with AFL and FUM contamination. Greater calcium carbonate content in soil was negatively correlated with AFL contamination, which was noticeable in Southern IL. Greater soil moisture and available water-holding capacity throughout Southern IL were positively correlated with high FUM contamination. The higher clay percentage in the northeastern areas of IL negatively correlated with FUM contamination.
Category-sourcematerial: Mycotoxin contamination of corn is a pervasive problem that negatively impacts human and animal health and causes economic losses to the agricultural industry worldwide. [...] Historical aflatoxin (AFL) and fumonisin (FUM) mycotoxin contamination data of corn, daily weather data, satellite data, dynamic geospatial soil properties, and land usage parameters were modeled to identify factors significantly contributing to the outbreaks of mycotoxin contamination of corn grown in Illinois (IL) [...] Our models revealed that soil, NDVI, year-specific weekly average precipitation, and temperature were the most important factors that correlated with mycotoxin contamination. These findings serve as reliable guidelines for future modeling efforts to identify novel data inputs for the prediction of AFL and FUM outbreaks and potential farm-level management practices.
Approach-sourcematerial: Two methods were used: a gradient boosting machine (GBM) and a neural network (NN).
Methodology: Gradient boosting machine (GBM) and neural network (NN) models were used to analyze historical mycotoxin contamination data, weather data, satellite data, soil properties, and land usage parameters; models were dynamic at the state-county level and incorporated GPS coordinates linked to soil properties.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Historical aflatoxin (AFL) and fumonisin (FUM) mycotoxin contamination data of corn, daily weather data, satellite data, dynamic geospatial soil properties, and land usage parameters were modeled to identify factors significantly contributing to the outbreaks of mycotoxin contamination of corn grown in Illinois (IL), AFL >20 ppb, and FUM >5 ppm. Two methods were used: a gradient boosting machine (GBM) and a neural network (NN). Both the GBM and NN models were dynamic at a state-county geospatial level because they used GPS coordinates of the counties linked to soil properties.
Highlights: Significant factors like temperature, precipitation, NDVI, and soil characteristics influence aflatoxin and fumonisin contamination in Illinois corn, with predictive models showing strong performance, useful for mycotoxin management and prediction.
Highlights-sourcematerial: GBM identified temperature and precipitation prior to sowing as significant influential factors contributing to high AFL and FUM contamination...Our models revealed that soil, NDVI, year-specific weekly average precipitation, and temperature were the most important factors that correlated with mycotoxin contamination. NN models showed high class-specific performance for 1-year predictive validation for AFL (73%) and FUM (85%), highlighting their accuracy for annual mycotoxin prediction. These findings serve as reliable guidelines for future modeling efforts to identify novel data inputs for the prediction of AFL and FUM outbreaks and potential farm-level management practices.
More Notes: Models indicate that mycotoxin contamination (aflatoxin and fumonisin) in Illinois corn is influenced by temperature, precipitation, NDVI, and soil characteristics like calcium carbonate content, soil moisture, available water-holding capacity, and clay percentage.
More Notes-sourcematerial: "GBM identified temperature and precipitation prior to sowing as significant influential factors contributing to high AFL and FUM contamination... the dynamic geospatial models showed that soil characteristics were correlated with AFL and FUM contamination. Greater calcium carbonate content in soil was negatively correlated with AFL contamination... Greater soil moisture and available water-holding capacity throughout Southern IL were positively correlated with high FUM contamination. The higher clay percentage in the northeastern areas of IL negatively correlated with FUM contamination... Our models revealed that soil, NDVI, year-specific weekly average precipitation, and temperature were the most important factors that correlated with mycotoxin contamination."

Paper Title: Estuarine Seagrass and Climate Change
Outcome(s) of Interest: Changing phenology and growth patterns, shifting geographic distributions, invasive species outcompeting native seagrasses, declining areal extent.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: As a result, estuarine seagrasses are changing phenology and growth patterns, shifting geographic distributions, and invasive species are outcompeting native seagrasses. Despite their importance to coastal systems, seagrass meadows are threatened worldwide, with their known global areal extent declining by 5,602 km2 since 1880.
Drivers of Risk: Sea-level rise; increasing water temperatures; greater storm frequency; changes in salinity regimes; modified watersheds; increased nutrient and sediment inputs; coastal acidification; coastal development; dredging.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "Losses are attributed to both natural and anthropogenic sources, including direct impacts of climate change via sea-level rise, increasing water temperatures, greater storm frequency, and changes in salinity regimes. In addition, estuarine seagrasses are found in highly utilized coastal areas where climate stressors interact with non-climate impacts such as modified watersheds, increased nutrient and sediment inputs, coastal acidification, and direct impacts from coastal development or dredging."
Category-sourcematerial: Estuarine seagrasses, submerged flowering plants found in tidally influenced coastal systems, deliver numerous essential ecosystem functions. These important foundation species provide habitat and nursery grounds, sequester carbon, and stabilize shallow coastal sediments. Despite their importance to coastal systems, seagrass meadows are threatened worldwide...
Approach-sourcematerial: There has been an increased effort to monitor and protect seagrasses at a global scale and to include seagrasses in management actions that will help mitigate climate and anthropogenic stressors.

Consequently, seagrasses exhibit changing phenology, growth patterns, and geographic distributions, and face competition from invasive species. Paper Title: Gradient boosting machine learning model to predict aflatoxins in Iowa corn
Outcome(s) of Interest: Aflatoxin contamination in corn (20-ppb and 5-ppb thresholds).
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "We evaluated the performance of AFL prediction with gradient boosting machine (GBM) learning and feature engineering in Iowa corn for two AFL risk thresholds for high contamination events: 20-ppb and 5-ppb."
Drivers of Risk: Vegetative index (August), Aflatoxin Risk Indices (ARI) in May (both thresholds) & July (5-ppb), Latitude (20-ppb), Soil-saturated hydraulic conductivity (Ksat).
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Analyses for AFL showed satellite-acquired vegetative index during August significantly improved the prediction of corn contamination at the end of the growing season for both risk thresholds. Prediction of high AFL contamination levels was linked to aflatoxin risk indices (ARI) in May. However, ARI in July was an influential factor for the 5-ppb threshold but not for the 20-ppb threshold. Similarly, latitude was an influential factor for the 20-ppb threshold but not the 5-ppb threshold. Furthermore, soil-saturated hydraulic conductivity (Ksat) influenced both risk thresholds.
Category-sourcematerial: Introduction: Aflatoxin (AFL), a secondary metabolite produced from filamentous fungi, contaminates corn, posing significant health and safety hazards for humans and livestock through toxigenic and carcinogenic effects. Corn is widely used as an essential commodity for food, feed, fuel, and export markets; therefore, AFL mitigation is necessary to ensure food and feed safety within the United States (US) and elsewhere in the world. In this case study, an Iowa-centric model was developed to predict AFL contamination using historical corn contamination, meteorological, satellite, and soil property data in the largest corn-producing state in the US.

Methodology: Aflatoxin contamination prediction in Iowa corn was evaluated using gradient boosting machine (GBM) learning and feature engineering with historical corn contamination, meteorological, satellite, and soil property data; a 90%-10% training-to-testing ratio was used for 2010, 2011, 2012, and 2021, with independent validation in 2020, for AFL risk thresholds of 20-ppb and 5-ppb.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Methods: We evaluated the performance of AFL prediction with gradient boosting machine (GBM) learning and feature engineering in Iowa corn for two AFL risk thresholds for high contamination events: 20-ppb and 5-ppb. A 90%–10% training-to-testing ratio was utilized in 2010, 2011, 2012, and 2021 (n = 630), with independent validation using the year 2020 (n = 376).
Highlights: Aflatoxin (AFL) contamination in corn, a significant threat to food and feed safety, can be predicted using a gradient boosting machine (GBM) model incorporating historical contamination data, meteorological factors, satellite-acquired vegetative index, and soil properties; satellite data and aflatoxin risk indices (ARI) were significant predictors of corn contamination.

Climate features-sourcematerial: Introduction: Aflatoxin (AFL), a secondary metabolite produced from filamentous fungi, contaminates corn, posing significant health and safety hazards for humans and livestock through toxigenic and carcinogenic effects. Corn is widely used as an essential commodity for food, feed, fuel, and export markets; therefore, AFL mitigation is necessary to ensure food and feed safety within the United States (US) and elsewhere in the world. In this case study, an Iowa-centric model was developed to predict AFL contamination using historical corn contamination, meteorological, satellite, and soil property data in the largest corn-producing state in the US. Methods: We evaluated the performance of AFL prediction with gradient boosting machine (GBM) learning and feature engineering in Iowa corn for two AFL risk thresholds for high contamination events: 20-ppb and 5-ppb. A 90%–10% training-to-testing ratio was utilized in 2010, 2011, 2012, and 2021 (n = 630), with independent validation using the year 2020 (n = 376). Results: The GBM model had an overall accuracy of 96.77% for AFL with a balanced accuracy of 50.00% for a 20-ppb risk threshold, whereas GBM had an overall accuracy of 90.32% with a balanced accuracy of 64.88% for a 5-ppb threshold. The GBM model had a low power to detect high AFL contamination events, resulting in a low sensitivity rate. Analyses for AFL showed satellite-acquired vegetative index during August significantly improved the prediction of corn contamination at the end of the growing season for both risk thresholds. Prediction of high AFL contamination levels was linked to aflatoxin risk indices (ARI) in May. However, ARI in July was an influential factor for the 5-ppb threshold but not for the 20-ppb threshold. Similarly, latitude was an influential factor for the 20-ppb threshold but not the 5-ppb threshold. Furthermore, soil-saturated hydraulic conductivity (Ksat) influenced both risk thresholds. Discussion: Developing these AFL prediction models is practical and implementable in commodity grain handling environments to achieve the goal of preventative rather than reactive mitigations. Finding predictors that influence AFL risk annually is an important cost-effective risk tool and, therefore, is a high priority to ensure hazard management and optimal grain utilization to maximize the utility of the nation’s corn crop.
Adaptation considered: The abstract implies adaptation is considered through the development of AFL prediction models for preventative mitigation in grain handling environments. Finding predictors that influence AFL risk annually is viewed as a cost-effective risk tool for hazard management and optimal grain utilization. The extent is tied to improving prediction accuracy using meteorological, satellite, and soil data to inform preventative strategies.
Adaptation considered-sourcematerial: Developing these AFL prediction models is practical and implementable in commodity grain handling environments to achieve the goal of preventative rather than reactive mitigations. Finding predictors that influence AFL risk annually is an important cost-effective risk tool and, therefore, is a high priority to ensure hazard management and optimal grain utilization to maximize the utility of the nation’s corn crop.

Paper Title: From identification to forecasting: the potential of image recognition and artificial intelligence for aphid pest monitoring
Outcome(s) of Interest: Improved aphid abundance prediction; better plant virus spread prediction; reduced pesticide use; resistance management; forecasting models.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: It has been shown that long-term monitoring of migrating aphids using suction traps can be used to make, adjust and improve predictions of their abundance so that the risk of plant viruses spreading through aphids can be more accurately predicted. With the increasing demand for alternatives to conventional pesticide use in crop protection, the need for predictive models is growing, e.g. as a basis for resistance development and as a measure for resistance management. ... Using aphids as a model organism, we demonstrate the possibilities of systematic monitoring of insect pests and the potential of future technical developments in the subsequent automated identification of individuals through to the use of case data for intelligent forecasting models.
Drivers of Risk: Advancing climate change influencing aphid abundance and peak occurrences.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Advancing climate change has a strong influence on the total abundance of migrating aphids as well as on the peak occurrences of aphids within a year.
Category-sourcematerial: Aphids are important agricultural pests that pose a significant risk to several important crops and cause high economic losses through feeding damage and transmission of plant viruses. With the increasing demand for alternatives to conventional pesticide use in crop protection, the need for predictive models is growing, e.g. as a basis for resistance development and as a measure for resistance management.
Approach-sourcematerial: "Using aphids as a model organism, we demonstrate the possibilities of systematic monitoring of insect pests and the potential of future technical developments in the subsequent automated identification of individuals through to the use of case data for intelligent forecasting models."
Methodology: The paper uses suction traps for long-term aphid monitoring, addressing the bottleneck of manual identification with machine learning, image recognition, and AI to automate specimen identification from images and develop intelligent forecasting models.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Insect monitoring has gained global public attention in recent years in the context of insect decline and biodiversity loss. ... passive collection methods, e.g. suction traps, provide standardized and comparable data sets, ... machine learning, image recognition and artificial intelligence have emerged as promising tools to address the shortcomings of manual identification and quantification ... Using aphids as an example, we show the potential for systematic monitoring of insect pests through technical developments in the automated identification of individuals from static images ... We discuss the potential applications with regard to the automatic processing of insect case data and the development of intelligent prediction models.
Highlights: Machine learning and image recognition can automate aphid identification from suction trap samples, addressing bottlenecks in traditional taxonomic analysis. This enables improved prediction of aphid abundance, plant virus transmission risk, and ultimately supports alternatives to conventional pesticides in the face of climate change impacts on aphid populations.

Paper Title: Climate change through the essentials–nature's offering and humankind's sine qua non
Outcome(s) of Interest: Peak temperature rise 1°C, peak sea level rise 5m after 1000 years, economic burden 1.4% of current global GDP.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Climate change is decomposed into the driving terms allowing long-term projection of the natural and economic impacts. As a result, in the case of carbon emissions reduction by 2% per year from the present, the atmospheric CO2 concentration is expected to return to preindustrial values in about 1,000 years, temperature and sea level rise to approximately peak at 1°C and 5 m above the present levels by then, and the entailed economic burden to grow to 1.4% of the current global gross domestic product.
Drivers of Risk: CO2 emissions; economic growth; lack of global coherence in regulations.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: (i) CO2 emissions reduction must be at least 2%/year at global level; (ii) economic growth may not continue to jeopardize emissions reduction efforts, thus far contributing with an emissions rise of 1.7%/year; (iii) due to the economic interlinkage, global coherence of regulatory measures must be established

Methodology: Climate change is decomposed into driving terms to project long-term natural and economic impacts.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Climate change is decomposed into the driving terms allowing long-term projection of the natural and economic impacts.
Highlights: Reducing carbon emissions by 2% annually could limit temperature and sea level rise to 1°C and 5m respectively in 1,000 years, with a 1.4% GDP economic burden, achievable through cost-neutral methods if economic growth doesn't impede emissions reduction and global regulatory coherence is established, starting with penalized non-participants.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Climate change is decomposed into the driving terms allowing long-term projection of the natural and economic impacts. As a result, in the case of carbon emissions reduction by 2% per year from the present, the atmospheric CO2 concentration is expected to return to preindustrial values in about 1,000 years, temperature and sea level rise to approximately peak at 1°C and 5 m above the present levels by then, and the entailed economic burden to grow to 1.4% of the current global gross domestic product. Ninety percent of the required emissions reduction are anticipated achievable through cost neutrality. To take advantage of the potentially bearable impact, humankind is obliged to fulfill certain prerequisites near-time: (i) CO2 emissions reduction must be at least 2%/year at global level; (ii) economic growth may not continue to jeopardize emissions reduction efforts, thus far contributing with an emissions rise of 1.7%/year; (iii) due to the economic interlinkage, global coherence of regulatory measures must be established, proposedly commencing with a sizable group of countries, the rules comprising economic penalization of non-participants.
More Notes: Sustained 2%/year CO2 emissions reduction, largely achievable through cost-neutral measures, could eventually return atmospheric CO2 to preindustrial levels, but with peak temperature rise of 1°C and sea level rise of 5m, incurring economic burden of 1.4% of current global GDP; this necessitates immediate, globally coherent action, including penalizing non-participants, and addressing economic growth undermining emissions reduction efforts.

Paper Title: Adapting Hydropower Operations to Support Renewable Energy Transitions and Freshwater Sustainability in the Columbia River Basin
Outcome(s) of Interest: Robust hydropower operations policy balancing renewable energy integration, environmental constraints, flood control, hydropower production, and economic benefits under future climate and energy scenarios.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "Our optimization is able to find a robust compromise policy that balances the system's conflicting objectives well both now and in the future...This will require not only filling supply gaps from other renewable energy sources, but also balancing other conflicting objectives to be fulfilled by the dam operations, such as minimizing environmental spill violations, maximizing hydropower production, maximizing flood protection, and maximizing economic benefits...We then simulate their operations over alternative possible future climate change and energy development scenarios to find a recommended set of operations that are robust to these uncertainties."
Drivers of Risk: Earth warming, fossil fuel burning, renewable energy transition, hydropower re-operation, Mid-Columbia market, California renewables, intermittent supply, climate change scenarios.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: There is unequivocal evidence that the Earth is warming at an unprecedented rate, and that the burning of fossil fuels is the principal cause. This situation is fostering a growing interest in shifting global energy production toward renewable energy sources such as solar, wind, and hydropower. Hydropower plays an important role in meeting global carbon mitigation targets... However, other renewable energy sources in the Mid-C market and connected California Independent System Operator (CAISO) power grid are expanding significantly, particularly solar power in California (CA). Thus, hydropower operations at plants within the connected Mid-C market may need to be re-operated to balance the more intermittent supply from renewables in CA so that energy supplies are in phase with demands...We then simulate their operations over alternative possible future climate change and energy development scenarios to find a recommended set of operations that are robust to these uncertainties.
Category-sourcematerial: There is unequivocal evidence that the Earth is warming at an unprecedented rate, and that the burning of fossil fuels is the principal cause. This situation is fostering a growing interest in shifting global energy production toward renewable energy sources such as solar, wind, and hydropower. Hydropower plays an important role in meeting global carbon mitigation targets and eventually achieving net-zero carbon emissions, especially within the Mid-Columbia (Mid-C) energy market in the Pacific Northwest (PNW), where hydropower currently comprises 50-65% of its generation. ... In this study, our goal is to re-design hydropower operations in the Columbia River Basin (CRB) of the PNW to achieve a 95% renewable energy power grid in CA and the PNW by the year 2035.
Approach-sourcematerial: We use multi-objective optimization to design alternative operations at four CRB dams to balance these objectives over the historical record. We then simulate their operations over alternative possible future climate change and energy development scenarios to find a recommended set of operations that are robust to these uncertainties.
Methodology: Multi-objective optimization is used to redesign operations at four Columbia River Basin dams to balance conflicting objectives (environmental spill violations, hydropower production, flood protection, and economic benefits) while achieving a 95% renewable energy power grid in California and the Pacific Northwest by 2035, simulating operations under alternative climate change and energy development scenarios (including NREL's Mid-Case and a business-as-usual scenario) to find a robust set of operational policies.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "We use multi-objective optimization to design alternative operations at four CRB dams to balance these objectives over the historical record. We then simulate their operations over alternative possible future climate change and energy development scenarios to find a recommended set of operations that are robust to these uncertainties. The energy scenarios include the National Renewable Energy Lab's (NREL) Mid-Case Energy Scenario for the years 2025, 2030 and 2035, which achieve 95% Renewables by 2035, as well as a business as usual (BAU), or base case, scenario represented by the historical energy mix. The four climate scenarios are made from combinations of low or high warming and low or high streamflow for three overlapping time steps: 2020-2029, 2025-2034, and 2030-2039. Our optimization is able to find a robust compromise policy that balances the system's conflicting objectives well both now and in the future."
Highlights: The study demonstrates the use of multi-objective optimization to redesign hydropower operations in the Columbia River Basin, aiming to achieve a 95% renewable energy power grid in California and the Pacific Northwest by 2035, balancing renewable energy supply with hydropower, flood protection, environmental, and economic objectives under varying climate and energy scenarios.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "In this study, our goal is to re-design hydropower operations in the Columbia River Basin (CRB) of the PNW to achieve a 95% renewable energy power grid in CA and the PNW by the year 2035... We use multi-objective optimization to design alternative operations at four CRB dams to balance these objectives over the historical record. We then simulate their operations over alternative possible future climate change and energy development scenarios to find a recommended set of operations that are robust to these uncertainties. The energy scenarios include the National Renewable Energy Lab's (NREL) Mid-Case Energy Scenario for the years 2025, 2030 and 2035, which achieve 95% Renewables by 2035, as well as a business as usual (BAU), or base case, scenario represented by the historical energy mix. ... Our optimization is able to find a robust compromise policy that balances the system's conflicting objectives well both now and in the future."
More Notes: The study redesigns Columbia River Basin hydropower operations to achieve a 95% renewable energy grid in California and the Pacific Northwest by 2035, using multi-objective optimization to balance renewable supply, environmental impacts, flood protection, and economic benefits amid climate and energy uncertainties.
More Notes-sourcematerial: "In this study, our goal is to re-design hydropower operations in the Columbia River Basin (CRB) of the PNW to achieve a 95% renewable energy power grid in CA and the PNW by the year 2035... This will require not only filling supply gaps from other renewable energy sources, but also balancing other conflicting objectives to be fulfilled by the dam operations, such as minimizing environmental spill violations, maximizing hydropower production, maximizing flood protection, and maximizing economic benefits. We use multi-objective optimization to design alternative operations at four CRB dams to balance these objectives over the historical record. We then simulate their operations over alternative possible future climate change and energy development scenarios to find a recommended set of operations that are robust to these uncertainties."
Hazard definition-sourcematerial: The four climate scenarios are made from combinations of low or high warming and low or high streamflow for three overlapping time steps: 2020-2029, 2025-2034, and 2030-2039.
