Paper Title: Effects of three dissolved oxygen modes on oxidative stress and physiological metabolism in Ruditapes philippinarum gill tissue and hemolymph; [溶氧变化模式对菲律宾蛤仔鳃组织和血淋巴液氧化应激及生理代 谢的影响]
Outcome(s) of Interest: Long-term hypoxia leads to clam mortality, increased disease risk, adverse economic impacts, and damaged ecosystems.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Long term hypoxia can kill a large number of marine organisms, including clams. Clams are more vulnerable to various adverse environmental factors in an increasingly intensive breeding environment, which increases the risk of disease outbreaks. This is unfavorable for coastal economic development and ecosystem maintenance in China.
Drivers of Risk: Climate change, coastal eutrophication, hypoxia (severity, duration), intensive breeding environment.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Ruditapes philippinarum is one of the most important shellfish species cultured on the beach in China. In recent years, global climate change and coastal seawater eutrophication have expanded the hypoxic area of the tidal flat ecosystem. The severity and duration of hypoxia will be exacerbated by eutrophication and may deteriorate with climate change. Clams are more vulnerable to various adverse environmental factors in an increasingly intensive breeding environment, which increases the risk of disease outbreaks.
Category-sourcematerial: Ruditapes philippinarum is one of the most important shellfish species cultured on the beach in China. In recent years, global climate change and coastal seawater eutrophication have expanded the hypoxic area of the tidal flat ecosystem... This is unfavorable for coastal economic development and ecosystem maintenance in China. The results of this study will provide a scientific foundation for adjusting bivalve culture methods and disease prevention methods.
Approach-sourcematerial: We set up three modes of dissolved oxygen changes to investigate the oxidative stress response and physiological metabolism of R. philippinarum gill tissue and hemolymph in response to dissolved oxygen changes, namely, maintaining normal dissolved oxygen (C treatment), normal dissolved oxygen-acute hypoxia-acute reoxygenation (AHR treatment), and normal dissolved oxygenchronic hypoxia-chronic reoxygenation (CHR treatment)... The superoxide dismutase (SOD) activity, catalase (CAT) activity, glutathione peroxidase (GSH-px) activity, malondialdehyde (MDA) content... were measured using the microplate method with 96 samples... The results of this study will provide a scientific foundation for adjusting bivalve culture methods and disease prevention methods.
Methodology: The study set up three dissolved oxygen conditions—normal, acute hypoxia-acute reoxygenation, and chronic hypoxia-chronic reoxygenation—in Ruditapes philippinarum, measuring antioxidative enzyme activities, metabolic enzyme activities, and products using 96 samples with the microplate method to evaluate responses of gill tissue and hemolymph.
Methodology-sourcematerial: We set up three modes of dissolved oxygen changes to investigate the oxidative stress response and physiological metabolism of R. philippinarum gill tissue and hemolymph in response to dissolved oxygen changes, namely, maintaining normal dissolved oxygen (C treatment), normal dissolved oxygen-acute hypoxia-acute reoxygenation (AHR treatment), and normal dissolved oxygen-chronic hypoxia-chronic reoxygenation (CHR treatment). The superoxide dismutase (SOD) activity, catalase (CAT) activity, glutathione peroxidase (GSH-px) activity, malondialdehyde (MDA) content, pyruvate kinase (PK) activity, hexokinase (HK) activity, lactic dehydrogenase (LDH) activity, ATPase activity, proline hydroxylase (PHD) activity, and lipid peroxide (LPO) content of hemolymph and gill tissue under the three modes of dissolved oxygen change were measured using the microplate method with 96 samples.
Highlights: *Ruditapes philippinarum* clam gill tissue responds immediately and intensely to dissolved oxygen changes, while hemolymph responds later and more moderately; SOD and CAT are key antioxidative enzymes mitigating dissolved oxygen change damage; PK and HK regulate glycolysis, LDH regulates anaerobic metabolism, and MDH reflects dissolved oxygen damage; and chronic dissolved oxygen changes cause slow, long-term damage difficult to detect.
Highlights-sourcematerial: The gill tissue of R. philippinarum was the first to respond to the change in dissolved oxygen, and the response was immediate and intense, whereas the response of the haemolymph was delayed and moderate...SOD and CAT can help to mitigate the damage of dissolved oxygen change on the clam body...PK and HK regulate the efficiency of glycolysis as the dissolved oxygen changes, whereas LDH primarily regulates the efficiency of anaerobic metabolism, and the MDH content reflects the extent of the dissolved oxygen damage to the clam...the response to the chronic changes in dissolved oxygen is slow, resulting in long-term damage to the clam body that is difficult to detect.
More Notes: Hypoxia, exacerbated by climate change and eutrophication, negatively impacts clam physiology by inducing oxidative stress and altering metabolic processes in gill tissue and hemolymph, with gill tissue exhibiting a faster, more intense response than hemolymph.
More Notes-sourcematerial: Ruditapes philippinarum is one of the most important shellfish species cultured on the beach in China...global climate change and coastal seawater eutrophication have expanded the hypoxic area of the tidal flat ecosystem...Long term hypoxia can kill a large number of marine organisms, including clams...The respiratory organ of the clam is the gill, and haemolymph transports oxygen and nutrients in the clam. The change in dissolved oxygen directly affects the oxidative stress response and metabolic index in the gill tissue and blood cells...the gill tissue of R. philippinarum was the first to respond to the change in dissolved oxygen, and the response was immediate and intense, whereas the response of the haemolymph was delayed and moderate.
Hazard definition-sourcematerial: "In recent years, global climate change and coastal seawater eutrophication have expanded the hypoxic area of the tidal flat ecosystem. The severity and duration of hypoxia will be exacerbated by eutrophication and may deteriorate with climate change. Long term hypoxia can kill a large number of marine organisms, including clams." and "normal dissolved oxygenchronic hypoxia-chronic reoxygenation (CHR treatment)" and "the response to the chronic changes in dissolved oxygen is slow, resulting in long-term damage to the clam body that is difficult to detect."
Impact persistence-sourcematerial: The body of R. philippinarum can quickly adjust the oxidative stress and physiological metabolic responses to acute changes in dissolved oxygen, but the response to the chronic changes in dissolved oxygen is slow, resulting in long-term damage to the clam body that is difficult to detect.

The severity and duration of hypoxia will be exacerbated by eutrophication and may deteriorate with climate change. Long term hypoxia can kill a large number of marine organisms, including clams. Clams are more vulnerable to various adverse environmental factors in an increasingly intensive breeding environment, which increases the risk of disease outbreaks."

While it addresses complexities in the physiological response of clams to environmental changes, it doesn't explicitly mention climatic tipping points or large-scale climate complexities. The focus is on biochemical reactions and metabolic adjustments rather than broader climatic phenomena like tipping points.
Adaptation considered: The study suggests adjusting bivalve culture and disease prevention methods as a potential adaptation strategy, based on understanding the clam's physiological response to changing dissolved oxygen levels.

Paper Title: Advance Equitable Livelihoods
Outcome(s) of Interest: Equitable livelihoods; poverty reduction; sustainable food systems; reduced inequalities; improved nutrition; increased resilience.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "advance equitable livelihoods for producers, businesses, workers across the food system and consumers"; "reducing inequalities and power imbalances"; "reduce poverty"; "sustainable, equitable livelihoods"; "addressing inequalities in food systems, including rights-based approaches"; "prioritizing the availability and affordability of nutritious food for all"; "overall production, resilience and rural transformation".
Drivers of Risk: biophysical/environmental drivers (soil, water, climate change), unequal access to resources, inadequate innovation/technology access, economic/political factors (inequality, power imbalances), socio-cultural/demographic drivers (vulnerable populations), COVID-19 pandemic (market disruptions, economic impacts).
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Small and medium-sized producers and people who rely on food systems in rural and urban areas for livelihoods are disproportionately affected by all biophysical and environmental drivers, including soil and water resources and climate change. Unequal opportunity in access to all types of resources reduces overall production, resilience and rural transformation. Advances in innovation, technology and infrastructure have had important impacts on food production and sustainability, transportation and processing along food value chains, marketing, and, ultimately, diets, including the consumption of both nutritious and unhealthy foods. However, achievement of equitable livelihoods in food systems will require that issues of access to contextually suitable innovation and technology, inclusive of indigenous knowledge, be substantially enhanced. Many economic and political factors can be essential causes of inequality and power imbalances at the household, community, national and global levels, which may constrain the ability of food system transformation to deliver poverty reduction and sustainable, equitable livelihoods. Finally, vast evidence illustrates that several socio-cultural and demographic drivers underpin inequalities among and within societies and constrain the potential for some to benefit from actions to improve their livelihoods, particularly women, youths, the disabled, the elderly and indigenous peoples. These issues have been exacerbated by the COVID-19 pandemic. The pandemic is having a significant impact on global commodity markets and trading systems, economic growth, incomes, and poverty levels, with a likely disproportionate burden falling on vulnerable communities in both urban and rural areas.
Category-sourcematerial: Small and medium-sized producers and people who rely on food systems in rural and urban areas for livelihoods are disproportionately affected by all biophysical and environmental drivers, including soil and water resources and climate change. ... Advances in innovation, technology and infrastructure have had important impacts on food production and sustainability...
Approach-sourcematerial: The abstract focuses on exploring the drivers of food system inequities and proposing recommendations for equitable livelihoods, without mentioning any specific modeling or quantitative analysis techniques. It frames the issues using the High Level Panel of Experts of the UN Committee on World Food Security (HLPE 2020) and discusses factors like globalization, trade, technology, and demographic trends. There is no indication of econometric modeling, macro-modeling, or stylized modeling being used.
Methodology: The methodology involves exploring strategies and approaches to transform food systems by addressing inequalities, power imbalances, and inequities, using drivers of food systems as framed by the High Level Panel of Experts of the UN Committee on World Food Security, while considering the impact of COVID-19 on these dynamics.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "The task of Action Track 4 is to explore approaches to doing so that will advance equitable livelihoods... using the drivers of food systems as articulated by the High Level Panel of Experts of the UN Committee on World Food Security (HLPE 2020) as framing... To address these issues, we must transform not only food systems, but the structures and systems that continue to enable and exacerbate inequities... These issues have been exacerbated by the COVID-19 pandemic."
Highlights: Food system transformation prioritizes nutritious food availability and affordability for all, addressing inequalities and power imbalances that disproportionately affect vulnerable producers and communities impacted by climate change and resource scarcity; equitable access to innovation, technology, and inclusive decision-making, alongside rights-based approaches and increased investment, are crucial for creating opportunities and protecting livelihoods.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Small and medium-sized producers and people who rely on food systems in rural and urban areas for livelihoods are disproportionately affected by all biophysical and environmental drivers, including soil and water resources and climate change...achievement of equitable livelihoods in food systems will require that issues of access to contextually suitable innovation and technology, inclusive of indigenous knowledge, be substantially enhanced...we must transform not only food systems, but the structures and systems that continue to enable and exacerbate inequities...effective solutions for combating inequalities in food systems, including rights-based approaches...recommendations aimed at enhancing inclusive decision-making, protecting the livelihoods of those living in situations of vulnerability while creating opportunities, adapting institutions and policies to favor equitable food system livelihoods, and increasing investment so as to realize the potential of improved institutional and policy actions.
More Notes: Food system inequities are driven by interconnected biophysical, environmental, technological, economic, political, socio-cultural, and demographic factors, exacerbated by events like the COVID-19 pandemic, requiring transformative solutions that address underlying structures and systems to ensure equitable livelihoods, particularly for vulnerable populations.

Paper Title: Climate Change Impacts on Animal Production
Outcome(s) of Interest: Reduced animal production (forage, water, breeding, milk); Salination impacts production; Fish resource threat; Acidification reduces fish supply; Parasite growth increases morbidity/death; Heat stress reduces yield (dairy, beef, poultry); Economic loss; Food security influence.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Climatic variations are the utmost stressors of animal production as it exerts great influence on the forage quality, water accessibility, breeding, milk production, and the overall cattle farming sector. Salination of freshwater river systems due to the upsurge in sea level lessens the hygienic status of the production. Any transition in the temperature threatens the fish resources equally. Besides warming, climatic variability generates acidic conditions in the water bodies, which in turn curtail the global fish supply. Rising temperature hastens the growth of parasites that intensifies the potential for morbidity and death. Augmentation in heat stress reduces the yield in the dairy, beef, and poultry industry and thus induces heavy economic loss. Climate change has adverse implications on animal production and productivity which accordingly influence food security.

Paper Title: The hazard components of representative key risks. The physical climate perspective
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increasing risk of impacts on human and natural systems, driven by changes in climate phenomena with every increment of CO2 and warming, absent adaptation or changes in exposure/vulnerability.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: In almost all cases, however, the basic message remains that every small increment in CO2 concentration in the atmosphere and associated warming will bring changes in climate phenomena that will contribute to increasing risk of impacts on human and natural systems, in the absence of compensating changes in these systems’ exposure and vulnerability, and in the absence of effective adaptation.
Drivers of Risk: Climate impact-drivers, sea level rise, global warming levels, heat extremes, extreme precipitation, droughts, tropical cyclones, fire weather, ocean acidification, ENSO variability, regional warming, storm surges, permafrost thaw, cyclone intensity, adaptation, exposure, vulnerability.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Page 2: "We identify the relevant hazards for each RKR, based upon the WGII authors’ assessment, and we report on their current state and expected future changes in magnitude, intensity and/or frequency, linking these changes to Global Warming Levels (GWLs) to the extent possible."; Page 3: "ocean pH and air pollution - the composition of the scenario of anthropogenic emissions is most relevant, rather than the warming reached."; Page 4: hazards including sea level rise, warming of sea surface temperatures, thawing of permafrost, and extreme events like marine heatwaves, storm surges, tropical and extratropical cyclones, and wildfires.; Page 6: Changes in precipitation patterns, extreme precipitation events, droughts, snow cover changes, glacier mass loss, sea level rise (SLR), and increased frequency of extreme events.; Page 10: Warming Global Surface Air Temperature (GSAT) is the main driver impacting heat extremes, cold extremes, and heavy precipitation.; Page 11: Inland floods induced by heavy precipitation, drought events, fire weather conditions, ENSO variability, tropical cyclones, and extra-tropical storms are all discussed as drivers of risk.; Page 13: Shocks such as sea level rise and increased storm surge frequency are identified as drivers impacting coastal flooding and related outcomes.; Page 15: hazards considered can be put in a direct, strong relationship with global temperature changes and patterns of regional warming and mean precipitation change, sea surface temperature warming, snow cover, permafrost thawing,...heat extremes, tropical cyclone intensity, marine heatwaves, droughts and fire weather, storm surges as results of winds and sea level rise combined.; Page 14: acknowledge that exposure, vulnerability, adaptation, and mitigation also contribute to the ultimate outcome.
Category-sourcematerial: Risks involve economic impacts at various scales, human health focusing on heat-related outcomes, and food and water security. Discusses risks to peace and human mobility, including armed conflict and involuntary immobility. Also considers water availability, flooding, droughts, and snow cover/glacier loss impacts on ways of life and culture. Extreme heat (frequency and intensity) The paper evaluates the changes in various climate impact metrics such as glacier mass, permafrost volume, snow cover, sea level rise, and frequency and intensity of climate events. The paper focuses on the hazard component of risk, acknowledging that exposure, vulnerability, adaptation, and mitigation also contribute to the ultimate outcome.
Approach-sourcematerial: Page 2: Representation of the future evolution of hazard components of RKRs based on current literature and WGII authors’ assessment.; Page 3: Identification and characterization of CIDs relevant to Representative Key Risks (RKRs) and their relation to GWLs.; Page 6: Analysis is based on Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 6 (CMIP6) experiments under various future anthropogenic radiative forcing scenarios. Uses Global Warming Levels (GWLs) based on Global Surface Air Temperature (GSAT) warming relative to the 1850-1900 baseline. Employs pattern scaling for temperature and precipitation.; Page 8: Analysis is based on IPCC AR6 WGI assessment. Relationship between GSAT warming and CID/hazards.; Page 9: Analysis focuses on 2081-2100 outcomes under SSP5-8.5 and SSP1-2.6 scenarios.; Page 10: Pattern scaling is used to represent the scaling of mean precipitation with GSAT changes, using models such as CMIP6.; Page 12: Uses discrete global warming levels (GWLs) to project changes in impact metrics and assesses them against pre-industrial or 1995–2014 conditions.; Page 14: The analysis identifies climatic impact-drivers (CIDs) that can become hazards and contribute to the severity of representative key risks (RKRs). The map shows the global warming level (GWL) at which a current 100-yr coastal flood event becomes an annual event, based on projections of relative sea level rise by 2100 at each location. Labels 2.0C+ and 5.0C+ indicate sea level rise projections for those two GWLs that also include the effects of ice-sheet instability.; Page 15: Synthesis based on global warming levels rather than specific emissions scenarios; examines how hazards change with GSAT levels.
Methodology: The methodology represents the future evolution of RKR hazard components using literature, IPCC assessments, CMIP6 scenarios, pattern scaling, and GWLs to characterize and quantify CIDs, primarily drawing from AR6 WGI.

Highlights: Every increment of global warming matters significantly for climate risk.

More Notes: Acknowledges the hazard component is only one determinant of climate risks, requiring assessment of exposure, vulnerability, adaptation, and mitigation for a realistic risk evaluation.
More Notes-sourcematerial: "the hazard component as only one of the determinants, sometimes not even the major one, of climate risks" and "obtain a realistic assessment of future risk and possibly future opportunities that associated adaptation responses may present".
Hazard definition-sourcematerial: For some of these quantities - like regional trends in oceanic and atmospheric temperature and precipitation, some heat and precipitation extremes, permafrost thaw and Northern Hemisphere snow cover - a strong and quantitative relationship with increasing GWLs has been identified. For others - like frequency and intensity of tropical cyclones and extra-tropical storms, and fire weather - that link can only be described qualitatively. For some processes - like the behavior of ice sheets, or changes in circulation dynamics - large uncertainties about the effects of different GWLs remain, and for a few others - like ocean pH and air pollution - the composition of the scenario of anthropogenic emissions is most relevant, rather than the warming reached.
Impact persistence-sourcematerial: The analysis identifies climatic impact-drivers (CIDs) that can become hazards and contribute to the severity of representative key risks (RKRs).
Space disaggregation-sourcematerial: Page 3: For some of these quantities - like regional trends in oceanic and atmospheric temperature and precipitation, some heat and precipitation extremes, permafrost thaw and Northern Hemisphere snow cover - a strong and quantitative relationship with increasing GWLs has been identified.
Impact interactions-sourcematerial: In this paper, we offer the limited but necessary perspective of the physical climate science, by identifying climatic impact-drivers (CIDs, Ranasinghe et al., 2021; Ruane et al., 2022) that have the potential to become hazards relevant to the various risks constituting the eight RKRs. Once identified, the relation between the CIDs/hazards and the magnitude of global warming is characterized and, where possible, quantified. With this work we are offering a physical climate perspective on drivers of risk, i.e., we focus on the hazard component, aware that it is only one of multiple factors - including exposure, vulnerability and possible adaptation and mitigation responses - contributing to the ultimate outcome. In particular, we present a catalogue of climatic impact-drivers (CIDs) that, by having the potential to become hazards, can be expected to contribute to the severity of representative key risks (RKRs).

Climate features-sourcematerial: For some processes - like the behavior of ice sheets, or changes in circulation dynamics - large uncertainties about the effects of different GWLs remain, and for a few others - like ocean pH and air pollution - the composition of the scenario of anthropogenic emissions is most relevant, rather than the warming reached. Extreme air pollution events can be favored by specific meteorological conditions (e.g. heatwaves, temperature inversions and atmospheric stagnation episodes) over highly polluted areas. These conditions are influenced by climate change, but the correlation between such meteorological conditions and high concentrations of ozone and PM2.5 have been shown to be regionally and metric dependent. The effects of future climate change on air pollution through changes in natural sources of pollution precursors remain very uncertain (Szopa et al., 2021). Surface ozone levels could respond linearly to GSAT increases but with differing effects depending on the ozone regime.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is acknowledged as a factor influencing risk alongside hazard, exposure, and vulnerability. Analysis focuses on the hazard component of risk. Mentions that realistic assessment of future risk involves adaptation responses.

Paper Title: Climate Change Adaptation Strategies for Road Transportation Infrastructure: A Systematic Review on Flooding Events
Outcome(s) of Interest: Flooding impacts, road transportation, infrastructure damage, network connectivity loss, repair costs, adaptation strategies, climate risk assessment, site-specific analyses, hard and soft adaptation.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: During the last decades, the number of flooding events has increased significantly... resulting in major physical disruption to water and wastewater systems, life and economic losses, and damage to the critical infrastructure. For the road transportation sector... as severe flooding events tend to severely damage the transportation infrastructure and reduce the network connectivity, increasing repair, maintenance, and construction costs... a repository of 213 publications on adaptation strategies applied to reduce the impacts of flooding on road infrastructure is developed... the climate risk assessment, involving the creation of current and future flood risk maps, is essential for determining the best climate change adaptation strategies for road transportation infrastructure. As flood damages and costs are largely and strictly site-specific, analyses are critical for guiding land use decisions and evaluating adaptation strategies that can be divided into hard adaptation... and soft adaptation...
Drivers of Risk: Urbanization, climate change (increased flooding events).
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: During the last decades, the number of flooding events has increased significantly, due to the global trend of urbanization and climate change, becoming a recurring biophysical impact, resulting in major physical disruption to water and wastewater systems, life and economic losses, and damage to the critical infrastructure.
Category-sourcematerial: During the last decades, the number of flooding events has increased significantly... For the road transportation sector, this reality is indisputable, as severe flooding events tend to severely damage the transportation infrastructure... a repository of 213 publications on adaptation strategies applied to reduce the impacts of flooding on road infrastructure is developed. It should also be noted that the climate risk assessment, involving the creation of current and future flood risk maps, is essential for determining the best climate change adaptation strategies... damages and costs are largely and strictly site-specific, analyses are critical for guiding land use decisions and evaluating adaptation strategies... hard adaptation (optimization or redesign of hydraulic components, installation of protective structures and optimization of environmental conditions) and soft adaptation (creation of transportation-focused master plans and development of quantitative models and systems).
Approach-sourcematerial: Through a systematic literature review, with direct database searches and application of inclusion and qualification (quality and applicability) filters, a repository of 213 publications on adaptation strategies applied to reduce the impacts of flooding on road infrastructure is developed.
Methodology: A systematic literature review was conducted using database searches and inclusion/qualification filters, resulting in a repository of 213 publications on adaptation strategies for flooding impacts on road infrastructure.

Paper Title: Spatial heterogeneity of preferences for sea-level rise adaptation: Empirical evidence from yearlong and seasonal residents in Florida
Outcome(s) of Interest: Public preferences and economic value (WTP) for different attributes of SLR adaptation programs in Florida.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "This study aims to investigate public preferences and estimate the economic value of sea-level rise (SLR) adaptation projects in Florida. We compute the households’ willingness to pay (WTP) for different attributes of SLR adaptation programs using a series of choice experiments embedded within a household survey of selected communities in Florida."
Drivers of Risk: Global warming linked to accelerating sea level rise, impacting low-elevation coastal communities. Risk perception and income levels influence willingness to pay for adaptation, with seasonal residents showing higher WTP than year-long residents.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "A growing body of evidence suggests that the global sea level has been increasing at an accelerating rate. This trend, which is linked to global warming, poses a significant threat to the communities living in low elevation coastal areas...Florida's seasonal residents are willing to pay more than yearlong residents due to their higher risk perceptions and higher income levels."
Category-sourcematerial: A growing body of evidence suggests that the global sea level has been increasing at an accelerating rate. This trend, which is linked to global warming, poses a significant threat to the communities living in low elevation coastal areas. This study aims to investigate public preferences and estimate the economic value of sea-level rise (SLR) adaptation projects in Florida.
Approach-sourcematerial: We compute the households’ willingness to pay (WTP) for different attributes of SLR adaptation programs using a series of choice experiments embedded within a household survey of selected communities in Florida. We find strong spatially heterogeneous preferences in both the short-term and long-term adaptation plans. Moreover, Florida's seasonal residents are willing to pay more than yearlong residents due to their higher risk perceptions and higher income levels.
Methodology: The methodology involved using choice experiments embedded within a household survey of selected communities in Florida to estimate households' willingness to pay for various attributes of sea-level rise adaptation programs, capturing spatially heterogeneous preferences across both short-term and long-term plans.

We find strong spatially heterogeneous preferences in both the short-term and long-term adaptation plans."
Highlights: Examines public preferences and economic value of sea-level rise (SLR) adaptation in Florida, revealing spatially heterogeneous preferences, higher willingness to pay among seasonal residents, and insights for designing optimal adaptation programs.

Paper Title: Culturing for conservation: the need for timely investments in reef aquaculture
Outcome(s) of Interest: Species recovery, resilience to climate stressors, increased efficacy of interventions, scaled-up reef recovery, increased climate resilience of reef builders, long-term efficacy of interventions, resilient reef and human communities.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "promoting species recovery and bolstering resilience to stressors...early aquaculture interventions to increase efficacy...scaling up aquaculture-assisted reef recovery...increase climate resilience of reef builders...tracking of all interventions over time to determine their long-term efficacy...support resilient reef and human communities"
Drivers of Risk: Climate impacts, anthropogenic stressors.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Globally, reefs are imperiled by multiple anthropogenic stressors, particularly climate impacts.
Category-sourcematerial: Temperate oyster and tropical coral reefs are analogous systems that create habitat for economically, ecologically, and culturally important species, and they provide countless ecosystem services to human coastal communities. [...] Using aquaculture to support conservation goals - known as conservation aquaculture - is a relatively new approach for many reef building species, but it shows great promise for promoting species recovery and bolstering resilience to stressors. [...] Here, we combine expertise from coral and oyster reef ecosystems to consider the role of aquaculture as a conservation intervention for reefs, and provide recommendations for its timely development and targeted implementation. We highlight the importance of evaluating reef systems - alongside local stakeholders and Indigenous communities - to determine where and when the benefits of using aquaculture are most likely to outweigh the risks. [...] Developing conservation aquaculture approaches now is critical to position managers, scientists, and restoration practitioners to implement this intervention in timely and effective ways to support resilient reef and human communities worldwide.
Approach-sourcematerial: There is no indication of any specific modeling or analysis approach being used in the analysis in this abstract.
Methodology: The paper combines expertise from coral and oyster reef ecosystems, emphasizing reef system evaluation with stakeholders, proactive monitoring for decline detection, early aquaculture interventions, novel aquaculture technologies for multi-generational reefs, scaling up using high-volume methods, climate resilience technique development, proof-of-concept projects, intervention tracking, and novel partnerships to facilitate reef conservation.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "Here, we combine expertise from coral and oyster reef ecosystems to consider the role of aquaculture as a conservation intervention for reefs, and provide recommendations for its timely development and targeted implementation. We highlight the importance of evaluating reef systems - alongside local stakeholders and Indigenous communities - to determine where and when the benefits of using aquaculture are most likely to outweigh the risks. We spotlight the importance of proactive monitoring to detect reef population declines, and the value of early aquaculture interventions to increase efficacy. Novel aquaculture approaches and technologies specifically designed for reef builders are considered, including techniques for building complex, multi-generational and multi-species reefs. We address the need for scaling up aquaculture-assisted reef recovery, particularly of corals, using high volume methods like those that have been successfully employed for oysters. We also recommend the immediate assessment and development of techniques to increase climate resilience of reef builders and we identify the challenges and trade-offs of these approaches. We highlight the use of proof-of-concept projects to test these promising methods, and we advise tracking of all interventions over time to determine their long-term efficacy. Finally, we outline opportunities to leverage novel partnerships among conservation, industry, and community interests that utilize aquaculture to facilitate the conservation of reefs."
Highlights: Aquaculture can support reef conservation by promoting species recovery and resilience, but its implementation is often delayed until reefs are severely degraded. Proactive monitoring, early intervention, novel aquaculture techniques for reef builders, and scaling up methods are needed. Climate resilience enhancement, proof-of-concept projects, long-term tracking, and leveraging partnerships among conservation, industry, and communities are crucial for effective reef conservation.

Finally, we outline opportunities to leverage novel partnerships among conservation, industry, and community interests that utilize aquaculture to facilitate the conservation of reefs."

Paper Title: Riverine nitrogen flux and its response to management, climate, and other environmental factors in Northeast and Midwest United States
Outcome(s) of Interest: Changes in total reactive nitrogen (TN) flux in rivers.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "we investigate the individual effects of a set of management, technology, and policy mechanisms that alter total reactive nitrogen (TN) flux through rivers" and "We evaluate SFE performance in terms of differences in TN flux..." and "The largest effect on TN flux are related to reduction in cropland area and atmospheric nitrogen deposition." and "Multi-factor experiments (MFEs) suggest that increasingly efficient corn cultivars had a larger influence than increasing fertilizer application rate, while population growth has a larger influence than wastewater treatment." and "Extreme climate SFEs suggest that persistent wet conditions increase TN flux throughout the study region. Meanwhile, persistent hot years result in reduced TN flux. The persistent dry climate SFE leads to increased TN flux in the NE and reduced TN flux in the MW."
Drivers of Risk: Anthropogenic nitrogen alteration, cropland reduction, atmospheric nitrogen deposition, corn cultivars efficiency, fertilizer application, population growth, wastewater treatment, wet conditions, hot years, dry climate.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: The rate and extent of anthropogenic alteration of the global nitrogen cycle...the largest effect on TN flux are related to reduction in cropland area and atmospheric nitrogen deposition...increasingly efficient corn cultivars had a larger influence than increasing fertilizer application rate, while population growth has a larger influence than wastewater treatment. Extreme climate SFEs suggest that persistent wet conditions increase TN flux throughout the study region. Meanwhile, persistent hot years result in reduced TN flux. The persistent dry climate SFE leads to increased TN flux in the NE and reduced TN flux in the MW.
Category-sourcematerial: The paper investigates the effects of management, technology, and policy mechanisms on reactive nitrogen flux through rivers in the Northeast and Midwest of the United States, focusing on aquatic systems and the impact of different environmental conditions and management practices on riverine and coastal water quality.
Approach-sourcematerial: "we investigate the individual effects of a set of management, technology, and policy mechanisms that alter total reactive nitrogen (TN) flux through rivers, using a modified, spatially detailed SPARROW TN model, between 1980 and 2019 in the Northeast (NE) and Midwest (MW) of the United States."
Methodology: A modified, spatially detailed SPARROW TN model was used to investigate the individual effects of management, technology, and policy mechanisms on total reactive nitrogen (TN) flux through rivers in the Northeast (NE) and Midwest (MW) of the United States between 1980 and 2019 via single-factor experiments (SFEs) comparing a historical baseline to scenarios where individual factors were held at 1980s levels.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "In this paper, we investigate the individual effects of a set of management, technology, and policy mechanisms that alter total reactive nitrogen (TN) flux through rivers, using a modified, spatially detailed SPARROW TN model, between 1980 and 2019 in the Northeast (NE) and Midwest (MW) of the United States...We simulate and validate a historical baseline, to which we compare a set of climate and non-climate single factor experiments (SFEs) in which individual factors are held at 1980s levels while all other factors change dynamically."
Highlights: Persistent wet conditions increase total reactive nitrogen (TN) flux; persistent hot conditions decrease TN flux; persistent dry conditions increase TN flux in the Northeast but decrease it in the Midwest; the potential for TN removal through aquatic decay is greatest in the Midwest.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Extreme climate SFEs suggest that persistent wet conditions increase TN flux throughout the study region. Meanwhile, persistent hot years result in reduced TN flux. The persistent dry climate SFE leads to increased TN flux in the NE and reduced TN flux in the MW. We find that the potential for TN removal through aquatic decay is greatest in MW, due to the role of long travel time of rivers draining into the Lower Mississippi River.
More Notes: Extreme climate scenarios show persistent wet conditions increase total reactive nitrogen (TN) flux, persistent hot conditions decrease TN flux, and persistent dry conditions increase TN flux in the Northeast but decrease it in the Midwest.
More Notes-sourcematerial: Extreme climate SFEs suggest that persistent wet conditions increase TN flux throughout the study region. Meanwhile, persistent hot years result in reduced TN flux. The persistent dry climate SFE leads to increased TN flux in the NE and reduced TN flux in the MW.

It doesn't explicitly model adaptation, but rather examines the potential effectiveness of different management options under varying climate conditions (wet, hot, dry) in different regions.

Extreme climate SFEs suggest that persistent wet conditions increase TN flux... persistent hot years result in reduced TN flux... persistent dry climate SFE leads to increased TN flux... This paper sheds light on how a geographically and climatologically diverse region would respond to a representative selection of management options."

Paper Title: Quantifying fish range shifts across poorly defined management boundaries
Outcome(s) of Interest: Species distribution shifts, marine resource management, spatial boundaries, adaptive policy, regulatory units, allocation method impact, stock distribution changes, climate change adaptation.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Management regimes of marine resources that rely on spatial boundaries might be poorly adapted to climate change shifts in species distributions. Transitioning to dynamic rules in spatial management has been suggested as a solution for mismatches between species distributions and the spatial boundaries... In terms of the distribution of allocation, we find that, while seasonal differences exist, the biggest differences in the proportion of the stock spatial distribution attributed to each state come from the method for designing regulatory units. Here, we show that the method used to define allocation units can have meaningful impacts on resulting adaptive policy.
Drivers of Risk: Climate change; shifting species distributions; mismatch between distributions and spatial management boundaries; poorly defined spatial boundaries; shared fish stocks crossing management jurisdictions; quota allocation; state-level impact variation.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "Management regimes of marine resources that rely on spatial boundaries might be poorly adapted to climate change shifts in species distributions...This is of specific concern for the management of fish stocks that cross management jurisdictions, known as shared stocks...in many cases spatial boundaries are not clearly drawn...the distribution of all three stocks has changed relative to the years used to determine the current quota allocation across states...As climate change-driven conflicts in fishing resource allocation are expected to increase and deepen around the world..."
Category-sourcematerial: Management regimes of marine resources that rely on spatial boundaries might be poorly adapted to climate change shifts in species distributions. This is of specific concern for the management of fish stocks that cross management jurisdictions, known as shared stocks... Here, we use black sea bass (Centropristis striata), summer flounder (Paralichthys dentatus) and scup (Stenotomus chrysops) as case studies to explore different approaches to designing spatial regulatory units to facilitate the adaptation of fisheries management to shifting distributions of shared stocks.
Approach-sourcematerial: Here, we use black sea bass (Centropristis striata), summer flounder (Paralichthys dentatus) and scup (Stenotomus chrysops) as case studies to explore different approaches to designing spatial regulatory units to facilitate the adaptation of fisheries management to shifting distributions of shared stocks. First, we determine the yearly distribution of each stock within the United States Exclusive Economic Zone from 1951 to 2019 during Fall and Spring sampling seasons. Second, we explore two approaches for drawing regulatory units based on state waters and historical landings. Finally, we estimate each state's proportion of the stock's distribution and compare historical and recent values.
Methodology: The yearly distributions of black sea bass, summer flounder, and scup within the U.S. EEZ were determined from 1951-2019 using Fall and Spring sampling data; two approaches were explored for drawing regulatory units based on state waters and historical landings; and each state's proportion of stock distribution was estimated and compared historically and recently.
Methodology-sourcematerial: First, we determine the yearly distribution of each stock within the United States Exclusive Economic Zone from 1951 to 2019 during Fall and Spring sampling seasons. Second, we explore two approaches for drawing regulatory units based on state waters and historical landings. Finally, we estimate each state's proportion of the stock's distribution and compare historical and recent values.
Highlights: Climate change is shifting the distribution of black sea bass, summer flounder, and scup stocks in the US Exclusive Economic Zone, benefiting central-northern states while disadvantaging southernmost states; the method used to define regulatory units significantly impacts the resulting stock allocation among states, underscoring the importance of data-driven, transparent approaches for adaptive fisheries management.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "We show that the distribution of all three stocks has changed relative to the years used to determine the current quota allocation across states, with an overall gain for central-northern states at the expense of the southernmost states." "the biggest differences in the proportion of the stock spatial distribution attributed to each state come from the method for designing regulatory units." "the method used to define allocation units can have meaningful impacts on resulting adaptive policy." "we provide a replicable approach to make an informed and transparent choice to support data-driven decision-making."
More Notes: Climate change is shifting the distribution of shared fish stocks (black sea bass, summer flounder, and scup) in the US Exclusive Economic Zone, impacting state allocations, and the method used to define regulatory units significantly affects the resulting adaptive policy, highlighting the need for data-driven decision-making to address increasing resource allocation conflicts.
More Notes-sourcematerial: "Management regimes of marine resources that rely on spatial boundaries might be poorly adapted to climate change shifts in species distributions...We use black sea bass (Centropristis striata), summer flounder (Paralichthys dentatus) and scup (Stenotomus chrysops) as case studies...We show that the distribution of all three stocks has changed relative to the years used to determine the current quota allocation across states, with an overall gain for central-northern states at the expense of the southernmost states...the biggest differences in the proportion of the stock spatial distribution attributed to each state come from the method for designing regulatory units...the method used to define allocation units can have meaningful impacts on resulting adaptive policy...As climate change-driven conflicts in fishing resource allocation are expected to increase and deepen around the world, we provide a replicable approach to make an informed and transparent choice to support data-driven decision-making."
Hazard definition-sourcematerial: "Management regimes of marine resources that rely on spatial boundaries might be poorly adapted to climate change shifts in species distributions." and "As climate change-driven conflicts in fishing resource allocation are expected to increase and deepen around the world..."

Finally, we estimate each state's proportion of the stock's distribution and compare historical and recent values...with an overall gain for central-northern states at the expense of the southernmost states.

It explores designing spatial regulatory units to facilitate fisheries management adaptation to shifting distributions of shared stocks, using black sea bass, summer flounder, and scup as case studies. The method for defining allocation units can significantly impact adaptive policy.

Paper Title: Vulnerability to climate change of managed stocks in the California Current large marine ecosystem
Outcome(s) of Interest: Vulnerability assessment, species response, California Current Ecosystem, climate change impact, fisheries sustainability.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Overall, two-thirds of the species were judged to have Moderate or greater vulnerability to climate change, and only one species was anticipated to have a positive response. As climate-related changes intensify, this work provides key information for both scientists and managers as they address the long-term sustainability of fisheries in the region.
Drivers of Risk: Complex life histories, habitat specialization (hypoxia), long lifespan/low growth, commercial value + non-climate stressors.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Species classified as Highly or Very Highly vulnerable share one or more characteristics including: 1) having complex life histories that utilize a wide range of freshwater and marine habitats; 2) having habitat specialization, particularly for areas that are likely to experience increased hypoxia; 3) having long lifespans and low population growth rates; and/or 4) being of high commercial value combined with impacts from non-climate stressors such as anthropogenic habitat degradation.
Category-sourcematerial: This study applies the NOAA (National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration) Fisheries Climate Vulnerability Assessment method to 64 federally-managed species in the California Current Large Marine Ecosystem to assess their vulnerability to climate change... As climate-related changes intensify, this work provides key information for both scientists and managers as they address the long-term sustainability of fisheries in the region.
Approach-sourcematerial: This study applies the NOAA (National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration) Fisheries Climate Vulnerability Assessment method to 64 federally-managed species in the California Current Large Marine Ecosystem to assess their vulnerability to climate change, where vulnerability is a function of a species’ exposure to environmental change and its biological sensitivity to a set of environmental conditions, which includes components of its resiliency and adaptive capacity to respond to these new conditions.
Methodology: The NOAA Fisheries Climate Vulnerability Assessment method was applied to 64 federally-managed species in the California Current Large Marine Ecosystem to evaluate their climate change vulnerability, considering species’ exposure to environmental changes and their biological sensitivity, including resiliency and adaptive capacity.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Methods: This study applies the NOAA (National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration) Fisheries Climate Vulnerability Assessment method to 64 federally-managed species in the California Current Large Marine Ecosystem to assess their vulnerability to climate change, where vulnerability is a function of a species’ exposure to environmental change and its biological sensitivity to a set of environmental conditions, which includes components of its resiliency and adaptive capacity to respond to these new conditions.
Highlights: Vulnerability assessments of 64 managed species in the California Current found two-thirds have moderate or greater vulnerability to climate change, with highly vulnerable species sharing characteristics like complex life histories, habitat specialization, long lifespans, low growth rates, and high commercial value combined with non-climate stressors; less vulnerable species are habitat generalists, occupy deep-water, or are highly mobile.

Species with Low or Moderate vulnerability are either habitat generalists, occupy deep-water habitats or are highly mobile and likely to shift their ranges.

Paper Title: Predicting Short-Term Deformation in the Central Valley Using Machine Learning
Outcome(s) of Interest: Reduced aquifer storage, damaged infrastructure, economic loss (crop production), groundwater withdrawal policy guidance, hazard mitigation.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Land subsidence caused by excessive groundwater pumping in Central Valley, California, is a major issue that has several negative impacts such as reduced aquifer storage and damaged infrastructures which, in turn, produce an economic loss due to the high reliance on crop production... The model results can directly aid policy makers in determining the appropriate rate of groundwater withdrawal while maintaining the safety and well-being of the population as well as the aquifers’ integrity... the potential for incorporation in hazard mitigation models.
Drivers of Risk: Excessive groundwater pumping, high reliance on crop production.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Land subsidence caused by excessive groundwater pumping in Central Valley, California, is a major issue that has several negative impacts such as reduced aquifer storage and damaged infrastructures which, in turn, produce an economic loss due to the high reliance on crop production.
Category-sourcematerial: Land subsidence caused by excessive groundwater pumping in Central Valley, California, is a major issue that has several negative impacts such as reduced aquifer storage ... Water storage changes are also directly analyzed by the use of data from the Gravity Recovery and Climate Experiment (GRACE) twin satellites and the Global Land Data Assimilation System (GLDAS). The model results can directly aid policy makers in determining the appropriate rate of groundwater withdrawal while maintaining the safety and well-being of the population as well as the aquifers’ integrity.
Approach-sourcematerial: The first goal is realized through the use of Principal Component Analysis (PCA) applied to a series of Interferomtric Synthetic Aperture Radar (InSAR) images... Water storage changes are also directly analyzed by the use of data from the Gravity Recovery and Climate Experiment (GRACE) twin satellites and the Global Land Data Assimilation System (GLDAS). The second goal is accomplished by building a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) model to predict short-term deformation after developing an InSAR time series using LiCSBAS, an open-source InSAR time series package.
Methodology: Deformation is characterized using Principal Component Analysis (PCA) on Interferometric Synthetic Aperture Radar (InSAR) images, along with water storage change analysis using data from the Gravity Recovery and Climate Experiment (GRACE) and the Global Land Data Assimilation System (GLDAS); deformation is predicted using a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) model built upon an InSAR time series developed with LiCSBAS.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "The first goal is realized through the use of Principal Component Analysis (PCA) applied to a series of Interferomtric Synthetic Aperture Radar (InSAR) images that produces eigenimages displaying the key characteristics of the subsidence. Water storage changes are also directly analyzed by the use of data from the Gravity Recovery and Climate Experiment (GRACE) twin satellites and the Global Land Data Assimilation System (GLDAS). The second goal is accomplished by building a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) model to predict short-term deformation after developing an InSAR time series using LiCSBAS, an open-source InSAR time series package."
Highlights: Characterizes and predicts land subsidence in California's Central Valley using InSAR, GRACE, GLDAS, PCA, and LSTM models, demonstrating the potential of machine learning to inform groundwater management and hazard mitigation policies.

Approach-sourcematerial: Using a global panel of daily precipitation and temperature from 1979 to 2016, we provide evidence that 4–5 week dry spells that occur during the hottest part of the year depress growth in aggregate crop production. [...] Excludes Sub-Saharan Africa due to concerns over data coverage and reliability; analysis accounts for prevailing climate regimes, trends, and droughts when isolating effects of weather events. [...] A series of fixed effects models are used to relate growth in the FAO CPI to dry spell exposure. [...] The analysis uses an additively separable linear model with controls for covariates including temperature and precipitation, and applies country and year fixed effects. [...] Inference uses clustered standard errors that allow for arbitrary autocorrelation and heteroskedasticity at the country level. [...] To address this concern, in robustness checks we employ sequential g-estimation. [...] Estimates based on sequential g-estimation, considering various covariates and robustness checks; analysis of heterogeneous effects and controlled direct impact of dry spells.
Methodology: Panel data analysis (1979-2016) relates aggregate crop production (FAO CPI) to country-specific dry spell exposure anomalies (daily precipitation data gridded to 0.5-degree latitude), considering dry spell duration (7-35 days), heat waves (daily temperature maxima), drought (SPEI), aridity, and irrigation; spatial weighting by cropland area; fixed effects models and sequential g-estimation with bootstrapping; robustness checks include alternative model specifications and weighting schemes; Sub-Saharan Africa excluded due to data concerns.
Methodology-sourcematerial: The analysis uses a global panel of daily precipitation and temperature data from 1979 to 2016 (Page 2). A panel dataset relates aggregate crop production in a country and year to exposure to dry spells and related environmental stressors, isolating country-specific dry spell exposure anomalies (Page 5). A dry spell variable counts abnormally dry days preceded by at least j-1 others (j=7, 14, 21, 28, 35 days), with data spanning from 1979 to 2016, gridded to 0.5-degree latitude (Page 6). Heat wave days are counted using daily temperature maxima (Page 8). The analysis employs spatially weighted averages based on cropland area (Page 7), using fixed effects models with country and year effects (Page 12). Sequential g-estimation is used alongside bootstrapping to account for potential clustering at the panel unit level (Page 15). Alternative model specifications and weighting schemes are explored for robustness (Page 16). Sub-Saharan Africa is excluded due to data coverage and reliability concerns (Page 10).
Highlights: Dry spells significantly depress crop production growth, compounded by heat waves, drought, or aridity, with effects less severe in irrigated areas.

Hazard definition-sourcematerial:
Source material here. More frequent and punctuated severe events such as dry spells are among the more salient manifestations of climate change. Because dry spells often co-occur with drought and heat waves, understanding the effects of dry spells and heat waves requires a multi-timescale and multi-stressor perspective. Using a global panel of daily precipitation and temperature from 1979 to 2016, we provide evidence that 4-5 week dry spells that occur during the hottest part of the year depress growth in crop production. The effects of these extreme events are modified by existing drought, aridity, and by heat waves. After accounting for productivity and crop choice, losses in yields from dry spells are concentrated in areas that are not equipped for irrigation, suggesting a possible role of adaptation in dampening the effects of dry spells.
...
irrigation, we partition cells based on whether the fraction of cropland within a cell that is equipped for irrigation is above or below the median fraction among all cells with any area equipped for irrigation. The former captures exposure in more irrigated areas, while the latter captures exposure in less irrigated areas. We detail data sources for drought, aridity, and irrigation along with those for other covariates next.
To complete our dataset, we assemble data on longer-run water stress, average temperature, annual precipitation, aridity, and irrigation. We include drought using the Standardized Precipitation-Evaporation Index (SPEI; Vicente-Serrano et al., 2010), which reflects a locally-standardized difference between monthly precipitation and potential evapotranspiration, averaged across some number of months. For our primary results, we use a 12-month SPEI which averages that standardized difference across all months within the year. In supplementary analyses, we also consider the SPEI averaged over only the months prior to the start of the hottest 90 days in a given location, which we term start-of-season drought. Average temperature and total precipitation are derived from the same Climate Prediction Center data underlying our dry spell and heat wave variables. We use the FAO’s aridity index (FAO, 2009), provided on a 10 arc-minute grid. That source defines aridity as the ratio of precipitation and average annual potential evapotranspiration calculated using a version of the Penman-Monteith Equation (Allen, 1998), with both quantities derived from the Climate Research Unit’s CL 2.0 Global Climate Dataset. Irrigation data from FAO’s AQUASTAT database provides an estimated fraction of the area irrigated per pixel (circa 2005) on a gridded global raster at 5 arc-minute resolution. In analyses where one of these variables is required at the national scale, we construct national weighted exposure using the same spatial weighting scheme as for the dry spell variable.

Impact persistence-sourcematerial: Page 12: Any effects of dry spells on contemporaneous growth in the FAO CPI may reflect both transitory impacts on levels of crop production in a single year and effects that may persist across more than a single year (see, e.g., Dell et al., 2012). To help distinguish between these two possibilities, we estimate distributed lag variants of equation (1) (see Appendix). Evidence from those models (Appendix Table A2) suggest that the direct effects we estimate are transitory, though it remains possible that dry spells may exert more persistent influence via their exacerbation of drought.

The analysis employs national and sub-national data to evaluate how existing aridity, drought conditions, and irrigation infrastructure alter the influence of dry spells, emphasizing a nuanced approach to understanding and potentially facilitating adaptive strategies.

Paper Title: Reconciling disagreement on global river flood changes in a warming climate
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased risk of rainstorm-induced floods; offset by decreased snow-related floods; unapparent change in total global floods.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "Stratifying by flood type, we show a positive response of rainstorm-induced floods to extreme precipitation increases. However, this response is almost entirely offset by concurrent decreases in snow-related floods, leading to an overall unapparent change in total global floods in both historical observations and future climate projections. Our findings highlight an increasing rainstorm-induced flood risk under warming..."
Drivers of Risk: Intensified hydrological cycle, global warming, extreme precipitation, river floods, generating mechanisms, rainstorm-induced floods, snow-related floods, flood risk

[...] Our findings highlight an increasing rainstorm-induced flood risk under warming [...]
Approach-sourcematerial: Here we demonstrate that previously reported unapparent or even negative responses of river flood discharge (defined as annual maximum discharge) to extreme precipitation increases are largely caused by mixing the signals of floods with different generating mechanisms. Stratifying by flood type, we show a positive response of rainstorm-induced floods to extreme precipitation increases. However, this response is almost entirely offset by concurrent decreases in snow-related floods, leading to an overall unapparent change in total global floods in both historical observations and future climate projections.
Methodology: The study separates flood events by generating mechanism (rainstorm vs. snow-related) to reveal contrasting responses to extreme precipitation increases, analyzing both historical observations and future climate projections.

Climate features-sourcematerial: An intensified hydrological cycle with global warming is expected to increase the intensity and frequency of extreme precipitation events. However, whether and to what extent the enhanced extreme precipitation translates into changes in river floods remains controversial. Here we demonstrate that previously reported unapparent or even negative responses of river flood discharge (defined as annual maximum discharge) to extreme precipitation increases are largely caused by mixing the signals of floods with different generating mechanisms. Stratifying by flood type, we show a positive response of rainstorm-induced floods to extreme precipitation increases. However, this response is almost entirely offset by concurrent decreases in snow-related floods, leading to an overall unapparent change in total global floods in both historical observations and future climate projections. Our findings highlight an increasing rainstorm-induced flood risk under warming and the importance of distinguishing flood-generating mechanisms in assessing flood changes and associated social-economic and environmental risks.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is not explicitly considered in this abstract. The focus is on understanding and attributing changes in flood types (rainstorm-induced vs. snow-related) in response to extreme precipitation, highlighting the importance of distinguishing flood-generating mechanisms when assessing risks.
Adaptation considered-sourcematerial: "Our findings highlight an increasing rainstorm-induced flood risk under warming and the importance of distinguishing flood-generating mechanisms in assessing flood changes and associated social-economic and environmental risks."

Paper Title: Identifying leverage points in climate change migration systems through expert mental models
Outcome(s) of Interest: Resiliency; climate (im)mobility; migration changes.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Our findings identify physical infrastructure, social services, social capital, and political stability as places to intervene to increase resiliency in drought, flooding, and erosion climate migration systems. [...] We recommend selecting target factors with direct relationships to other highly influential factors (livelihoods, food security, and financial capital) to elicit the most positive cascading effects through the whole system, leading to changes in migration.
Drivers of Risk: Demographic factors, economic factors, environmental factors (sea level rise, drought, flooding, erosion), political factors, social factors.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "In this study, we survey thirty-two experts in the field of climate migration to explore how demographic, economic, environmental, political, and social factors interact to lead to climate (im)mobility and how these interactions change within sea level rise, drought, flooding, and erosion contexts."
Category-sourcematerial: Our findings identify physical infrastructure, social services, social capital, and political stability as places to intervene to increase resiliency in drought, flooding, and erosion climate migration systems...We also highlight the sea level rise climate migration system as highly complex in comparison to the other contexts examined and the need for multi-factored interventions in this context to create more resilient migration systems.
Approach-sourcematerial: We use system mapping and network analysis to determine which factors should be targeted as leverage points for policy makers and their resulting effects within each hazard context.
Methodology: The study employs surveys of thirty-two experts, system mapping, and network analysis to understand interactions of demographic, economic, environmental, political, and social factors affecting climate mobility within contexts like sea level rise, drought, flooding, and erosion, identifying leverage points for policy intervention.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "In this study, we survey thirty-two experts in the field of climate migration to explore how demographic, economic, environmental, political, and social factors interact to lead to climate (im)mobility and how these interactions change within sea level rise, drought, flooding, and erosion contexts. We use system mapping and network analysis to determine which factors should be targeted as leverage points for policy makers and their resulting effects within each hazard context."
Highlights: Identifies infrastructure, social services, social capital, and political stability as key intervention points to build resilience in drought, flooding, and erosion-related climate migration, while recommending targeting factors with direct links to livelihoods, food security, and financial capital for maximum impact; highlights the complexity of sea level rise-driven migration, requiring multi-faceted interventions.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Our findings identify physical infrastructure, social services, social capital, and political stability as places to intervene to increase resiliency in drought, flooding, and erosion climate migration systems. Using hazard context and community consultation, we recommend selecting target factors with direct relationships to other highly influential factors (livelihoods, food security, and financial capital) to elicit the most positive cascading effects through the whole system, leading to changes in migration. We also highlight the sea level rise climate migration system as highly complex in comparison to the other contexts examined and the need for multi-factored interventions in this context to create more resilient migration systems.
More Notes: Experts identified key leverage points (physical infrastructure, social services, social capital, political stability) to increase resilience in drought, flooding, and erosion contexts, recommending interventions targeting livelihoods, food security, and financial capital for cascading positive effects on migration, while highlighting the complexity of sea level rise and the need for multi-factored interventions.

Paper Title: An increase in marine heatwaves without significant changes in surface ocean temperature variability
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased MHW frequency; ecological and economic consequences.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Marine heatwaves (MHWs)—extremely warm, persistent sea surface temperature (SST) anomalies causing substantial ecological and economic consequences—have increased worldwide in recent decades.
Drivers of Risk: Climate change-driven mean SST increases; Nonlinear warming trends; Regional mean climate trends outweigh variability changes; Increased probability of exceeding MHW thresholds.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Marine heatwaves (MHWs) have increased worldwide...increases in global temperatures suggest that climate change impacted MHW occurrences...the long-term SST warming trend was not constant but instead had more rapid warming in recent decades...this nonlinear trend can...appear to increase SST variance and hence MHW frequency...most MHW increases resulted from regional mean climate trends that alone increased the probability of SSTs exceeding a MHW threshold...may not always reflect underlying changes in variability.
Category-sourcematerial: Marine heatwaves (MHWs)—extremely warm, persistent sea surface temperature (SST) anomalies causing substantial ecological and economic consequences—have increased worldwide in recent decades.
Approach-sourcematerial: Using a Linear Inverse Model to separate climate change contributions to SST means and internal variability, both in observations and CMIP6 historical simulations, we find that most MHW increases resulted from regional mean climate trends that alone increased the probability of SSTs exceeding a MHW threshold.
Methodology: A Linear Inverse Model was used to separate climate change contributions to sea surface temperature (SST) means and internal variability, analyzing both observations and CMIP6 historical simulations to determine the drivers of marine heatwave (MHW) increases.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Using a Linear Inverse Model to separate climate change contributions to SST means and internal variability, both in observations and CMIP6 historical simulations...
Highlights: Recent increases in marine heatwaves (MHWs) are primarily driven by regional mean climate trends increasing the likelihood of exceeding MHW temperature thresholds, rather than solely by changes in internal climate variability.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Marine heatwaves (MHWs)…have increased worldwide in recent decades... most MHW increases resulted from regional mean climate trends that alone increased the probability of SSTs exceeding a MHW threshold... Our results suggest the need to carefully attribute global warming-induced changes in climate extremes, which may not always reflect underlying changes in variability.

Concurrent increases in global temperatures suggest that climate change impacted MHW occurrences, beyond random changes arising from natural internal variability. Moreover, the long-term SST warming trend was not constant but instead had more rapid warming in recent decades. Here we show that this nonlinear trend can—on its own—appear to increase SST variance and hence MHW frequency. Using a Linear Inverse Model to separate climate change contributions to SST means and internal variability, both in observations and CMIP6 historical simulations, we find that most MHW increases resulted from regional mean climate trends that alone increased the probability of SSTs exceeding a MHW threshold. Approach-sourcematerial: Using energy audits data from Alaska Native Tribal Health Consortium (ANTHC), we investigate the annual energy consumption patterns for water treatment and distribution in 78 rural Alaska communities (average population < 500 people) along with seasonal, regional, and population impacts, and water treatment/distribution system types.
Methodology: Energy audits data from the Alaska Native Tribal Health Consortium (ANTHC) were used to investigate annual energy consumption patterns for water treatment and distribution in 78 rural Alaskan communities (average population < 500).
Methodology-sourcematerial: Using energy audits data from Alaska Native Tribal Health Consortium (ANTHC), we investigate the annual energy consumption patterns for water treatment and distribution in 78 rural Alaska communities (average population < 500 people)...
Highlights: Per capita energy consumption for water treatment and distribution in rural Alaska is significantly higher than the national average, with Interior Alaska showing the highest regional consumption, winter months exhibiting peak usage, and piped circulating systems consuming the most energy; space heating and electrical motors are the primary energy consumers at water treatment plants.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Regional trends of per capita annual energy consumption are as follows: Interior > Northern > Southwest > Gulf coast > Southeast regions of Alaska. Our results indicate that the per capita energy consumption is highest during the winter and lowest during the summer. Generally, the per capita energy consumption decreases with an increasing population. The variation of per capita energy consumption based on water distribution types shows that piped circulating systems consume the most energy, followed by washeteria, piped pressure, and closed haul. At the water treatment plant, space heating and electrical motors have the highest per capita energy consumption, followed by domestic hot water, tank heating, and lighting. The findings in this work suggest that per capita energy consumption (kWh/p) for water treatment and distribution in rural Alaska is about 12-26 times higher than the national average and about two orders of magnitude higher economic costs for the same.
More Notes: Energy consumption for water treatment and distribution in rural Alaska is significantly higher (12-26 times the national average) and costly than the national average, varying by region, season, population, and system type, with piped circulating systems consuming the most energy.
