Paper Title: Forage grass growth under future climate change scenarios affects fermentation and ruminant efficiency
Outcome(s) of Interest: Decreased digestibility, decreased methane production, linked to drought or flood.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: All varieties consistently showed decreased digestibility linked to decreased methane production as a result of drought or an acute flood treatment.
Drivers of Risk: Climate change, temperature increase, elevated CO2, drought, flooding, heat shock, acute stress, altered weather patterns, decreased digestibility, reduced methane production.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial:
"Models of near future climate change (2050) predict increases in temperature, CO2, precipitation and altered weather systems which will produce stress responses in field crops." (Page 2)

"Plants respond to abiotic and biotic stresses by physiological, biochemical, metabolic and molecular mechanisms... elevated levels of CO2 have been shown to improve the rate of photosynthesis in plants and alleviate the effects of drought stress by conserving water, increasing carbon fixation... high temperatures have been shown to inhibit photosynthesis." (Page 3)

"Shocks impacting the outcomes include flood, drought, and heat shock scenarios in 2050 compared to the baseline condition in 2020." (Page 6)

"Climate scenarios such as 2050, drought, flood, and heat shock impacting plant physiology and biochemical compounds." (Page 9)

"Chlorophyll levels were highest in the control 2020 scenario and the lowest in grasses subjected to heat shock and flood climate scenarios, indicating that photosynthesis was affected by both these treatments." (Page 10)

"Notably acute flood and drought caused decreased digestibility in those forages. As these conditions are predicted to increase in frequency over the next decades, unless addressed this will have a limiting effect on production efficiency." (Page 12)
Category-sourcematerial:
"The paper evaluates how future climate change scenarios affect forage grass growth, fermentation, and ruminant efficiency." (Page 2)

"The aim of this work was therefore to determine how responses to the growth environment, including “stress memory” may affect the subsequent forage degradation in the rumen and, therefore, to understand how ruminant feeding strategies might be affected by climate change." (Page 3)

"Effect of growth conditions on physiological characteristics of grass varieties at harvest. Overall, the grass varieties were clearly affected by the climate scenarios imposed during growth, indicating that the extent of the stress imposed was sufficient to elicit a physiological response." (Page 5)

"It is suggested that the plant response to climate has a consequence for the activity of the rumen microbiota; altering the nutrient provision to the colonising microbiota affects colonisation community profiles and in consequence, forage degradation parameters." (Page 10)

"Here we have investigated the former, to explore whether forage growth under an altered climate would produce legacy effects in forage that would impact on rumen fermentation when ingested." (Page 12)

"Notably acute flood and drought caused decreased digestibility in those forages. As these conditions are predicted to increase in frequency over the next decades, unless addressed this will have a limiting effect on production efficiency." (Page 12)
Approach-sourcematerial: Page 5: Data analysis includes using 2-way ANOVA and general linear models for gas production, with additional statistical software and packages to analyze and visualize results.; Page 6: The study uses a Discriminant Functional Analysis (DFA) of Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy and in vitro rumen fermentation experiments for plant chemistry analysis under different climate scenarios.; Page 8: Random forest analysis correctly classified at least 66% of samples in each climate scenario, despite only partial separation on the MDS plot.
Methodology: Ten forage grass varieties were grown for 3 months under five climate scenarios (current, future, future + flooding, drought, or heat shock), replicated three times; plant biochemical changes and in vitro rumen fermentation gas production were measured and analyzed using 2-way ANOVA, general linear models, Tukey adjustment, Discriminant Functional Analysis (DFA) of Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, and random forest classification of VOC profiles.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Page 2: "This “stress memory” effect was investigated by screening ten forage grass varieties in five differing climate scenarios, including current climate (2020), future climate (2050), or future climate plus flooding, drought or heat shock."; Page 3: "Ten different current commercial varieties of forage grasses (Table 1) were grown for 3 months in five different climate scenarios, replicated three times at 1-week intervals."; Page 4: "The analysis examines the effects of different climate scenarios on plant growth using scenarios such as increased CO2 levels, flooding, drought, and heat shock, followed by measurements of plant biochemical changes and gas production from in vitro fermentation."; Page 5: "Data analysis was conducted in R version 3.6.0 using RStudio... Paper Title: The impact of heat on kidney stone presentations in South Carolina under two climate change scenarios
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased kidney stone presentations and associated healthcare costs.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: The estimated total statewide kidney stone presentations attributable to heat are projected to increase by 2.2% in RCP 4.5 and 3.9% in RCP 8.5 by 2085–89 (vs. 2010–2014), with an associated total excess cost of ~ $57 million and ~ $99 million, respectively.
Drivers of Risk: Higher temperatures; increased insensible water loss; more concentrated urine; altered urinary flow; climate change (RCP 4.5 & RCP 8.5).
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: The risk of kidney stone presentations increases after hot days, likely due to greater insensible water losses resulting in more concentrated urine and altered urinary flow. It is thus expected that higher temperatures from climate change will increase the global prevalence of kidney stones if no adaptation measures are put in place. Two climate change models were considered—one assuming aggressive reduction in greenhouse gas emissions (RCP 4.5) and one representing uninibited greenhouse gas emissions (RCP 8.5).
Category: Health > Heat and Cold (Cardiovascular, Respiratory, etc.)
Category-sourcematerial: The risk of kidney stone presentations increases after hot days...quantify the impact of heat on kidney stone presentations through 2089...estimated total statewide kidney stone presentations attributable to heat are projected to increase...
Approach-sourcematerial: We used a time series analysis of historical kidney stone presentations (1997–2014) and distributed lag non-linear models to estimate the temperature dependence of kidney stone presentations, and then quantified the projected impact of climate change on future heat-related kidney stone presentations using daily projections of wet-bulb temperatures to 2089, assuming no adaptation or demographic changes.
Methodology: Used time series analysis of historical kidney stone presentations (1997–2014) and distributed lag non-linear models to estimate the temperature dependence of kidney stone presentations, then projected the impact of climate change on future heat-related kidney stone presentations using daily projections of wet-bulb temperatures to 2089, considering RCP 4.5 and RCP 8.5 scenarios, and assuming no adaptation or demographic changes.
Methodology-sourcematerial: We used a time series analysis of historical kidney stone presentations (1997–2014) and distributed lag non-linear models to estimate the temperature dependence of kidney stone presentations, and then quantified the projected impact of climate change on future heat-related kidney stone presentations using daily projections of wet-bulb temperatures to 2089, assuming no adaptation or demographic changes. Two climate change models were considered—one assuming aggressive reduction in greenhouse gas emissions (RCP 4.5) and one representing uninibited greenhouse gas emissions (RCP 8.5).
Highlights: Climate change is projected to increase heat-related kidney stone presentations in South Carolina by 2.2% (RCP 4.5) and 3.9% (RCP 8.5) by 2085-89, resulting in ~$57 million and ~$99 million in excess costs, respectively.

Paper Title: Climate gentrification in Miami: A real climate change-minded investment practice?
Outcome(s) of Interest: Climate gentrification drivers; economic investment; flood insurance focus; developer utilization; policy implications; knowledge gap.

Paper Title: Climate change contributions to future atmospheric river flood damages in the western United States
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased AR-related flood damages in the western US; $2.3 billion by 2090s (RCP4.5); $3.2 billion by 2090s (RCP8.5); County-level risk identification.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "The models predict that annual expected AR-related flood damages in the western United States could increase from $1 billion in the historical period to $2.3 billion in the 2090s under the RCP4.5 scenario or to $3.2 billion under the RCP8.5 scenario. County-level projections were developed to identify counties at greatest risk, allowing policymakers to target efforts to increase resilience to climate change."
Drivers of Risk: AR intensity increase (climate change), flood damages exponential with AR intensity, emissions scenarios (RCP4.5, RCP8.5).
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Atmospheric rivers (ARs) generate most of the economic losses associated with flooding in the western United States and are projected to increase in intensity with climate change. This is of concern as flood damages have been shown to increase exponentially with AR intensity. [...] Damage functions were applied to 14 CMIP5 global climate models under the RCP4.5 “intermediate emissions” and RCP8.5 “high emissions” scenarios...
Category-sourcematerial: Atmospheric rivers (ARs) generate most of the economic losses associated with flooding in the western United States and are projected to increase in intensity with climate change.
Approach-sourcematerial: To assess how AR-related flood damages are likely to respond to climate change, we constructed county-level damage models for the western 11 conterminous states using 40 years of flood insurance data linked to characteristics of ARs at landfall.
Methodology: County-level damage models were constructed for the western U.S. using 40 years of flood insurance data linked to atmospheric river characteristics; these models were then applied to 14 CMIP5 global climate models under RCP4.5 and RCP8.5 scenarios, assuming constant spatial patterns of exposure, vulnerability, and flood protection.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "we constructed county-level damage models for the western 11 conterminous states using 40 years of flood insurance data linked to characteristics of ARs at landfall. Damage functions were applied to 14 CMIP5 global climate models under the RCP4.5 “intermediate emissions” and RCP8.5 “high emissions” scenarios, under the assumption that spatial patterns of exposure, vulnerability, and flood protection remain constant at present day levels."
Highlights: AR-related flood damages in the western US are projected to increase significantly by the 2090s, reaching $2.3 billion under the RCP4.5 scenario and $3.2 billion under the RCP8.5 scenario, with county-level projections identifying areas at greatest risk.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "The models predict that annual expected AR-related flood damages in the western United States could increase from $1 billion in the historical period to $2.3 billion in the 2090s under the RCP4.5 scenario or to $3.2 billion under the RCP8.5 scenario. County-level projections were developed to identify counties at greatest risk, allowing policymakers to target efforts to increase resilience to climate change."
More Notes: AR-related flood damages in the western US are projected to increase significantly by the 2090s, potentially reaching $2.3 billion under an intermediate emissions scenario and $3.2 billion under a high emissions scenario, based on damage models applied to climate model projections.

Paper Title: High-resolution surface water dynamics in Earth’s small and medium-sized reservoirs
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased vulnerability to climate extremes (especially for communities reliant on small reservoirs); geographically uneven distribution of long-term changes; potential for monitoring water scarcity, food insecurity, and facilitating transboundary cooperation; improved access to water resource information globally.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Our analysis shows that for small reservoirs, the inter-annual and intra-annual variability is much higher than for medium-sized reservoirs worldwide. This implies that the communities reliant on small reservoirs are more vulnerable to climate extremes, both short-term (within seasons) and longer-term (across seasons). Our findings show that the long-term inter-annual and intra-annual changes in these reservoirs are not equally distributed geographically. Through several cases, we demonstrate that this technology can help monitor water scarcity conditions and emerging food insecurity, and facilitate transboundary cooperation. It has the potential to provide operational information on conditions in ungauged or upstream riparian countries that do not share such data with neighboring countries. This may help to create a more level playing field in water resource information globally.
Drivers of Risk: Climate variability; small reservoir vulnerability; inter-annual variability; intra-annual variability; geographic distribution of changes; water scarcity; food insecurity.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "small and medium-sized reservoirs play an important role in water systems that need to cope with climate variability...the inter-annual and intra-annual variability is much higher than for medium-sized reservoirs worldwide. This implies that the communities reliant on small reservoirs are more vulnerable to climate extremes...long-term inter-annual and intra-annual changes in these reservoirs are not equally distributed geographically...demonstrate that this technology can help monitor water scarcity conditions and emerging food insecurity"
Category-sourcematerial: "Small and medium-sized reservoirs play an important role in water systems that need to cope with climate variability and various other man-made and natural challenges... Our analysis shows that for small reservoirs, the inter-annual and intra-annual variability is much higher than for medium-sized reservoirs worldwide. This implies that the communities reliant on small reservoirs are more vulnerable to climate extremes, both short-term (within seasons) and longer-term (across seasons). Our findings show that the long-term inter-annual and intra-annual changes in these reservoirs are not equally distributed geographically. Through several cases, we demonstrate that this technology can help monitor water scarcity conditions..."
Approach-sourcematerial: We use multi-annual multi-sensor satellite data, combined with cloud analytics, to monitor the state of small (10–100 ha) to medium-sized (> 100 ha, excluding 479 large ones) artificial water reservoirs globally for the first time. ... We combine the results of multiple studies to identify 71,208 small to medium-sized reservoirs, followed by reconstructing surface water area changes from satellite data using a novel method introduced in this study. ... The dataset is validated using 768 daily in-situ water level and storage measurements ... Our analysis shows that for small reservoirs, the inter-annual and intra-annual variability is much higher than for medium-sized reservoirs worldwide.
Methodology: Multi-annual, multi-sensor satellite data combined with cloud analytics is used to monitor surface water area changes in 71,208 globally identified small to medium-sized artificial water reservoirs (10-100 ha and >100 ha, respectively) using a novel method to reconstruct these changes from satellite data; the dataset is validated using 768 daily in-situ water level and storage measurements.
Methodology-sourcematerial: We use multi-annual multi-sensor satellite data, combined with cloud analytics, to monitor the state of small (10–100 ha) to medium-sized (> 100 ha, excluding 479 large ones) artificial water reservoirs globally for the first time...We combine the results of multiple studies to identify 71,208 small to medium-sized reservoirs, followed by reconstructing surface water area changes from satellite data using a novel method introduced in this study. The dataset is validated using 768 daily in-situ water level and storage measurements (r2 > 0.7 for 67% of the reservoirs used for the validation)...
Highlights: Utilizing satellite data and cloud analytics, the study globally monitors 71,208 small to medium-sized reservoirs, showing higher variability in smaller reservoirs, impacting communities and highlighting potential for improved water scarcity and transboundary cooperation monitoring.

Our analysis shows that for small reservoirs, the inter-annual and intra-annual variability is much higher than for medium-sized reservoirs worldwide. This implies that the communities reliant on small reservoirs are more vulnerable to climate extremes... Through several cases, we demonstrate that this technology can help monitor water scarcity conditions and emerging food insecurity, and facilitate transboundary cooperation."

Paper Title: Cyclone generation Algorithm including a THERmodynamic module for Integrated National damage Assessment (CATHERINA 1.0) compatible with Coupled Model Intercomparison Project (CMIP) climate data
Outcome(s) of Interest: Cyclone damage estimation, national level impacts, economic quantification, physical risks, RCP damage projections, climate model bias correction.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "estimating cyclone-related damage at a national level is a challenge"; "damage projections in representative concentration pathways (RCPs)"; "correct biases in climate models"; "economic and financial community with reliable signals allowing for a better quantification of physical risks"; "estimate... impact on sovereign debt."
Drivers of Risk: Tropical cyclones, climate change, global climate models (CMIP), thermodynamic relationships, synthetic tracks, local climate conditions, damage estimation, bias correction.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "Tropical cyclones are responsible for a large share of global damage resulting from natural disasters...climate change...global climate models, whose outputs are available from the Coupled Model Intercomparison Project (CMIP), do not resolve tropical cyclones...statistical and thermodynamic relationships to generate synthetic tracks sensitive to local climate conditions and estimates the damage induced by tropical cyclones...The framework is designed to be compatible with the data from CMIP models...The algorithm contains a module to correct biases in climate models..."
Category-sourcematerial: Tropical cyclones are responsible for a large share of global damage resulting from natural disasters, and estimating cyclone-related damage at a national level is a challenge attracting growing interest in the context of climate change.
Approach-sourcematerial: The Cyclone generation Algorithm including a THERmodynamic module for Integrated National damage Assessment (CATHERINA), presented in this paper, couples statistical and thermodynamic relationships to generate synthetic tracks sensitive to local climate conditions and estimates the damage induced by tropical cyclones at a national level.
Methodology: A cyclone generation algorithm (CATHERINA) couples statistical and thermodynamic relationships to generate synthetic tropical cyclone tracks sensitive to local climate conditions, estimating damage at a national level, designed to be compatible with CMIP model data and correct biases using climate reanalyses.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Tropical cyclones are responsible for a large share of global damage resulting from natural disasters...The global climate models...do not resolve tropical cyclones. The Cyclone generation Algorithm including a THERmodynamic module for Integrated National damage Assessment (CATHERINA)...couples statistical and thermodynamic relationships to generate synthetic tracks sensitive to local climate conditions and estimates the damage induced by tropical cyclones at a national level. The framework is designed to be compatible with the data from CMIP models offering a reliable solution to resolve tropical cyclones in climate projections...The algorithm contains a module to correct biases in climate models based on the distributions of the climate variables in the reanalyses.
Highlights: Presents CATHERINA, an algorithm coupling statistical and thermodynamic relationships to generate synthetic tropical cyclone tracks sensitive to local climate conditions for national-level damage estimation, designed to be compatible with CMIP models and correct for biases, aiming to provide reliable long-term physical risk signals for the economic and financial community.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Tropical cyclones are responsible for a large share of global damage resulting from natural disasters, and estimating cyclone-related damage at a national level is a challenge attracting growing interest in the context of climate change... The Cyclone generation Algorithm including a THERmodynamic module for Integrated National damage Assessment (CATHERINA), presented in this paper, couples statistical and thermodynamic relationships to generate synthetic tracks sensitive to local climate conditions and estimates the damage induced by tropical cyclones at a national level... The framework is designed to be compatible with the data from CMIP models offering a reliable solution to resolve tropical cyclones in climate projections... The algorithm contains a module to correct biases in climate models based on the distributions of the climate variables in the reanalyses... This model was primary developed to provide the economic and financial community with reliable signals allowing for a better quantification of physical risks in the long term, to estimate, for example, the impact on sovereign debt.
More Notes: A model (CATHERINA) uses statistical and thermodynamic relationships to generate synthetic cyclone tracks from CMIP data, enabling damage estimation at a national level, including bias correction and RCP scenario projections, primarily for economic and financial risk assessment.

We illustrate this by producing damage projections in representative concentration pathways (RCPs) at the global level and for individual countries.

Impact interactions-sourcematerial: The abstract focuses on developing a model (CATHERINA) to generate synthetic tropical cyclone tracks and estimate damage at a national level, linking climate model outputs (CMIP) with statistical and thermodynamic relationships. It does not mention interactions between different types of impacts (e.g., how sea-level rise might exacerbate storm surge damage, or how changes in precipitation patterns might affect agriculture in conjunction with cyclone damage). The focus is on improving cyclone representation and damage estimation, rather than exploring compound or cascading effects.
Economic dynamics-sourcematerial: The abstract mentions that the CATHERINA model estimates the damage induced by tropical cyclones at a national level and is designed to be compatible with climate projection data. However, there is no specific mention of considering the dynamic nature of the economy, changes over time, or shifts in economic behavior in response to impacts.
Climate features-sourcematerial: Tropical cyclones are responsible for a large share of global damage resulting from natural disasters, and estimating cyclone-related damage at a national level is a challenge attracting growing interest in the context of climate change. The global climate models, whose outputs are available from the Coupled Model Intercomparison Project (CMIP), do not resolve tropical cyclones. The Cyclone generation Algorithm including a THERmodynamic module for Integrated National damage Assessment (CATHERINA), presented in this paper, couples statistical and thermodynamic relationships to generate synthetic tracks sensitive to local climate conditions and estimates the damage induced by tropical cyclones at a national level. The framework is designed to be compatible with the data from CMIP models offering a reliable solution to resolve tropical cyclones in climate projections. We illustrate this by producing damage projections in representative concentration pathways (RCPs) at the global level and for individual countries. The algorithm contains a module to correct biases in climate models based on the distributions of the climate variables in the reanalyses. This model was primary developed to provide the economic and financial community with reliable signals allowing for a better quantification of physical risks in the long term, to estimate, for example, the impact on sovereign debt. Adaptation considered: Adaptation consideration is implicit in the model's aim to provide reliable signals for the economic and financial community to quantify and manage physical risks, facilitating better preparedness. The tool's compatibility with climate projections and its function to correct biases suggests that it indirectly supports adaptation planning by offering insights into potential future impacts of tropical cyclones.

"The algorithm contains a module to correct biases in climate models based on the distributions of the climate variables in the reanalyses."

Paper Title: Effects of global climate change on the hydrological cycle and crop growth under heavily irrigated management – A comparison between CMIP5 and CMIP6
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased ETa during winter wheat growing season; variable ETa changes for summer maize; increased precipitation, air temperatures, and surface runoff; decreased irrigation; early emergence and maturity of crops; elevated daily total biomass; increased final yields of winter wheat and summer maize.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Results showed that during winter wheat growing season, average actual evapotranspiration (ETa) increased by 3%, 3%, 4%, and 5%, respectively, under 2041–2070 RCP4.5, 2041–2070 SSP2-4.5, 2041–2100 RCP4.5, and 2071–2100 SSP2-4.5 scenarios, compared to the historical period (1971–2000). During summer maize growing season, those changes in ETa were 4%, 2%, −0.2%, and −3%. Predicted future precipitation, air temperatures, and surface runoff could increase, while irrigation could decrease as precipitation increased. The dynamic patterns of leaf area index of winter wheat and summer maize indicated that there is a tendency for early emergence and maturity of both crops in the future, and the daily total biomass elevated with a corresponding increase in final yields. The highest increases in yields of winter wheat and summer maize were 18.9% and 16.7%.
Drivers of Risk: Climate change (GCM projections), increased ETa (winter wheat), variable ETa (summer maize), precipitation changes, temperature increases, surface runoff changes, irrigation decreases, altered crop phenology (early emergence/maturity).
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Quantifying the impact of global climate change on the water cycle and crop production is essential for water resource management and agricultural production planning. [...] Results showed that during winter wheat growing season, average actual evapotranspiration (ETa) increased by 3%, 3%, 4%, and 5% [...] During summer maize growing season, those changes in ETa were 4%, 2%, −0.2%, and −3%. Predicted future precipitation, air temperatures, and surface runoff could increase, while irrigation could decrease as precipitation increased. [...] The dynamic patterns of leaf area index of winter wheat and summer maize indicated that there is a tendency for early emergence and maturity of both crops in the future.
Category-sourcematerial: Quantifying the impact of global climate change on the water cycle and crop production is essential for water resource management and agricultural production planning...Results showed that during winter wheat growing season, average actual evapotranspiration (ETa) increased...During summer maize growing season, those changes in ETa were...Predicted future precipitation, air temperatures, and surface runoff could increase, while irrigation could decrease as precipitation increased...the daily total biomass elevated with a corresponding increase in final yields. The highest increases in yields of winter wheat and summer maize were 18.9% and 16.7%...This study provides technical and data support for...decision making in best management practices for groundwater conservation and agricultural production.
Approach-sourcematerial: "This study compared multiple GCM (General Circulation Model) projections of CMIP5 (Coupled Model Intercomparison Project 5) and CMIP6 and further used the selected GCMs to quantify the impact of future climate change on hydrology and crop production under intensive irrigation management in the North China Plain using an improved SWAT model (SWAT-MAD)."
Methodology: Multiple GCM projections from CMIP5 and CMIP6 were evaluated using Taylor's skill score to select the best-performing models, which then drove the improved SWAT-MAD model to assess climate impacts on hydrology and crop production in the North China Plain under various future scenarios.
Methodology-sourcematerial: This study compared multiple GCM (General Circulation Model) projections of CMIP5 (Coupled Model Intercomparison Project 5) and CMIP6 and further used the selected GCMs to quantify the impact of future climate change on hydrology and crop production under intensive irrigation management in the North China Plain using an improved SWAT model (SWAT-MAD). Taylor's skill score was used firstly to screen out six groups of GCMs with better simulation performance from 10 pairs of homologous GCMs of CMIP5 and CMIP6. The selected GCMs of CMIP5 and CMIP6 were further used to drive SWAT-MAD for a robust evaluation of climate change impacts.
Highlights: Study uses CMIP5/CMIP6 GCMs and an improved SWAT model (SWAT-MAD) in the North China Plain to project climate change impacts on hydrology and crop production, finding increased ETa, precipitation, air temperatures, surface runoff, earlier crop emergence/maturity, and increased yields for both winter wheat and summer maize under various scenarios.

More Notes: Climate change in the North China Plain may lead to increased evapotranspiration and altered crop growth patterns, potentially increasing yields for winter wheat and summer maize under intensive irrigation, while also impacting water resource management through changes in precipitation, runoff, and irrigation needs.
More Notes-sourcematerial: "Quantifying the impact of global climate change on the water cycle and crop production... The highest increases in yields of winter wheat and summer maize were 18.9% and 16.7%."

Impact interactions-sourcematerial: The selected GCMs of CMIP5 and CMIP6 were further used to drive SWAT-MAD for a robust evaluation of climate change impacts. Results showed that during winter wheat growing season, average actual evapotranspiration (ETa) increased by 3%, 3%, 4%, and 5%, respectively, under 2041–2070 RCP4.5, 2041–2070 SSP2-4.5, 2041–2100 RCP4.5, and 2071–2100 SSP2-4.5 scenarios, compared to the historical period (1971–2000). During summer maize growing season, those changes in ETa were 4%, 2%, −0.2%, and −3%. Predicted future precipitation, air temperatures, and surface runoff could increase, while irrigation could decrease as precipitation increased. The dynamic patterns of leaf area index of winter wheat and summer maize indicated that there is a tendency for early emergence and maturity of both crops in the future, and the daily total biomass elevated with a corresponding increase in final yields. The highest increases in yields of winter wheat and summer maize were 18.9% and 16.7%.

Climate features-sourcematerial: Quantifying the impact of global climate change on the water cycle and crop production is essential for water resource management and agricultural production planning. This study compared multiple GCM (General Circulation Model) projections of CMIP5 (Coupled Model Intercomparison Project 5) and CMIP6 and further used the selected GCMs to quantify the impact of future climate change on hydrology and crop production under intensive irrigation management in the North China Plain using an improved SWAT model (SWAT-MAD). Taylor's skill score was used firstly to screen out six groups of GCMs with better simulation performance from 10 pairs of homologous GCMs of CMIP5 and CMIP6. The selected GCMs of CMIP5 and CMIP6 were further used to drive SWAT-MAD for a robust evaluation of climate change impacts. Results showed that during winter wheat growing season, average actual evapotranspiration (ETa) increased by 3%, 3%, 4%, and 5%, respectively, under 2041–2070 RCP4.5, 2041–2070 SSP2-4.5, 2041–2100 RCP4.5, and 2071–2100 SSP2-4.5 scenarios, compared to the historical period (1971–2000). During summer maize growing season, those changes in ETa were 4%, 2%, −0.2%, and −3%. Predicted future precipitation, air temperatures, and surface runoff could increase, while irrigation could decrease as precipitation increased. The dynamic patterns of leaf area index of winter wheat and summer maize indicated that there is a tendency for early emergence and maturity of both crops in the future, and the daily total biomass elevated with a corresponding increase in final yields. The highest increases in yields of winter wheat and summer maize were 18.9% and 16.7%. The findings not only contribute to enhancing the confidence of future projections using CMIP6 but also facilitate our understanding of the relative uncertainty of GCMs. This study provides technical and data support for pre-selections of GCMs and decision making in best management practices for groundwater conservation and agricultural production. © 2022 Elsevier B.V.

Irrigation levels are also predicted to change in response to precipitation changes.

"Predicted future precipitation, air temperatures, and surface runoff could increase, while irrigation could decrease as precipitation increased." "This study provides technical and data support for pre-selections of GCMs and decision making in best management practices for groundwater conservation and agricultural production."

Paper Title: Gradient boosting and bayesian network machine learning models predict aflatoxin and fumonisin contamination of maize in Illinois – First USA case study
Outcome(s) of Interest: Mycotoxin contamination (aflatoxin and fumonisin) in Illinois corn.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Mycotoxin contamination of corn results in significant agroeconomic losses and poses serious health issues worldwide. This paper presents the first report utilizing machine learning and historical aflatoxin and fumonisin contamination levels in-order-to develop models that can confidently predict mycotoxin contamination of corn in Illinois, a major corn producing state in the USA.
Drivers of Risk: Early-year meteorological events; March aflatoxin risk index & vegetative index; June aflatoxin risk index; February/March/September precipitation; March vegetative index; June temperature range.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "Meteorological events prior to corn planting in the field have high influence on predicting aflatoxin and fumonisin contamination levels at the end of the year."; "Analyses for aflatoxin and fumonisin with GBM showed that meteorological and satellite-acquired vegetative index data during March significantly influenced grain contamination at the end of the corn growing season."; "Prediction of high aflatoxin contamination levels was linked to high aflatoxin risk index in March/June/July, high vegetative index in March and low vegetative index in July."; "Correspondingly, high levels of fumonisin contamination were linked to high precipitation levels in February/March/September and high vegetative index in March."; "During corn flowering time in June, higher temperatures range increased prediction of high levels of fumonisin contamination, while high aflatoxin contamination levels were linked to high aflatoxin risk index."
Category-sourcematerial: Mycotoxin contamination of corn results in significant agroeconomic losses and poses serious health issues worldwide. This paper presents the first report utilizing machine learning and historical aflatoxin and fumonisin contamination levels in-order-to develop models that can confidently predict mycotoxin contamination of corn in Illinois, a major corn producing state in the USA.
Approach-sourcematerial: This paper presents the first report utilizing machine learning and historical aflatoxin and fumonisin contamination levels in-order-to develop models that can confidently predict mycotoxin contamination of corn in Illinois...historical monthly meteorological data from a 14-year period combined with corresponding aflatoxin and fumonisin contamination data from the State of Illinois were used to engineer input features...in combination with gradient boosting (GBM) and bayesian network (BN) modeling.
Methodology: Machine learning (Gradient Boosting and Bayesian Network modeling) was used to predict aflatoxin and fumonisin contamination in Illinois corn, utilizing 14 years of historical monthly meteorological data and corresponding mycotoxin contamination data to engineer input features linking weather, fungal growth, and aflatoxin production.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Historical monthly meteorological data from a 14-year period combined with corresponding aflatoxin and fumonisin contamination data from the State of Illinois were used to engineer input features that link weather, fungal growth, and aflatoxin production in combination with gradient boosting (GBM) and bayesian network (BN) modeling.
Highlights: Machine learning models based on meteorological and vegetative index data predict mycotoxin contamination in Illinois corn with 94% accuracy, identifying weather patterns before planting as key risk indicators for aflatoxin and fumonisin contamination.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "This paper presents the first report utilizing machine learning and historical aflatoxin and fumonisin contamination levels in-order-to develop models that can confidently predict mycotoxin contamination of corn in Illinois... The GBM and BN models developed can predict mycotoxin contamination with overall 94% accuracy... Analyses for aflatoxin and fumonisin with GBM showed that meteorological and satellite-acquired vegetative index data during March significantly influenced grain contamination at the end of the corn growing season... Meteorological events prior to corn planting in the field have high influence on predicting aflatoxin and fumonisin contamination levels at the end of the year."
More Notes: Machine learning models using historical weather and contamination data predict mycotoxin contamination in Illinois corn with 94% accuracy, revealing that early-season meteorological events significantly influence end-of-season contamination levels, enabling informed decision-making for farmers.
More Notes-sourcematerial: Historical monthly meteorological data from a 14-year period combined with corresponding aflatoxin and fumonisin contamination data from the State of Illinois were used to engineer input features that link weather, fungal growth, and aflatoxin production in combination with gradient boosting (GBM) and bayesian network (BN) modeling. The GBM and BN models developed can predict mycotoxin contamination with overall 94% accuracy. Meteorological events prior to corn planting in the field have high influence on predicting aflatoxin and fumonisin contamination levels at the end of the year. These early-year events detected by the models can directly assist farmers and stakeholders to make informed decisions to prevent mycotoxin contamination of Illinois grown corn.

These early-year events detected by the models can directly assist farmers and stakeholders to make informed decisions to prevent mycotoxin contamination of Illinois grown corn.

Paper Title: Nighttime light data reveal lack of full recovery after hurricanes in Southern US
Outcome(s) of Interest: Long-term (3 years) reduction in nighttime light (NTL) radiance (2-14%) as proxy for economic activity and population density in areas affected by Category 4/5 hurricanes in the southern US (2014-2020); FEMA aid moderates short-term, but not long-term, recovery; age and employment more important than demographics for individual-level recovery.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "We find that after three years, affected areas demonstrate a reduction in NTL radiance levels of between 2% and 14% compared to the pre-disaster period."; "Utilizing Visible Infrared Imaging Radiometer Suite nighttime light (NTL) data as a proxy for economic activity and population density..."; "We find counties demonstrating smaller reductions in NTL radiance levels in the months following the hurricane are buoyed by the amount of FEMA aid received, but that this aid does not foster a longer-term return to normal radiance levels."; "Investigating areas receiving FEMA aid at the household and individual level, we find age and employment are more important than other demographic factors in determining hurricane recovery over time."
Drivers of Risk: Hurricane category, FEMA aid (short-term vs long-term), age, employment.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "...hurricanes of category 4 or 5...affected areas demonstrate a reduction in NTL radiance levels of between 2% and 14%...counties demonstrating smaller reductions in NTL radiance levels in the months following the hurricane are buoyed by the amount of FEMA aid received, but that this aid does not foster a longer-term return to normal radiance levels...age and employment are more important than other demographic factors in determining hurricane recovery over time."
Category-sourcematerial: As the climate warms, many areas of the world are experiencing more frequent and extreme weather events. Hurricanes carry some of the costliest short-term socioeconomic repercussions via economic losses and people displaced.
Approach-sourcematerial: Utilizing Visible Infrared Imaging Radiometer Suite nighttime light (NTL) data as a proxy for economic activity and population density, we build a timeline of recovery via NTL radiance levels. We exploit the difference in the eligibility for aid from the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) to apply a quasi-experimental method to identify changes in NTL radiance attributable to hurricanes. Combining these results with machine learning techniques, we are able to investigate those factors that contribute to recovery.
Methodology: Utilizes Visible Infrared Imaging Radiometer Suite nighttime light data as a proxy for economic activity and population density, applying a quasi-experimental method exploiting differences in FEMA aid eligibility to assess changes in NTL radiance attributable to hurricanes, supplemented with machine learning to explore recovery factors.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Utilizing Visible Infrared Imaging Radiometer Suite nighttime light (NTL) data as a proxy for economic activity and population density, we build a timeline of recovery via NTL radiance levels. We exploit the difference in the eligibility for aid from the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) to apply a quasi-experimental method to identify changes in NTL radiance attributable to hurricanes. Combining these results with machine learning techniques, we are able to investigate those factors that contribute to recovery.
Highlights: Category 4 and 5 hurricanes in the US South (2014-2020) lead to long-term (3 year) reductions in economic activity/population density (2-14% decrease in nighttime light radiance) despite FEMA aid; FEMA aid supports short-term recovery for public entities but isn't sufficient for full, long-term recovery, and age/employment are key factors for individual recovery.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "areas affected by hurricanes of category 4 or 5 in the southern US between 2014 and 2020 generally do not demonstrate full recovery in the longer term...after three years, affected areas demonstrate a reduction in NTL radiance levels of between 2% and 14% compared to the pre-disaster period...counties demonstrating smaller reductions in NTL radiance levels in the months following the hurricane are buoyed by the amount of FEMA aid received, but that this aid does not foster a longer-term return to normal radiance levels...aid may be more important in motivating short-term recovery for public entities than for individuals but is not sufficient to guarantee complete recovery in the longer term...age and employment are more important than other demographic factors in determining hurricane recovery over time...Utilizing Visible Infrared Imaging Radiometer Suite nighttime light (NTL) data as a proxy for economic activity and population density..."
More Notes: Severe hurricanes in the Southern US (2014-2020) lead to a long-term reduction in economic activity and population density, with FEMA aid promoting short-term recovery but not guaranteeing a full return to pre-disaster levels, and age and employment being key factors in individual recovery.
More Notes-sourcematerial: As the climate warms, many areas of the world are experiencing more frequent and extreme weather events. Hurricanes carry some of the costliest short-term socioeconomic repercussions via economic losses and people displaced. ... we show that areas affected by hurricanes of category 4 or 5 in the southern US between 2014 and 2020 generally do not demonstrate full recovery in the longer term. ... after three years, affected areas demonstrate a reduction in NTL radiance levels of between 2% and 14% compared to the pre-disaster period. ... counties demonstrating smaller reductions in NTL radiance levels in the months following the hurricane are buoyed by the amount of FEMA aid received, but that this aid does not foster a longer-term return to normal radiance levels. Investigating areas receiving FEMA aid at the household and individual level, we find age and employment are more important than other demographic factors in determining hurricane recovery over time. These findings suggest that aid may be more important in motivating short-term recovery for public entities than for individuals but is not sufficient to guarantee complete recovery in the longer term.

Paper Title: Multi-peril pathogen risks to global wheat production: A probabilistic loss and investment assessment
Outcome(s) of Interest: Global wheat production losses, economic losses, food security impacts.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "Crop diseases cause significant food and economic losses... recurring losses attributable to this set of fungal diseases are upwards of 62 million tons of wheat production per year...representing calories sufficient to feed up to 173 million people each year."
Drivers of Risk: Climate suitability of fungal pathogens, global wheat production, modeled crop loss distributions.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: We examined the joint, probabilistic, long-term, bio-economic impact of five major fungal pathogens of wheat on global wheat production by combining spatialized estimates of their climate suitability with global wheat production and modeled distributions of potential crop losses.
Category-sourcematerial: We examined the joint, probabilistic, long-term, bio-economic impact of five major fungal pathogens of wheat on global wheat production by combining spatialized estimates of their climate suitability with global wheat production and modeled distributions of potential crop losses. [...] Our high-loss regime translates to around 8.5% of the world’s wheat production on average—representing calories sufficient to feed up to 173 million people each year.
Approach-sourcematerial: We examined the joint, probabilistic, long-term, bio-economic impact of five major fungal pathogens of wheat on global wheat production by combining spatialized estimates of their climate suitability with global wheat production and modeled distributions of potential crop losses.
Methodology: Combined spatialized estimates of climate suitability for five major fungal pathogens of wheat with global wheat production and modeled distributions of potential crop losses to assess the joint, probabilistic, long-term, bio-economic impact on global wheat production.
Methodology-sourcematerial: We examined the joint, probabilistic, long-term, bio-economic impact of five major fungal pathogens of wheat on global wheat production by combining spatialized estimates of their climate suitability with global wheat production and modeled distributions of potential crop losses.
Highlights: Fungal diseases threaten 90% of global wheat area, causing average annual losses of 8.5% of global wheat production (enough calories for 173 million people), justifying increased research funding.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Crop diseases cause significant food and economic losses...almost 90% of the global wheat area is at risk from at least one of these fungal diseases...recurring losses attributable to this set of fungal diseases are upwards of 62 million tons of wheat production per year...translates to around 8.5% of the world’s wheat production on average...calories sufficient to feed up to 173 million people each year...worldwide research expenditure of $350-$974 million...can be economically justified.
More Notes: Fungal diseases threaten 90% of global wheat area, causing annual losses of 62 million tons, and justify increased research funding.
More Notes-sourcematerial: "almost 90% of the global wheat area is at risk from at least one of these fungal diseases, and that the recurring losses attributable to this set of fungal diseases are upwards of 62 million tons of wheat production per year... a worldwide research expenditure of $350-$974 million (2018 prices) annually on these five fungal diseases of wheat...can be economically justified"
Hazard definition-sourcematerial: We examined the joint, probabilistic, long-term, bio-economic impact of five major fungal pathogens of wheat on global wheat production... recurring losses attributable to this set of fungal diseases are upwards of 62 million tons of wheat production per year.
Impact persistence-sourcematerial: We examined the joint, probabilistic, long-term, bio-economic impact of five major fungal pathogens of wheat on global wheat production by combining spatialized estimates of their climate suitability with global wheat production and modeled distributions of potential crop losses.
Space disaggregation-sourcematerial: We examined the joint, probabilistic, long-term, bio-economic impact of five major fungal pathogens of wheat on global wheat production by combining spatialized estimates of their climate suitability with global wheat production and modeled distributions of potential crop losses. We determined that almost 90% of the global wheat area is at risk from at least one of these fungal diseases, and that the recurring losses attributable to this set of fungal diseases are upwards of 62 million tons of wheat production per year. Our high-loss regime translates to around 8.5% of the world’s wheat production on average—representing calories sufficient to feed up to 173 million people each year.
Impact interactions-sourcematerial: The abstract focuses on the impact of five specific fungal pathogens of wheat and their combined effect on wheat production, without mentioning any interaction with other climate impacts or sectors.
Economic dynamics-sourcematerial: Crop diseases cause significant food and economic losses. We examined the joint, probabilistic, long-term, bio-economic impact of five major fungal pathogens of wheat on global wheat production by combining spatialized estimates of their climate suitability with global wheat production and modeled distributions of potential crop losses. We determined that almost 90% of the global wheat area is at risk from at least one of these fungal diseases, and that the recurring losses attributable to this set of fungal diseases are upwards of 62 million tons of wheat production per year. Our high-loss regime translates to around 8.5% of the world’s wheat production on average—representing calories sufficient to feed up to 173 million people each year. We estimate that a worldwide research expenditure of $350-$974 million (2018 prices) annually on these five fungal diseases of wheat, let alone other pathogens, can be economically justified, equivalent to 2 to 5 times more than the amount we estimate is currently spent on all wheat disease-related public R&D. Copyright © 2022 Chai, Senay, Horvath and Pardey.
Climate features-sourcematerial: N/A - The abstract focuses on the economic impact of fungal diseases on wheat production and doesn't mention any tipping points or climate complexities beyond the impact of climate suitability on disease spread.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is implicitly considered through the suggestion of increased research and development (R&D) investment to mitigate the impacts of fungal diseases on wheat production. The paper argues that a substantial increase in R&D spending is economically justifiable, implying a proactive adaptation strategy.
Adaptation considered-sourcematerial: "We estimate that a worldwide research expenditure of $350-$974 million (2018 prices) annually on these five fungal diseases of wheat, let alone other pathogens, can be economically justified, equivalent to 2 to 5 times more than the amount we estimate is currently spent on all wheat disease-related public R&D."

Paper Title: Adaptation of water resources management under climate change
Outcome(s) of Interest: Climate adaptation strategies (managed aquifer recharge, greenhouses, efficient irrigation) affect agricultural drought resilience; combined strategies are most effective; water entitlements are vulnerable in consecutive droughts.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "We selected four indicators of system resilience and sustainability to evaluate four adaptation methods...in alleviating drought impacts on agriculture...managed aquifer recharge appeared to be a cost-effective and easy-to-adopt option, whereas water entitlements are likely to get exhausted during multiple consecutive drought events. Greenhouses and water-efficient technologies are more effective in improving irrigation reliability...implementing all adaptation methods together is the only way to alleviate most of the drought impacts projected in the future."
Drivers of Risk: Water scarcity, climate change, frequent droughts, agricultural demand, drought impact.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: The rapid growth of demand in agricultural production has created water scarcity issues worldwide. Simultaneously, climate change scenarios have projected that more frequent and severe droughts are likely to occur. We analyzed the characteristics of four adaptation methods ... to overcome drought impact.
Category-sourcematerial: The rapid growth of demand in agricultural production has created water scarcity issues worldwide... We applied this tool to the Yakima River Basin (YRB) in Washington State, USA... We selected four indicators of system resilience and sustainability to evaluate four adaptation methods associated with adoption behaviors in alleviating drought impacts on agriculture... We analyzed the characteristics of four adaptation methods, including greenhouses, crop planting time, irrigation technology, and managed aquifer recharge as well as alternating supply and demand dynamics to overcome drought impact... The water resources management tool helps stakeholders and researchers gain insights in the roles of modern inventions in agricultural water cycle dynamics in the context of interactive multi-sector systems.
Approach-sourcematerial: We enhanced an aggregated water resource management tool that represents integrated agriculture, water, energy, and social systems. We applied this tool to the Yakima River Basin (YRB) in Washington State, USA. We selected four indicators of system resilience and sustainability to evaluate four adaptation methods associated with adoption behaviors in alleviating drought impacts on agriculture under RCP4.5 and RCP 8.5 climate change scenarios.
Methodology: An aggregated water resource management tool, representing integrated agriculture, water, energy, and social systems, was enhanced and applied to the Yakima River Basin (YRB) in Washington State, USA, to evaluate four adaptation methods (greenhouses, crop planting time, irrigation technology, and managed aquifer recharge) under RCP4.5 and RCP8.5 climate change scenarios using four indicators of system resilience and sustainability.
Methodology-sourcematerial: In this study, we enhanced an aggregated water resource management tool that represents integrated agriculture, water, energy, and social systems. We applied this tool to the Yakima River Basin (YRB) in Washington State, USA. We selected four indicators of system resilience and sustainability to evaluate four adaptation methods associated with adoption behaviors in alleviating drought impacts on agriculture under RCP4.5 and RCP 8.5 climate change scenarios.
Highlights: Managed aquifer recharge is a cost-effective drought adaptation strategy, while water entitlements may be depleted during prolonged droughts; greenhouses and water-efficient technologies improve irrigation reliability under severe climate change (RCP 8.5) when widely adopted; comprehensive adaptation strategies are needed to fully mitigate future drought impacts.
Highlights-sourcematerial: The results show that climate conditions with severe and consecutive droughts require more financial and natural resources to achieve well-implemented adaptation strategies. For long-term impact analysis, managed aquifer recharge appeared to be a cost-effective and easy-to-adopt option, whereas water entitlements are likely to get exhausted during multiple consecutive drought events. Greenhouses and water-efficient technologies are more effective in improving irrigation reliability under RCP 8.5 when widely adopted. However, implementing all adaptation methods together is the only way to alleviate most of the drought impacts projected in the future.
More Notes: Adaptive water resources management in the Yakima River Basin, Washington, utilizes integrated tools to assess resilience and sustainability against droughts under climate scenarios RCP4.5 and RCP8.5; findings emphasize the cost-effectiveness of managed aquifer recharge and the importance of combining greenhouses, irrigation technology, and other methods to mitigate drought impacts.
More Notes-sourcematerial: "Adaptive water resources management has been suggested as one strategy to better coordinate surface water and groundwater resources (i.e., conjunctive water use) to address droughts... We applied this tool to the Yakima River Basin (YRB) in Washington State, USA... We selected four indicators of system resilience and sustainability to evaluate four adaptation methods... under RCP4.5 and RCP 8.5 climate change scenarios... managed aquifer recharge appeared to be a cost-effective and easy-to-adopt option... implementing all adaptation methods together is the only way to alleviate most of the drought impacts projected in the future."
Hazard definition-sourcematerial: climate change scenarios have projected that more frequent and severe droughts are likely to occur.
Impact persistence-sourcematerial: "Adaptive water resources management has been suggested as one strategy...","...four adaptation methods associated with adoption behaviors in alleviating drought impacts..." , "We analyzed the characteristics of four adaptation methods..." , "implementing all adaptation methods together is the only way to alleviate most of the drought impacts projected in the future."
Space disaggregation-sourcematerial: We applied this tool to the Yakima River Basin (YRB) in Washington State, USA.
Impact interactions-sourcematerial: "We enhanced an aggregated water resource management tool that represents integrated agriculture, water, energy, and social systems." and "The water resources management tool helps stakeholders and researchers gain insights in the roles of modern inventions in agricultural water cycle dynamics in the context of interactive multi-sector systems."
Economic dynamics-sourcematerial: The results show that climate conditions with severe and consecutive droughts require more financial and natural resources to achieve well-implemented adaptation strategies. For long-term impact analysis, managed aquifer recharge appeared to be a cost-effective and easy-to-adopt option, whereas water entitlements are likely to get exhausted during multiple consecutive drought events. Greenhouses and water-efficient technologies are more effective in improving irrigation reliability under RCP 8.5 when widely adopted. However, implementing all adaptation methods together is the only way to alleviate most of the drought impacts projected in the future.
Climate features-sourcematerial: The abstract focuses on adaptive water resource management strategies in response to drought conditions projected under different climate change scenarios (RCP4.5 and RCP8.5) in the Yakima River Basin. It analyzes the effectiveness of various adaptation methods like greenhouses, crop planting time adjustments, irrigation technology, and managed aquifer recharge. There is no mention of tipping points or other climate complexities beyond the projected drought scenarios.
Adaptation considered: The study evaluates four adaptation methods (greenhouses, crop planting time, irrigation technology, and managed aquifer recharge) and their effectiveness in alleviating drought impacts on agriculture under different climate change scenarios (RCP4.5 and RCP8.5) in the Yakima River Basin. It also considers alternating supply and demand dynamics. The analysis assesses the impact of these methods on system resilience and sustainability. Implementing all methods together is considered the most effective approach.
Adaptation considered-sourcematerial: "We selected four indicators of system resilience and sustainability to evaluate four adaptation methods associated with adoption behaviors in alleviating drought impacts on agriculture under RCP4.5 and RCP 8.5 climate change scenarios. We analyzed the characteristics of four adaptation methods, including greenhouses, crop planting time, irrigation technology, and managed aquifer recharge as well as alternating supply and demand dynamics to overcome drought impact... implementing all adaptation methods together is the only way to alleviate most of the drought impacts projected in the future."

Paper Title: A Risk-Based Framework to Evaluate Infrastructure Investment Options for a Water Supply System
Outcome(s) of Interest: Annual reliability, maximum delivery capacity, probability of unsuccessful status, magnitude of potential water shortage reduction, infrastructure expansion benefits.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "Different 'industry understood' performance metrics, e.g., annual reliability, maximum delivery capacity, the probability of unsuccessful status, and the magnitude of potential water shortage, can be employed to evaluate the performance of water supply systems...Results reveal that annual reliability would increase with the expansion of surface water treatment plant capacity...the duration and magnitude of potential water shortage could be reduced in both scenarios with engineering intervention."
Drivers of Risk: Increasing water demand, socio-economic development, supply-demand gap, probabilistic demand projections, climate conditions, spatio-temporal variability, low demand realizations, potential water shortage.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Increasing water demand due to socio-economic development...bridge the gap between demand and water supply...probabilistic demand projections...consider socio-economic projections and climate conditions based on the statistics of historical observations...spatio-temporal supply and demand variability...Except for low demand realizations in the future, the duration and magnitude of potential water shortage...
Category-sourcematerial: This study focuses on evaluating infrastructure investment options from the reliability perspectives of a water supply system...This framework has been applied to Tampa Bay Water, a regional supply agency on the west coast of Florida, United States...Results reveal that annual reliability would increase with the expansion of surface water treatment plant capacity...the duration and magnitude of potential water shortage could be reduced in both scenarios with engineering intervention.
Approach-sourcematerial: The evaluation framework is comprised of probabilistic demand projections, stochastic streamflow generation, a mixed-integer programming, and system performance evaluation. The probabilistic demand projections for the future (2021-2040) consider socio-economic projections and climate conditions based on the statistics of historical observations. The Latin Hypercube Sampling algorithm is used to capture the bivariate distribution of supply and demand. This mixed-integer programming is a daily system optimization that minimizes operational cost and considers operational constraints and preferences, e.g., water withdrawal permits, facility production capacity, and water production preferences.
Methodology: A framework using probabilistic demand projections (considering socio-economic projections and climate statistics), stochastic streamflow generation, mixed-integer programming for daily system optimization, and performance evaluation (annual reliability, maximum delivery capacity, probability of unsuccessful status, potential water shortage magnitude) assesses water supply system infrastructure investment options.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "The evaluation framework is comprised of probabilistic demand projections, stochastic streamflow generation, a mixed-integer programming, and system performance evaluation. Different 'industry understood' performance metrics, e.g., annual reliability, maximum delivery capacity, the probability of unsuccessful status, and the magnitude of potential water shortage, can be employed to evaluate the performance of water supply systems. The probabilistic demand projections for the future (2021-2040) consider socio-economic projections and climate conditions based on the statistics of historical observations. The Latin Hypercube Sampling algorithm is used to capture the bivariate distribution of supply and demand. This mixed-integer programming is a daily system optimization that minimizes operational cost and considers operational constraints and preferences, e.g., water withdrawal permits, facility production capacity, and water production preferences."
Highlights: Evaluates water supply infrastructure expansion options for Tampa Bay Water using probabilistic demand projections considering socio-economic and historical climate data, finding that increasing surface water treatment plant capacity improves annual reliability and reduces water shortages, though firm yield is limited by supply-demand variability.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "This study focuses on evaluating infrastructure investment options from the reliability perspectives of a water supply system...This framework has been applied to Tampa Bay Water...The probabilistic demand projections for the future (2021-2040) consider socio-economic projections and climate conditions based on the statistics of historical observations...Results reveal that annual reliability would increase with the expansion of surface water treatment plant capacity...the spatio-temporal supply and demand variability determines that the firm yield from the surface water treatment is less than its expanded production capacity...the duration and magnitude of potential water shortage could be reduced in both scenarios with engineering intervention."
More Notes: Evaluates water supply infrastructure expansion options under probabilistic demand projections considering socio-economic and historical climate conditions, finding that increased surface water treatment capacity improves reliability and reduces potential water shortages, though firm yield is limited by spatio-temporal variability.
More Notes-sourcematerial: "This study focuses on evaluating infrastructure investment options from the reliability perspectives of a water supply system...probabilistic demand projections for the future (2021-2040) consider socio-economic projections and climate conditions based on the statistics of historical observations...Results reveal that annual reliability would increase with the expansion of surface water treatment plant capacity. The spatio-temporal supply and demand variability determines that the firm yield from the surface water treatment is less than its expanded production capacity. Except for low demand realizations in the future, the duration and magnitude of potential water shortage could be reduced in both scenarios with engineering intervention."
Hazard definition-sourcematerial: The probabilistic demand projections for the future (2021-2040) consider socio-economic projections and climate conditions based on the statistics of historical observations.
Impact persistence-sourcematerial: The probabilistic demand projections for the future (2021-2040) consider socio-economic projections and climate conditions based on the statistics of historical observations.
Space disaggregation-sourcematerial: This framework has been applied to Tampa Bay Water, a regional supply agency on the west coast of Florida, United States.
Impact interactions-sourcematerial: No direct evidence of interactions between climate impacts can be found in the abstract provided.
Economic dynamics-sourcematerial: The probabilistic demand projections for the future (2021-2040) consider socio-economic projections and climate conditions based on the statistics of historical observations.
Climate features-sourcematerial: The abstract focuses on evaluating infrastructure investment options for water supply systems, considering probabilistic demand projections, stochastic streamflow generation, mixed-integer programming, and system performance evaluation. It does not mention or address tipping points or complex climate mechanisms explicitly.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is considered through infrastructure expansion by evaluating and planning increased treatment capacity. The framework assesses different planning scenarios for expanding water treatment capacity, focusing on enhancing reliability and reducing potential water shortages. This approach includes probabilistic demand projections and considers socio-economic and climatic conditions to guide decision-making.
Adaptation considered-sourcematerial: "Evaluating different expansion options is critical to making informed decisions... This study focuses on evaluating infrastructure investment options from the reliability perspectives of a water supply system... Two additional planning scenarios of expanding the treatment capacity of a surface water treatment plant by 75,708 m3/day [20 million gallons per day (mgd)] and 113,562 m3/day (30 mgd) in the year 2028 are investigated... Results reveal that annual reliability would increase with the expansion of surface water treatment plant capacity...the duration and magnitude of potential water shortage could be reduced in both scenarios with engineering intervention."

Paper Title: Policy interventions to address water security impacted by climate change: Adaptation strategies of three case studies across different geographic regions
Outcome(s) of Interest: Reallocation of water, stakeholder engagement, public awareness, short/long-term management plans, context-specific solutions.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Based on this cross-case analysis, we present key strategies moving forward, including: reallocation of water based on different community and sector needs, the importance of stakeholders engagement and public awareness, and a need to implement both short and long-term management plans. There is no one-size fits all policy solution to water scarcity. Understanding the context, scale, and cultural environment will be a key to future water security-focused interventions and policies.
Drivers of Risk: Physical water scarcity, economic water scarcity, climate change threat, regional water security challenges
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Water shortage in terms of both physical and economic water scarcity is still a major issue globally. The looming climate change threat has increased the adverse threats to water security in different regions.
Category-sourcematerial: Water shortage in terms of both physical and economic water scarcity is still a major issue globally. The looming climate change threat has increased the adverse threats to water security in different regions.
Approach-sourcematerial: We reviewed relevant research papers consisting of peer-reviewed journal articles, conference proceedings and gray literature using a content analysis approach based on keywords such as water scarcity, water shortage, climate change, policies, interventions and solutions. Based on this cross-case analysis, we present key strategies moving forward...
Methodology: The study uses a content analysis approach to review research papers (peer-reviewed articles, conference proceedings, and gray literature) based on keywords related to water scarcity, climate change, and relevant policies, interventions, and solutions, across case studies in California (USA), Cape Town (South Africa), and Bangladesh.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "We reviewed relevant research papers consisting of peer-reviewed journal articles, conference proceedings and gray literature using a content analysis approach based on keywords such as water scarcity, water shortage, climate change, policies, interventions and solutions. These case studies highlight various implemented policies and their effectiveness in each context."
Highlights: Water security policy solutions vary geographically and by scale; successful policies are critical to scaling up; key strategies include water reallocation, stakeholder engagement, and short/long-term management plans; context, scale, and cultural environment are key to future interventions.

There is no one-size fits all policy solution to water scarcity. Understanding the context, scale, and cultural environment will be a key to future water security-focused interventions and policies."
More Notes: Climate change exacerbates water scarcity globally, requiring context-specific policy solutions that consider scale, cultural environment, stakeholder engagement, public awareness, and both short- and long-term management plans, as illustrated by case studies in California, Cape Town, and Bangladesh.

Paper Title: Measuring and Attributing Sedimentary and Geomorphic Responses to Modern Climate Change: Challenges and Opportunities
Outcome(s) of Interest: Effects on health and safety, infrastructure, water–food–energy security, economics, and ecosystems.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "so that societies will be better prepared to manage the effects on health and safety, infrastructure, water–food–energy security, economics, and ecosystems that follow from climate-driven physical landscape change."
Drivers of Risk: Short/incomplete data, land use, seismicity, data biases, signal attenuation.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: This commentary discusses the challenges of measuring climate-driven physical landscape responses to modern global warming: short and incomplete data records, land use and seismicity masking climatic effects, biases in data availability and resolution, and signal attenuation in sedimentary systems.
Category-sourcematerial: This commentary discusses the challenges of measuring climate-driven physical landscape responses to modern global warming...so that societies will be better prepared to manage the effects on health and safety, infrastructure, water–food–energy security, economics, and ecosystems that follow from climate-driven physical landscape change.
Approach-sourcematerial: The abstract is a commentary discussing challenges, opportunities, and encouragement for research, not a report of a specific modeling or analysis effort.
Methodology: Utilize historical and paleo data, focus on sensitive study sites, and report null results to improve understanding and attribution of climate-driven physical landscape changes.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "We identify opportunities to learn from historical and paleo data, select especially sensitive study sites, and report null results to better characterize the extent and nuances of climate-change effects. We then discuss efforts to improve attribution practices, which will lead to better predictive capabilities."
Highlights: Challenges exist in measuring climate-driven landscape responses due to data limitations, masking effects, biases, and signal attenuation; leveraging historical/paleo data, sensitive sites, and reporting null results can improve understanding and attribution, enabling better preparedness for societal impacts.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "challenges of measuring climate-driven physical landscape responses to modern global warming: short and incomplete data records, land use and seismicity masking climatic effects, biases in data availability and resolution, and signal attenuation in sedimentary systems. We identify opportunities to learn from historical and paleo data, select especially sensitive study sites, and report null results to better characterize the extent and nuances of climate-change effects. We then discuss efforts to improve attribution practices, which will lead to better predictive capabilities. We encourage the Earth-science community to prioritize scientific research on climate-driven physical landscape changes so that societies will be better prepared to manage the effects on health and safety, infrastructure, water–food–energy security, economics, and ecosystems that follow from climate-driven physical landscape change."
More Notes: Challenges exist in measuring climate-driven landscape changes due to data limitations, masking factors, biases, and signal attenuation, requiring improved attribution practices, historical/paleo data, sensitive site selection, and reporting of null results.
