Paper Title: Fifty years of Landsat science and impacts
Outcome(s) of Interest: Agricultural crop mapping and water use, climate change drivers and impacts, ecosystems and land cover monitoring, mapping the changing human footprint.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Major scientific and programmatic impacts have been realized in the areas of agricultural crop mapping and water use, climate change drivers and impacts, ecosystems and land cover monitoring, and mapping the changing human footprint.
Drivers of Risk: Climate change drivers, land cover monitoring, human footprint mapping.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "Major scientific and programmatic impacts have been realized in the areas of [...] climate change drivers and impacts, ecosystems and land cover monitoring, and mapping the changing human footprint."
Category-sourcematerial: Major scientific and programmatic impacts have been realized in the areas of agricultural crop mapping and water use, climate change drivers and impacts, ecosystems and land cover monitoring, and mapping the changing human footprint.
Approach-sourcematerial: Here, we highlight key developments over the past 50 years of the Landsat program that have influenced and changed our scientific understanding of the Earth system. Major scientific and programmatic impacts have been realized in the areas of agricultural crop mapping and water use, climate change drivers and impacts, ecosystems and land cover monitoring, and mapping the changing human footprint. The introduction of Landsat collection processing, coupled with the free and open data policy, facilitated a transition in Landsat data usage away from single images and towards time series analyses over large areas and has fostered the widespread use of science-grade data.
Methodology: Landsat's continuous 50-year digital, multispectral, medium spatial resolution observations enabled new methods for analyzing large datasets of Earth's surface, transitioning data usage from single images to time series analyses, especially after the 2008 free and open data policy.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Since 1972, the Landsat program has been continually monitoring the Earth, to now provide 50 years of digital, multispectral, medium spatial resolution observations...the ability to acquire and work with large datasets was limited...The early years of the Landsat program delivered a series of technological breakthroughs, pioneering new methods...Free and open access to Landsat data, enacted in 2008...facilitated a transition in Landsat data usage away from single images and towards time series analyses over large areas.
Highlights: Landsat's 50 years of Earth observation have significantly advanced scientific understanding in agricultural crop mapping and water use, climate change drivers and impacts, ecosystem and land cover monitoring, and mapping the changing human footprint, facilitated by free data access and time series analyses.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Major scientific and programmatic impacts have been realized in the areas of agricultural crop mapping and water use, climate change drivers and impacts, ecosystems and land cover monitoring, and mapping the changing human footprint. The introduction of Landsat collection processing, coupled with the free and open data policy, facilitated a transition in Landsat data usage away from single images and towards time series analyses over large areas and has fostered the widespread use of science-grade data.
More Notes: Landsat's 50-year Earth observation program has significantly advanced understanding of climate change drivers and impacts, among other Earth system areas, through continuous monitoring, technological breakthroughs, and free data access, fostering time series analyses and widespread science-grade data use.
More Notes-sourcematerial: Major scientific and programmatic impacts have been realized in the areas of agricultural crop mapping and water use, climate change drivers and impacts, ecosystems and land cover monitoring, and mapping the changing human footprint. Paper Title: Effect modification of greenness on the association between heat and mortality: A multi-city multi-country study
Outcome(s) of Interest: Heat-related mortality risk, heat-related attributable fraction, excess deaths.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "Cities with high greenspace value had the lowest heat-mortality relative risk of 1·19 (95% CI: 1·13, 1·25), while the heat-related relative risk was 1·46 (95% CI: 1·31, 1·62) for cities with low greenspace when comparing the 99th temperature and the minimum mortality temperature. A 20% increase of greenspace is associated with a 9·02% (95% CI: 8·88, 9·16) decrease in the heat-related attributable fraction, and if this association is causal (which is not within the scope of this study to assess), such a reduction could save approximately 933 excess deaths per year in 24 countries."
Drivers of Risk: Climate change, urbanization, temperature, greenspace (EVI), population density, GDP.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Background: Identifying how greenspace impacts the temperature-mortality relationship in urban environments is crucial, especially given climate change and rapid urbanization. ... We used Enhanced Vegetation Index (EVI) as the greenspace measurement. ... the estimates were pooled adjusting for city-specific average temperature, city-specific temperature range, city-specific population density, and gross domestic product (GDP).
Category: Health > Heat and Cold (Cardiovascular, Respiratory, etc.)
Category-sourcematerial: Cities with high greenspace value had the lowest heat-mortality relative risk... while the heat-related relative risk was...for cities with low greenspace... A 20% increase of greenspace is associated with a decrease in the heat-related attributable fraction... such a reduction could save approximately 933 excess deaths per year... Our findings can inform communities on the potential health benefits of greenspaces in the urban environment and mitigation measures regarding the impacts of climate change.
Approach-sourcematerial: distributed lag non-linear model
Methodology: Daily ambient temperature and mortality data were collected for 452 locations in 24 countries with greenspace measured by the Enhanced Vegetation Index (EVI), and a distributed lag non-linear model was used to estimate the heat-mortality relationship in each city, adjusting for city-specific factors such as average temperature, temperature range, population density, and GDP; effect modification of greenspace was assessed by comparing heat-related mortality risks across low, medium, and high greenspace groups.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Methods: We collected daily ambient temperature and mortality data for 452 locations in 24 countries and used Enhanced Vegetation Index (EVI) as the greenspace measurement. We used distributed lag non-linear model to estimate the heat-mortality relationship in each city and the estimates were pooled adjusting for city-specific average temperature, city-specific temperature range, city-specific population density, and gross domestic product (GDP). The effect modification of greenspace was evaluated by comparing the heat-related mortality risk for different greenspace groups (low, medium, and high), which were divided into terciles among 452 locations.
Highlights: Urban greenspace is associated with reduced heat-related mortality; cities with high greenspace had a lower heat-mortality relative risk (1.19) compared to cities with low greenspace (1.46); a 20% increase in greenspace could reduce heat-related deaths by approximately 933 per year across the 24 countries studied.

A 20% increase of greenspace is associated with a 9·02% (95% CI: 8·88, 9·16) decrease in the heat-related attributable fraction, and if this association is causal (which is not within the scope of this study to assess), such a reduction could save approximately 933 excess deaths per year in 24 countries."

Paper Title: Knowledge for a warmer world: A patent analysis of climate change adaptation technologies
Outcome(s) of Interest: CCATs show technological complementarities with mitigation, with >25% having mitigation benefits.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: CCATs show strong technological complementarities with mitigation as more than 25% of CCATs bear mitigation benefits.
Drivers of Risk: Low awareness of climate risk solutions among investors; potentially influenced by environmental regulation, energy prices, and public support.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "in contrast to mitigation, innovation in CCATs only slowly takes off, indicating a relatively low awareness of investors for solutions to cope with climate risks. We discuss how historical trends in environmental regulation, energy prices, and public support may have contributed to patenting in CCATs."
Category-sourcematerial: Technologies can help strengthen the resilience of our economy against existential climate-risks. We investigate climate change adaptation technologies (CCATs) in US patents to understand (1) historical patterns and possible drivers of innovation; (2) scientific and technological requirements to develop and use CCATs; and (3) CCATs’ potential technological synergies with mitigation. First, in contrast to mitigation, innovation in CCATs only slowly takes off, indicating a relatively low awareness of investors for solutions to cope with climate risks. We discuss how historical trends in environmental regulation, energy prices, and public support may have contributed to patenting in CCATs. Second, CCATs form two main clusters: science-intensive ones in agriculture, health, and monitoring technologies; and engineering-intensive ones in coastal, water, and infrastructure technologies. Analyses of technology-specific scientific and technological knowledge bases inform directions for how to facilitate advancement, transfer and use of CCATs. Lastly, CCATs show strong technological complementarities with mitigation as more than 25% of CCATs bear mitigation benefits. While not judging about the complementarity of mitigation and adaptation in general, our results suggest how policymakers can harness these technological synergies to achieve both goals simultaneously. © 2022 The Author(s)
Approach-sourcematerial: We investigate climate change adaptation technologies (CCATs) in US patents to understand (1) historical patterns and possible drivers of innovation; (2) scientific and technological requirements to develop and use CCATs; and (3) CCATs’ potential technological synergies with mitigation. Analyses of technology-specific scientific and technological knowledge bases inform directions for how to facilitate advancement, transfer and use of CCATs.
Methodology: Analysis of US patents related to climate change adaptation technologies (CCATs) to understand innovation patterns, drivers, scientific/technological requirements, and synergies with mitigation.
Methodology-sourcematerial: We investigate climate change adaptation technologies (CCATs) in US patents to understand (1) historical patterns and possible drivers of innovation; (2) scientific and technological requirements to develop and use CCATs; and (3) CCATs’ potential technological synergies with mitigation.
Highlights: CCAT innovation lags mitigation, likely due to lower investor awareness; CCATs fall into science-intensive (agriculture, health, monitoring) and engineering-intensive (coastal, water, infrastructure) clusters; and many CCATs offer mitigation co-benefits, presenting policy opportunities for synergistic climate action.

Paper Title: Uncertainties in estimating global potential yields and their impacts for long-term modeling
Outcome(s) of Interest: Different potential yield estimation methods (Empirical vs. Simulated) lead to vastly different outcomes for global agricultural sustainability: Simulated favors Africa, Asia, South America (low land use change, low prices, high self-sufficiency); Empirical favors temperate regions (high production/exports, high prices, more land use change).
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: In the Simulated potential yield closure scenario, Africa, Asia, and South America see comparatively favorable outcomes for agricultural sustainability over time: low land use change emissions, low crop prices, and high levels of self-sufficiency. In contrast, the Empirical potential yield scenario is characterized by a heavy reliance on production and exports in temperate regions that currently practice industrial agriculture. At the global level, this scenario has comparatively high crop commodity prices, and more land allocated to crop production (and associated land use change emissions) than either the baseline or Simulated potential yield scenarios. This study highlights the importance of the choice of methods of estimating potential yields for agro-economic modeling.
Drivers of Risk: Yield estimation methods, Tropical regions, Simulated vs. Empirical yields, Yield gap closure, Agricultural sustainability, Land use change emissions, Crop prices, Industrial agriculture, Global agro-economic outcomes.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "Estimating realistic potential yields by crop type and region is challenging; such yields depend on both biophysical characteristics (e.g., soil characteristics, climate, etc.), and the crop management practices available in any site or region (e.g., mechanization, irrigation, crop cultivars)."... "Potential yields by major crop and region are quite different between the two methods; in particular, Simulated potential yields are typically 200% higher than Empirical potential yields in tropical regions for major crops." ... "the two estimates of future potential yields lead to very different outcomes for the agricultural sector globally." ... "In the Empirical potential yield scenario is characterized by a heavy reliance on production and exports in temperate regions that currently practice industrial agriculture. At the global level, this scenario has comparatively high crop commodity prices, and more land allocated to crop production (and associated land use change emissions) than either the baseline or Simulated potential yield scenarios."

Paper Title: Sustainable Agro-Food Systems for Addressing Climate Change and Food Security
Outcome(s) of Interest: food security, hunger, nutrition, sustainability, climate impacts, greenhouse gas emissions, sustainable agricultural practices, equitable resource distribution
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Many more have low-quality, unhealthy diets and micronutrient deficiencies leading to obesity, diabetes, and other diet-related non-communicable diseases. Additionally, current agro-food systems significantly impact the environment and the climate, including soil and water resources. Frequent natural disasters resulting from climate change, pandemics, and conflicts weaken food systems and exacerbate food insecurity worldwide. In this review, we outline the current knowledge in alternative agricultural practices for achieving sustainability as well as policies and practices that need to be implemented for an equitable distribution of resources and food for achieving several goals in the UN 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development. According to the UN Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, animal husbandry, particularly ruminant meat and dairy, accounts for a significant proportion of agricultural greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions and land use but contributes only 18% of food energy. There is growing evidence that food security and adequate nutrition for the global population can be achieved using climate-smart, sustainable agricultural practices, while reducing negative environmental impacts of agriculture, including GHG emissions.
Drivers of Risk: Climate change-related natural disasters, pandemics, conflicts, agro-food system impacts (soil, water), animal husbandry (GHG emissions, land use), unsustainable agricultural practices, inequitable resource distribution.

Paper Title: Cyanobacterial Bloom Phenology in Green Bay Using MERIS Satellite Data and Comparisons with Western Lake Erie and Saginaw Bay
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased cyanobacteria blooms, economic and ecological damage, threats to human health.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Cyanobacteria blooms have been reported to be increasing worldwide. In addition to potentially causing major economic and ecological damage, these blooms can threaten human health.
Drivers of Risk: Warming climate, stratification (heat flux proxy), wind speed, water temperature, gelbstoff absorption, river discharge.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Furthermore, these blooms can be exacerbated by a warming climate. Modeled datasets of heat flux (as a proxy for stratification), wind speed, water temperature, and gelbstoff absorption along with in situ river discharge data were used to separate bloom seasons in Green Bay...
Category-sourcematerial: Cyanobacteria blooms have been reported to be increasing worldwide... can threaten human health. Furthermore, these blooms can be exacerbated by a warming climate... One approach to monitoring and modeling cyanobacterial biomass is to use processed satellite imagery to obtain long-term data sets... Modeled datasets of heat flux (as a proxy for stratification), wind speed, water temperature, and gelbstoff absorption... were used to separate bloom seasons in Green Bay from bloom seasons in nearby cyanobacteria bloom hotspots including western Lake Erie and Saginaw Bay.
Approach-sourcematerial: One approach to monitoring and modeling cyanobacterial biomass is to use processed satellite imagery to obtain long-term data sets. ... an existing algorithm for estimating cyanobacterial biomass previously developed for MERIS is validated for Green Bay using cyanobacteria biovolume estimates obtained from field samples. ... Modeled datasets of heat flux (as a proxy for stratification), wind speed, water temperature, and gelbstoff absorption along with in situ river discharge data were used to separate bloom seasons in Green Bay from bloom seasons in nearby cyanobacteria bloom hotspots including western Lake Erie and Saginaw Bay.
Methodology: A MERIS algorithm for estimating cyanobacterial biomass was validated for Green Bay using field samples of cyanobacteria biovolume; MERIS imagery was used to determine bloom phenology; modeled heat flux, wind speed, water temperature, and gelbstoff absorption, and in situ river discharge were used to separate bloom seasons in Green Bay from other hotspots, defining "high bloom" and "low bloom" years based on biomass.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "an existing algorithm for estimating cyanobacterial biomass previously developed for MERIS is validated for Green Bay using cyanobacteria biovolume estimates obtained from field samples. Once the algorithm was validated, the existing MERIS imagery was used to determine the bloom phenology of the cyanobacterial biomass in Green Bay. Modeled datasets of heat flux (as a proxy for stratification), wind speed, water temperature, and gelbstoff absorption along with in situ river discharge data were used to separate bloom seasons in Green Bay from bloom seasons in nearby cyanobacteria bloom hotspots including western Lake Erie and Saginaw Bay. Of the ten-year MERIS dataset used here, the highest five years were considered “high bloom” years, and the lowest five years from biomass were considered “low bloom” years and these definitions were used to separate Green Bay."
Highlights: Cyanobacteria blooms, exacerbated by warming climates, are monitored and modeled using satellite imagery. An algorithm validated for Green Bay, WI, using MERIS imagery and environmental datasets, revealed that Green Bay bloom intensity strongly relates to gelbstoff absorption, differing from western Lake Erie's reliance on river discharge and Saginaw Bay's lack of a dominant single parameter.

More Notes: Cyanobacteria blooms, exacerbated by climate warming, are monitored using satellite imagery; a validated algorithm estimates cyanobacterial biomass in Green Bay, revealing its bloom phenology and relationship with gelbstoff absorption, distinguishing it from bloom dynamics in western Lake Erie and Saginaw Bay.

One approach to monitoring and modeling cyanobacterial biomass is to use processed satellite imagery to obtain long-term data sets. ... an existing algorithm for estimating cyanobacterial biomass previously developed for MERIS is validated for Green Bay ... Once the algorithm was validated, the existing MERIS imagery was used to determine the bloom phenology of the cyanobacterial biomass in Green Bay. ... Green Bay had a strong relationship with gelbstoff absorption making it unique among the water bodies, while western Lake Erie responded strongly with river discharge as previously reported. Saginaw Bay, which has low interannual bloom variability, did not exhibit a largely influential single parameter.

Climate features-sourcematerial: Cyanobacteria blooms have been reported to be increasing worldwide. In addition to potentially causing major economic and ecological damage, these blooms can threaten human health. Furthermore, these blooms can be exacerbated by a warming climate. One approach to monitoring and modeling cyanobacterial biomass is to use processed satellite imagery to obtain long-term data sets. In this paper, an existing algorithm for estimating cyanobacterial biomass previously developed for MERIS is validated for Green Bay using cyanobacteria biovolume estimates obtained from field samples. Once the algorithm was validated, the existing MERIS imagery was used to determine the bloom phenology of the cyanobacterial biomass in Green Bay. Modeled datasets of heat flux (as a proxy for stratification), wind speed, water temperature, and gelbstoff absorption along with in situ river discharge data were used to separate bloom seasons in Green Bay from bloom seasons in nearby cyanobacteria bloom hotspots including western Lake Erie and Saginaw Bay. Of the ten-year MERIS dataset used here, the highest five years were considered “high bloom” years, and the lowest five years from biomass were considered “low bloom” years and these definitions were used to separate Green Bay. Green Bay had a strong relationship with gelbstoff absorption making it unique among the water bodies, while western Lake Erie responded strongly with river discharge as previously reported. Saginaw Bay, which has low interannual bloom variability, did not exhibit a largely influential single parameter. © 2022 by the authors.
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is not explicitly considered in the abstract. The study focuses on monitoring, modeling, and understanding the drivers of cyanobacteria blooms in different locations, but does not mention adaptation strategies or their effectiveness.
Adaptation considered-sourcematerial: The abstract focuses on "monitoring and modeling cyanobacterial biomass," validating an algorithm, determining bloom phenology, and identifying environmental factors influencing bloom seasons. There is no mention of adaptation strategies or their evaluation.

Paper Title: A machine learning based methodology for broken rail prediction on freight railroads: A case study in the United States
Outcome(s) of Interest: Reduced accidents and service disruptions; improved infrastructure safety; prioritized inspection and maintenance.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Predicting the occurrence of broken rails has safety and economic benefits, and reduces accidents and service disruptions... The model can potentially be used to prioritize inspection and maintenance activities on broken-rail-prone locations, and thus to further improve infrastructure safety given budgetary constraints.
Drivers of Risk: Weather conditions (minimum ambient temperature), detected rail defects, days since last broken rail, segment length, traffic density.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "The model also identifies that the number of detected rail defects within last 365 days, minimum ambient temperature of the last 30 days, days from the last broken rail, segment length, traffic density and other factors have significant influences on prediction results."
Category-sourcematerial: The weather condition data was collected from the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA). Based on time-series data partitioning, three different machine learning models are developed for predicting broken rail occurrence one month in advance. The selected models, including logistic regression, random forests, and gradient boosting, are trained using data from 2013 to 2018. The performance of three trained models are evaluated using the data from 2019. ...The model also identifies that the number of detected rail defects within last 365 days, minimum ambient temperature of the last 30 days, days from the last broken rail, segment length, traffic density and other factors have significant influences on prediction results. Using this model, 40.4% of broken rails can be successfully predicted one month in advance when focusing on 10% of the railroad network scanned.
Approach-sourcematerial: Predicting the occurrence of broken rails has safety and economic benefits, and reduces accidents and service disruptions. This paper aims to build a data-driven model for broken rail prediction using data related to infrastructure, operations, inspections, and weather conditions from 2013 to 2019. The railroad data was provided by one major Class I U.S. railroad. The weather condition data was collected from the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA). Based on time-series data partitioning, three different machine learning models are developed for predicting broken rail occurrence one month in advance. The selected models, including logistic regression, random forests, and gradient boosting, are trained using data from 2013 to 2018. The performance of three trained models are evaluated using the data from 2019. The relationship between the percentage of the network scanned and the percentage of broken rails found is used to identify locations that are more prone to broken rails. The findings of this study show that the gradient boosting model performs better than the other two methods for our datasets. The model also identifies that the number of detected rail defects within last 365 days, minimum ambient temperature of the last 30 days, days from the last broken rail, segment length, traffic density and other factors have significant influences on prediction results. Using this model, 40.4% of broken rails can be successfully predicted one month in advance when focusing on 10% of the railroad network scanned. The model can potentially be used to prioritize inspection and maintenance activities on broken-rail-prone locations, and thus to further improve infrastructure safety given budgetary constraints. © 2022 Elsevier Ltd
Methodology: Machine learning models (logistic regression, random forests, gradient boosting) were developed using railroad (infrastructure, operations, inspections) and NOAA weather data from 2013-2018, validated with 2019 data, to predict broken rail occurrence one month in advance, with gradient boosting performing best.

Paper Title: Avoided population exposure to extreme heat under two scenarios of global carbon neutrality by 2050 and 2060
Outcome(s) of Interest: Reduced population exposure to extreme heat under carbon neutrality scenarios.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "Global population exposure to extreme heat is mitigated by 27.3 billion person-days (7%) in the MODGREEN scenario and 39.9 billion person-days (11%) in the STRGREEN scenario during 2040-2049 relative to the SSP2-4.5 scenario."
Drivers of Risk: SSP2-4.5 scenario, Increased global mean extreme heat days, High population growth, Limited medical infrastructure.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "Global mean extreme heat days increase by 12.1 d yr−1 (108%) during 2040-2049 under the SSP2-4.5 scenario relative to the historical period (1995-2014). Paper Title: Matches and mismatches between the global distribution of major food crops and climate suitability
Outcome(s) of Interest: Mismatches between crop distributions and climate suitability; variations globally; high-income regions have better match; climate suitability not necessarily higher in regions of origin; divergence from climatic optima.

After investigating the role of biophysical, socio-economic and historical factors, we report that high-income world regions display a better match between crop distribution and climate suitability. In addition, although crops are farmed predominantly in the same climatic range as their wild progenitors, climate suitability is not necessarily higher there, a pattern that reflects the legacy of domestication history on current crop distribution."

Paper Title: Do water savings persist? Using survival models to plan for long-term responses to extreme drought
Outcome(s) of Interest: Residential water demand conservation survival and rebound after drought.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Understanding short- and long-term customer responses to these salient events is critical for infrastructure planning and capital investment... We find that, of 54% of customers with detected savings in 2014-2015, just 25% rebounded to prior consumption levels after 5 years, implying mean conservation survival of 8 years.
Drivers of Risk: Climate shocks, socio-economic stressors, health stressors, public awareness, media coverage, external drivers, drought severity
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Water utilities must maintain reliable service in a world where climate shocks and other socio-economic and health stressors are likely to disrupt water availability and demand more frequently. Although the short-term demand impacts of extreme droughts and related policy measures have been studied extensively, less is known about how these impacts persist—especially when driven by public awareness, media coverage, or other external drivers. Comparing the 2012-2016 drought to California’s milder 2007-2009 drought shows no evidence that drought severity associated with water savings persistence.
Category-sourcematerial: Water utilities must maintain reliable service in a world where climate shocks and other socio-economic and health stressors are likely to disrupt water availability and demand more frequently.
Approach-sourcematerial: Here, we introduce a novel approach combining survival models and change detection to assess water demand conservation ‘survival’ and rebound, using this method to analyze residential water demand in Costa Mesa, California after the state’s record-breaking 2012-2016 drought.
Methodology: Introduces a novel approach combining survival models and change detection to assess water demand conservation 'survival' and rebound after drought events.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Here, we introduce a novel approach combining survival models and change detection to assess water demand conservation ‘survival’ and rebound, using this method to analyze residential water demand in Costa Mesa, California after the state’s record-breaking 2012-2016 drought.
Highlights: Residential water conservation persists long-term after droughts, with 25% of Costa Mesa, CA customers rebounding to pre-drought consumption levels after 5 years, suggesting 8-year mean conservation survival; survival is greater in young, progressive neighborhoods and lower with occupancy changes.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Water demand conservation ‘survival’ and rebound were analyzed, revealing that of 54% of customers with detected savings in 2014-2015, just 25% rebounded to prior consumption levels after 5 years, implying mean conservation survival of 8 years. Survival was greater in young and politically progressive neighborhoods, smaller in residences with occupancy changes.
More Notes: Residential water demand in Costa Mesa, CA, showed significant conservation persistence after the 2012-2016 drought, with a mean conservation survival of 8 years, particularly in young and politically progressive neighborhoods; drought severity was not linked to persistence.

Paper Title: Maize stomatal responses against the climate change
Outcome(s) of Interest: Maize yield, drought and heat impact, climate change, stomatal regulation, gas exchange, stomatal traits, yield loss mitigation, elevated CO2 effects.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "Maize is one of most widely produced crops of the world...highly vulnerable to climate change, which triggers important losses in its productivity...maize copes with drought stress by reducing stomatal size and stomatal pore area, and increasing stomatal density, which, in turn, reduces transpiration and photosynthetic rate...Only when drought is not very severe, elevated CO2 levels reduce yield losses...knowledge of the upcoming climate changes together with the prediction of the developmental and physiological stomatal responses will allow not only to anticipate maize yield in the next years..."
Drivers of Risk: Drought, heat, climate change, increased stomatal density, reduced transpiration, reduced photosynthetic rate.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Drought and heat, in the context of climate change, are expected to increase in many agricultural areas across the globe. Among current abiotic stresses, they are the most limiting factors that influence crop growth and productivity. ... Despite its wide distribution in semi-arid regions, it is highly vulnerable to climate change, which triggers important losses in its productivity. This article explores how maize yield may persevere through climate change by focusing on the stomatal regulation of gas exchange. The emerging picture unravels that maize copes with drought stress by reducing stomatal size and stomatal pore area, and increasing stomatal density, which, in turn, reduces transpiration and photosynthetic rate.
Category-sourcematerial: Maize is one of most widely produced crops of the world...Despite its wide distribution in semi-arid regions, it is highly vulnerable to climate change, which triggers important losses in its productivity...anticipate maize yield in the next years, but also to contribute to the correct decision-making in the management of this important crop.
Approach-sourcematerial: "This article explores how maize yield may persevere through climate change by focusing on the stomatal regulation of gas exchange...maize copes with drought stress by reducing stomatal size and stomatal pore area, and increasing stomatal density, which, in turn, reduces transpiration and photosynthetic rate."
Methodology: The methodology focuses on examining how maize achieves drought resilience through its stomatal behavior modification, specifically by analyzing changes in stomatal size, pore area, and density in response to drought and heat conditions, and assessing how these changes affect transpiration, photosynthetic rates, and yield, including the interaction of ZmSHR1 and ZmSPCHs/ZmICE1 heterodimers.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "...maize copes with drought stress by reducing stomatal size and stomatal pore area, and increasing stomatal density, which, in turn, reduces transpiration and photosynthetic rate. When drought and heat co-occur, heat enhances stomatal response to drought stress. To avoid plant heat damage, the decline in stomatal aperture could trigger the expansion of the distance of action, from the longitudinal leaf veins, of ZmSHR1, which might act to positively regulate ZmSPCHs/ZmICE1 heterodimers, increasing the stomatal density...the prediction of the developmental and physiological stomatal responses will allow not only to anticipate maize yield in the next years..."
Highlights: Maize copes with drought by adjusting stomatal size, pore area, and density to reduce transpiration and photosynthesis; heat enhances this stomatal response to drought; elevated CO2 only mitigates yield losses under mild drought conditions; understanding these responses is crucial for predicting and managing maize yields under climate change.

Paper Title: Phenotypic variation from waterlogging in multiple perennial ryegrass varieties under climate change conditions
Outcome(s) of Interest: Reduced leaf-color intensity; lower plant performance and yield; tetraploids cope better than diploids; climate change treatment reduces color intensities further; reduced productivity via reductions in color pigments and root proliferation.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Long-term waterlogging was found to reduce leaf-color intensity, with younger plants having purple hues indicative of anthocyanins. Plant performance and yield was lower in waterlogged plants, with tetraploid varieties coping better than the diploid one. The climate change treatment was found to reduce color intensities further. Flooding was found to reduce plant productivity via reductions in color pigments and root proliferation.
Drivers of Risk: Climate change, waterlogging, temperature, CO2, flood-prone, phenotypic responses, productivity, extreme weather events, flooding, food security.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Climate change-induced waterlogging is understudied in comparison to temperature and CO2... the RCP8.5 climate change scenario in 2050 (+2°C and 550 ppm CO2)... increased frequency of extreme weather events and flooding... negative consequences for global food security.
Category-sourcematerial: Identifying how various components of climate change will influence ecosystems and vegetation subsistence will be fundamental to mitigate negative effects. ... Grasslands are especially vulnerable through the connection with global food security, with perennial ryegrass dominating many flood-prone pasturelands in North-western Europe. ... These effects will have negative consequences for global food security brought on by increased frequency of extreme weather events and flooding.
Approach-sourcematerial: Using image analysis and principal component analysis (PCA) methodologies, we assessed how multiple predictors (phenotypic, environmental, genotypic, and temporal) influenced overall plant performance, productivity and phenotypic responses.
Methodology: Perennial ryegrass (four varieties: one diploid, three tetraploid) was grown in growth chambers under ambient Northwestern Europe conditions and RCP8.5 2050 climate scenario (+2°C, 550 ppm CO2) with long-term waterlogging applied. Plant phenotypic measurements were taken monthly for two months, followed by harvesting and regrowth. Image analysis and principal component analysis (PCA) were used to assess the influence of phenotypic, environmental, genotypic, and temporal predictors on plant performance, productivity, and phenotypic responses.
Methodology-sourcematerial: We investigated the effect of long-term waterlogging on phenotypic responses of perennial ryegrass using four common varieties (one diploid and three tetraploid) grown in atmospherically controlled growth chambers during two months of peak growth. The climate treatments compare ambient climatological conditions in North-western Europe to the RCP8.5 climate change scenario in 2050 (+2°C and 550 ppm CO2). At the end of each month multiple phenotypic plant measurements were made, the plants were harvested and then allowed to grow back. Using image analysis and principal component analysis (PCA) methodologies, we assessed how multiple predictors (phenotypic, environmental, genotypic, and temporal) influenced overall plant performance, productivity and phenotypic responses.
Highlights: Long-term waterlogging negatively affects perennial ryegrass, reducing leaf color intensity and productivity, with tetraploid varieties performing better than diploid; climate change exacerbates these effects, impacting global food security, and imaging analysis may aid in remote plant health diagnostics.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Long-term waterlogging was found to reduce leaf-color intensity...Plant performance and yield was lower in waterlogged plants, with tetraploid varieties coping better than the diploid one. The climate change treatment was found to reduce color intensities further. Flooding was found to reduce plant productivity via reductions in color pigments and root proliferation. These effects will have negative consequences for global food security...Our imaging analysis approach to estimate effects of waterlogging can be incorporated into plant health diagnostics tools via remote sensing and drone-technology.
More Notes: Long-term waterlogging reduces leaf-color intensity (especially in younger plants), lowers plant performance and yield, and reduces root proliferation, with tetraploid perennial ryegrass varieties exhibiting better tolerance than diploid varieties; these effects are exacerbated by climate change conditions (+2°C and 550 ppm CO2) and have negative implications for global food security.

Flooding was found to reduce plant productivity via reductions in color pigments and root proliferation. These effects will have negative consequences for global food security brought on by increased frequency of extreme weather events and flooding.

Paper Title: Temperature Variability and the Macroeconomy: A World Tour
Outcome(s) of Interest: Productivity growth (positive/negative), welfare losses.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "higher intra-annual temperature variability reduces (increases) productivity in Europe and North America (Asia)...inter-annual temperature variability reduces (increases) productivity in North America (Europe)...welfare losses of 2.9% (1%) in Europe (North America)."
Drivers of Risk: Intra-annual temperature variability, inter-annual temperature variability, richer economies (Europe, North America), productivity reduction, welfare losses.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "First, higher intra-annual temperature variability reduces (increases) productivity in Europe and North America (Asia)." "Second, higher intra-annual temperature variability has no significant effects on productivity in Africa and South America." "(2) inter-annual temperature variability reduces (increases) productivity in North America (Europe)" "Taken together, these findings indicate that temperature variability shocks tend to have stronger adverse economic effects among richer economies." "In a production economy featuring long-run productivity and temperature volatility shocks, we quantify these negative impacts and find welfare losses of 2.9% (1%) in Europe (North America)."
Category-sourcematerial: We find two primary results. First, higher intra-annual temperature variability reduces (increases) productivity in Europe and North America (Asia)...Taken together, these findings indicate that temperature variability shocks tend to have stronger adverse economic effects among richer economies. In a production economy featuring long-run productivity and temperature volatility shocks, we quantify these negative impacts and find welfare losses of 2.9% (1%) in Europe (North America).
Approach-sourcematerial: This paper uses historical monthly temperature level data for a panel of 114 countries to identify the effects of within year temperature level variability on productivity growth in five different macro regions...Additional empirical tests indicate also the following...
Methodology: Uses historical monthly temperature data for 114 countries to assess the impact of within-year temperature variability on productivity growth across five macro regions (Africa, Asia, Europe, North America, and South America).
Methodology-sourcematerial: This paper uses historical monthly temperature level data for a panel of 114 countries to identify the effects of within year temperature level variability on productivity growth in five different macro regions, i.e., (1) Africa, (2) Asia, (3) Europe, (4) North America and (5) South America.
Highlights: Higher intra-annual temperature variability reduces productivity in Europe and North America, but increases it in Asia; Africa and South America see no significant effects; rising intra-annual variability reduces productivity in both tropical and non-tropical regions; inter-annual variability reduces productivity in North America but increases it in Europe; winter and summer inter-annual variability reduces productivity in Europe and North America; temperature variability shocks have stronger adverse economic effects among richer economies, resulting in welfare losses of 2.9% in Europe and 1% in North America.
Highlights-sourcematerial: First, higher intra-annual temperature variability reduces (increases) productivity in Europe and North America (Asia). Second, higher intra-annual temperature variability has no significant effects on productivity in Africa and South America... (1) rising intra-annual temperature variability reduces productivity (even thought less significantly)in both tropical and non-tropical regions, (2) inter-annual temperature variability reduces (increases) productivity in North America (Europe) and (3) winter and summer inter-annual temperature variability generates a drop in productivity in both Europe and North America. Taken together, these findings indicate that temperature variability shocks tend to have stronger adverse economic effects among richer economies. In a production economy featuring long-run productivity and temperature volatility shocks, we quantify these negative impacts and find welfare losses of 2.9% (1%) in Europe (North America).
More Notes: Rising intra-annual temperature variability reduces productivity in both tropical and non-tropical regions, while inter-annual temperature variability reduces productivity in North America but increases it in Europe; winter and summer inter-annual temperature variability generates a drop in productivity in both Europe and North America.

Paper Title: Comparison of sustainable flood risk management by four countries - the United Kingdom, the Netherlands, the United States, and Japan - and the implications for Asian coastal megacities
Outcome(s) of Interest: Lessons for developing coastal megacities in achieving long-term SFRM considering socio-economic and environmental concerns; informing policymakers on adopting "mixed options" for sustainable flood management.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "These case studies give good lessons in achieving long-term SFRM to deliver sound flood management practices considering socio-economic and environmental concerns...this paper explores how policymakers can adopt 'mixed options' to move towards long-term thinking about sustainability with social, economic, and environmental considerations...Most developing coastal megacities especially in Asia are still heavily reliant on a traditional hard-engineering approach, which may not be enough to mitigate substantial risks...to highlight lessons for coastal megacities in development."
Drivers of Risk: Flooding, urbanization, climate change, sea-level rise, population growth, socio-economic expansion, land subsidence, storm intensity, tropical cyclones, impermeable surfaces
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Flooding is a key driver, exacerbated by human factors like large population and rapid socio-economic growth, and natural factors such as climate change, sea-level rise, and land subsidence. (Page 1) Growth of assets exposed to flood hazards, including people and property, due to rapid land-use change, urbanization, and economic expansion. (Page 2) Increasing climatic extremes such as intensive precipitation are making it difficult to defend against all flooding. (Page 3) Severe flooding and tropical cyclones are identified as main risk drivers for the USA. (Page 5) Population expansion in coastal areas and climate change are likely to increase flood risk in the USA. (Page 6) Flooding is the primary driver of risk, exacerbated by factors like urban development reducing wetland storage and increasing impermeable surfaces in the USA. (Page 8)
Category-sourcematerial: According to the US National Weather Service (NWS), there was an average of 87 deaths per year from ﬂooding between 1989 and 2018...and 29 ﬂood events during the same period that resulted in over USD1 billion in economic losses, with an average event cost of USD4.3 million.
Approach-sourcematerial: The paper reviews past and current flood management experiences in the UK, the Netherlands, the US, and Japan to identify lessons for developing coastal megacities.
Methodology: The study reviews flood risk management (FRM) experiences in the UK, the Netherlands, the US, and Japan, and sustainable FRM (SFRM) concepts, to identify lessons for developing coastal megacities, also reviewing flood management practices in five selected Asian coastal cities.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "in this paper, we review FRM experience in four economies which are at the front end in applying the FRM approach (the UK, the Netherlands, the United States, and Japan) (Sect. 2). It additionally explores the wider concept of sustainable FRM (SFRM), which extends the scope of FRM to consider wider social and environmental goals (Sect. 3)." "The paper also reviews the development of ﬂood management practices in ﬁve selected Asian coastal cities (Jakarta, Indonesia; Bangkok, Thailand; Ho Chi Minh City, Vietnam; Guangzhou,China;andShanghai,China)todemonstratethe latest progress of FRM in these cities"
Highlights: Highlights from the paper include: a shift from flood defense to integrating sustainability and wider social/environmental goals in flood risk management (SFRM); the evolution towards flood preparedness and public awareness; recognition of unaffordability of traditional flood defenses, and the need for mixed options; UK integrating FRM with land-use planning; US experiencing severe flooding and significant economic losses; Mississippi River flood leading to emphasis on managing floodplains as natural ecosystems; Japan integrating hard/soft measures with community resilience; and Asian cities increasingly integrating SFRM.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Source material: Page 2: "It additionally explores the wider concept of sustainable FRM (SFRM), which extends the scope of FRM to consider wider social and environmental goals"; Page 3: "The emphasis had moved from protecting farmland to protecting a broader asset base that underpinned economic growth. Thus, policymakers were keen on implementing flood alleviation schemes and projects that defended people and property (homes and businesses)."; Page 5: "The United States of America (USA) regularly experiences severe ﬂooding."; Page 7: "Floodplains should be managed as part of the natural ecosystem, with risk-based forecasting used to inform ﬂood management."; Page 8: "While the USA has made important and successful strides towards SFRM, more is needed to achieve its goals."; Page 9: "Key aspects include the shift from hard-engineering approaches to integrating hard and soft measures, emphasis on community resilience, and legislative efforts to address flood risk in urban areas."; Page 15: "Confronted with climate change, the existing levels of protection are not enough. There is an urgency to improve flood protection standards with heavier investment financially, but we also need to address social and environmental perspectives...", "...A key impetus of the shift in practice from flood protection and defence paradigms to SFRM has been a recognition that the costs of traditional hard-engineering flood defences are increasingly unaffordable..."
More Notes: An important aspect of Flood Risk Management (FRM) involves stakeholder engagement across various groups to identify multi-disciplinary perspectives and solutions.
More Notes-sourcematerial: Page 3, More Notes: "An important aspect of FRM is to manage flood risk through engagement with stakeholders more widely (e.g. households, practitioners, politicians, flood engineers, planners, and communities) to identify multi-disciplinary perspectives and solutions (Pitt, 2008; Yang, 2020)."
Hazard definition-sourcematerial:
1. Page 1: This paper reviews the past and current flood management experiences from flood defence to SFRM in four developed countries to highlight lessons for coastal megacities in development.
2. Page 2: "ﬂood hazard is increasing as climate change raises sea surface temperature, driving oceanic thermal expansion and increasing the intensity and frequency of precipitation events and natural land subsidence."
3. Page 3: "In the 2000s, the focus changed again to more explicitly consider flood risk (Plate, 2002), including the probability of a given flood hazard (e.g. precipitation, storm frequency, sea-level rise)…"
4. Page 14: "These cities have experienced more floods, bringing greater urgency for governments to progress and consider wider socio-economic and environmental pillars in flood risk management."
Impact persistence-sourcematerial: the paper explores how policymakers can adopt “mixed options” to move towards long-term thinking about sustainability with social, economic, and environmental considerations.
Space disaggregation-sourcematerial: Comparison of sustainable flood risk management (SFRM) practices in four front-end economies: the UK, the Netherlands, the United States, and Japan, and review of development in five Asian coastal cities.
Impact interactions-sourcematerial: The paper reviews past and current flood management experiences in the UK, the Netherlands, the US, and Japan to identify lessons for developing coastal megacities.
Economic dynamics-sourcematerial: Drivers of Risk: "the assets exposed to ﬂood hazards, includingpeopleandproperty,aregrowingthroughrapidland-use change, urbanisation, and economic expansion"
Climate features-sourcematerial: Most developing coastal megacities especially in Asia are still heavily reliant on a traditional hard-engineering approach, which may not be enough to mitigate substantial risks due to human factors (e.g. large population, rapid socio-economic growth, subsidence from excessive groundwater extraction) and natural factors (e.g. climate change including sea-level rise and landsubsidence).
Adaptation considered: Adaptation is considered through flood warning and evacuation systems (Japan), integrating FRM with land-use planning (UK), and raising flood protection levels of coastal defenses (Shanghai). The paper also refers to the development of flood preparedness and public awareness in Europe and the shift to considering flood risk, including sea-level rise.
Adaptation considered-sourcematerial: "Japan has developed an advanced ﬂood warning and evacuation contingency system to prepare for climatic extremes.", "UK ﬂood policy thus seeks to integrate FRM with land-use planning, considering future development and ﬂood risk, which addresses social, economic, and environmental criteria.", "For example, the Shanghai authority acted to raise the flood protection level of coastal defence from a 1-in-100-year to 1-in-1000-year protection level during the early 2000s...", "This change in focus gave greater attention to flood preparedness and public awareness through, for example, the flood forecasting and warning systems that were developed across Europe.", "In the 2000s, the focus changed again to more explicitly consider flood risk (Plate, 2002), including the probability of a given flood hazard (e.g. precipitation, storm frequency, sea-level rise)…"

Paper Title: The past, present, and future of coral reef growth in the Florida Keys
Outcome(s) of Interest: Shift from positive to negative carbonate budgets, reduced reef-building capacity, decline in essential ecological/geological/socioeconomic functions.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Coral-reef degradation is driving global-scale reductions in reef-building capacity and the ecological, geological, and socioeconomic functions it supports...declines in reef-building Acropora palmata and Orbicella spp. corals following a series of thermal stress events and coral disease outbreaks resulted in a shift from positive to negative budgets for most reefs in the region.
Drivers of Risk: Thermal stress, coral disease outbreaks, reef degradation, declining Acropora palmata, declining Orbicella spp., negative carbonate budgets, erosion, nonbiological physical and chemical erosion, environmental changes.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Coral-reef degradation is driving global-scale reductions in reef-building capacity... declines in reef-building Acropora palmata and Orbicella spp. corals following a series of thermal stress events and coral disease outbreaks resulted in a shift from positive to negative budgets for most reefs in the region... By 2019, only ~15% of reefs had positive net carbonate production... census-based carbonate budgets may underestimate total erosion by ~1 mm year−1 (−1.15 kg CaCO3 m−2 year−1).
Category-sourcematerial: Coral-reef degradation is driving global-scale reductions in reef-building capacity and the ecological, geological, and socioeconomic functions it supports.
Approach-sourcematerial: Here, we reconstructed census-based carbonate budgets of 46 reefs throughout the Florida Keys from 1996 to 2019. We evaluated the environmental and ecological drivers of changing budget states and compared historical trends in reef-accretion potential to millennial-scale baselines of accretion from reef cores and future projections with coral restoration.
Methodology: Reconstructed census-based carbonate budgets of 46 reefs throughout the Florida Keys from 1996 to 2019, evaluated environmental and ecological drivers of changing budget states, and compared historical trends in reef-accretion potential to millennial-scale baselines from reef cores and future projections with coral restoration.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Here, we reconstructed census-based carbonate budgets of 46 reefs throughout the Florida Keys from 1996 to 2019. We evaluated the environmental and ecological drivers of changing budget states and compared historical trends in reef-accretion potential to millennial-scale baselines of accretion from reef cores and future projections with coral restoration.
Highlights: Florida Keys coral reefs have shifted from net accretion to net erosion due to coral decline from thermal stress and disease; only ~15% of reefs now show positive carbonate production, primarily in low-irradiance inshore habitats; restoration offers potential mitigation but doesn't address the underlying climate change threat.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Coral-reef degradation is driving global-scale reductions in reef-building capacity and the ecological, geological, and socioeconomic functions it supports. ... declines in reef-building Acropora palmata and Orbicella spp. corals following a series of thermal stress events and coral disease outbreaks resulted in a shift from positive to negative budgets for most reefs in the region. By 2019, only ~15% of reefs had positive net carbonate production. Most of those reefs were in inshore, Lower Keys patch-reef habitats with low water clarity, supporting the hypothesis that environments with naturally low irradiance may provide a refugia from thermal stress. ... the restoration of reef-building corals has the potential to help mitigate declines in reef accretion in some locations, which could allow some key ecosystem functions to be maintained until the threat of global climate change is addressed.
More Notes: Florida Keys coral reefs have shifted from net accretion to net erosion due to coral loss from thermal stress and disease, with only 15% showing positive net carbonate production by 2019; inshore, low-irradiance reefs may offer refugia, but census-based carbonate budgets likely overestimate accretion by not accounting for physical and chemical erosion, though coral restoration shows potential for mitigating declines.

By 2019, only ~15% of reefs had positive net carbonate production. Most of those reefs were in inshore, Lower Keys patch-reef habitats with low water clarity, supporting the hypothesis that environments with naturally low irradiance may provide a refugia from thermal stress. ...census-based carbonate budgets may underestimate total erosion by ~1 mm year−1 (−1.15 kg CaCO3 m−2 year−1). Although the present state of Florida's reefs is dire, we demonstrate that the restoration of reef-building corals has the potential to help mitigate declines in reef accretion in some locations...

Paper Title: Examining the Relationship between Climate Change and Vibriosis in the United States: Projected Health and Economic Impacts for the 21st Century
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased vibriosis incidence, morbidity, mortality, medical costs, lost income, mortality-related indirect costs.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Vibriosis is an illness contracted through food-and waterborne exposures to various Vibrio species... RESULTS: By 2090, increases in SST are estimated to result in a 51% increase in cases annually... The cost of these illnesses is projected to reach $5:2 billion annually under RCP4.5, and $7:3 billion annually under RCP8.5... Evidence suggests that other factors... may contribute to further increases in vibri-osis cases... costing billions of dollars. These costs are mostly attributable to deaths, pri-marily from exposure to V. vulnificus.
Drivers of Risk: Sea surface temperature (SST), Vibrio species (V. parahaemolyticus, V. vulnificus, V. alginolyticus, V. spp.), climate change (RCP4.5, RCP8.5).
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "increases in SST are estimated to result in a 51% increase in cases annually relative to the baseline era (centered on 1995) under Representative Concentration Pathway (RCP) 4.5, and a 108% increase under RCP8.5" ... "separately reported results for V. parahaemolyticus, V. vulnificus, V. alginolyticus, and “V. spp.,” given the different associated health burden of each."
Category-sourcematerial: BACKGROUND: This paper represents, to our knowledge, the first national-level (United States) estimate of the economic impacts of vibriosis cases as exacerbated by climate change. Vibriosis is an illness contracted through food-and waterborne exposures to various Vibrio species (e.g., nonV. chol-erae O1 and O139 serotypes) found in estuarine and marine environments, including within aquatic life, such as shellfish and finfish. RESULTS: By 2090, increases in SST are estimated to result in a 51% increase in cases annually relative to the baseline era (centered on 1995) under Representative Concentration Pathway (RCP) 4.5, and a 108% increase under RCP8.5. The cost of these illnesses is projected to reach $5:2 billion annually under RCP4.5, and $7:3 billion annually under RCP8.5, relative to $2:2 billion in the baseline (2018 U.S. dollars), equivalent to 140% and 234% increases respectively. DISCUSSION: Vibriosis incidence is likely to increase in the United States under moderate and unmitigated climate change scenarios through increases in SST, resulting in a substantial burden of morbidity and mortality, and costing billions of dollars.
Approach-sourcematerial: METHODS: For our analysis to identify climate links to vibriosis incidence, we constructed three logistic regression models by Vibrio species, using vibriosis data sourced from the Cholera and Other Vibrio Illness Surveillance system and historical SSTs. We relied on previous estimates of the cost-per-case of vibriosis to estimate future total annual medical costs, lost income from productivity loss, and mortality-related indirect costs throughout the United States.
Methodology: Logistic regression models were constructed by Vibrio species using vibriosis data from the Cholera and Other Vibrio Illness Surveillance system and historical sea surface temperatures to identify climate links to vibriosis incidence, then previous estimates of the cost-per-case of vibriosis were used to estimate future total annual medical costs, lost income from productivity loss, and mortality-related indirect costs throughout the United States.
Methodology-sourcematerial: For our analysis to identify climate links to vibriosis incidence, we constructed three logistic regression models by Vibrio species, using vibriosis data sourced from the Cholera and Other Vibrio Illness Surveillance system and historical SSTs. We relied on previous estimates of the cost-per-case of vibriosis to estimate future total annual medical costs, lost income from productivity loss, and mortality-related indirect costs throughout the United States.
Highlights: National-level estimates project a significant increase in vibriosis cases and associated economic burden in the U.S. by 2090 due to rising sea surface temperatures, with costs reaching $5.2 to $7.3 billion annually under RCP4.5 and RCP8.5 scenarios, driven mainly by fatalities from V. vulnificus.
Highlights-sourcematerial: By 2090, increases in SST are estimated to result in a 51% increase in cases annually relative to the baseline era (centered on 1995) under Representative Concentration Pathway (RCP) 4.5, and a 108% increase under RCP8.5. The cost of these illnesses is projected to reach $5:2 billion annually under RCP4.5, and $7:3 billion annually under RCP8.5, relative to $2:2 billion in the baseline (2018 U.S. dollars), equivalent to 140% and 234% increases respectively. ...Vibriosis incidence is likely to increase in the United States under moderate and unmitigated climate change scenarios through increases in SST, resulting in a substantial burden of morbidity and mortality, and costing billions of dollars. These costs are mostly attributable to deaths, pri-marily from exposure to V. vulnificus.
More Notes: Climate change, specifically rising sea surface temperatures, is projected to significantly increase vibriosis cases and associated costs in the U.S. by 2090, primarily due to V. vulnificus related deaths, with potential contributions from other factors like sea surface salinity.
More Notes-sourcematerial: "By 2090, increases in SST are estimated to result in a 51% increase in cases annually relative to the baseline era (centered on 1995) under Representative Concentration Pathway (RCP) 4.5, and a 108% increase under RCP8.5...The cost of these illnesses is projected to reach $5:2 billion annually under RCP4.5, and $7:3 billion annually under RCP8.5, relative to $2:2 billion in the baseline (2018 U.S. dollars)...These costs are mostly attributable to deaths, pri-marily from exposure to V. vulnificus...Evidence suggests that other factors, including sea surface salinity, may contribute to further increases in vibri-osis cases in some regions of the United States and should also be investigated."

The results also discuss expected increases in vibriosis cases and costs by 2090 under different climate scenarios, further supporting the categorization as a chronic trend.

DISCUSSION: Vibriosis incidence is likely to increase in the United States under moderate and unmitigated climate change scenarios through increases in SST, resulting in a substantial burden of morbidity and mortality, and costing billions of dollars.

Paper Title: Remotely sensed reservoir water storage dynamics (1984-2015) and the influence of climate variability and management at a global scale
Outcome(s) of Interest: Reservoir storage changes; Regional declines (Australia, SW USA, E Brazil); Regional gains (Nile, Mediterranean, S Africa); Precipitation/river inflow drivers; Minimal evaporation impact; Water release impact uncertain.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Many of the observed reservoir changes could be explained by changes in precipitation and river inflows, emphasizing the importance of multi-decadal precipitation changes for reservoir water storage. We find reservoir storage has diminished substantially for 23% of reservoirs over the three decades, but increased for 21%. The greatest declines were for dry basins in southeastern Australia (-29%), southwestern USA (-10%), and eastern Brazil (-9%). The greatest gains occurred in the Nile Basin (+67%), Mediterranean basins (+31%) and southern Africa (+22%). Our results also show that there is generally little impact from changes in net evaporation on storage trends. A more definitive conclusion about the impact of changes in water releases at the global or local scale would require data that unfortunately are not publicly available for the vast majority of reservoirs globally.
Drivers of Risk: precipitation changes, river inflows, multi-decadal precipitation, water withdrawal
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Many of the observed reservoir changes could be explained by changes in precipitation and river inflows, emphasizing the importance of multi-decadal precipitation changes for reservoir water storage... Based on the reservoir water balance, we deduce it is unlikely that water release trends dominate global trends in reservoir storage dynamics. This inference is further supported by different spatial patterns in water withdrawal and storage trends globally.
Category-sourcematerial: We relate reservoir storage to resilience and vulnerability and investigate interactions between precipitation, streamflow, evaporation, and reservoir water storage. ... We find reservoir storage has diminished substantially for 23% of reservoirs over the three decades, but increased for 21%. The greatest declines were for dry basins in southeastern Australia (-29%), southwestern USA (-10%), and eastern Brazil (-9%). The greatest gains occurred in the Nile Basin (+67%), Mediterranean basins (+31%) and southern Africa (+22%). Many of the observed reservoir changes could be explained by changes in precipitation and river inflows, emphasizing the importance of multi-decadal precipitation changes for reservoir water storage.
Approach-sourcematerial: We overcame this by using optical (Landsat) and altimetry remote sensing to reconstruct monthly water storage for 6695 reservoirs worldwide between 1984 and 2015. We relate reservoir storage to resilience and vulnerability and investigate interactions between precipitation, streamflow, evaporation, and reservoir water storage. This is based on a comprehensive analysis of streamflow from a multi-model ensemble and as observed at ca. 8000 gauging stations, precipitation from a combination of station, satellite and forecast data, and open water evaporation estimates. Uncertainty in the analysis can come from, among others, the relatively low Landsat imaging frequency for parts of the Earth and the simple geo-statistical bathymetry model used.
Methodology: Optical (Landsat) and altimetry remote sensing were used to reconstruct monthly water storage for 6695 reservoirs worldwide (1984-2015), relating storage to resilience/vulnerability and investigating interactions between precipitation, streamflow (from multi-model ensemble & 8000 gauging stations), evaporation, and reservoir water storage, alongside analysis of precipitation data from station, satellite, and forecast sources, and open water evaporation estimates.

Highlights: Global analysis using Landsat and altimetry data from 1984-2015 reveals significant changes in reservoir storage, with notable reductions in dry basins and increases in others, emphasizing precipitation's role while showing minimal evaporation impact on storage trends.

Paper Title: An ecosystem-based natural capital evaluation framework that combines environmental and socio-economic implications of offshore renewable energy developments
Outcome(s) of Interest: Ecosystem change (physical & biological indicators), changes in ecosystem goods and services, changes in socio-economic value (due to ORE deployment & climate change).
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: To address this shortfall, we propose an ecosystem-based natural capital evaluation framework that builds on a dynamic Bayesian modelling approach which accounts for the multiplicity of interactions between physical (e.g. bottom temperature), biological (e.g. net primary production) indicators and anthropogenic marine use (i.e. fishing) and their changes across space and over time. The proposed assessment framework measures ecosystem change, changes in ecosystem goods and services and changes in socio-economic value in response to ORE deployment scenarios as well as climate change, to provide objective information for decision processes seeking to integrate new uses into our marine ecosystems.
Drivers of Risk: Offshore renewable energy, environmental effects, bio-physical changes, ecosystem impacts, marine spatial conflicts, marine planning.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "the nexus between drivers, such as changes in the bio-physical environment from the introduction of structures and extraction of energy, and the consequent impacts on ecosystem services delivery and natural capital assets is poorly understood...spatial conflicts with marine uses like shipping, fishing, and recreation...direct and indirect environmental effects...ORE deployment scenarios as well as climate change"
Category-sourcematerial: Future marine planning needs to assess these changes as part of national policy level assessments but also to inform practitioners about the benefits and trade-offs between different uses of natural resources when making decisions to balance environmental and energy sustainability and socio-economic impacts.
Approach-sourcematerial: To address this shortfall, we propose an ecosystem-based natural capital evaluation framework that builds on a dynamic Bayesian modelling approach which accounts for the multiplicity of interactions between physical (e.g. bottom temperature), biological (e.g. net primary production) indicators and anthropogenic marine use (i.e. fishing) and their changes across space and over time.
Methodology: An ecosystem-based natural capital evaluation framework is proposed, utilizing dynamic Bayesian modeling to assess the interactions between physical, biological, and anthropogenic factors in response to offshore renewable energy deployment and climate change, measuring impacts on ecosystem goods, services, and socio-economic value.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "To address this shortfall, we propose an ecosystem-based natural capital evaluation framework that builds on a dynamic Bayesian modelling approach which accounts for the multiplicity of interactions between physical (e.g. bottom temperature), biological (e.g. net primary production) indicators and anthropogenic marine use (i.e. fishing) and their changes across space and over time...The proposed assessment framework measures ecosystem change, changes in ecosystem goods and services and changes in socio-economic value in response to ORE deployment scenarios as well as climate change..."
Highlights: Offshore renewable energy (ORE) deployment, while combating climate change, necessitates trade-offs between environmental impacts, spatial conflicts with marine uses, and poorly understood ecosystem effects; an ecosystem-based natural capital evaluation framework using dynamic Bayesian modelling can assess these changes, measuring ecosystem, goods/services, and socio-economic value shifts under ORE and climate scenarios to inform balanced marine resource management decisions.
Highlights-sourcematerial: There is about to be an abrupt step-change in the use of coastal seas around the globe, specifically by the addition of large-scale offshore renewable energy (ORE) developments to combat climate change. Developing this sustainable energy supply will require trade-offs between both direct and indirect environmental effects, as well as spatial conflicts with marine uses like shipping, fishing, and recreation. However, the nexus between drivers, such as changes in the bio-physical environment from the introduction of structures and extraction of energy, and the consequent impacts on ecosystem services delivery and natural capital assets is poorly understood and rarely considered through a whole ecosystem perspective. Future marine planning needs to assess these changes as part of national policy level assessments but also to inform practitioners about the benefits and trade-offs between different uses of natural resources when making decisions to balance environmental and energy sustainability and socio-economic impacts. To address this shortfall, we propose an ecosystem-based natural capital evaluation framework that builds on a dynamic Bayesian modelling approach which accounts for the multiplicity of interactions between physical (e.g. bottom temperature), biological (e.g. net primary production) indicators and anthropogenic marine use (i.e. fishing) and their changes across space and over time. The proposed assessment framework measures ecosystem change, changes in ecosystem goods and services and changes in socio-economic value in response to ORE deployment scenarios as well as climate change, to provide objective information for decision processes seeking to integrate new uses into our marine ecosystems. Such a framework has the potential of exploring the likely outcomes in the same metrics (both ecological and socio-economic) from alternative management and climate scenarios, such that objective judgements and decisions can be made, as to how to balance the benefits and trade-offs between a range of marine uses to deliver long-term environmental sustainability, economic benefits, and social welfare.
More Notes: Offshore renewable energy development, while crucial for climate change mitigation, introduces trade-offs between environmental impacts, spatial conflicts with existing marine uses, and ecosystem service delivery, necessitating an ecosystem-based natural capital evaluation framework for informed decision-making.
