Paper Title: Crop genetic diversity uncovers metabolites, elements, and gene networks predicted to be associated with high plant biomass yields in maize
Outcome(s) of Interest: Increased biomass yield of maize under adverse environmental factors.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "...open new opportunities for efficient, rapid, and cost-effective plant breeding to increase biomass yield of the cereal crop maize under adverse environmental factors."
Drivers of Risk: Climate change, population growth, demand for food/feed/bioenergy, need for increased biomass production, nutrient deficiencies (specifically phosphate), genotype-dependent nutrient uptake/assimilation/allocation.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Rapid population growth and increasing demand for food, feed, and bioenergy in these times of unprecedented climate change require breeding for increased biomass production on the world s croplands...We found that S and P content in the source leaf and P content in the root during the vegetative stage contributed the most to predicting plant performance at the reproductive stage...our data demonstrate that genotype-dependent uptake, assimilation, and allocation of essential nutrient elements (especially C and N) during vegetative growth under phosphate starvation plays an important role in determining plant biomass...
Category-sourcematerial: Rapid population growth and increasing demand for food, feed, and bioenergy in these times of unprecedented climate change require breeding for increased biomass production on the world s croplands...integrative multiomics results revealed key factors underlying maize productivity and open new opportunities for efficient, rapid, and cost-effective plant breeding to increase biomass yield of the cereal crop maize under adverse environmental factors.
Approach-sourcematerial: Analysis of the datasets, integrated using regression modeling and network analysis revealed key metabolites, elements, gene transcripts, and gene networks, whose contents during vegetative growth substantially influence the build-up of plant biomass in the reproductive phase.
Methodology: Large-scale multiomics datasets from genetically diverse maize lines grown in organic and conventional systems were analyzed using regression modeling and network analysis to identify key metabolites, elements, gene transcripts, and gene networks influencing biomass production.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "large-scale multiomics datasets were created with genetically diverse maize lines, all grown in long-term organic and conventional cropping systems. Analysis of the datasets, integrated using regression modeling and network analysis revealed key metabolites, elements, gene transcripts, and gene networks, whose contents during vegetative growth substantially influence the build-up of plant biomass in the reproductive phase."
Highlights: The study utilizes large-scale multiomics datasets of genetically diverse maize lines to identify key metabolites, elements, and gene networks influencing biomass production, highlighting the critical roles of S and P content in leaves and roots during vegetative growth for predicting reproductive phase performance, and emphasizing the impact of genotype-dependent nutrient uptake under phosphate deficiency on maize productivity and breeding efficiency.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Rapid population growth and increasing demand for food, feed, and bioenergy in these times of unprecedented climate change require breeding for increased biomass production on the world s croplands... large-scale multiomics datasets were created with genetically diverse maize lines... Analysis of the datasets...revealed key metabolites, elements, gene transcripts, and gene networks, whose contents during vegetative growth substantially influence the build-up of plant biomass in the reproductive phase. We found that S and P content in the source leaf and P content in the root during the vegetative stage contributed the most to predicting plant performance at the reproductive stage... Furthermore, our data demonstrate that genotype-dependent uptake, assimilation, and allocation of essential nutrient elements (especially C and N) during vegetative growth under phosphate starvation plays an important role in determining plant biomass...These integrative multiomics results revealed key factors underlying maize productivity and open new opportunities for efficient, rapid, and cost-effective plant breeding to increase biomass yield of the cereal crop maize under adverse environmental factors.
More Notes: Multiomics analysis of maize lines identified key metabolites, elements (S, P, C, N), gene transcripts, and networks influencing biomass, highlighting the importance of nutrient uptake and allocation under phosphate deficiency for improving maize productivity.
More Notes-sourcematerial: "Analysis of the datasets, integrated using regression modeling and network analysis revealed key metabolites, elements, gene transcripts, and gene networks, whose contents during vegetative growth substantially influence the build-up of plant biomass in the reproductive phase...Furthermore, our data demonstrate that genotype-dependent uptake, assimilation, and allocation of essential nutrient elements (especially C and N) during vegetative growth under phosphate starvation plays an important role in determining plant biomass by controlling root traits related to nutrient uptake."
Hazard definition-sourcematerial: The abstract focuses on increasing biomass production in maize under "adverse environmental factors" and phosphate deficiency, aiming to improve breeding programs for greater yield. There is no specific mention of climate hazards, acute shocks, or chronic trends.
Impact persistence-sourcematerial: Rapid population growth and increasing demand for food, feed, and bioenergy in these times of unprecedented climate change require breeding for increased biomass production on the world s croplands. To accelerate breeding programs, knowledge of the relationship between biomass features and underlying gene networks is needed to guide future breeding efforts. To this end, large-scale multiomics datasets were created with genetically diverse maize lines, all grown in long-term organic and conventional cropping systems. Analysis of the datasets, integrated using regression modeling and network analysis revealed key metabolites, elements, gene transcripts, and gene networks, whose contents during vegetative growth substantially influence the build-up of plant biomass in the reproductive phase. We found that S and P content in the source leaf and P content in the root during the vegetative stage contributed the most to predicting plant performance at the reproductive stage. In agreement with the Gene Ontology enrichment analysis, the cis-motifs and identified transcription factors associated with upregulated genes under phosphate deficiency showed great diversity in the molecular response to phosphate deficiency in selected lines. Furthermore, our data demonstrate that genotype-dependent uptake, assimilation, and allocation of essential nutrient elements (especially C and N) during vegetative growth under phosphate starvation plays an important role in determining plant biomass by controlling root traits related to nutrient uptake. These integrative multiomics results revealed key factors underlying maize productivity and open new opportunities for efficient, rapid, and cost-effective plant breeding to increase biomass yield of the cereal crop maize under adverse environmental factors. © The Author(s) 2022. Paper Title: Model-based prediction of the potential geographical distribution of the invasive coconut mite, Aceria guerreronis Keifer (Acari: Eriophyidae) based on MaxEnt
Outcome(s) of Interest: Potential global distribution, Aceria guerreronis, habitat suitability, MaxEnt model, climate change impacts, 2040-2060, SSP1-2.6, SSP5-8.5, geographical risk areas
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "Therefore, we predicted the potential global distribution A. guerreronis using a machine learning algorithm based on maximum entropy. The potential future distribution for A. guerreronis covered the 2040 and 2060 periods under two climate change emission scenarios (SSP1-2.6 and SSP5-8.5) in the context of the sixth assessment report (AR6) of the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change. The MaxEnt model predicts the habitat suitability for A. guerreronis outside its present distribution, with suitable habitats in Oceania, Asia, Africa, and the Americas. The habitat suitability for the pest will decrease from 2040 to 2060. The areas with the highest risk of A. guerreronis are those with an annual average temperature of around 25°C, mean annual precipitation of about 1459 mm, mean precipitation seasonality close to 64%, an average variation of daytime temperature of about 8.6°C, and mean seasonality of temperature of about 149.7°C."
Drivers of Risk: Climate change, temperature (seasonality, average, daytime variation), precipitation (annual, seasonality), global trade, IPCC AR6 scenarios (SSP1-2.6, SSP5-8.5).

Approach-sourcematerial: Page 1: "We predicted the potential global distribution A. guerreronis using a machine learning algorithm based on maximum entropy."; Page 3: "...for the first time, we used MaxEnt to model and map regions suitable for coconut mites..."; Page 9: "The study uses the MaxEnt model to predict current and future distributions of the coconut mite, Aceria guerreronis."
Methodology: MaxEnt was utilized to predict the potential distribution of Aceria guerreronis, using climatic data from WorldClim, emphasizing nineteen bioclimatic variables. The model used occurrence records primarily from Ghana sampled between 2019-2021, supplemented by literature data, while accounting for spatial autocorrelation via spatial filtering. The dataset was split into 80% for training and 20% for testing, validated with an AUC value exceeding 0.9, supported by jackknife tests and logistic response curves. Variables such as temperature seasonality, annual average temperature, and annual precipitation were important predictors. Two climate change emission scenarios (SSP1-2.6 and SSP5-8.5) were applied to assess future distribution impacts, indicating habitat contraction due to climate factors dominating over others like precipitation.

[...] In the present study, we used two socioeconomic pathways (SSPs 2.6 and 8.5)."; Page 3: "The climatic data used for this study were gathered from the WorldClim environmental database (http://www.worldclim.org/)..."; Page 9: "Our model performance was high, with an AUC value of more than 0.9, suggesting that the model’s predictions are reliable."; Page 11: "For the SSPs, the model predicts a contraction of suitable areas from SSPs 2.6 to 8.5."
Highlights: For the first time, MaxEnt was used to model and map regions suitable for coconut mites to provide information for the implementation of quarantine measures and highlight the need for policy formulation; Model performance was high with an AUC value of more than 0.9, indicating reliable predictions; Rising global temperatures will mean that regions that are at present unsuitable to coconut mites, especially in Brazil, India, Australia, Nigeria, and Kenya, will become suitable habitats from now until 2040.

Paper Title: Global Health Impacts for Economic Models of Climate Change A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
Outcome(s) of Interest: Net increases in mortality risk associated with increased average annual temperatures (ranging from 0.1% to 1.1% per 1°C) were estimated for all global regions.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Results: Effect estimates and associated uncertainties varied by global region, but net increases in mortality risk associated with increased average annual temperatures (ranging from 0.1% to 1.1% per 1°C) were estimated for all global regions.
Drivers of Risk: Temperature increase, socioeconomic trajectories, adaptation factors, air pollution.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "Regionally resolved effect estimates of unit increases in temperature on net all-cause mortality risk were generated...but net increases in mortality risk associated with increased average annual temperatures (ranging from 0.1% to 1.1% per 1°C) were estimated for all global regions...accounting for socioeconomic trajectories and adaptation factors when estimating health damages; and carefully considering how air pollution impacts should be incorporated in economic-climate models."
Category: Health > Heat and Cold (Cardiovascular, Respiratory, etc.)
Category-sourcematerial: Rationale: Avoiding excess health damages attributable to climate change is a primary motivator for policy interventions to reduce greenhouse gas emissions. ... Regionally resolved effect estimates of unit increases in temperature on net all-cause mortality risk were generated through random-effects pooling of studies identified through a systematic review. Results: Effect estimates and associated uncertainties varied by global region, but net increases in mortality risk associated with increased average annual temperatures (ranging from 0.1% to 1.1% per 1°C) were estimated for all global regions.
Approach-sourcematerial: Methods: Regionally resolved effect estimates of unit increases in temperature on net all-cause mortality risk were generated through random-effects pooling of studies identified through a systematic review. Results: Effect estimates and associated uncertainties varied by global region, but net increases in mortality risk associated with increased average annual temperatures (ranging from 0.1% to 1.1% per 1°C) were estimated for all global regions.
Methodology: Regionally resolved effect estimates of temperature increases on all-cause mortality risk were generated through random-effects pooling of studies identified via systematic review.
Methodology-sourcematerial: Regionally resolved effect estimates of unit increases in temperature on net all-cause mortality risk were generated through random-effects pooling of studies identified through a systematic review.
Highlights: Expert panel meta-analysis of temperature-related mortality risk found net increases globally (0.1-1.1% per 1°C), recommending broader mortality impact inclusion, improved climate parameters, adaptation/socioeconomic factor consideration, and careful air pollution impact integration in economic-climate models.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Regionally resolved effect estimates of unit increases in temperature on net all-cause mortality risk were generated through random-effects pooling of studies identified through a systematic review. Results: Effect estimates and associated uncertainties varied by global region, but net increases in mortality risk associated with increased average annual temperatures (ranging from 0.1% to 1.1% per 1°C) were estimated for all global regions. Key recommendations for the development and utilization of health damage modules were provided by the expert panel and included the following: not relying on individual methodologies in estimating health damages; incorporating a broader range of cause-specific mortality impacts; improving the climate parameters available in economic models; accounting for socioeconomic trajectories and adaptation factors when estimating health damages; and carefully considering how air pollution impacts should be incorporated in economic-climate models.
More Notes: An expert panel of health researchers and climate economists conducted a systematic review and meta-analysis to refine the estimation of health impacts in economic-climate models, providing key recommendations such as diversifying methodologies, considering a wider range of mortality impacts, enhancing climate parameters in models, accounting for socioeconomic factors, and carefully integrating air pollution impacts.
More Notes-sourcematerial: An expert panel of 26 health researchers and climate economists gathered ... to conduct a systematic review and meta-analysis and recommend improvements to the estimation of health impacts in economic-climate models. ... Key recommendations for the development and utilization of health damage modules were provided by the expert panel and included the following: not relying on individual methodologies in estimating health damages; incorporating a broader range of cause-specific mortality impacts; improving the climate parameters available in economic models; accounting for socioeconomic trajectories and adaptation factors when estimating health damages; and carefully considering how air pollution impacts should be incorporated in economic-climate models.

Paper Title: Valuation of long-term coastal wetland changes in the U.S.
Outcome(s) of Interest: Wetland loss (2.0-10.7 million acres by end of century), increased restoration costs ($1.5-$3.1 billion annually by end of century), lost ecosystem services ($2.5-$6.1 billion annually by end of century).
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "By the end of the century, total wetland losses range from 2.0 to 10.7 million acres across sea level rise scenarios... By the end of the century, annual restoration costs reach $1.5 and $3.1 billion for RCP 4.5 and RCP8.5, respectively. The lost ecosystem services, together, reach annual economic impacts that are much higher, reaching $2.5 billion for RCP4.5 and $6.1 billion for RCP8.5."
Drivers of Risk: Sea level rise; greenhouse gas emissions (RCP4.5, RCP8.5); accretion rate uncertainty.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Sea level rise threatens the coastal landscape...we estimate an ensemble of future changes in coastal wetland areas considering both sea level rise, future greenhouse gas emissions, and accretion rate uncertainty...For Representative Concentration Pathway (RCP) 4.5 and RCP8.5, respectively, cummulative net wetland area loss is 1.8 and 2.4 million acres by 2050 and 3.5 and 5.2 million acres by 2100.
Category-sourcematerial: Sea level rise threatens the coastal landscape, including coastal wetlands...By the end of the century, total wetland losses range from 2.0 to 10.7 million acres across sea level rise scenarios...We then estimate economic impacts with two distinct approaches: restoration cost and ecosystem services.

Methodology: The study uses the NOAA marsh migration model to estimate future changes in coastal wetland areas based on sea level rise, greenhouse gas emissions scenarios (RCP4.5 and RCP8.5), and accretion rate uncertainty; economic impacts are then estimated using restoration cost and ecosystem services valuation approaches, focusing on coastal property protection from flooding and carbon sequestration (using the social cost of carbon).

This loss could result in annual restoration costs of $1.5 to $3.1 billion and lost ecosystem services (coastal property protection and carbon sequestration) with annual economic impacts of $2.5 to $6.1 billion by the end of the century, depending on the emissions scenario.

Paper Title: Population genomics provides insights into lineage divergence and local adaptation within the cotton bollworm
Outcome(s) of Interest: Local adaptation of cotton bollworm lineages; climate adaptation genes identified; potential for improved pest management.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "Our findings reveal fundamental knowledge of the local adaptation of different cotton bollworm lineages and will guide the formulation of cotton bollworm management measures at different scales." ... "Selective sweep analyses identified a series of Gene Ontology categories related to climate adaptation, feeding behaviour and insecticide tolerance." ... "Historical inference showed that effective population size changes coincided with global temperature fluctuation."
Drivers of Risk: Global temperature fluctuation; climate adaptation; insecticide tolerance.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: Historical inference showed that effective population size changes coincided with global temperature fluctuation. Selective sweep analyses identified a series of Gene Ontology categories related to climate adaptation, feeding behaviour and insecticide tolerance.
Category-sourcematerial: "Despite the significant threat it poses to economic crops worldwide" and "will guide the formulation of cotton bollworm management measures at different scales."
Approach-sourcematerial: Historical inference showed that effective population size changes coincided with global temperature fluctuation. Selective sweep analyses identified a series of Gene Ontology categories related to climate adaptation...
Methodology: De novo assembled a chromosome-level reference genome of H. a. armigera, constructed an ultradense variation map across 14 cotton bollworm populations, performed historical inference based on effective population size changes, and used selective sweep analyses to identify Gene Ontology categories.
Methodology-sourcematerial: We de novo assembled a high-quality chromosome-level reference genome of H. a. armigera (contig N50 = 7.34 Mb), with 99.13% of the HaSCD2 assembly assigned to 31 chromosomes (Z-chromosome + 30 autosomes). We constructed an ultradense variation map across 14 cotton bollworm populations...Historical inference showed that effective population size changes coincided with global temperature fluctuation...Selective sweep analyses identified a series of Gene Ontology categories related to climate adaptation, feeding behaviour and insecticide tolerance.
Highlights: A high-quality chromosome-level genome of the cotton bollworm Helicoverpa armigera was assembled, revealing a novel lineage in northwestern China, historical population size changes coinciding with global temperature fluctuations, and genes related to circadian rhythm, climate adaptation, feeding behavior, and insecticide tolerance exhibiting differentiation and selective sweeps.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "We de novo assembled a high-quality chromosome-level reference genome of H. a. armigera...We constructed an ultradense variation map across 14 cotton bollworm populations and identified a novel lineage in northwestern China. Historical inference showed that effective population size changes coincided with global temperature fluctuation. We identified nine differentiated genes...of which per and clk genes are involved in circadian rhythm. Selective sweep analyses identified a series of Gene Ontology categories related to climate adaptation, feeding behaviour and insecticide tolerance."
More Notes: Cotton bollworm population size fluctuates with global temperatures, and genetic analysis reveals genes linked to climate adaptation, feeding behavior, and insecticide tolerance in different lineages.

Paper Title: Integrated community-based approaches to urban pluvial flooding research, trends and future directions: A review
Outcome(s) of Interest: Range of socio-environmental impacts increasing due to biophysical change and human-environment interactions.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: Urban pluvial flooding (UPF) resulting from localized, intense, rainfall-generated ponding and overland flow causes a range of socio-environmental impacts. ... Its impacts are increasing due to both biophysical change (e.g., global warming) and the interactions between the human and physical dimensions of the urban environment (e.g., land-use change).
Drivers of Risk: biophysical change (global warming); human-environment interactions (land-use change); physical, historical, social, cultural, institutional, and economic conditions.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: UPF is driven by a complex set of interconnected factors, including physical, historical, social, cultural, institutional, and economic conditions. Its impacts are increasing due to both biophysical change (e.g., global warming) and the interactions between the human and physical dimensions of the urban environment (e.g., land-use change).
Category-sourcematerial: Urban pluvial flooding (UPF) resulting from localized, intense, rainfall-generated ponding and overland flow causes a range of socio-environmental impacts.
Approach-sourcematerial: This paper presents findings on a review of current integrated community-based approaches to UPF research and discusses how scholars have approached this problem and its management.
Methodology: The paper reviews current integrated community-based approaches to urban pluvial flooding (UPF) research, examining how scholars have approached the problem and its management, and identifying limitations in capturing the multi-dimensional nature of UPF.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "This paper presents findings on a review of current integrated community-based approaches to UPF research and discusses how scholars have approached this problem and its management. The limitations of these approaches to fully capture the multi-dimensional nature of UPF are explored in detail, and research gaps are identified."
Highlights: Urban pluvial flooding (UPF), caused by intense rainfall and overland flow, is increasing due to global warming and land-use change, impacting socio-environmental conditions; integrated community-based approaches to UPF research, including citizen science and socio-hydrology, are being explored but have limitations in capturing UPF's multi-dimensional nature, necessitating further transdisciplinary research.
Highlights-sourcematerial: Urban pluvial flooding (UPF) resulting from localized, intense, rainfall-generated ponding and overland flow causes a range of socio-environmental impacts. UPF is driven by a complex set of interconnected factors, including physical, historical, social, cultural, institutional, and economic conditions. Its impacts are increasing due to both biophysical change (e.g., global warming) and the interactions between the human and physical dimensions of the urban environment (e.g., land-use change). Different integrated approaches such as citizen science and socio-hydrology have tried to address UPF by coupling humans and environmental systems, reflecting the possible outcomes from the interaction between disciplines—albeit not without limitations. The limitations of these approaches to fully capture the multi-dimensional nature of UPF are explored in detail, and research gaps are identified. Finally, the paper provides suggestions for future research based on a transdisciplinary, transformative citizen science approach.
More Notes: Urban pluvial flooding (UPF) is a complex issue driven by interconnected physical and human factors, exacerbated by climate change and land-use changes, requiring comprehensive, integrated, and transdisciplinary approaches like citizen science and socio-hydrology to address its increasing socio-environmental impacts.

Paper Title: Decreasing causal impacts of El Niño–Southern Oscillation on future fire activities
Outcome(s) of Interest: Future fire activities (fire carbon emissions) in tropical/subtropical regions of Africa, Asia, Oceania, and America; decreased ENSO impact on global fire activities in future projections compared to historical period.
Outcome(s) of Interest-sourcematerial: "Here we use model outputs from the Coupled Modeling Intercomparison Project Phase 6 to systematically evaluate the response of future fire activities (i.e., fire carbon emissions) to ENSO during the period 2015–2100 over different continents. Our results demonstrate that the impacts of ENSO on fires are found in the tropical and subtropical regions of Africa, Asia, Oceania, and America... These results imply a decrease in ENSO impacts on global fire activities in future projections compared to the historical period 1915–2000."
Drivers of Risk: ENSO, warming environment, fuel load, ignition sources.
Drivers of Risk-sourcematerial: "further understanding of the main drivers of wildfires is necessary...The El Niño-Southern Oscillation (ENSO) is a major climate mode and is expected to affect global wildfires...future fires and ecosystems recovery might become increasingly challenging to manage under warming environment..."
Category-sourcematerial: Wildfires alter the composition and structure of ecosystems and result in huge economic costs. [...] Here we use model outputs from the Coupled Modeling Intercomparison Project Phase 6 to systematically evaluate the response of future fire activities (i.e., fire carbon emissions) to ENSO during the period 2015–2100 over different continents. [...] This work might contribute to the ENSO-based forecasts of global fire activities.
Approach-sourcematerial: Here we use model outputs from the Coupled Modeling Intercomparison Project Phase 6 to systematically evaluate the response of future fire activities (i.e., fire carbon emissions) to ENSO during the period 2015–2100 over different continents.
Methodology: Uses Coupled Modeling Intercomparison Project Phase 6 (CMIP6) model outputs to evaluate the response of future fire carbon emissions to ENSO from 2015-2100 across continents, comparing historical (1915-2000) and future (SSP2-4.5 & SSP5-8.5) scenarios to quantify changes in ENSO's influence on global fire activity.
Methodology-sourcematerial: "Here we use model outputs from the Coupled Modeling Intercomparison Project Phase 6 to systematically evaluate the response of future fire activities (i.e., fire carbon emissions) to ENSO during the period 2015–2100 over different continents...In historical experiment, the areas with significant ENSO effects on wildfires account for approximately 5.85% of land-area and this ratio decreases to approximately 5.39% and 2.92% of land-area in the future scenarios SSP2–4.5 and SSP5–8.5, respectively...This work might contribute to the ENSO-based forecasts of global fire activities."
Highlights: ENSO significantly influences fire carbon emissions in tropical and subtropical regions of Africa, Asia, Oceania, and America, but has limited impact in high-latitude regions like Alaska and far eastern Europe; its influence on global fire activities is projected to decrease in the future under SSP2-4.5 and SSP5-8.5 scenarios compared to the historical period.
Highlights-sourcematerial: "The impacts of ENSO on fires are found in the tropical and subtropical regions of Africa, Asia, Oceania, and America, while ENSO impacts over high latitude regions are very limited over Alaska and far eastern Europe...the role of ENSO on fire activities over subtropical regions might be more important than previously understood...the areas with significant ENSO effects on wildfires account for approximately 5.85% of land-area and this ratio decreases to approximately 5.39% and 2.92% of land-area in the future scenarios SSP2–4.5 and SSP5–8.5, respectively. These results imply a decrease in ENSO impacts on global fire activities in future projections compared to the historical period 1915–2000."
More Notes: ENSO's influence on wildfires, primarily affecting tropical and subtropical regions, is projected to decrease globally in future scenarios compared to the historical period.

These results imply a decrease in ENSO impacts on global fire activities in future projections compared to the historical period 1915–2000."

Space disaggregation-sourcematerial: Here we use model outputs from the Coupled Modeling Intercomparison Project Phase 6 to systematically evaluate the response of future fire activities (i.e., fire carbon emissions) to ENSO during the period 2015–2100 over different continents. Our results demonstrate that the impacts of ENSO on fires are found in the tropical and subtropical regions of Africa, Asia, Oceania, and America, while ENSO impacts over high latitude regions are very limited over Alaska and far eastern Europe.

Economic dynamics-sourcematerial: Wildfires alter the composition and structure of ecosystems and result in huge economic costs. While future fires and ecosystems recovery might become increasingly challenging to manage under warming environment, further understanding of the main drivers of wildfires is necessary. The El Niño-Southern Oscillation (ENSO) is a major climate mode and is expected to affect global wildfires. Nevertheless, there is uncertainty regarding the causal impacts of ENSO on future fire activities at the global scale. Here we use model outputs from the Coupled Modeling Intercomparison Project Phase 6 to systematically evaluate the response of future fire activities (i.e., fire carbon emissions) to ENSO during the period 2015–2100 over different continents. Our results demonstrate that the impacts of ENSO on fires are found in the tropical and subtropical regions of Africa, Asia, Oceania, and America, while ENSO impacts over high latitude regions are very limited over Alaska and far eastern Europe. We showed that the role of ENSO on fire activities over subtropical regions might be more important than previously understood. High consistency between models is observed for the impacted regions. In historical experiment, the areas with significant ENSO effects on wildfires account for approximately 5.85% of land-area and this ratio decreases to approximately 5.39% and 2.92% of land-area in the future scenarios SSP2–4.5 and SSP5–8.5, respectively. These results imply a decrease in ENSO impacts on global fire activities in future projections compared to the historical period 1915–2000. This work might contribute to the ENSO-based forecasts of global fire activities. © 2022 Elsevier B.V.
